text: Kyiv, also Kiev, is the capital and most populous city of Ukraine.
title: Kyiv

text: Located in the north-central part of the country, it straddles both banks of the Dnieper River.
title: Kyiv

text: As of January 2022, the population of Kyiv was 2,952,301, making it the seventh-most populous city in Europe.
title: Kyiv

text: Kyiv is an important industrial, scientific, educational, and cultural center.
title: Kyiv

text: It is home to high-tech industries, higher education institutions, and historical landmarks.
title: Kyiv

text: The city has an extensive public transport system, which includes the Kyiv Metro.
title: Kyiv

text: The city's name is said to derive from the name of Kyi, one of its four legendary founders.
title: Kyiv

text: One of the oldest cities in Eastern Europe, during its history, it has passed through several stages of prominence and obscurity.
title: Kyiv

text: It probably existed as a commercial center as early as the 5th century.
title: Kyiv

text: A Slavic settlement on the great trade route between Scandinavia and Constantinople, it was a tributary of the Khazars, until its capture by the Varangians (Vikings) in the mid-9th century.
title: Kyiv

text: Under Varangian rule, the city became a capital of Kievan Rus', the first East Slavic state.
title: Kyiv

text: During the Siege of Kiev (1240), Kyiv was left in ruins; it lost most of its influence for the centuries to come.
title: Kyiv

text: Kyiv grew into a centre of Eastern Orthodox learning during the 16th century.
title: Kyiv

text: It prospered during the Russian Empire's Industrial Revolution in the late 19th century, becoming a centre of industry, commerce, and administration.
title: Kyiv

text: In 1918, the Ukrainian People's Republic declared independence from the Russian Republic after the October Revolution, and Kyiv became the new republic's capital.
title: Kyiv

text: Following the Ukrainian-Soviet and Polish-Soviet wars, Kyiv became part of the Ukrainian SSR, of which it became the capital in 1934.
title: Kyiv

text: The city suffered significant destruction during World War II, but recovered after the war as the Soviet Union's third-largest city.
title: Kyiv

text: Following the collapse of the Soviet Union and Ukrainian independence in 1991, during the country's transformation into a market economy and electoral democracy, Kyiv has continued to be Ukraine's largest and wealthiest city.
title: Kyiv

text: According to Oleg Trubachyov's etymological dictionary, the name derives from the Old East Slavic *Kyjevŭ gordŭ (literally, "Kyi's castle", "Kyi's gord"), from Proto-Slavic *kyjevъ.
title: Kyiv

text: This etymology has been questioned, for instance by Mykhailo Hrushevsky, who called it an "etymological myth", and meant that the names of the legendary founders are in turn based on place names.
title: Kyiv

text: According to the Ukrainian-Canadian linguist Jaroslav Rudnyckyj, the name can be connected to the Proto-Slavic root *kyjь, but should be interpreted as meaning "stick, pole" as in its modern Ukrainian equivalent Кий.
title: Kyiv

text: The name should in that case be interpreted as "palisaded settlement".
title: Kyiv

text: Kyiv is the official romanized Ukrainian name for the city, and it is used for legislative and official acts.
title: Kyiv

text: The population around Kyiv during the Bronze Age formed part of the so-called Trypillian culture, as evidenced by artifacts from that culture found in the area.
title: Kyiv

text: During the early Iron Age certain tribes settled around Kyiv that practiced land cultivation, husbandry and trading with the Scythians and ancient states of the northern Black Sea coast.
title: Kyiv

text: The traditional founding date is 482 CE, so the city celebrated its 1,500th anniversary in 1982.
title: Kyiv

text: Archaeological data indicates a founding in the sixth or seventh centuries, with some researchers dating the founding as late as the late 9th century.
title: Kyiv

text: There are several legendary accounts of the origin of the city.
title: Kyiv

text: One tells of members of a Slavic tribe (Eastern Polans), brothers Kyi (the eldest, after whom the city was named), Shchek, Khoryv, and their sister Lybid, who founded the city (See the Primary Chronicle).
title: Kyiv

text: Another legend states that Saint Andrew passed through the area in the 1st century.
title: Kyiv

text: Where the city is now he erected a cross, where a church later was built.
title: Kyiv

text: Since the Middle Ages an image of Saint Michael has represented the city as well as the duchy.
title: Kyiv

text: There is little historical evidence pertaining to the period when the city was founded.
title: Kyiv

text: Scattered Slavic settlements existed in the area from the 6th century, but it is unclear whether any of them later developed into the city.
title: Kyiv

text: On the Ptolemy world map there are several settlements indicated along the mid-stream of Borysthenes, among which is Azagarium, which some historians believe to be the predecessor to Kyiv.
title: Kyiv

text: However, according to the 1773 Dictionary of Ancient Geography of Alexander Macbean, that settlement corresponds to the modern city of Chernobyl.
title: Kyiv

text: Just south of Azagarium, there is another settlement, Amadoca, believed to be the capital of the Amadoci people living in an area between the marshes of Amadoca in the west and the Amadoca mountains in the east.
title: Kyiv

text: Another name for Kyiv mentioned in history, the origin of which is not completely clear, is Sambat, which apparently has something to do with the Khazar Empire.
title: Kyiv

text: The Primary Chronicle says the residents of Kyiv told Askold "there were three brothers Kyi, Shchek, and Khoriv.
title: Kyiv

text: They founded this town and died, and now we are staying and paying taxes to their relatives the Khazars".
title: Kyiv

text: In De Administrando Imperio, Constantine Porphyrogenitus mentions a caravan of small cargo boats which assembled annually, and writes, "They come down the river Dnieper and assemble at the strong-point of Kyiv (Kioava), also called Sambatas".
title: Kyiv

text: At least three Arabic-speaking 10th century geographers who traveled the area mention the city of Zānbat as the chief city of the Russes.
title: Kyiv

text: Among them are ibn Rustah, Abu Sa'id Gardezi, and an author of the Hudud al-'Alam.
title: Kyiv

text: The texts of those authors were discovered by Russian orientalist Alexander Tumansky.
title: Kyiv

text: The etymology of Sambat has been argued by many historians, including Grigoriy Ilyinsky, Nikolay Karamzin, Jan Potocki, Nikolay Lambin, Joachim Lelewel, and Guðbrandur Vigfússon.
title: Kyiv

text: The Primary Chronicle states that at some point during the late 9th or early 10th century Askold and Dir, who may have been of Viking or Varangian descent, ruled in Kyiv.
title: Kyiv

text: Other historians suggest that Magyar tribes ruled the city between 840 and 878, before migrating with some Khazar tribes to the Carpathian Basin.
title: Kyiv

text: The Primary Chronicle mentions Hungarians passing near Kyiv.
title: Kyiv

text: Askold's Grave was previously known as "Uhorske urochyshche" (Hungarian place).
title: Kyiv

text: According to the aforementioned scholars the building of the fortress of Kyiv was finished in 840 under the leadership of Keő (Keve), Csák, and Geréb, three brothers, possibly members of the Tarján tribe.
title: Kyiv

text: The three names appear in the Kyiv Chronicle as Kyi, Shchek, and Khoryv and may be not of Slavic origin, as Russian historians have always struggled to account for their meanings and origins.
title: Kyiv

text: In 968 the nomadic Pechenegs attacked and then besieged the city.
title: Kyiv

text: By 1000 CE the city had a population of 45,000.
title: Kyiv

text: According to Thietmar of Merseburg, who described Kyiv in his chronicle from 1017, during that time the city had over 400 churches and 8 markets.
title: Kyiv

text: Its significance as the capital of Rus' was underlined by large-scale construction projects, such as the erection of St.
title: Kyiv

text: During the period of feudal divisions in Rus', Kyiv retained its special meaning and became an object of numerous campaigns by various princes.
title: Kyiv

text: Between 1146 and 1246 the city changed hands 47 times, being ruled by 24 princes, and in 35 cases their tenure lasted less than a year.
title: Kyiv

text: In March 1169, Grand Prince Andrey Bogolyubsky of Vladimir-Suzdal sacked Kyiv, leaving the old town and the prince's hall in ruins.
title: Kyiv

text: He took many pieces of religious artwork – including the Theotokos of Vladimir icon – from Vyshhorod.
title: Kyiv

text: In 1203, Prince Rurik Rostislavich and his Kipchak allies captured and burned Kyiv.
title: Kyiv

text: In the 1230s, the city was besieged and ravaged several times by different Rus princes.
title: Kyiv

text: Deprived of its own dynasty, under the Golden Horde Kyiv was ruled according to yarlyks issued by Mongol rulers, but its princes only nominally controlled the city, barely appearing there.
title: Kyiv

text: In the early 1320s, a Lithuanian army led by Grand Duke Gediminas defeated a Slavic army led by Stanislav of Kyiv at the Battle on the Irpen' River and conquered the city.
title: Kyiv

text: The Tatars, who also claimed Kyiv, retaliated in 1324–1325, so while Kyiv was ruled by a Lithuanian prince, it had to pay tribute to the Golden Horde.
title: Kyiv

text: In 1482, Crimean Tatars sacked and burned much of Kyiv.
title: Kyiv

text: The 1658 Treaty of Hadiach envisaged Kyiv becoming the capital of the Grand Duchy of Rus' within the Polish–Lithuanian–Ruthenian Commonwealth, but this provision of the treaty never went into operation.
title: Kyiv

text: Local clergy supported the rebellion and provided its ideological foundations, encouraging Khmelnytsky to depict himself as a protector of Orthodox faith and defender of Ruthenian people.
title: Kyiv

text: Occupied by Russian troops since the 1654 Treaty of Pereyaslav, Kyiv became a part of the Tsardom of Russia from 1667 with the Truce of Andrusovo and enjoyed self-government inside of an autonomous Cossack Hetmanate.
title: Kyiv

text: None of the Polish-Russian treaties concerning Kyiv have ever been ratified.
title: Kyiv

text: In the Russian Empire, Kyiv was a primary Christian centre, attracting pilgrims, and the cradle of many of the empire's most important religious figures, but until the 19th century, the city's commercial importance remained marginal.
title: Kyiv

text: In 1834, the Russian government established Saint Vladimir University, now known as the Taras Shevchenko National University of Kyiv in honour of Ukrainian poet Taras Shevchenko (1814–1861), who worked as a field researcher and editor for its geography department.
title: Kyiv

text: The medical faculty of Saint Vladimir University, separated into an independent institution in 1919–1921 during the Soviet period, became the Bogomolets National Medical University in 1995.
title: Kyiv

text: During the 18th and 19th centuries, the Imperial Russian Army and ecclesiastical authorities dominated the city life; the Russian Orthodox Church had involvement in a significant part of Kyiv's infrastructure and commercial activity.
title: Kyiv

text: Following the gradual loss of Ukraine's autonomy, Kyiv experienced growing Russification in the 19th century, by means of Russian migration, administrative actions, and social modernization.
title: Kyiv

text: At the beginning of the 20th century the Russian-speaking part of the population dominated the city centre, while the lower classes living on the outskirts retained Ukrainian folk culture to a significant extent.
title: Kyiv

text: However, enthusiasts among ethnic Ukrainian aristocrats, soldiers, and merchants made attempts to preserve the native culture in Kyiv, by clandestine book-printing, amateur theatre, folk studies, etc.
title: Kyiv

text: During the Russian industrial revolution in the late 19th century, Kyiv became an important trade and transportation centre of the Russian Empire, specialising in sugar and grain export by railway and on the Dnieper river.
title: Kyiv

text: By 1900, the city had also become a significant industrial centre, with a population of 250,000.
title: Kyiv

text: Landmarks of that period include the railway infrastructure, the foundation of numerous educational and cultural facilities, and notable architectural monuments (mostly merchant-oriented).
title: Kyiv

text: In 1892, the first electric tram line of the Russian Empire started running in Kyiv (the third in the world).
title: Kyiv

text: During the last three months of 1919, Kyiv was intermittently controlled by the White Army.
title: Kyiv

text: Kyiv changed hands sixteen times from the end of 1918 to August 1920.
title: Kyiv

text: From 1921 to 1991, the city formed part of the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic, which became a founding republic of the Soviet Union in 1922.
title: Kyiv

text: In 1934, Kyiv became the capital of Soviet Ukraine.
title: Kyiv

text: The city boomed again during the years of Soviet industrialization as its population grew rapidly and many industrial giants were established, some of which exist today.
title: Kyiv

text: In World War II, the city again suffered significant damage, and Nazi Germany occupied it from 19 September 1941 to 6 November 1943.
title: Kyiv

text: Axis forces killed or captured more than 600,000 Soviet soldiers in the great encircling Battle of Kyiv in 1941.
title: Kyiv

text: Allegedly in response to the actions of the NKVD, the Germans rounded up all the local Jews they could find, nearly 34,000, and massacred them at Babi Yar in Kyiv on 29 and 30 September 1941.
title: Kyiv

text: In the months that followed, thousands more were taken to Babi Yar where they were shot.
title: Kyiv

text: It is estimated that the Germans murdered more than 100,000 people of various ethnic groups, mostly civilians, at Babi Yar during World War II.
title: Kyiv

text: Kyiv recovered economically in the post-war years, becoming once again the third-most important city of the Soviet Union.
title: Kyiv

text: The catastrophic accident at the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant in 1986 occurred only 100 km (62 mi) north of the city.
title: Kyiv

text: In 2004–2005, the city played host to the largest post-Soviet public demonstrations up to that time, in support of the Orange Revolution.
title: Kyiv

text: From November 2013 until February 2014, central Kyiv became the primary location of Euromaidan.
title: Kyiv

text: During the onset of the Russian invasion of Ukraine in February 2022, Russian forces attempted to seize Kyiv but were repelled by Ukrainian forces on the outskirts of the city; Kyiv itself escaped major damage.
title: Kyiv

text: However, the city's unique landscape distinguishes it from the surrounding region.
title: Kyiv

text: Originally on the west bank, today Kyiv is on both sides of the Dnieper, which flows southwards through the city towards the Black Sea.
title: Kyiv

text: The older and higher western part of the city sits on numerous wooded hills (Kyiv Hills), with ravines and small rivers.
title: Kyiv

text: Kyiv's geographical relief contributed to its toponyms, such as Podil ("lower"), Pechersk ("caves"), and uzviz (a steep street, "descent").
title: Kyiv

text: The historical core of Kyiv is a part of the larger Dnieper Upland adjoining the western bank of the Dnieper in its mid-flow, which contributes to the city's elevation change.
title: Kyiv

text: The northern outskirts of the city border the Polesian Lowland.
title: Kyiv

text: Kyiv expanded into the Dnieper Lowland on the left bank (to the east) as late as the 20th century.
title: Kyiv

text: The whole portion of Kyiv on the left bank of the Dnieper is generally referred to as the Left Bank (Лівий берег, Livyi bereh).
title: Kyiv

text: Significant areas of the left bank Dnieper valley were artificially sand-deposited, and are protected by dams.
title: Kyiv

text: Within the city the Dnieper River forms a branching system of tributaries, isles, and harbors within the city limits.
title: Kyiv

text: The city is close to the mouth of the Desna River and the Kyiv Reservoir in the north, and the Kaniv Reservoir in the south.
title: Kyiv

text: Both the Dnieper and Desna rivers are navigable at Kyiv, although regulated by the reservoir shipping locks and limited by winter freeze-over.
title: Kyiv

text: In total, there are 448 bodies of open water within the boundaries of Kyiv, which include the Dnieper itself, its reservoirs, and several small rivers, dozens of lakes and artificially created ponds.
title: Kyiv

text: Additionally, the city has 16 developed beaches (totalling 140 hectares) and 35 near-water recreational areas (covering more than 1,000 hectares).
title: Kyiv

text: Many are used for pleasure and recreation, although some of the bodies of water are not suitable for swimming.
title: Kyiv

text: The warmest months are June, July, and August, with mean temperatures of 13.8 to 24.8 °C (56.8 to 76.6 °F).
title: Kyiv

text: The coldest are December, January, and February, with mean temperatures of −4.6 to −1.1 °C (23.7 to 30.0 °F).
title: Kyiv

text: The highest ever temperature recorded in the city was 39.4 °C (102.9 °F) on 30 July 1936.
title: Kyiv

text: The coldest temperature ever recorded in the city was −32.9 °C (−27.2 °F) on 11 January 1951.
title: Kyiv

text: The most significant difference is that the city is considered as a region of Ukraine (see Regions of Ukraine).
title: Kyiv

text: It is the only city that has double jurisdiction.
title: Kyiv

text: The Head of City State Administration – the city's governor – is appointed by the president of Ukraine, while the Head of the City Council – the mayor of Kyiv – is elected by local popular vote.
title: Kyiv

text: The mayor of Kyiv is Vitali Klitschko, who was sworn in on 5 June 2014, after he had won the 25 May 2014 Kyiv mayoral elections with almost 57% of the votes.
title: Kyiv

text: Since 25 June 2014, Klitschko is also Head of Kyiv City Administration.
title: Kyiv

text: Klitschko was last reelected in the 2020 Kyiv local election with 50.52% of the votes, in the first round of the election.
title: Kyiv

text: Most key buildings of the national government are along Hrushevsky Street (vulytsia Mykhaila Hrushevskoho) and Institute Street (vulytsia Instytutska).
title: Kyiv

text: Hrushevsky Street is named after the Ukrainian academician, politician, historian, and statesman Mykhailo Hrushevsky, who served as the chairman of the Central Rada and wrote multiple academic books, notably History of Ukraine-Rus'.
title: Kyiv

text: That portion of the city is also unofficially known as the government quarter (урядовий квартал).
title: Kyiv

text: The city state administration and council is in the Kyiv City council building on Khreshchatyk Street.
title: Kyiv

text: Historically on the western right bank of the river, the city expanded into the left bank only in the 20th century.
title: Kyiv

text: Most of Kyiv's attractions as well as the majority of business and governmental institutions are on the right bank.
title: Kyiv

text: There are large industrial and green areas in both the Right Bank and the Left Bank.
title: Kyiv

text: In 1833–1834 according to Tsar Nicholas I's decree, Kyiv was subdivided into 6 police raions (districts); later being increased to 10.
title: Kyiv

text: In 1917, there were 8 Raion Councils (Duma), which were reorganised by bolsheviks into 6 Party-Territory Raions.
title: Kyiv

text: During the Soviet era, as the city was expanding, the number of raions also gradually increased.
title: Kyiv

text: The last district reform took place in 2001 when the number of districts was decreased from 14 to 10.
title: Kyiv

text: Under Oleksandr Omelchenko (mayor from 1999 to 2006), there were further plans for the merger of some districts and revision of their boundaries, and the total number of districts had been planned to be decreased from 10 to 7.
title: Kyiv

text: With the election of the new mayor-elect (Leonid Chernovetskyi) in 2006, these plans were shelved.
title: Kyiv

text: The historic changes in population are shown in the side table.
title: Kyiv

text: According to the census, some 1,393,000 (53.3%) were female and 1,219,000 (46.7%) were male.
title: Kyiv

text: Comparing the results with the previous census (1989) shows the trend of population ageing which, while prevalent throughout the country, is partly offset in Kyiv by the inflow of working age migrants.
title: Kyiv

text: Some 1,069,700 people had higher or completed secondary education, a significant increase of 21.7% since 1989.
title: Kyiv

text: According to the census of March 2, 1874, conducted by the local branch of the Russian Geographical Society, there were 127,205 people living in Kyiv.
title: Kyiv

text: Of these, 80% spoke "Russian", 11% spoke "Jewish", 6% spoke Polish and 2% spoke German.
title: Kyiv

text: Of the "Russian" speakers, 39% were recorded as speaking Little Russian (Ukrainian), which meant that Ukrainian speakers accounted for 30% of the city as a whole.
title: Kyiv

text: Of the remaining "Russian" speakers, however, there were only 10% who spoke Greater Russian (Russian) and 2% who spoke Belarusian.
title: Kyiv

text: The remaining 49% spoke in "generally Russian speech".
title: Kyiv

text: According to the official census of 1897, the number of Great Russian speakers rose to 54%; speakers of Little Russian accounted for 22%.
title: Kyiv

text: By the September 1917 city-census of Kyiv, conducted by the authorities of the Ukrainian People's Republic, the Ukrainian share of the population had been reduced to only 16%, while Russians now made up a majority at 50%.
title: Kyiv

text: The March 1919 Kyiv city census, conducted by the Bolshevik authorities, showed an increase in the percentage of the population identifying as Ukrainian to 25%.
title: Kyiv

text: From then on, the city's Ukrainian population once again began to expand in terms of their share of the population, slowly returning to its former level.
title: Kyiv

text: By the 1926 Soviet census, Ukrainians, at 41.6%, had once again begun to outnumber Russians, who made up 25.5%.
title: Kyiv

text: By the 1959 Soviet census, Kyiv was once more a Ukrainian majority city, with 60% of the population identifying as such, the same percentage as in 1874.
title: Kyiv

text: According to the 2001 census data, more than 130 nationalities and ethnic groups reside within the territory of Kyiv.
title: Kyiv

text: Ukrainians constitute the largest ethnic group in Kyiv, accounting for 2,110,800 people, or 82.2% of the population.
title: Kyiv

text: Russians comprise 337,300 (13.1%), Jews 17,900 (0.7%), Belarusians 16,500 (0.6%), Poles 6,900 (0.3%), Armenians 4,900 (0.2%), Azerbaijanis 2,600 (0.1%), Tatars 2,500 (0.1%), Georgians 2,400 (0.1%), Moldovans 1,900 (0.1%).
title: Kyiv

text: A 2015 study by the International Republican Institute found that 94% of Kyiv was ethnic Ukrainian, and 5% ethnic Russian.
title: Kyiv

text: According to a 2006 survey, Ukrainian is used at home by 23% of Kyivans, 52% use Russian, and 24% switch between both.
title: Kyiv

text: In the 2003 sociological survey, when the question "What language do you use in everyday life?" was asked, 52% said "mostly Russian", 32% "both Russian and Ukrainian in equal measure", 14% "mostly Ukrainian", and 4.3% "exclusively Ukrainian".
title: Kyiv

text: According to the census of 1897, of Kyiv's approximately 240,000 people approximately 56% of the population spoke the Russian language, 23% spoke the Ukrainian language, 13% spoke Yiddish, 7% spoke Polish and 1% spoke the Belarusian language.
title: Kyiv

text: The Jewish population remained relatively small until the nineteenth century.
title: Kyiv

text: A series of pogroms was carried out in 1882, and another in 1905.
title: Kyiv

text: On the eve of World War I, the city's Jewish population was over 81,000.
title: Kyiv

text: In 1939 there were approximately 224,000 Jews in Kyiv, some of whom fled the city ahead of the German invasion of the Soviet Union that began in June 1941.
title: Kyiv

text: On 29 and 30 September 1941, nearly 34,000 Kyivan Jews were massacred at Babi Yar by the Wehrmacht, SS, Ukrainian Auxiliary Police, and local collaborators.
title: Kyiv

text: Jews began returning to Kyiv at the end of the war, but experienced another pogrom in September 1945.
title: Kyiv

text: In the 21st century, Kyiv's Jewish community numbers about 20,000.
title: Kyiv

text: When the capital of the Ukrainian SSR was moved from Kharkiv to Kyiv many new buildings were commissioned to give the city "the gloss and polish of a capital".
title: Kyiv

text: In the discussions that centered on how to create a showcase city center, the current city center of Khreshchatyk and Maidan Nezalezhnosti (Independence Square) were not the obvious choices.
title: Kyiv

text: Some of the early, ultimately not materialised, ideas included a part of Pechersk, Lypky, European Square, and Mykhailivska Square.
title: Kyiv

text: The plans of building massive monuments (of Vladimir Lenin and Stalin) were also abandoned, due to lack of money (in the 1930s–1950s) and because of Kyiv's hilly landscape.
title: Kyiv

text: Experiencing rapid population growth between the 1970s and the mid-1990s, the city has continued its consistent growth after the turn of the millennium.
title: Kyiv

text: As a result, Kyiv's central districts provide a dotted contrast of new, modern buildings among the pale yellows, blues, and greys of older apartments.
title: Kyiv

text: Urban sprawl has gradually reduced, while population densities of suburbs has increased.
title: Kyiv

text: The most expensive properties are in the Pechersk and Khreshchatyk areas.
title: Kyiv

text: It is also prestigious to own a property in newly constructed buildings in the Kharkivskyi neighborhood or Obolon along the Dnieper.
title: Kyiv

text: Ukrainian independence at the turn of the millennium has heralded other changes.
title: Kyiv

text: Western-style residential complexes, modern nightclubs, classy restaurants and prestigious hotels opened in the centre.
title: Kyiv

text: And most importantly, with the easing of the visa rules in 2005, Ukraine is positioning itself as a prime tourist attraction, with Kyiv, among the other large cities, looking to profit from new opportunities.
title: Kyiv

text: The centre of Kyiv has been cleaned up and buildings have been restored and redecorated, especially Khreshchatyk and Maidan Nezalezhnosti.
title: Kyiv

text: Many historic areas of Kyiv, such as Andriivskyi Descent, have become popular street vendor locations, where one can find traditional Ukrainian art, religious items, books, game sets (most commonly chess) as well as jewellery for sale.
title: Kyiv

text: At the United Nations Climate Change Conference 2009, Kyiv was the only Commonwealth of Independent States city to have been inscribed into the TOP30 European Green City Index (placed 30th).
title: Kyiv

text: Kyiv's most famous historical architecture complexes are the St.
title: Kyiv

text: Sophia Cathedral and the Kyiv Pechersk Lavra (Monastery of the Caves), which are recognized by UNESCO as a World Heritage Site.
title: Kyiv

text: Noteworthy historical architectural landmarks also include the Mariinskyi Palace (designed and constructed from 1745 to 1752, then reconstructed in 1870), several Eastern Orthodox churches such as St.
title: Kyiv

text: One of Kyiv's widely recognized modern landmarks is the highly visible giant Mother Ukraine statue made of titanium standing at the National Museum of the History of Ukraine in the Second World War on the Right bank of the Dnieper.
title: Kyiv

text: Other notable sites is the cylindrical Salut hotel, across from Glory Square and the eternal flame at the World War Two memorial Tomb of the Unknown Soldier, and the House with Chimaeras.
title: Kyiv

text: Among Kyiv's best-known monuments are Mikhail Mikeshin's statue of Bohdan Khmelnytsky astride his horse near St.
title: Kyiv

text: Sophia Cathedral, the venerated Vladimir the Great (St.
title: Kyiv

text: Vladimir), the baptizer of Rus', overlooking the river above Podil from Saint Volodymyr Hill, the monument to Kyi, Shchek and Khoryv and Lybid, the legendary founders of the city at the Dnieper embankment.
title: Kyiv

text: Kyiv retained through centuries its cultural importance and even at times of relative decay, it remained the centre of primary importance of Eastern Orthodox Christianity.
title: Kyiv

text: The above-mentioned sites are also part of the Seven Wonders of Ukraine collection.
title: Kyiv

text: In September 2023, the UNESCO World Heritage Committee placed the Saint Sophia Cathedral and Kyiv Pechersk Lavra on the List of World Heritage in Danger.
title: Kyiv

text: The list is protected by the 1972 UNESCO Convention, ratified by both Russia and Ukraine.
title: Kyiv

text: Inclusion on the list is intended to mobilize urgent international support.
title: Kyiv

text: Kyiv's theatres include the Kyiv Opera House, Ivan Franko National Academic Drama Theater, Lesya Ukrainka National Academic Theater, the Kyiv Puppet Theater, October Palace, National Philharmonic of Ukraine and others.
title: Kyiv

text: In 1946 Kyiv had four theatres, one opera house and one concert hall, but most tickets then were allocated to "privileged groups".
title: Kyiv

text: Other significant cultural centres include the Dovzhenko Film Studios, and the Kyiv Circus.
title: Kyiv

text: In 2005, Kyiv hosted the 50th annual Eurovision Song Contest and in 2017 the 62nd annual Eurovision Song Contest.
title: Kyiv

text: Numerous songs and paintings were dedicated to the city.
title: Kyiv

text: Some songs became part of Russian, Ukrainian and Jewish folklore.
title: Kyiv

text: The most popular songs are "How not to love you, Kyiv of mine?" and "Kyiv Waltz".
title: Kyiv

text: Kyiv is known as a green city with two botanical gardens and numerous large and small parks.
title: Kyiv

text: The National Museum of the History of Ukraine in the Second World War is here, which offers both indoor and outdoor displays of military history and equipment surrounded by verdant hills overlooking the Dnieper river.
title: Kyiv

text: Among the numerous islands, Venetsiiskyi (or Hydropark) is the most developed.
title: Kyiv

text: It is accessible by metro or by car, and includes an amusement park, swimming beaches, boat rentals, and night clubs.
title: Kyiv

text: Other major islands include Trukhaniv, Muromets, and Dolobetskyi.
title: Kyiv

text: The Victory Park (Park Peremohy) near Darnytsia subway station is a popular destination for strollers, joggers, and cyclists.
title: Kyiv

text: Boating, fishing, and water sports are popular pastimes in Kyiv.
title: Kyiv

text: The area lakes and rivers freeze over in the winter and ice fishermen are a frequent sight, as are children with their ice skates.
title: Kyiv

text: However, the peak of summer draws out a greater mass of people to the shores for swimming or sunbathing, with daytime high temperatures sometimes reaching 30 to 34 °C (86 to 93 °F).
title: Kyiv

text: The centre of Kyiv (Maidan Nezalezhnosti and Khreshchatyk Street) becomes a large outdoor party place at night during summer months, with thousands of people having a good time in nearby restaurants, clubs and outdoor cafes.
title: Kyiv

text: The central streets are closed for auto traffic on weekends and holidays.
title: Kyiv

text: Andriivskyi Descent is one of the best known historic streets and a major tourist attraction in Kyiv.
title: Kyiv

text: A wide variety of farm produce is available in many of Kyiv's farmer markets with the Bessarabskyi Market in the very centre of the city being most famous.
title: Kyiv

text: Each residential region has its own market, or rynok.
title: Kyiv

text: Here one will find table after table of individuals hawking everything imaginable: vegetables, fresh and smoked meats, fish, cheese, honey, dairy products such as milk and home-made smetana (sour cream), caviar, cut flowers, housewares, tools and hardware, and clothing.
title: Kyiv

text: Each of the markets has its own unique mix of products with some markets devoted solely to specific wares such as automobiles, car parts, pets, clothing, flowers, and other things.
title: Kyiv

text: At the city's southern outskirts, near the historic Pyrohiv village, there is an outdoor museum officially called the Museum of Folk Architecture and Life of Ukraine.
title: Kyiv

text: It has an area of 1.5 square kilometres (1 sq mi).
title: Kyiv

text: This territory houses several "mini-villages" that represent by region the traditional rural architecture of Ukraine.
title: Kyiv

text: Kyiv also has numerous recreational attractions like bowling alleys, go-cart tracks, paintball venues, billiard halls and even shooting ranges.
title: Kyiv

text: In 2009 they recorded a total of 4.3 million visits.
title: Kyiv

text: The National Museum of the History of Ukraine in the Second World War is a memorial complex commemorating the Eastern Front of World War II in the hills on the right-bank of the Dnieper in Pechersk.
title: Kyiv

text: Kyiv Fortress is the 19th-century fortification buildings situated in Ukrainian capital Kyiv, that once belonged to western Russian fortresses.
title: Kyiv

text: These structures (once a united complex) were built in the Pechersk and neighbourhoods by the Russian army.
title: Kyiv

text: Some of the buildings are restored and turned into a museum, while others are in use in various military and commercial installations.
title: Kyiv

text: The National Art Museum of Ukraine is a museum dedicated to Ukrainian art.
title: Kyiv

text: The Golden Gate is a historic gateway in the ancient city's walls.
title: Kyiv

text: The name Zoloti Vorota (Golden Gate) is also used for a nearby theatre and a station of the Kyiv Metro.
title: Kyiv

text: Of these clubs, Dynamo Kyiv has had the most success over the course of its history.
title: Kyiv

text: Other prominent non-football sport clubs in the city include: the Sokil Kyiv ice hockey club and BC Budivelnyk basketball club.
title: Kyiv

text: Both of these teams play in the highest Ukrainian leagues for their respective sports.
title: Kyiv

text: Budivelnyk was founded in 1945, Sokil was founded in 1963, during the existence of the Soviet Union.
title: Kyiv

text: Both these teams play their home games at the Kyiv Palace of Sports.
title: Kyiv

text: During the 1980 Summer Olympics held in the Soviet Union, Kyiv held the preliminary matches and the quarter-finals of the football tournament at its Olympic Stadium, which was reconstructed specially for the event.
title: Kyiv

text: Other notable sport stadiums/sport complexes in Kyiv include the Valeriy Lobanovskyi Dynamo Stadium, the Palace of Sports, among many others.
title: Kyiv

text: Most Ukrainian national teams play their home international matches in Kyiv.
title: Kyiv

text: Before the 2008–2009 Ukrainian financial crisis, the average annual growth in the number of foreign visits in Kyiv was 23% over a three-year period.
title: Kyiv

text: In 2009, a total of 1.6 million tourists stayed in Kyiv hotels, of whom almost 259,000 (c. 16%) were foreigners.
title: Kyiv

text: After UEFA Euro 2012, the city became a popular destination for European tourists.
title: Kyiv

text: A record number of 1.8 million foreign tourists was registered then along with about 2.5 million domestic tourists.
title: Kyiv

text: More than 850,000 foreign tourists visited Kyiv in the first half of 2018, as compared to 660,000 tourists over the same period in 2013.
title: Kyiv

text: As of 2018, the hotel occupancy rate from May to September averages 45–50%.
title: Kyiv

text: Hostels and three-star hotels are approximately 90% full, four-star hotels 65–70%.
title: Kyiv

text: It is the largest city in Ukraine in terms of both population and area and enjoys the highest levels of business activity.
title: Kyiv

text: On 1 January 2010, there were around 238,000 business entities registered in Kyiv.
title: Kyiv

text: Official figures show that between 2004 and 2008 Kyiv's economy outstripped the rest of the country's, growing by an annual average of 11.5%.
title: Kyiv

text: Following the Great Recession, Kyiv's economy suffered a severe setback in 2009 with gross regional product contracting by 13.5% in real terms.
title: Kyiv

text: Although a record high, the decline in activity was 1.6 percentage points smaller than that for the country as a whole.
title: Kyiv

text: The economy in Kyiv, as in the rest of Ukraine, recovered somewhat in 2010 and 2011.
title: Kyiv

text: Kyiv is a middle-income city, with prices comparable to many mid-size American cities (i.e., considerably lower than Western Europe).
title: Kyiv

text: Because the city has a large and diverse economic base and is not dependent on any single industry or company, its unemployment rate has historically been relatively low – only 3.75% over 2005–2008.
title: Kyiv

text: Indeed, even as the rate of joblessness jumped to 7.1% in 2009, it remained far below the national average of 9.6%.
title: Kyiv

text: As of January 2022, the average monthly salary in Kyiv reached 21,347 UAH (€540) gross and 17,184 UAH (€430) net.
title: Kyiv

text: Kyiv is the undisputed center of business and commerce of Ukraine and home to the country's largest companies, such as Naftogaz Ukrainy, Energorynok and Kyivstar.
title: Kyiv

text: In 2010, the city accounted for 18% of national retail sales and 24% of all construction activity.
title: Kyiv

text: Real estate is one of the major forces in Kyiv's economy.
title: Kyiv

text: Average prices of apartments are the highest in the country and among the highest in eastern Europe.
title: Kyiv

text: Kyiv also ranks high in terms of commercial real estate and has Ukraine's tallest office buildings (such as Gulliver and Parus) and some of Ukraine's biggest shopping malls (such as Dream Town and Ocean Plaza).
title: Kyiv

text: Kyiv is home to Ukraine's ministry of education and science, and is also noted for its contributions to medical and computer science research.
title: Kyiv

text: In 2016, UNIT Factory (Ukrainian National IT Factory) opened.
title: Kyiv

text: It offers a completely new format of IT education.
title: Kyiv

text: The education is completely free for all trainees subject to compliance with the terms of the program.
title: Kyiv

text: Of these, the Mohyla Academy is the oldest, founded as a theological school in 1632, but Shevchenko University, founded in 1834, is the oldest in continuous operation.
title: Kyiv

text: The total number of institutions of higher education in Kyiv approaches 200, allowing young people to pursue almost any line of study.
title: Kyiv

text: The National Library is affiliated with the Academy of Sciences in so far as it is a deposit library and thus serves as the academy's archives' store.
title: Kyiv

text: The national library is the world's foremost repository of Jewish folk music recorded on Edison wax cylinders.
title: Kyiv

text: There is also an intra-city ring railway service.
title: Kyiv

text: The publicly owned and operated Kyiv Metro is the fastest, the most convenient and affordable network that covers most, but not all, of the city.
title: Kyiv

text: The Metro is expanding towards the city limits to meet growing demand, having three lines with a total length of 66.1 kilometres (41.1 miles) and 51 stations (some of which are renowned architectural landmarks).
title: Kyiv

text: The Metro carries around 1.422 million passengers daily accounting for 38% of the Kyiv's public transport load.
title: Kyiv

text: In 2011, the total number of trips exceeded 519 million.
title: Kyiv

text: The historic tram system was the first electric tramway in the former Russian Empire and the third one in Europe after the Berlin Straßembahn and the Budapest tramway.
title: Kyiv

text: The tram system consists of 139.9 km (86.9 mi) of track, including 14 km (8.7 mi) two Rapid Tram lines, served by 21 routes with the use of 523 tram cars.
title: Kyiv

text: Once a well maintained and widely used method of transport, the system is now gradually being phased out in favor of buses and trolleybuses.
title: Kyiv

text: It connects the historic Uppertown, and the lower commercial neighborhood of Podil through the steep Saint Volodymyr Hill overseeing the Dnieper River.
title: Kyiv

text: All public road transport (except for some minibuses) is operated by the united Kyivpastrans municipal company.
title: Kyiv

text: The Kyiv public transport system, except for taxi, uses a simple flat rate tariff system regardless of distance traveled: tickets or tokens must be purchased each time a vehicle is boarded.
title: Kyiv

text: Digital ticket system is already established in Kyiv Metro, with plans for other transport modes.
title: Kyiv

text: The cost of one ride is far lower than in Western Europe.
title: Kyiv

text: The taxi market in Kyiv is expansive but not regulated.
title: Kyiv

text: In particular, the taxi fare per kilometer is not regulated.
title: Kyiv

text: There are eight Dnieper bridges and dozens of grade-separated intersections in the city.
title: Kyiv

text: There are plans to build a full-size, fully grade-separated ring road around Kyiv.
title: Kyiv

text: In 2009, Kyiv's roads were in poor technical condition and maintained inadequately.
title: Kyiv

text: The city has a developed railroad infrastructure including a long-distance passenger station, 6 cargo stations, depots, and repairing facilities.
title: Kyiv

text: However, this system still fails to meet the demand for passenger service.
title: Kyiv

text: Particularly, the Kyiv-Pasazhyrskyi railway station is the city's only long-distance passenger terminal (vokzal).
title: Kyiv

text: Construction is underway for turning the large Darnytsia railway station on the left-bank part of Kyiv into a long-distance passenger hub, which may ease traffic at the central station.
title: Kyiv

text: Bridges over the Dnieper River are another problem restricting the development of city's railway system.
title: Kyiv

text: Presently, only one rail bridge out of two is available for intense train traffic.
title: Kyiv

text: A new combined rail-auto bridge is under construction, as a part of Darnytsia project.
title: Kyiv

text: In 2011, the Kyiv city administration established the new Urban Train for Kyiv.
title: Kyiv

text: This service runs at standard 4- to 10-minute intervals throughout the day and follows a circular route around the city centre, which allows it to serve many of Kyiv's inner suburbs.
title: Kyiv

text: Interchanges between the Kyiv Metro and Fast Tram exist at many of the urban train's station stops.
title: Kyiv

text: Suburban 'Kyiv City Express' trains (colloquially known as elektrychka) are serviced by the publicly owned Ukrainian Railways.
title: Kyiv

text: The suburban train service is fast, and unbeatably safe in terms of traffic accidents.
title: Kyiv

text: But the trains are not reliable, as they may fall significantly behind schedule, may not be safe in terms of crime, and the elektrychka cars are poorly maintained and overcrowded in rush hours.
title: Kyiv

text: It operates a 2700 km network, two of the largest combined heat and power plants in Ukraine CHP-5 and CHP-6, as well as the only waste incineration plant Energia (plant) operating in Ukraine.
title: Kyiv

text: Kyivvodokanal is a private joint-stock company that provides such services to Kyiv.
title: Kyiv

text: The length of water supply networks is 4231 km, of which 1798 km are dilapidated.
