text: Lviv is the fifth-largest city in Ukraine, as well as the largest city in the western part of the country, with a population of 723,403 (2025 estimate).
title: Lviv

text: It serves as the administrative centre of Lviv Oblast and Lviv Raion, and is one of the main cultural centres of Ukraine.
title: Lviv

text: The city also hosts the administration of Lviv urban hromada.
title: Lviv

text: Lviv was named after Leo I of Galicia, the eldest son of Daniel, King of Ruthenia.
title: Lviv

text: During its history the city has had several names.
title: Lviv

text: Lviv emerged as the centre of the historical regions of Red Ruthenia and Galicia in the 14th century, superseding Halych, Chełm, Belz, and Przemyśl.
title: Lviv

text: It was the capital of the Kingdom of Galicia–Volhynia from 1272 to 1340, when it went to King Casimir III the Great of Poland in a war of succession.
title: Lviv

text: In 1356, Casimir the Great granted it town rights.
title: Lviv

text: From 1434, it was the regional capital of the Ruthenian Voivodeship in the Kingdom of Poland.
title: Lviv

text: In 1772, after the First Partition of Poland, the city became the capital of the Habsburg semi-autonomous Polish-dominated Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria.
title: Lviv

text: Between 1918 and 1939, the city was the centre of the Lwów Voivodeship in the Second Polish Republic.
title: Lviv

text: During this period the city's culture and industry flourished, as did its academic institutions, such as the Lwów School of Mathematics, the Lwów Historical School, and the Lwów School of Economics.
title: Lviv

text: After the German-Soviet invasion of Poland in 1939, Lwów was annexed by the Soviet Union.
title: Lviv

text: It survived Soviet and German occupations during World War II largely unscathed.
title: Lviv

text: In February 1946, the city became a part of the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic, having lost an estimated 80-90% of its prewar population.
title: Lviv

text: In 1991, with the dissolution of the Soviet Union, Lviv stayed part of Ukraine.
title: Lviv

text: The historical heart of the city has cobblestone streets and an assortment of Renaissance, Baroque, Neo-classicism and Art Nouveau architecture.
title: Lviv

text: The historic city centre is on the UNESCO World Heritage List.
title: Lviv

text: It is listed as an endangered site due to the Russian invasion of Ukraine.
title: Lviv

text: Lviv has a number of industries and institutions of higher education, such as Lviv University and Lviv Polytechnic.
title: Lviv

text: The coat of arms, and the logo and banner of the Lviv City Council are the officially approved symbols of Lviv.
title: Lviv

text: The names or images of architectural and historical monuments are also considered symbols of the city.
title: Lviv

text: The city's modern coat of arms is based on that of a mid-14th-century seal.
title: Lviv

text: The modern seal depicts a stone gate with three towers, and in the opening of the gate walks a golden lion.
title: Lviv

text: Lviv's large coat of arms is a shield, with the coat of arms of the city, crowned with a silver crown with three edges, held by a lion and an ancient warrior.
title: Lviv

text: Lviv's flag is a blue square banner with an image of the city emblem and with yellow and blue triangles at the edges.
title: Lviv

text: Lviv's logo is an image of five colourful towers in Lviv and the slogan "Lviv – open to the world" under them.
title: Lviv

text: The city of Lviv was founded in 1250 by King Daniel of Galicia in honour of his son Lev as Lvihorod which is consistent with names of other Ukrainian cities, such as Myrhorod, Sharhorod, Novhorod, Bilhorod, Horodyshche, and Horodok.
title: Lviv

text: Earlier there was a settlement in the form of a borough with a characteristic layout element—an elongated market square.
title: Lviv

text: Daniel's foundation of the stronghold was its next reconstruction after the Batu Khan invasion of 1240.
title: Lviv

text: Lviv was invaded by the Mongols in 1261.
title: Lviv

text: Various sources relate the events, which range from the destruction of the castle to a complete razing of the town.
title: Lviv

text: All sources agree that it was on the orders of the Mongol general Burundai.
title: Lviv

text: The Shevchenko Scientific Society says that Burundai issued the order to raze the city.
title: Lviv

text: The Galician-Volhynian chronicle states that in 1261 "Said Buronda to Vasylko: 'Since you are at peace with me then raze all your castles'".
title: Lviv

text: Basil Dmytryshyn states that the order was implied to be the fortifications as a whole: "If you wish to have peace with me, then destroy [all fortifications of] your towns".
title: Lviv

text: After Daniel's death, King Lev rebuilt the town around 1270, choosing Lviv as his residence, and made it the capital of Galicia-Volhynia.
title: Lviv

text: Around 1280 Armenians lived in Galicia and were mainly based in Lviv where they had their own archbishop.
title: Lviv

text: In the 13th and early 14th centuries, Lviv was largely a wooden city, except for its several Galician-style stone churches.
title: Lviv

text: Some of them, like the Church of Saint Nicholas, have survived, although in a thoroughly rebuilt form.
title: Lviv

text: The town was inherited by the Grand Duchy of Lithuania in 1340 and ruled by voivode Dmytro Dedko, the favourite of the Lithuanian prince Liubartas, until 1349.
title: Lviv

text: Poland ultimately gained control over Lviv and the adjacent region in 1349.
title: Lviv

text: From then on the population was subjected to attempts to both Polonize and Catholicize the population.
title: Lviv

text: The Lithuanians ravaged Lviv land in 1351 during the Halych-Volhyn Wars with Lviv being plundered and destroyed by duke Liubartas in 1353.
title: Lviv

text: Casimir built a new city center (or founded a new town) in a basin, surrounded it by walls, and replaced the wooden palace by masonry castle – one of the two built by him.
title: Lviv

text: The kingdom was transformed into the Ruthenian domain of the Crown with Lwów as the capital.
title: Lviv

text: On 17 June 1356 King Casimir III the Great moved the city to a new location and granted it Magdeburg rights, which implied that all city matters were to be resolved by a council elected by the wealthy citizens.
title: Lviv

text: In 1362, the High Castle was built with stone.
title: Lviv

text: In 1358, the city became a seat of Roman Catholic Archdiocese, which initiated the spread of Latin Church onto the Ruthenian lands.
title: Lviv

text: The city's prosperity during the following centuries is owed to the trade privileges granted to it by Casimir, Queen Jadwiga, and the subsequent Polish monarchs.
title: Lviv

text: Germans, Poles and Czechs formed the largest groups of newcomers.
title: Lviv

text: Most of the settlers were polonised by the end of the 15th century, and the city became a Polish island surrounded by the Ruthenian Orthodox population.
title: Lviv

text: In 1356, the Armenian diocese was founded centered at the Armenian Cathedral.
title: Lviv

text: Lwów was one of two main cultural and religious centers of Armenians in Poland alongside Kamieniec Podolski.
title: Lviv

text: In the early modern period, it also became one of the largest concentrations of Scots and Italians in Poland.
title: Lviv

text: In 1412, the local archdiocese has developed into the Roman Catholic Metropolis, which since 1375 as diocese had been in Halych.
title: Lviv

text: The new metropolis included regional diocese in Lwów, Przemyśl, Chełm, Włodzimierz, Łuck, Kamieniec, as well as Siret and Kijów (see Old Cathedral of St.
title: Lviv

text: The first Catholic Archbishop who resided in Lwów was Jan Rzeszowski.
title: Lviv

text: In 1434, the Ruthenian domain of the Crown was transformed into the Ruthenian Voivodeship.
title: Lviv

text: In 1444, the city was granted the staple right, which resulted in its growing prosperity and wealth, as it became one of the major trading centres on the merchant routes between Central Europe and Black Sea region.
title: Lviv

text: It was also transformed into one of the main fortresses of the kingdom.
title: Lviv

text: As one of the largest and most influential royal cities of Poland, it enjoyed voting rights in the Royal elections in Poland, alongside other major cities such as Kraków, Poznań, Warsaw or Gdańsk.
title: Lviv

text: During the 17th century, it was the second largest city of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, with a population of about 30,000.
title: Lviv

text: In 1572, one of the first publishers of books in what is now Ukraine, Ivan Fedorov, a graduate of the University of Kraków, settled here for a brief period.
title: Lviv

text: The city became a significant centre for Eastern Orthodoxy with the establishment of an Orthodox brotherhood, a Greek-Slavonic school, and a printer which published the first full versions of the Bible in Church Slavonic in 1580.
title: Lviv

text: A Jesuit Collegium was founded in 1608, and on 20 January 1661 King John II Casimir of Poland issued a decree granting it "the honour of the academy and the title of the university".
title: Lviv

text: The 17th century brought invading armies of Swedes, Hungarians, Turks, Russians and Cossacks to its gates.
title: Lviv

text: In 1648 an army of Cossacks and Crimean Tatars besieged the town.
title: Lviv

text: They captured the High Castle, murdering its defenders.
title: Lviv

text: The city itself was not sacked due to the fact that the leader of the revolution Bohdan Khmelnytsky accepted a ransom of 250,000 ducats, and the Cossacks marched north-west towards Zamość.
title: Lviv

text: It was one of two major cities in Poland which was not captured during the so-called Deluge: the other one was Gdańsk.
title: Lviv

text: At that time, Lwów witnessed a historic scene, as here King John II Casimir made his famous Lwów Oath.
title: Lviv

text: In 1672, it was surrounded by the Ottomans who also failed to conquer it.
title: Lviv

text: Three years later, the Battle of Lwów (1675) took place near the city.
title: Lviv

text: Lwów was captured for the first time since the Middle Ages by a foreign army in 1704, when Swedish troops under King Charles XII entered the city after a short siege.
title: Lviv

text: Known in German as Lemberg, the city became the capital of the Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria.
title: Lviv

text: During Habsburg rule, Lviv became one of the most important Polish, Ukrainian and Jewish cultural centres.
title: Lviv

text: In Lviv, according to the Austrian census of 1910, which listed religion and language, 51% of the city's population was Roman Catholics, 28% Jews, and 19% belonged to the Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church.
title: Lviv

text: Linguistically, 86% of the city's population used the Polish language and 11% preferred Ruthenian.
title: Lviv

text: In 1773, the first newspaper in Lemberg, Gazette de Leopoli, began to be published.
title: Lviv

text: In 1784, a Latin language university was opened with lectures in German, Polish and even Ruthenian; after closing in 1805, it was reopened in 1817.
title: Lviv

text: By 1825, German became the sole language of instruction.
title: Lviv

text: Lemberg University was opened by Maria Theresa in 1784.
title: Lviv

text: By 1787, her successor Joseph II, Holy Roman Emperor opened "Studium Ruthenum" for students who did not know enough Latin to take regular courses.
title: Lviv

text: During the 19th century, the Austrian administration attempted to Germanise the city's educational and governmental institutions.
title: Lviv

text: A number of cultural organisations which did not have a pro-German orientation were closed.
title: Lviv

text: After the revolutions of 1848, the language of instruction at the university shifted from German to include Ukrainian and Polish.
title: Lviv

text: Around that time, a certain sociolect developed in the city known as the Lwów dialect.
title: Lviv

text: Considered to be a type of Polish dialect, it draws its roots from a number of other languages besides Polish.
title: Lviv

text: In 1853, kerosene lamps as street lighting were introduced by Ignacy Łukasiewicz and Jan Zeh.
title: Lviv

text: Then in 1858, these were updated to gas lamps, and in 1900 to electric ones.
title: Lviv

text: After the so-called "Ausgleich" of February 1867, the Austrian Empire was reformed into a dualist Austria-Hungary and a slow yet steady process of liberalisation of Austrian rule in Galicia started.
title: Lviv

text: From 1873, Galicia was de facto an autonomous province of Austria-Hungary, with Polish and Ruthenian as official languages.
title: Lviv

text: Germanisation was halted and censorship lifted as well.
title: Lviv

text: Galicia was subject to the Austrian part of the Dual Monarchy, but the Galician Sejm and provincial administration, both established in Lviv, had extensive privileges and prerogatives, especially in education, culture, and local affairs.
title: Lviv

text: In 1894, the General National Exhibition was held in Lviv.
title: Lviv

text: The city started to grow rapidly, becoming the fourth largest in Austria-Hungary, according to the census of 1910.
title: Lviv

text: Multiple Belle Époque public edifices and tenement houses were erected, with a number of the buildings from the Austrian period, such as the Lviv Theatre of Opera and Ballet, built in the Viennese neo-Renaissance style.
title: Lviv

text: Furthermore, Lviv was the centre of a number of Polish independence organisations.
title: Lviv

text: In June 1908, Józef Piłsudski, Władysław Sikorski and Kazimierz Sosnkowski founded the Union of Active Struggle in the city.
title: Lviv

text: Two years later, the paramilitary organisation, called the Riflemen's Association, was also founded in the city by Polish activists.
title: Lviv

text: At the same time, Lviv became the city where famous Ukrainian writers (such as Ivan Franko, Panteleimon Kulish and Ivan Nechuy-Levytsky) published their work.
title: Lviv

text: It was a centre of Ukrainian cultural revival.
title: Lviv

text: However, the Polish-dominated city council blocked Ukrainian attempts to create visible monuments for their own.
title: Lviv

text: The most important streets had names referring to Polish history and literature, and only minor roads referred to Ukrainians.
title: Lviv

text: The historian Pál Kelemen provided a first-hand account of the chaotic evacuation of the city by the Austro-Hungarian Army and civilians alike.
title: Lviv

text: The town was retaken by Austria-Hungary in June of the following year during the Gorlice–Tarnów offensive.
title: Lviv

text: Both nations perceived the city as an integral part of their new statehoods which at that time were forming in the former Austrian territories.
title: Lviv

text: The city's Polish majority opposed the Ukrainian declaration and began to fight against the Ukrainian troops.
title: Lviv

text: During this combat an important role was taken by young Polish city defenders called Lwów Eaglets.
title: Lviv

text: The Ukrainian forces withdrew outside Lwów's confines by 21 November 1918, after which elements of Polish soldiers began to loot and burn much of the Jewish and Ukrainian quarters of the city, killing approximately 340 civilians (see: Lwów pogrom).
title: Lviv

text: The pogromists were tried by Polish authorities and three were executed.
title: Lviv

text: The Sich riflemen reformed into the Ukrainian Galician Army (UHA).
title: Lviv

text: The Polish forces aided from central Poland, including General Haller's Blue Army, equipped by the French, relieved the besieged city in May 1919 forcing the UHA to the east.
title: Lviv

text: Despite Entente mediation attempts to cease hostilities and reach a compromise between belligerents the Polish–Ukrainian War continued until July 1919 when the last UHA forces withdrew east of the River Zbruch.
title: Lviv

text: In August 1920, Lviv was attacked by the Red Army under the command of Aleksandr Yegorov and Stalin during the Polish–Soviet War but the city repelled the attack.
title: Lviv

text: For the courage of its inhabitants Lviv was awarded the Virtuti Militari cross by Józef Piłsudski on 22 November 1920.
title: Lviv

text: Following Warsaw, Lwów was the second most important cultural and academic centre of interwar Poland.
title: Lviv

text: For example, in 1920 Professor Rudolf Weigl of Lwów University developed a vaccine against typhus fever.
title: Lviv

text: Furthermore, the geographic location of Lwów gave it an important role in stimulating international trade and fostering the city's and Poland's economic development.
title: Lviv

text: A major trade fair named Targi Wschodnie was established in 1921.
title: Lviv

text: In the academic year 1937–1938, there were 9,100 students attending five institutions of higher education, including Lwów University as well as the Polytechnic.
title: Lviv

text: While about two-thirds of the city's inhabitants were Poles, some of whom spoke the characteristic Lwów dialect, the eastern part of the Lwów Voivodeship had a relative Ukrainian majority in most of its rural areas.
title: Lviv

text: Polish authorities were obliged through international agreements to provide Eastern Galicia with autonomy (including the creation of a separate Ukrainian university in Lwów), and even though a bill was enacted the Polish Sejm in September 1922, this was not fulfilled.
title: Lviv

text: The Polish government discontinued multiple Ukrainian schools which functioned during the Austrian rule, and closed down Ukrainian departments at the University of Lwów with the exception of one.
title: Lviv

text: Prewar Lwów also had a large and thriving Jewish community, which constituted about a quarter of the population, but were accused of having collaborated with the Ukrainians.
title: Lviv

text: On the other hand, Ukrainians strove to create their own memorial culture in the town.
title: Lviv

text: Subsequently, the Soviets invaded Poland on 17 September.
title: Lviv

text: On 22 September 1939 Lwów capitulated to the Red Army.
title: Lviv

text: The USSR annexed the eastern half of the Second Polish Republic with Ukrainian and Belarusian populations.
title: Lviv

text: The city became the capital of the newly formed Lviv Oblast.
title: Lviv

text: The Soviets reopened uni-lingual Ukrainian schools, which had been discontinued by the Polish government.
title: Lviv

text: The only change over imposed by the Soviets was the language of instruction, with the actual net loss of about 1,000 schools in short order.
title: Lviv

text: Ukrainian was made compulsory in the University of Lviv with almost all its books in Polish.
title: Lviv

text: It became thoroughly Ukrainized and was renamed after Ukrainian writer Ivan Franko.
title: Lviv

text: In the initial stage of Operation Barbarossa (30 June 1941) Lviv was taken by the Germans.
title: Lviv

text: The evacuating Soviets killed most of the prison population, with arriving Wehrmacht forces easily discovering evidence of the Soviet mass murders in the city committed by the NKVD and NKGB.
title: Lviv

text: The German administration falsely blamed the massacres on Jews, contributing to the start of pogroms against the city's Jewish community.
title: Lviv

text: On 30 June 1941 Yaroslav Stetsko proclaimed in Lviv the Government of an independent Ukrainian state allied with Nazi Germany.
title: Lviv

text: This was done without preapproval from the Germans and after 15 September 1941, the organisers were arrested.
title: Lviv

text: The Sikorski–Mayski Agreement signed in London on 30 July 1941 between the Polish government-in-exile and the Soviet government invalidated the September 1939 Soviet-German partition of Poland, as the Soviets declared it null and void.
title: Lviv

text: Meanwhile, German-occupied Eastern Galicia at the beginning of August 1941 was incorporated into the General Government as Distrikt Galizien with Lviv as the district's capital.
title: Lviv

text: German policy towards the Polish population in this area was as harsh as in the rest of the General Government.
title: Lviv

text: During the occupation of the city, the Germans committed multiple atrocities, including the killing of Polish university professors in 1941.
title: Lviv

text: German Nazis viewed the Ukrainian Galicians, former inhabitants of Austrian Crown Land, as more aryanised and civilised than the Ukrainian population living in the territories belonging to the USSR before 1939.
title: Lviv

text: As a result, they escaped the full extent of German acts in comparison to Ukrainians who lived to the east, in the German-occupied Soviet Ukraine turned into the Reichskommissariat Ukraine.
title: Lviv

text: According to the Third Reich's racial policies, local Jews then became the main target of German repressions in the region.
title: Lviv

text: Following the German occupation, the Jewish population was concentrated in the Lwów Ghetto established in the city's Zamarstynów (today Zamarstyniv) district and the Janowska concentration camp was also set up.
title: Lviv

text: In the Janowska concentration camp, the Nazis conducted torture and executions to music.
title: Lviv

text: The Lviv National Opera members, who were prisoners, played one and the same tune, Tango of Death.
title: Lviv

text: On the eve of Lviv's liberation, German Nazis ordered 40 orchestra musicians to form a circle.
title: Lviv

text: The security ringed the musicians tightly and ordered them to play.
title: Lviv

text: Then the commandant ordered the musicians to come to the center of the circle one by one, put their instruments onto the ground and strip naked, after which they were killed by a headshot.
title: Lviv

text: A photo of the orchestra players was one of the incriminating documents at the Nuremberg trials.
title: Lviv

text: In 1931 there were 75,316 Yiddish-speaking inhabitants, but by 1941 approximately 100,000 Jews were present in Lviv.
title: Lviv

text: The majority of these Jews were either killed within the city or deported to Belzec extermination camp.
title: Lviv

text: In the summer of 1943, on the orders of Heinrich Himmler, SS-Standartenführer Paul Blobel was tasked with the destruction of any evidence of Nazi mass murders in the Lviv area.
title: Lviv

text: On 15 June Blobel, using forced labourers from Janowska, dug up a number of mass graves and incinerated the remains.
title: Lviv

text: Later, on 19 November 1943, inmates at Janowska staged an uprising and attempted a mass escape.
title: Lviv

text: A few succeeded, but most were recaptured and killed.
title: Lviv

text: The SS staff and their local auxiliaries then, at the time of the Janowska camp's liquidation, murdered at least 6,000 more inmates, as well as the Jews in other forced labour camps in Galicia.
title: Lviv

text: Soon thereafter, the local commanders of Polish Armia Krajowa were invited to a meeting with the commanders of the Red Army.
title: Lviv

text: During the meeting, they were arrested, as it turned out to be a trap set by the Soviet NKVD.
title: Lviv

text: Those arrested were released only after they had signed papers in which they agreed to emigrate to Poland, which postwar borders were to be shifted westwards in accordance with the Yalta Conference settlements.
title: Lviv

text: In Yalta, despite Polish objections, the Allied leaders, Joseph Stalin, Franklin D.
title: Lviv

text: Roosevelt and Winston Churchill decided that Lwów should remain within the borders of the Soviet Union.
title: Lviv

text: Roosevelt wanted Poland to have Lwów and the surrounding oilfields, but Stalin refused to allow it.
title: Lviv

text: On 16 August 1945, a border agreement was signed in Moscow between the government of the Soviet Union and the Provisional Government of National Unity installed by the Soviets in Poland.
title: Lviv

text: In the treaty, Polish authorities formally ceded the prewar eastern part of the country to the Soviet Union, agreeing to the Polish-Soviet border to be drawn according to the Curzon Line.
title: Lviv

text: Poles who stayed in Lviv later formed the Association of Polish Culture of the Lviv Land.
title: Lviv

text: In March 1946 Lviv became the site of a Church synod organized under the pressure of KGB, which formally dissolved the Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church by attaching its parishes to the Russian Orthodox Church.
title: Lviv

text: According to various estimates, Lviv lost between 80% and 90% of its prewar population.
title: Lviv

text: Immigration from Russia and Russian-speaking regions of eastern Ukraine was encouraged, and during the 1950s ethnic Russians comprised almost a third of Lviv's population.
title: Lviv

text: During the same period, 28,000 Jews resided in the city, most of them being Russian-speaking immigrants from other parts of the Soviet Union.
title: Lviv

text: On 8 September 1956 the population of Lviv reached the number of 400,000 inhabitants.
title: Lviv

text: In 1950 the first regional agricultural exhibition was opened in Stryi Park, and in 1951 a children's railway was launched.
title: Lviv

text: In 1952 the city's first trolleybus line was opened.
title: Lviv

text: By 1960, the city was served by 262 buses, 380 taxis and over 50 trolleybuses.
title: Lviv

text: In 1955 the new airport terminal started operations.
title: Lviv

text: By 1955 Lviv had by 415 restaurants, cafes and canteens, but it was still not enough to cover the needs of the growing population.
title: Lviv

text: Several public parks, including Shevchenkivskyi Hai, were established during the 1950s.
title: Lviv

text: Due to the development of industrial enterprises in old city quarters, local authorities introduced measures against noise pollution.
title: Lviv

text: At the same time, until 1958 Lviv continued to serve as a transit point for heavy military equipment.
title: Lviv

text: In 1958 a house in one of the city's streets collapsed due to the movement of self-propelled artillery in a nearby street.
title: Lviv

text: As a result, the transport of military hardware through the old town quarters was outlawed, and the division stationed in Lviv was transferred to Yavoriv.
title: Lviv

text: During the early 1950s the Soviet government adopted a resolution to turn Lviv into a major industrial centre.
title: Lviv

text: In 1954 the Soviet Union's first kinescope tube was produced on a local factory, and in 1958 serial production of TV sets started.
title: Lviv

text: In 1956 the Lviv Bus Factory launched its production.
title: Lviv

text: A tank repair plant was also established in the city.
title: Lviv

text: In order to accommodate the working population, authorities ordered the construction of cheap housing.
title: Lviv

text: At the same time, much more comfortable buildings in Stalinist style were erected for the Communist elite.
title: Lviv

text: Despite the new construction, many people continued to live in communal flats or even in cellars.
title: Lviv

text: Central heating, water supplies and sewers were absent in many districts.
title: Lviv

text: Throughout the 1950s, several nearby villages and towns were administratively incorporated into Lviv, including Briukhovychi and Vynnyky.
title: Lviv

text: In December 1957 the Lviv TV centre made its first broadcast from the city's Opera Theatre.
title: Lviv

text: In the 1950s and 1960s, the city expanded both in population and size mostly due to its rapidly growing industrial base.
title: Lviv

text: Due to the fight of SMERSH with the Ukrainian Insurgent Army, Lviv obtained a nickname with a negative connotation: Banderstadt, meaning the city of Stepan Bandera.
title: Lviv

text: The German suffix for the city stadt was added instead of the Russian grad to imply alienation.
title: Lviv

text: Over the years the residents of the city found this so ridiculous that even people not familiar with Bandera accepted it as sarcasm in reference to the Soviet perception of western Ukraine.
title: Lviv

text: By the time of the fall of the Soviet Union the name became a proud mark for the Lviv natives culminating in the creation of a local rock band under the name Khloptsi z Bandershtadtu (Boys from Banderstadt).
title: Lviv

text: During the 1960s, inhabitants of Lviv and the surrounding region benefitted from their access to Polish television and radio, as well as press publications from fellow Warsaw Pact countries.
title: Lviv

text: Many migrants from Ukrainian villages, who settled in the city during that time, had relatives abroad and could engage in correspondence across the Iron Curtain.
title: Lviv

text: In order to combat "bourgeois nationalism", Soviet authorities outlawed the combination of blue and yellow in printed production and media.
title: Lviv

text: In order to combat religion, the regime closed down Lviv's Dominican Cathedral and organized a museum of atheism in its building.
title: Lviv

text: Despite government pressure, Lviv's inhabitants continued to follow religious traditions, with many districts organizing vertep performances.
title: Lviv

text: During the 1960s Lviv also developed its own youth subcultures, which formed under the influence of Western music.
title: Lviv

text: During the 1970s the policies of Ukrainian Soviet leadership under Volodymyr Shcherbytsky resulted in a new wave of Russification.
title: Lviv

text: Many inscriptions and advertisements in Lviv's streets and in public transport were now written in Russian language.
title: Lviv

text: Meanwhile the economic situation worsened, with numerous goods disappearing form shops by the middle part of the decade.
title: Lviv

text: By the end of 1970s inflation became a major concern.
title: Lviv

text: At the same time, the city's trade network continued to expand, with more than 200 shops being built.
title: Lviv

text: The 1970s saw the rise of the hippie movement in Lviv.
title: Lviv

text: In 1975-1977 the garden of the local Carmelite Monastery became a centre of gatherings for nonconformist youth from various parts of the Soviet Union.
title: Lviv

text: Despite the conditions of Soviet Russification, Lviv became a major centre of the dissident movement.
title: Lviv

text: Starting from 1972, many of Lviv's dissident activists were arrested and held in the infamous Prison on Łącki Street.
title: Lviv

text: In the period of liberalisation from the Soviet system in the 1980s, the city became the centre of political movements advocating Ukrainian independence from the USSR.
title: Lviv

text: Hundreds of thousands of people would gather in freezing temperatures to demonstrate for the Orange camp.
title: Lviv

text: Acts of civil disobedience forced the head of the local police to resign and the local assembly issued a resolution refusing to accept the fraudulent first official results.
title: Lviv

text: Lviv remains today one of the main centres of Ukrainian culture and the origin of much of the nation's political class.
title: Lviv

text: In support of the Euromaidan movement, Lviv's executive committee declared itself independent of the rule of President Viktor Yanukovych on 19 February 2014.
title: Lviv

text: In 2019, the citizens of Lviv strongly supported Petro Poroshenko over Volodymyr Zelenskyy during the 2019 Ukrainian presidential election.
title: Lviv

text: The percentage of votes counted for Poroshenko was more than 90%.
title: Lviv

text: Despite this level of support in Lviv, he lost the national vote.
title: Lviv

text: Until 18 July 2020, Lviv was incorporated as a city of oblast significance and the center of Lviv Municipality.
title: Lviv

text: The municipality was abolished in July 2020 as part of the administrative reform of Ukraine, which reduced the number of raions of Lviv Oblast to seven.
title: Lviv

text: Lviv also became a safe haven for the Ukrainians fleeing other parts of the country affected by the invasion, their number exceeding 200,000 by 18 March 2022.
title: Lviv

text: A number of them used the city as a stopping point on their way to Poland.
title: Lviv

text: Lviv and the larger region around it also served as crucial arms and humanitarian supply route.
title: Lviv

text: Bracing for Russian attacks, local government and citizens, helped by Polish and Croatian advisers, worked to protect the city's cultural heritage by erecting makeshift barriers around historical monuments, wrapping statues, and safeguarding art treasures.
title: Lviv

text: During the course of the war, the area in and around Lviv has been struck by Russian missile attacks.
title: Lviv

text: On 18 April 2022, the city was hit by five missile strikes, killing seven civilians and wounding 11, according to mayor Andriy Sadovyi.
title: Lviv

text: Regional governor Maksym Kozystkiy said that the targets were military factories and a tyre shop.
title: Lviv

text: A hotel housing evacuees was also hit, damaging its windows.
title: Lviv

text: The Russian Ministry of Defence claimed that all locations were struck by Russian missiles during the night of 18 April were military targets.
title: Lviv

text: Lviv was targeted during the 10 October 2022 missile strikes on Ukraine, resulting in a city-wide blackout.
title: Lviv

text: On 11 October 2022, Sadovyi announced that the city was hit by a missile strike, resulting in a power outage and water supply shortage.
title: Lviv

text: On October 5, 2025, the Russian on Lviv was the largest of the war in the Lviv region, said governor Maksym Kozytskyi, adding it involved 140 drones and 23 missiles. 5 civilians were killed and dozens were injured.
title: Lviv

text: On the night of 8 January 2026, Russian forces launched an Oreshnik missile from the Kapustin Yar test site towards Lviv.
title: Lviv

text: The average altitude of Lviv is 296 metres (971 feet) above sea level.
title: Lviv

text: Its highest point is the Vysokyi Zamok (High Castle), 409 meters (1,342 feet) above sea level.
title: Lviv

text: This castle has a commanding view of the historic city centre with its distinctive, green-domed churches and intricate architecture.
title: Lviv

text: The old walled city was at the foothills of the High Castle on the banks of the Poltva River.
title: Lviv

text: In the 13th century, the river was used to transport goods.
title: Lviv

text: The average temperatures are −3 °C (27 °F) in January and 18 °C (64 °F) in July.
title: Lviv

text: Average humidity level is dry, with summer humidity ranging from comfortable to muggy.
title: Lviv

text: The average annual rainfall is 745 mm (29 in) with the maximum in summer.
title: Lviv

text: Zaliznychnyi District (Залізничний район), literally "railway neighborhood" Lychakivskyi District (Личаківський район) Sykhivskyi District (Сихівський район) Frankivskyi District (Франківський район), named after Ivan Franko.
title: Lviv

text: Shevchenkivskyi District (Шевченківський район), named after Taras Shevchenko.
title: Lviv

text: In 2010 the average life expectancy was 71 among men and 79.5 years among women.
title: Lviv

text: The fertility rates have been steadily increasing between 2001 and 2010; however, the effects of low fertility in the previous years remained noticeable even though the birth rates grew.
title: Lviv

text: However, there is an acute shortage of young people under the age of 25.
title: Lviv

text: Year 1544: approx. 3,000 inhabitants in the Old Town (number had decreased by about 30% due to the fire of 1527), and additionally approx. 2,700 in the suburbs.
title: Lviv

text: Year 1840: approx. 67,000 inhabitants, including 20,000 Jews.
title: Lviv

text: Year 1850: nearly 80,000 inhabitants (together with the four suburbs), including more than 25,000 Jews.
title: Lviv

text: Year 1869: 87,109 inhabitants, among them 46,252 Roman Catholics, 26,694 Jews, 12,406 members of the Greek Uniate Churches.
title: Lviv

text: Year 1890: 127,943 inhabitants (64,102 male, 63,481 female), among them 67,280 Catholics, 36,130 Judaic, 21,876 members of the Greek Uniate Churches, 2,061 Protestants, 596 Orthodox and others.
title: Lviv

text: Year 1900: 159,877 inhabitants, including the military (10,326 men).
title: Lviv

text: Of these inhabitants, 82,597 were members of the Roman Catholic Church, 29,327 members of the Greek Uniate Churches, and 44,258 were Jews.
title: Lviv

text: As their language of communication, 120,634 used Polish, 20,409 German or Yiddish, and 15,159 Ukrainian.
title: Lviv

text: Year 1921: 219,388 inhabitants, including 136,519 Poles, 60,431 Jews and 19,866 Ukrainians.
title: Lviv

text: Year 1931: 312,231 inhabitants, including 198,212 Poles, 75,316 Jews and 35,137 Ukrainians.
title: Lviv

text: Year 2001: 725,000 inhabitants, of whom 88% were Ukrainians, 9% Russians and 1% Poles.
title: Lviv

text: A further 200,000 people commuted daily from suburbs.
title: Lviv

text: The use of Ukrainian in the city has surged since the 1970s, while the use of Russian has declined since the 1980s.
title: Lviv

text: In 2000, it was estimated that 80% of Lvivians spoke Ukrainian.
title: Lviv

text: Lwów served as Poland's major cultural and economic centre for several centuries, during the Polish Golden Age, and until the partitions of Poland perpetrated by Russia, Prussia, and Austria.
title: Lviv

text: In the Second Polish Republic, the Lwów Voivodeship (inhabited by 2,789,000 people in 1921) grew to 3,126,300 inhabitants in ten years.
title: Lviv

text: As a result of World War II, Lviv was de-Polonised, mainly through Soviet-arranged population exchange in 1944–1946 but also by early deportations to Siberia.
title: Lviv

text: Those who remained on their own volition after the border shift became a small ethnic minority in Lviv.
title: Lviv

text: By 1959 Poles made up only 4% of the local population.
title: Lviv

text: During the Soviet decades only two Polish schools continued to function: No. 10 (with 8 grades) and No. 24 (with 10 grades).
title: Lviv

text: In the 1980s the process of uniting groups into ethnic associations was allowed.
title: Lviv

text: In 1988 a Polish-language newspaper was permitted (Gazeta Lwowska).
title: Lviv

text: The Polish population of the city continues to use the dialect of the Polish language known as Lwów dialect (Polish: gwara lwowska).
title: Lviv

text: The oldest remaining Jewish tombstone dates back to 1348.
title: Lviv

text: Apart from the Rabbanite Jews there were many Karaites who had settled in the city after coming from the East and from Byzantium.
title: Lviv

text: After Casimir III conquered Lviv in 1349 the Jewish citizens received a number of privileges equal to that of other citizens of Poland.
title: Lviv

text: Lviv had two separate Jewish quarters, one within the city walls and one outside on the outskirts of the city.
title: Lviv

text: Each had its separate synagogue, although they shared a cemetery, which was also used by the Crimean Karaite community.
title: Lviv

text: Before the Holocaust about one-third of the city's population was Jewish (more than 140,000 on the eve of World War II).
title: Lviv

text: Most of the Jewish population was killed in the Holocaust.
title: Lviv

text: Meanwhile, the Nazis also destroyed the Jewish cemetery, which was subsequently "paved over by the Soviets".
title: Lviv

text: Due to the Holocaust and migration, the original Jewish population of the city all but vanished.
title: Lviv

text: After the war, the remnant was replenished by a newer Jewish population, formed from among the hundreds of thousands of Russians and Ukrainians who migrated to the city.
title: Lviv

text: The post-war Jewish population peaked at 30,000 in the 1970s.
title: Lviv

text: Currently, the Jewish population has shrunk considerably as a result of emigration (mainly to Israel and the United States) and, to a lesser degree, assimilation, and is estimated to number a few thousand.
title: Lviv

text: A number of organisations continue to be active.
title: Lviv

text: The Sholem Aleichem Jewish Culture Society in Lviv initiated the construction of a monument to the victims of the ghetto in 1988.
title: Lviv

text: On 23 August 1992, the memorial complex to the victims of the Lwów ghetto (1941–1943) was officially opened.
title: Lviv

text: During 2011–2012, some antisemitic acts against the memorial took place.
title: Lviv

text: On 20 March 2011, it was reported that the slogan "death to the Jews" with a swastika was sprayed on the monument.
title: Lviv

text: As of 1 January 2011, the city has invested 837.1 million US dollars into the economy, accounting for almost two-thirds of total investment in the Lviv region.
title: Lviv

text: In 2015, the companies of Lviv received $14.3 million of foreign direct investment; which is however half as much as a year earlier ($30.9 million in 2014).
title: Lviv

text: During January–September 2017 the general amount of direct foreign investment received by the local government in Lviv was $52.4 million.
title: Lviv

text: According to the statistics administration, foreign capital was invested by 31 countries (some of the main investors: Poland – 47.7%; Australia – 11.3%; Cyprus – 10.7% and the Netherlands – 6%).
title: Lviv

text: The total revenue of the city budget of Lviv for 2015 was set at about UAH 3.81 billion, which was 23% more than a year earlier (UAH 2.91 billion in 2014).
title: Lviv

text: As of 10 November 2017, the deputies of the Lviv City Council approved a budget in amount of UAH 5.4 billion ($204 million), most of which (UAH 5.12 billion) was the revenue of the fund of Lviv.
title: Lviv

text: The average wage in Lviv in 2015 in the business sector amounted to 14,041 UAH, in the budget sphere – 9,475 UAH.
title: Lviv

text: On 1 February 2014, registered unemployment was 0.6%.
title: Lviv

text: Lviv is one of the largest cities in Ukraine and is growing rapidly.
title: Lviv

text: According to the Ministry of Economy of Ukraine the monthly average salary in Lviv is a little less than the average for Ukraine which in February 2013 was 6050 UAH ($755).
title: Lviv

text: According to the World Bank classification Lviv is a middle-income city.
title: Lviv

text: In June 2019, the average wage amounted to 23,000 UAH ($920), which was 18.9% more than in the previous year.
title: Lviv

text: Lviv has 218 large industrial enterprises, more than 40 commercial banks, 4 exchanges, 13 investment companies, 80 insurance and 24 leasing companies, 77 audit firms and almost 9,000 small ventures.
title: Lviv

text: For a number of years machinery-building and electronics were leading industries in Lviv.
title: Lviv

text: The city-based public company Electron, trademark of national television sets manufacturing, produces the 32 and 37-inches liquid-crystal TV-sets.
title: Lviv

text: The Electrontrans specializes in design and production of modern electric transport including trams, trolleybuses, electric buses, and spare parts.
title: Lviv

text: In 2013 Elektrotrans JV started producing low-floor trams, the first Ukrainian 100% low-floor tramways.
title: Lviv

text: LAZ is a bus manufacturing company in Lviv with its own rich history.
title: Lviv

text: Founded in 1945, LAZ started bus production in the early 1950s.
title: Lviv

text: Innovative design ideas of Lviv engineers have become the world standard in bus manufacturing.
title: Lviv

text: The total volume of industrial production sold in 2015 amounted to UAH 24.2 billion, which was 39% more than a year earlier (UAH 14.6 billion in 2014).
title: Lviv

text: There are several banks based in Lviv, such as Kredobank, Idea Bank, VS Bank, Oksi Bank and Lviv Bank.
title: Lviv

text: None of these banks have bankrupted during the political and economic crisis of 2014–2016, which can be explained by the presence of foreign capital in most of them.
title: Lviv

text: From 2015 to 2019, the city experienced a construction boom.
title: Lviv

text: In Q1 2019, according to statistical data, growth in the volume of new housing construction was recorded in Lviv (3.2 times, to 377,900 square meters).
title: Lviv

text: Lviv is a major business center between Warsaw and Kyiv.
title: Lviv

text: According to the Lviv Economic Development Strategy, the main branches of the city's economy by 2025 were to be tourism and information technologies (IT), with business services and logistics also considered priorities.
title: Lviv

text: In addition, the Nestlé service center is in Lviv.
title: Lviv

text: This center guides the company's divisions in 20 countries of Central and Eastern Europe.
title: Lviv

text: Also during 2016 the Global Service Center VimpelCom in Lviv was launched, which serves finance, procurement and HR operations in eight foreign branches of this company.
title: Lviv

text: There are multiple restaurants and shops as well as street vendors of food, books, clothes, traditional cultural items and tourist gifts.
title: Lviv

text: Banking and money trading are an important part of the economy of Lviv with banks and exchange offices throughout the city.
title: Lviv

text: Over 15% of all IT specialists in Ukraine work in Lviv, with over 4100 new IT graduates coming from local universities each year.
title: Lviv

text: About 2,500 tech enthusiasts attended Lviv IT Arena, the largest technology conference in Western Ukraine.
title: Lviv

text: Over 24,000 IT specialists work in Lviv as of 2019.
title: Lviv

text: Lviv is among top five most popular Ukrainian cities for opening R&D center in IT and IT outsourcing spheres together with Kyiv, Dnipro, Kharkiv and Odesa.
title: Lviv

text: In 2009, KPMG, one of the well-known international auditing companies, included Lviv as one of the top 30 cities with the greatest potential of information technology development.
title: Lviv

text: As of December 2015, there were 192 IT companies operating in the city, of which 4 were large (with more than 400 employees), 16 were average (150–300 employees), 97 were small (10–110 employees) and 70 were micro companies (3–7 employees).
title: Lviv

text: From 2017 to 2018 the number of IT companies increased to 317.
title: Lviv

text: The turnover of Lviv's IT industry in 2015 amounted to $300 million.
title: Lviv

text: About 50% of IT services are exported to the US, 37% to Europe, and the rest to other countries.
title: Lviv

text: As of 2015, about 15 thousand specialists were employed in this industry with an average salary of 28 thousand UAH.
title: Lviv

text: According to a study of the Economic Effect of the Lviv IT-Market, which was conducted by Lviv IT Cluster and sociological agency "The Farm", there were 257 IT companies operating in Lviv in 2017 which employed about 17 thousand specialists.
title: Lviv

text: The economic impact of the IT industry in Lviv is $734 million.
title: Lviv

text: There are 15 top universities in Lviv, 5 of which prepare highly skilled specialists in computer and IT technologies and supply over 1,000 IT graduates to the market annually.
title: Lviv

text: Lviv IT outsourcing companies gathered all kinds of Ukrainian developers in one place, resulting in multiple front-end interns, JavaScript developers, back-end and full-stack coders with proper qualifications, experience, and good English language skills.
title: Lviv

text: It is known as a centre of art, literature, music and theatre.
title: Lviv

text: Nowadays, the evidence of the city's cultural richness is the number of theatres, concert halls, and creative unions, and the high number of artistic activities (more than 100 festivals annually, 60 museums, and 10 theatres).
title: Lviv

text: Lviv's historic centre has been on the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO) World Heritage list since 1998.
title: Lviv

text: In recent centuries Lviv was spared some of the invasions and wars that destroyed other Ukrainian cities.
title: Lviv

text: Its architecture reflects various European styles and periods.
title: Lviv

title: Lviv

title: Lviv

title: Lviv

text: Awiata was the oldest Polish organization of this kind and it concentrated its activities mainly on exhibitions such as the First Aviation Exhibition which took place in 1910 and featured models of aircraft built by Lviv students.
title: Lviv

title: Lviv

title: Lviv

text: The Floriański brothers' plane was the first Polish-made aircraft.
title: Lviv

text: On 5 November 1918, a crew consisting of Stefan Bastyr and Janusz de Beaurain carried out the first-ever flight under the Polish flag taking off from Lviv's Lewandówka (now Ukrainian: Levandivka) airport.
title: Lviv

text: In the interbellum period Lwów was a major centre of gliding with a notable Gliding School in Bezmiechowa which opened in 1932.
title: Lviv

title: Lviv

title: Lviv

text: The interwar Lwów was also a major centre of the Polish Air Force with the Sixth Air Regiment located there.
title: Lviv

title: Lviv

text: The airport is located 6 km (4 mi) from the city centre.
title: Lviv

title: Lviv

title: Lviv

text: In 2011 the City of Lviv ratified an ambitious 9-year program for the set-up of cycling infrastructure – until the year 2019 an overall length of 270 km (168 mi) cycle lanes and tracks shall be realized.
title: Lviv

text: A working group formally organised within the Lviv City Council, bringing together representatives of the city administration, members of planning and design institutes, local NGOs and other stakeholders.
title: Lviv

text: Events like the All-Ukrainian Bikeday or the European Mobility Week show the popularity of cycling among Lviv's citizens.
title: Lviv

text: By September 2011, 8 km (5 mi) of new cycling infrastructure had been built.
title: Lviv

text: It can be expected that until the end of 2011 50 km (31 mi) will be ready for use.
title: Lviv

text: The cycling advisor in Lviv – the first such position in Ukraine – is supervising and pushing forward the execution of the cycling plan and coordinates with various people in the city.
title: Lviv

text: The development of cycling in Ukraine is currently hampered by outdated planning norms and the fact, that most planners didn't yet plan and experience cycling infrastructure.
title: Lviv

text: The update of national legislation and training for planners is therefore necessary.
title: Lviv

text: In 2015, the first stations have been set up for a new bike-sharing system Nextbike – the first of its kind in Ukraine.
title: Lviv

text: New bike lanes are also under construction, making Lviv the most bike-friendly city in the country.
title: Lviv

text: The city contains a total of 12 universities, 8 academies and a number of smaller schools of higher education.
title: Lviv

text: In addition, within Lviv, there is a total of eight institutes of the National Academy of Science of Ukraine and more than forty research institutes.
title: Lviv

text: In Soviet times, the city of Lviv was the location where the software for the Lunokhod programme was developed.
title: Lviv

text: The technology for the Venera series probes and the first orbital shuttle Buran were also developed in Lviv.
title: Lviv

text: A considerable scientific potential is concentrated in the city: by the number of doctors of sciences, candidates of sciences, scientific organisations Lviv is the fourth city in Ukraine.
title: Lviv

text: Lviv is also known for ancient academic traditions, founded by the Assumption Brotherhood School and the Jesuit Collegium.
title: Lviv
