text: Odesa, also spelled Odessa, is the third-most populous city and municipality in Ukraine and a major seaport and transport hub located in the south-west of the country, on the northwestern shore of the Black Sea.
title: Odesa

text: The city is also the administrative centre of the Odesa Raion and Odesa Oblast, as well as a multiethnic cultural centre.
title: Odesa

text: As of January 2021, Odesa's population was approximately 1,010,537.
title: Odesa

text: On 25 January 2023, its historic city centre was declared a World Heritage Site and added to the List of World Heritage in Danger by the UNESCO World Heritage Committee in recognition of its multiculturality and 19th-century urban planning.
title: Odesa

text: The declaration was made in response to the bombing of Odesa during the Russian invasion of Ukraine, which has damaged or destroyed buildings across the city.
title: Odesa

text: In classical antiquity a large Greek settlement existed at its location no later than the middle of the 6th century BC.
title: Odesa

text: It has been researched as a possible site of the ancient Greek settlement of Histria.
title: Odesa

text: The first chronicle mention of the Slavic settlement-port of Kotsiubijiv, which was part of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania, dates back to 1415, when a ship was sent from here to Constantinople by sea.
title: Odesa

text: After the Grand Duchy lost control, the port and its surroundings became part of the domain of the Ottoman Empire in 1529, under the name Hacibey, and remained in it until the Ottomans' defeat in the Russo-Turkish War (1787–1792).
title: Odesa

text: In 1794, a decree of the Russian empress Catherine II was issued to establish a navy harbor and trading place in Khadjibey, which was named Odessa soon after.
title: Odesa

text: From 1819 to 1858, Odesa was a free port.
title: Odesa

text: During the Soviet period, it was an important trading port and a naval base.
title: Odesa

text: During the 19th century, Odesa was the fourth largest city of the Russian Empire, after Moscow, Saint Petersburg and Warsaw.
title: Odesa

text: Its historical architecture is more Mediterranean than Russian, having been heavily influenced by French and Italian styles.
title: Odesa

text: Some buildings were built in a mixture of styles, including Art Nouveau, Renaissance and Classicist.
title: Odesa

text: The city of Odesa hosts both the Port of Odesa and Port Pivdennyi, a significant oil terminal situated in the city's suburbs.
title: Odesa

text: Another notable port, Chornomorsk, is located in the same oblast, to the south-west of Odesa.
title: Odesa

text: Together they represent a major transport hub integrating with railways.
title: Odesa

text: Odesa's oil and chemical processing facilities are connected to the Russian and other European networks by strategic pipelines.
title: Odesa

text: In 1795 the city was named Odessa in accordance with the Greek Plan of Empress Catherine II.
title: Odesa

text: Catherine's Secretary of State Adrian Gribovsky claimed in his memoirs that the name was his suggestion.
title: Odesa

text: Some expressed doubts about this claim, while others noted the reputation of Gribovsky as an honest and modest man.
title: Odesa

text: Odesa is located between the ancient Greek cities of Tyras and Olbia and it was named using a Slavic feminine form for the ancient Greek city of Odessos (Ancient Greek: Ὀδησσός; in Roman times, Odessus).
title: Odesa

text: This refers to the second ancient Odessos, founded between the end of the 5th and beginning of the 4th centuries BC (the first one, identified with modern Varna in Bulgaria, is the older of the two, founded c. 610 BC).
title: Odesa

text: The exact location of this ancient Odessos is unknown, but modern efforts have attempted to localize it 40 km northeast of Odesa, near the village of Koshary, Odesa Oblast., near the Tylihul Estuary.
title: Odesa

text: Odessa, the transliteration of the name from Russian, was the traditional English spelling of the city's name favoured before Ukraine's independence in 1991 (similar to the spelling of Kiev versus Kyiv).
title: Odesa

text: It remained the predominant spelling until the mid-2010s, when international usage began to adopt the official transliteration Odesa as endorsed by the Ukrainian government.
title: Odesa

text: This spelling appears in Encyclopædia Britannica and in dictionaries as the spelling for the Ukrainian city.
title: Odesa

text: The spelling Odesa has also been adopted as the Library of Congress Name Authority Heading.
title: Odesa

text: Some scholars believe it to have been a trade settlement established by the Greek city of Histria.
title: Odesa

text: Whether the Bay of Odesa is the ancient "Port of the Histrians" cannot yet be considered a settled question based on the available evidence.
title: Odesa

text: Archaeological artifacts confirm extensive links between the Odesa area and the eastern Mediterranean.
title: Odesa

text: In the Middle Ages successive rulers of the Odesa region included various nomadic tribes (Petchenegs, Cumans), the Golden Horde, the Crimean Khanate, the Grand Duchy of Lithuania, and the Ottoman Empire.
title: Odesa

text: Yedisan Crimean Tatars traded there in the 14th century.
title: Odesa

text: Since the middle of the 13th century the city's territory belonged to the Golden Horde domain.
title: Odesa

text: On Italian navigational maps of 14th century on the place of Odesa is indicated the castle of Ginestra, at the time the center of a Gazarian colony of the Republic of Genoa.
title: Odesa

text: During the reign of Khan Hacı I Giray of Crimea (1441–1466), the Khanate was endangered by the Golden Horde and the Ottoman Turks and, in search of allies, the khan agreed to cede the area to Lithuania.
title: Odesa

text: The site of present-day Odesa was then a fortress known as Khadjibey (named for Hacı I Giray, and also spelled Kocibey in English, Hacıbey or Hocabey in Turkish, and Hacıbey in Crimean Tatar).
title: Odesa

text: Khadjibey was first mentioned in 1415 in Polish chronicles by Jan Długosz, when a ship with grain sailed from there to Constantinople.
title: Odesa

text: Stable commercial activity in this region in practice in the past required both security through the overland routes, and knowledge of where products could go overseas.
title: Odesa

text: In antiquity, various Greek colonies had taken this role, followed by the Varangians who established Kievan Rus' in the 9th century, as well as various Italian colonies after the Mongol invasion of Europe.
title: Odesa

text: Under Catherine the Great, Russia gained, via the Treaty of Küçük Kaynarca, the lands where Mariupol, Kherson, and Mykolaiv would be founded.
title: Odesa

text: However, they were all handicapped in various ways relative to how much commercial interest there was.
title: Odesa

text: For example, the latter two cities were situated in lowlands near marshes, which provided for poor sanitary conditions in the technology available at that time.
title: Odesa

text: During the Russian-Turkish War of 1787–1792, on 25 September 1789, a detachment of Russian forces, including Zaporozhian Cossacks under Alexander Suvorov and Ivan Gudovich, took Khadjibey and Yeni Dünya for the Russian Empire.
title: Odesa

text: One section of the troops came under command of a Spaniard in Russian service: Major General José de Ribas (known in Russia as Osip Mikhailovich Deribas); today, the main street in Odesa, Deribasivska Street, is named after him.
title: Odesa

text: Russia formally gained possession of the Sanjak of Özi (Ochakiv Oblast) as a result of the Treaty of Jassy (Iaşi) in 1792 and it became a part of Yekaterinoslav Viceroyalty.
title: Odesa

text: The Russian Empire took full control of Crimea, as well as land between the Southern Bug and the Dniester, including the Khadzhibey Estuary where the Turkish fortress of Khadjibey was located.
title: Odesa

text: The newly acquired Ochakov Oblast was promised to the Cossacks by the Russian government for resettlement.
title: Odesa

text: On permission of the Archbishop of Yekaterinoslav Amvrosiy, the Black Sea Kosh Host, that was located around the area between Bender and Ochakiv, built a second wooden church of Saint Nicholas, after the one in Sucleia.
title: Odesa

text: By the Highest rescript of 17 June 1792 addressed to General Kakhovsky it was ordered to establish the Dniester Border Line of fortresses.
title: Odesa

text: The commander of the land forces in Ochakiv Oblast was appointed Graf (Count) Suvorov-Rymnikskiy.
title: Odesa

text: The main fortress was built near Sucleia at the mouth of river Botna as the Head Dniester Fortress by Engineer-Major de Wollant.
title: Odesa

text: Near the new fortress saw the formation of a new "Vorstadt" (suburb) where people moved from Sucleia and Parkan.
title: Odesa

text: With the establishment of the Voznesensk Governorate on 27 January 1795, the Vorstadt was named Tiraspol.
title: Odesa

text: The Namestnik of Yekaterinoslav and Voznesensk, Platon Zubov (one of Catherine's favorites), supported this proposal.
title: Odesa

text: In 1794 Catherine issued a Rescript to José de Ribas: "Considering favorable Khadjibey location...
title: Odesa

text: I order to establish here a navy harbor and trading pierce..." and invested the first money (26.000 rubles) in construction.
title: Odesa

text: Franz de Voland drew up a plan that would end up being the city's plan.
title: Odesa

text: However, adjacent to the new official locality, a Moldavian colony already existed, which by the end of the 18th century was an independent settlement named Moldavanka.
title: Odesa

text: Another version posits that the settlement appeared after Odesa itself was founded, as a settlement of Moldavians, Greeks, and Albanians fleeing the Ottoman yoke.
title: Odesa

text: Under Paul I of Russia, construction of Odesa was stopped, Franz de Voland was removed from the project, and José de Ribas was implicated in a plot to assassinate the Emperor.
title: Odesa

text: After Paul's assassination in 1801, the city resumed construction, and used a plan largely from de Voland's work.
title: Odesa

text: The first census that was conducted in Odesa was in 1797 which accounted for 3,455 people.
title: Odesa

text: Since 1795, the city had its own city magistrate, and since 1796 a city council of six members and the Odesa Commodity Exchange.
title: Odesa

text: In 1801, in Odesa had opened the first commercial bank.
title: Odesa

text: In 1803, the city accounted for 9,000 people.
title: Odesa

text: In their settlement, also known as Novaya Slobodka, the Moldavians owned relatively small plots on which they built village-style houses and cultivated vineyards and gardens.
title: Odesa

text: What became Mykhailovsky Square was the center of this settlement and the site of its first Orthodox church, the Church of the Dormition, built in 1821 close to the seashore, as well as a cemetery.
title: Odesa

text: Nearby stood the military barracks and the country houses (dacha) of the city's wealthy residents, including that of the Duc de Richelieu, appointed by Tsar Alexander I as Governor of Odesa in 1803.
title: Odesa

text: Richelieu played a role during Ottoman plague epidemic which hit Odesa in the autumn 1812.
title: Odesa

text: Dismissive of any attempt to forge a compromise between quarantine requirements and free trade, Prince Kuriakin (the Saint Petersburg-based High Commissioner for Sanitation) countermanded Richelieu's orders.
title: Odesa

text: In the period from 1795 to 1814, the population of Odesa increased 15 times over and reached almost 20 thousand people.
title: Odesa

text: The first city plan was designed by the engineer F.
title: Odesa

text: Colonists of various ethnicities settled mainly in the area of the former colony, outside of the official boundaries, and as a consequence, in the first third of the 19th century, Moldavanka emerged as the dominant settlement.
title: Odesa

text: The new city quickly became a major success although initially, it received little state funding and privileges.
title: Odesa

text: Its early growth owed much to the work of the Duc de Richelieu, who served as the city's governor between 1803 and 1814.
title: Odesa

text: Having fled the French Revolution, he had served in Catherine's army against the Turks.
title: Odesa

text: He is credited with designing the city and organizing its amenities and infrastructure, and is considered one of the founding fathers of Odesa, together with another Frenchman, Count Andrault de Langeron, who succeeded him in office.
title: Odesa

text: Richelieu is commemorated by a bronze statue, unveiled in 1828 to a design by Ivan Martos.
title: Odesa

text: His contributions to the city are mentioned by Mark Twain in his travelogue Innocents Abroad: "I mention this statue and this stairway because they have their story.
title: Odesa

text: In 1819, Odesa became a free port, a status it retained until 1859.
title: Odesa

text: The Filiki Eteria, a Greek freemasonry-style society that was to play an important role in the Greek War of Independence, was founded in Odesa in 1814 before relocating to Constantinople in 1818.
title: Odesa

text: Hundreds of refugees from the Chios massacre settled in Odesa.
title: Odesa

text: Odesa's cosmopolitan nature was documented by the great Russian poet Alexander Pushkin, who lived in internal exile in Odesa between 1823 and 1824.
title: Odesa

text: In his letters, he wrote that Odesa was a city where "the air is filled with all Europe, French is spoken and there are European papers and magazines to read".
title: Odesa

text: Odesa's growth was interrupted by the Crimean War of 1853–1856, during which it was bombarded by British and Imperial French naval forces.
title: Odesa

text: It soon recovered and the growth in trade made Odesa Russia's largest grain-exporting port.
title: Odesa

text: In 1866, the city was linked by rail with Kyiv and Kharkiv as well as with Iaşi in Romania.
title: Odesa

text: The city became the home of a large Jewish community during the 19th century, and by 1897 Jews were estimated to comprise some 37% of the population.
title: Odesa

text: The community, however, was repeatedly subjected to antisemitism and anti-Jewish agitation from almost all Christian segments of the population.
title: Odesa

text: Pogroms were carried out in 1821, 1859, 1871, 1881 and 1905.
title: Odesa

text: Many Odesan Jews fled abroad after 1882, particularly to the Ottoman region that became Palestine, and the city became an important base of support for Zionism.
title: Odesa

text: Until the 1870s, Odesa's Italian population grew steadily.
title: Odesa

text: From the following decade this growth stopped, and the decline of the Italian community in Odesa began.
title: Odesa

text: The reason was mainly one, namely the gradual integration into the Slavic population of Odesa, i.e.
title: Odesa

text: The revolution of 1917 sent many of them to Italy, or to other cities in Europe.
title: Odesa

text: In Soviet times, only a few dozen Italians remained in Odesa, most of whom no longer knew their own language.
title: Odesa

text: Over time they merged with the local population, losing the ethnic connotations of origin.
title: Odesa

text: At the top of the steps, which lead down to the port, stands a statue of the Duc de Richelieu.
title: Odesa

text: The actual massacre took place in streets nearby, not on the steps themselves, but the film caused many to visit Odesa to see the site of the "slaughter".
title: Odesa

text: The "Odesa Steps" continue to be a tourist attraction in Odesa.
title: Odesa

text: The film was made at Odesa's Cinema Factory, one of the oldest cinema studios in the former Soviet Union.
title: Odesa

text: After signing of the Brest-Litovsk Treaty all Bolshevik forces were driven out by 13 March 1918 by the combined armed forces of the Austro-Hungarian Army, providing support to the Ukrainian People's Republic.
title: Odesa

text: With the end of the World War I and the withdrawal of armies of Central Powers, the Soviet forces fought for control over the country with the army of the Ukrainian People's Republic.
title: Odesa

text: A few months later the city was occupied by the French Army and the Greek Army that supported the Russian White Army in its struggle with the Bolsheviks.
title: Odesa

text: The Ukrainian general Nykyfor Hryhoriv who sided with Bolsheviks managed to drive the Triple Entente forces out of the city, but Odesa was soon retaken by the Russian White Army.
title: Odesa

text: By 1920 the Soviet Red Army managed to overpower both the Ukrainian and Russian White Army and secure the city.
title: Odesa

text: The people of Odesa suffered badly from a famine that resulted from the Russian Civil War in 1921–1922 due to the Soviet policies of prodrazverstka.
title: Odesa

text: The Siege of Odessa (1941) started on 5 August and lasted for 73 days.
title: Odesa

text: The defense was organized on three lines with emplacements consisting of trenches, anti-tank ditches and pillboxes.
title: Odesa

text: The first line was 80 km (50 mi) long and situated some 25 to 30 km (16 to 19 mi) from the city.
title: Odesa

text: The second and main line of defense was situated 6 to 8 km (3.7 to 5.0 mi) from the city and was about 30 km (19 mi) long.
title: Odesa

text: The third and last line of defense was organized inside the city itself.
title: Odesa

text: Lyudmila Pavlichenko, the famous female sniper, took part in the battle for Odesa.
title: Odesa

text: She recorded 187 confirmed kills during the defense of Odesa.
title: Odesa

text: Pavlichenko's confirmed kills during World War II totaled 309 (including 36 enemy snipers).
title: Odesa

text: The city fell to the Axis on 16 October 1941, and it was henceforth subject to Romanian administration.
title: Odesa

text: By that time, the Soviet authorities had been able to evacuate 200,000 people as well as weaponry and industrial equipment.
title: Odesa

text: A day later, Odesa was made the capital of Transnistria.
title: Odesa

text: Partisan fighting continued, however, in the city's catacombs.
title: Odesa

text: Following the siege, and the Axis occupation, approximately 25,000 Odesans were murdered in the outskirts of the city and over 35,000 deported; this came to be known as the 1941 Odessa massacre.
title: Odesa

text: After the Nazi forces began to lose ground on the Eastern Front, the Romanian administration changed its policy, refusing to deport the remaining Jewish population to extermination camps in German occupied Poland, and allowing Jews to work as hired labourers.
title: Odesa

text: As a result, despite the events of 1941, the survival of the Jewish population in this area was higher than in other areas of occupied eastern Europe.
title: Odesa

text: A Soviet medal, "For the Defence of Odesa", was established on 22 December 1942.
title: Odesa

text: It was one of the first four Soviet cities to be awarded the title of "Hero City" in 1945. (The others were Leningrad, Stalingrad, and Sevastopol).
title: Odesa

text: The city suffered severe damage and sustained many casualties over the course of the war.
title: Odesa

text: Many parts of Odesa were damaged during both its siege and recapture on 10 April 1944, when the city was finally liberated by the Red Army.
title: Odesa

text: Nevertheless, the majority of Odesa's Jews emigrated to Israel, the United States and other Western countries between the 1970s and 1990s.
title: Odesa

text: Many ended up in the Brooklyn neighborhood of Brighton Beach, sometimes known as "Little Odesa".
title: Odesa

text: Domestic migration of the Odesan middle and upper classes to Moscow and Leningrad, cities that offered even greater opportunities for career advancement, also occurred on a large scale.
title: Odesa

text: Despite this, the city grew rapidly by filling the void of those left with new migrants from rural Ukraine and industrial professionals invited from all over the Soviet Union.
title: Odesa

text: As a part of the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic, the city preserved and somewhat reinforced its unique cosmopolitan mix of Russian/Ukrainian/Jewish culture and a predominantly Russophone environment with the uniquely accented dialect of Russian spoken in the city.
title: Odesa

text: Odesa is a city of more than 1 million people.
title: Odesa

text: The city's industries include shipbuilding, oil refining, chemicals, metalworking, and food processing.
title: Odesa

text: Odesa is also a Ukrainian naval base and home to a fishing fleet.
title: Odesa

text: It is known for its large outdoor market – the Seventh-Kilometer Market, the largest of its kind in Europe.
title: Odesa

text: Odesa was a contender for hosting Euro 2012 football matches in, but lost the competition to other cities in Ukraine.
title: Odesa

text: The city saw violence in the 2014 pro-Russian unrest in Ukraine during the 2014 Odesa clashes.
title: Odesa

text: The 2 May 2014 Odesa clashes between pro-Ukrainian and pro-Russian protestors killed 42 people.
title: Odesa

text: Four were killed during the protests, and at least 32 people were killed after a trade union building was set on fire after Molotov cocktails exchange between sides.
title: Odesa

text: Polls conducted from September to December 2014 found no support for joining Russia.
title: Odesa

text: Odesa was struck by three bomb blasts in December 2014, one of which killed one person (the injuries sustained by the victim indicated that he had dealt with explosives).
title: Odesa

text: Internal Affairs Ministry advisor Zorian Shkiryak said on 25 December that Odesa and Kharkiv had become "cities which are being used to escalate tensions" in Ukraine.
title: Odesa

text: Shkiryak said that he suspected that these cities were singled out because of their "geographic position".
title: Odesa

text: On 5 January 2015 the city's Euromaidan Coordination Center and a cargo train car were (non-lethally) bombed.
title: Odesa

text: Until 18 July 2020, Odesa was incorporated as a city of oblast significance.
title: Odesa

text: In July 2020, as part of the administrative reform of Ukraine, which reduced the number of raions of Odesa Oblast to seven, the city of Odesa was merged into newly established Odesa Raion.
title: Odesa

text: In the 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine, the city faced Russian bombing attacks.
title: Odesa

text: On 23 April 2022, Russian troops bombarded Odesa with cruise missiles.
title: Odesa

text: They destroyed both the city's military infrastructure and residential buildings, killing eight people and wounding another eighteen people.
title: Odesa

text: In addition, the Russian military destroyed more than 1,000 m2 of the territory of the cemetery.
title: Odesa

text: The city suffered further aerial attacks on regional infrastructure facilities in October 2022, cutting off power to 10,500 households and injuring three people.
title: Odesa

text: Throughout the war, Odesa came under repeated attack.
title: Odesa

text: On 25 January 2023, UNESCO announced that the historical city center of Odesa was added to UNESCO's World Heritage List.
title: Odesa

text: The international border between Ukraine and the Republic of Moldova lies some 40 km (25 mi) west of the city.
title: Odesa

text: The city is situated on terraced hills overlooking a small harbor.
title: Odesa

text: The average elevation at which the city is located is around 50 metres (160 feet).
title: Odesa

text: The maximum is 65 metres (213 feet) and minimum (on the coast) amounts to 4.2 metres (13.8 feet) above sea level.
title: Odesa

text: There are three large estuaries near the city: Kuialnyk, Khadzhibey and Sukhyi.
title: Odesa

text: The city currently covers a territory of 162.42 km2 (63 sq mi).
title: Odesa

text: The population density for which is around 6,139 persons/km2.
title: Odesa

text: Sources of running water in the city include the Dniester River, from which water is taken and then purified at a processing plant just outside the city.
title: Odesa

text: Being located in the south of Ukraine, the topography of the area surrounding the city is typically flat and there are no large mountains or hills for many kilometres around.
title: Odesa

text: Flora is of the deciduous variety and Odesa is known for its tree-lined avenues which, in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, made the city a favourite year-round retreat for the Russian aristocracy.
title: Odesa

text: The city's location on the coast of the Black Sea has also helped to create a booming tourist industry in Odesa.
title: Odesa

text: The city's Arkadia beach has long been a favourite place for relaxation, both for the city's inhabitants and its visitors.
title: Odesa

text: This is a large sandy beach which is located to the south of the city centre.
title: Odesa

text: Odesa's many sandy beaches are considered to be quite unique in Ukraine, as the country's southern coast (particularly in the Crimea) tends to be a location in which the formation of stoney and pebble beaches has proliferated.
title: Odesa

text: The coastal cliffs adjacent to the city are home to frequent landslides, resulting in a typical change of landscape along the Black Sea.
title: Odesa

text: Due to the fluctuating slopes of land, city planners are responsible for monitoring the stability of such areas, and for preserving potentially threatened building and other structures of the city above sea level near water.
title: Odesa

text: Also a potential danger to the infrastructure and architecture of the city is the presence of multiple openings underground.
title: Odesa

text: These cavities can cause buildings to collapse, resulting in a loss of money and business.
title: Odesa

text: This has, over the past few centuries, aided the city greatly in creating conditions necessary for the development of summer tourism.
title: Odesa

text: During the tsarist era, Odesa's climate was considered to be beneficial for the body, and thus many wealthy but sickly persons were sent to the city in order to relax and recuperate.
title: Odesa

text: This resulted in the development of spa culture and the establishment of a number of luxury hotels in the city.
title: Odesa

text: The average annual temperature of the sea is 13–14 °C (55–57 °F).
title: Odesa

text: Seasonal sea temperatures range from an average of 6 °C (43 °F) from January to March, to 23 °C (73 °F) in August.
title: Odesa

text: Typically, for a total of 4 months – from June to September – the average sea temperature in the Gulf of Odesa and the city's bay area exceeds 20 °C (68 °F).
title: Odesa

text: The city typically experiences dry, cold winters which are relatively mild when compared to most of Ukraine, as they are marked by temperatures that rarely fall below −10 °C (14 °F).
title: Odesa

text: Summers see an increased level of precipitation, and the city often experiences warm weather with temperatures often reaching the high 20s and low 30s °C.
title: Odesa

text: Snow cover is light to moderate due to the city's location on the northern coast of the Black Sea, and municipal services rarely experience problems that can often be found in other, more northern Ukrainian cities.
title: Odesa

text: Up until the early 1940s the city had a large Jewish population.
title: Odesa

text: As the result of mass deportation to extermination camps during the Second World War, the city's Jewish population declined considerably.
title: Odesa

text: Since the 1970s, the majority of the remaining Jewish population emigrated to Israel and other countries, shrinking the Jewish community.
title: Odesa

text: Through most of the 19th century and until the mid-20th century, the largest ethnic group in Odesa was Russians, with the second largest ethnic group being Jews.
title: Odesa

text: Italians trace their presence in what later would become Odessa to as early as 1200, when Genoese "Ginestra" trade ships anchored there.
title: Odesa

text: In 1797 there were about 800 Italians in Odesa, equal to 10% of the total population.
title: Odesa

text: For more than a century the Italians of Odesa greatly influenced the culture, art, industry, society, architecture, politics and economy of the city.
title: Odesa

text: Among the works created by the Italians of Odesa there were the Potemkin Stairs and the Odesa Opera and Ballet Theater.
title: Odesa

text: At the beginning of the 19th century the Italian language became the second official language in Odesa, after Russian.
title: Odesa

text: Until the 1870s, Odesa's Italian population grew steadily.
title: Odesa

text: From the following decade this growth stopped, and the decline of the Italian community in Odesa began.
title: Odesa

text: The reason was mainly one, namely the gradual integration into the Slavic population of Odesa, i.e.
title: Odesa

text: The revolution of 1917 sent many of them to Italy, or to other cities in Europe.
title: Odesa

text: In Soviet times, only a few dozen Italians remained in Odesa, most of whom no longer knew their own language.
title: Odesa

text: Over time they merged with the local population, losing the ethnic connotations of origin.
title: Odesa

text: The peculiarity of the ethnic composition of the modern population of the city of Odesa is its ethnic diversity.
title: Odesa

text: Ukrainian is gaining in popularity: in 2021, the share of residents speaking some Ukrainian at home has increased almost 5 times from 6% in 2015 to 29% in 2021.
title: Odesa

text: The city of Odesa was previously governed by a mayor and city council which work cooperatively to ensure the smooth-running of the city and procure its municipal bylaws.
title: Odesa

text: The city's budget was also controlled by the administration.
title: Odesa

text: The mayoralty played the role of the executive in the city's municipal administration.
title: Odesa

text: Above all came the mayor who was elected by the city's electorate for five years in a direct election.
title: Odesa

text: In the 2015 mayoral election Hennadii Trukhanov was re-elected in the first round with 52,9% of the vote, and again in the second round of the 2020 election, receiving 54.28% of the vote.
title: Odesa

text: On 15 October 2025 Ukrainian president Volodymyr Zelenskyy created the Odesa city military administration.
title: Odesa

text: Serhiy Lysak was appointed head of the military administration.
title: Odesa

text: Mayor Trukhanov was deemed ineligible to continue in office, as Ukrainian law prohibits officials from holding dual citizenship.
title: Odesa

text: One day earlier, Zelensky had signed a decree revoking Trukhanov's Ukrainian citizenship after the Security Service of Ukraine (SBU) released a Russian passport it said belonged to him.
title: Odesa

text: There are five deputy mayors, each of which is responsible for a certain particular part of the city's public policy.
title: Odesa

text: The City Council of the city makes up the administration's legislative branch, thus effectively making it a city 'parliament' or rada.
title: Odesa

text: The municipal council is made up of 120 elected members, who are each elected to represent a certain district of the city for a four-year term.
title: Odesa

text: The current council is the fifth in the city's modern history, and was elected in January 2011.
title: Odesa

text: In the regular meetings of the municipal council, problems facing the city are discussed, and annually the city's budget is drawn up.
title: Odesa

text: The council has seventeen standing commissions which play an important role in controlling the finances and trading practices of the city and its merchants.
title: Odesa

text: In 1887 one of the city's most well known architectural monuments was completed – the theatre, which still hosts a range of performances to this day; it is widely regarded as one of the world's finest opera houses.
title: Odesa

text: The first opera house was opened in 1810 and destroyed by fire in 1873.
title: Odesa

text: The modern building was constructed by Fellner and Helmer in neo-baroque; its luxurious hall was built in the rococo style.
title: Odesa

text: It is said that thanks to its unique acoustics, even a whisper from the stage can be heard in any part of the hall.
title: Odesa

text: The theatre was projected along the lines of Dresden's Semperoper built in 1878, with its nontraditional foyer following the curvatures of the auditorium; the building's most recent renovation was completed in 2007.
title: Odesa

text: The original 200 steps (now reduced to 192) were designed by Italian architect Francesco Boffo and built between 1837 and 1841.
title: Odesa

text: The steps were made famous by Sergei Eisenstein in his film, Battleship Potemkin.
title: Odesa

text: Most of the city's 19th-century houses were built of limestone mined nearby.
title: Odesa

text: Abandoned mines were later used and broadened by local smugglers.
title: Odesa

text: This created a gigantic, complicated labyrinth of tunnels beneath Odesa, known as the Odesa catacombs.
title: Odesa

text: During World War II, the catacombs served as a hiding place for partisans and natural shelter for civilians, who were escaping airplane bombing.
title: Odesa

text: Derybasivska Street, an attractive pedestrian avenue named after José de Ribas, the Neapolitan-born founder of Odesa and decorated Russian Navy Admiral from the Russo-Turkish War, is famous by its unique character and architecture.
title: Odesa

text: As one of the biggest on the Black Sea, Odesa's port is busy all year round.
title: Odesa

text: The Odesa Seaport is located on an artificial stretch of the Black Sea coast, along with the north-western part of the Gulf of Odesa.
title: Odesa

text: The total shoreline length of Odesa's seaport is around 7.23 kilometres (4.49 mi).
title: Odesa

text: The port is able to handle up to 14 million tons of cargo and about 24 million tons of oil products annually, whilst its passenger terminals can cater for around 4 million passengers a year at full capacity.
title: Odesa

text: As Odesa has been inhabited by peoples of different religions, it has a variety of religious buildings, such as the Orthodox Transfiguration Cathedral, Catholic Assumption of the Blessed Virgin Mary Cathedral, Lutheran St.
title: Odesa

text: There is also a number of historic cemeteries, including the Cossack Kuialnyk Cemetery.
title: Odesa

text: The city is also home to a university botanical garden, which recently celebrated its 200th anniversary, and a number of other smaller gardens.
title: Odesa

text: The City Garden, or Gorodskoy Sad, is perhaps the most famous of Odesa's gardens.
title: Odesa

text: Laid out in 1803 by Felix De Ribas (brother of the founder of Odesa, José de Ribas) on a plot of urban land he owned, the garden is located right in the heart of the city.
title: Odesa

text: When Felix decided that he was no longer able to provide enough money for the garden's upkeep, he decided to present it to the people of Odesa in 1806.
title: Odesa

text: The garden is home to a bandstand and is the traditional location for outdoor theater in the summertime.
title: Odesa

text: Numerous sculptures can also be found within the grounds, as well as a musical fountain, the waters of which are computer controlled to coordinate with the musical melody being played.
title: Odesa

text: Odesa's largest park, Shevchenko Park (previously Alexander Park), was founded in 1875, during a visit to the city by Emperor Alexander II.
title: Odesa

text: The park covers an area of around 700 by 900 metres (2,300 by 3,000 feet) and is located near the centre of the city, on the side closest to the sea.
title: Odesa

text: Within the park there are a variety of cultural and entertainment facilities, and wide pedestrian avenues.
title: Odesa

text: In the center of the park is the local top-flight football team's Chornomorets Stadium, the Alexander Column and municipal observatory.
title: Odesa

text: The Baryatinsky Bulvar is popular for its route, which starts at the park's gate before winding its way along the edge of the coastal plateau.
title: Odesa

text: The city's best-known and most prestigious university is the Odesa 'I.I.
title: Odesa

text: This university is the oldest in the city and was first founded by an edict of Tsar Alexander II of Russia in 1865 as the Imperial Novorossian University.
title: Odesa

text: Since then the university has developed to become one of modern Ukraine's leading research and teaching universities, with staff of around 1,800 and total of thirteen academic faculties.
title: Odesa

text: Other than the National University, the city is also home to the 1921-inaugurated Odesa National Economic University, the Odesa National Medical University (founded 1900), the 1918-founded Odesa National Polytechnic University.
title: Odesa

text: In addition to these universities, the city is home to the National University "Odesa Law Academy", the National Academy of Telecommunications, the Odesa State Environmental University and the Odesa National Maritime Academy.
title: Odesa

text: The South Ukrainian National Pedagogical University is also based in the city, this is one of the largest institutions for the preparation of educational specialists in Ukraine and is recognised as one of the country's finest of such universities.
title: Odesa

text: In addition to all the state-run universities mentioned above, Odesa is also home to many private educational institutes and academies which offer highly specified courses in a range of subjects.
title: Odesa

text: With regard to primary and secondary education, Odesa has many schools catering for all ages from kindergarten through to lyceum (final secondary school level) age.
title: Odesa

text: The Odesa Museum of Western and Eastern Art is big art museum; it has large European collections from the 16–20th centuries along with the art from the East on display.
title: Odesa

text: There are paintings from Caravaggio, Mignard, Hals, Teniers and Del Piombo.
title: Odesa

text: Of note is Odesa's Alexander Pushkin Museum, which is dedicated to detailing the short time Pushkin spent in exile in Odesa, a period during which he continued to write.
title: Odesa

text: The poet also has a city street named after him, as well as a statue.
title: Odesa

text: Other museums in the city include the Odesa Archeological Museum, which is housed in a neoclassical building, the Odesa Numismatics Museum, the Odesa Museum of the Regional History, Museum of Heroic Defense of Odesa (411th Battery).
title: Odesa

text: Among the city's public sculptures, two sets of Medici lions can be noted, at the Vorontsov Palace as well as in the Starosinnyi Garden.
title: Odesa

text: Jacob Adler, the major star of the Yiddish theatre in New York and father of the actor, director and teacher Stella Adler, was born and spent his youth in Odesa.
title: Odesa

text: The most popular Russian show business people from Odesa are Yakov Smirnoff (comedian), Mikhail Zhvanetsky (legendary humorist writer, who began his career as a port engineer) and Roman Kartsev (comedian).
title: Odesa

text: Zhvanetsky's and Kartsev's success in the 1970s, along with Odesa's KVN team, contributed to Odesa's established status as "capital of Soviet humor", culminating in the annual Humoryna festival, carried out around the beginning of April.
title: Odesa

text: Odesa was also the home of the late Armenian painter Sarkis Ordyan (1918–2003), the Ukrainian painter Mickola Vorokhta and the Greek philologist, author and promoter of Demotic Greek Ioannis Psycharis (1854–1929).
title: Odesa

text: Yuri Siritsov, bass player of the Israeli Metal band PallaneX is originally from Odesa.
title: Odesa

text: Israeli painter Avigdor Stematsky (1908–89) was born in Odesa.
title: Odesa

text: Odesa produced one of the founders of the Soviet violin school, Pyotr Stolyarsky.
title: Odesa

text: Odesa has produced many writers, including Isaac Babel, whose series of short stories, Odessa Tales, are set in the city.
title: Odesa

text: Edmund de Waal's best selling memoire, The Hare with the Amber Eyes is the story of his family, the Ephrussi, once a great banking dynasty based in Odesa.
title: Odesa

text: Other Odesans are the duo Ilf and Petrov—authors of The Twelve Chairs, and Yuri Olesha, author of "The Three Fat Men".
title: Odesa

text: Vera Inber, a poet and writer, as well as the poet and journalist Margarita Aliger, were both born in Odesa.
title: Odesa

text: The Italian writer, Slavist and anti-fascist dissident Leone Ginzburg was born in Odesa into a Jewish family, and then went to Italy where he grew up and lived.
title: Odesa

text: The Hebrew author and poet Haim Bialik established a publishing house and worked in the city from the 1890s to 1911.
title: Odesa

text: One of the most prominent pre-war Soviet writers, Valentin Kataev, was born here and began his writing career as early as high school (gymnasia).
title: Odesa

text: Before moving to Moscow in 1922, he made quite a few acquaintances here, including Yury Olesha and Ilya Ilf (Ilf's co-author Petrov was in fact Kataev's brother, Petrov being his pen-name).
title: Odesa

text: Kataev became a benefactor for these young authors, who would become some of the most talented and popular Russian writers of this period.
title: Odesa

text: In 1955 Kataev became the first chief editor of the Youth (Russian: Юность, Yunost'), one of the leading literature magazines of the Ottepel of the 1950s and 1960s.
title: Odesa

text: These authors and comedians played a great role in establishing the "Odesa myth" in the Soviet Union.
title: Odesa

text: Odesans were and are viewed in the ethnic stereotype as sharp-witted, street-wise and eternally optimistic.
title: Odesa

text: These qualities are reflected in the "Odesa dialect", which borrows chiefly from the characteristic speech of the Odesan Jews, and is enriched by a plethora of influences common for the port city.
title: Odesa

text: Practical joking is a central theme throughout, and Odesans dress in unique, colorful attire to express their spontaneous and comedic selves.
title: Odesa

text: The tradition has been celebrated since the early 1970s, when the humor of Ukraine's citizens were drawn to television and the media, further developing into a mass festival.
title: Odesa

text: Yefrosyniya Zarnytska (1867–1936), actress Pyotr Schmidt (1867–1906) (better known as "Lieutenant Schmidt"), one of the leaders of the Sevastopol uprising, was born in Odesa.
title: Odesa

text: He was commonly known as the national Hebrew poet.
title: Odesa

text: Sidney Reilly (ca.1873–1925), who was employed by William Melville (1850–1918) as one of the first spies of the British Secret Service Bureau, was a native Odesan.
title: Odesa

text: Another intelligence agent from Odesa was Genrikh Lyushkov (1900–1945), who joined the Odesa Cheka in 1920 and reached two-star rank in the NKVD before fleeing to Japanese-occupied Manchuria in 1938 to avoid being murdered.
title: Odesa

text: The composer Jacob Weinberg (1879–1956) was born in Odesa.
title: Odesa

text: He composed over 135 works and was the founder of the Jewish National Conservatory in Jerusalem before immigrating to the U.S. where he became "an influential voice in the promotion of American Jewish music".
title: Odesa

text: Ze'ev Jabotinsky (1880–1940), was born in Odesa, and largely developed his version of Zionism there in the early 1920s.
title: Odesa

text: Rodion Malinovsky (1898–1967), Marshal of the Soviet Union, military commander in World War II and Defense Minister of the Soviet Union, was born in Odesa, whilst renowned Nazi hunter Simon Wiesenthal (1908–2005) lived in the city in 1934/1935.
title: Odesa

text: Alexander Olshanetsky (1892 – 1946), an American composer, conductor, and violinist.
title: Odesa

text: He was a major figure within the Yiddish theatre scene in New York City, born in Odesa.
title: Odesa

text: Stasys Pundzevičius (1893–1980), Chief of the Lithuanian Air Force in the interwar Lithuania, was born in Odesa.
title: Odesa

text: Yitzhak Frenkel Frenel (1899–1981), a native Odesan painter and sculptor of the École de Paris who brought to Israel the influence of modern art and was its first abstract artist.
title: Odesa

text: He emigrated to Israel on the Ruslan in 1919.
title: Odesa

text: Emil Grigoryevich Gilels (1916–1985), widely regarded as one of the greatest pianists of all time, was born in Odesa.
title: Odesa

text: Janka Bryl (1917–2006), Belarusian writer, was born in Odesa.
title: Odesa

text: Mikhail Zhvanetsky (1934–2020), writer, satirist and performer best known for his shows targeting different aspects of the Soviet and post-Soviet everyday life, was born in Odesa.
title: Odesa

text: Yuriy Bazhal (born 1950), Ukrainian scientist, professor, academic.
title: Odesa

text: VitaliV (Vitali Vinogradov) (born 1957), an artist and sculptor based in London since 1989, was born in Odesa.
title: Odesa

text: Savva Libkin (born 1961), a Ukrainian restaurateur, popularizer of Odesa's cuisine.
title: Odesa

text: Valeria Lukyanova (born 1985), a girl from Odesa who looks very similar to a Barbie doll, has received attention on the Internet and from the media for her doll-like appearance.
title: Odesa

text: Kostyantyn Mykolayovych Bocharov (born 1997), better known by his stage name Mélovin is a native of Odesa.
title: Odesa

text: The nearly ice-free port lies near the mouths of the Dnieper, the Southern Bug, the Dniester and the Danube rivers, which provide good links to the hinterland.
title: Odesa

text: During the Soviet period (until 1991) the city functioned as the USSR's largest trading port; it continues in a similar role as independent Ukraine's busiest international port.
title: Odesa

text: The port complex contains an oil and gas transfer and storage facility, a cargo-handling area and a large passenger port.
title: Odesa

text: In 2007 the Port of Odesa handled 31,368,000 tonnes of cargo.
title: Odesa

text: The port of Odesa is one of the Ukrainian Navy's most important bases on the Black Sea.
title: Odesa

text: Rail transport is an important sector of the economy in Odesa – largely due to the role it plays in delivering goods and imports to and from the city's port.
title: Odesa

text: The Container Terminal Odesa (CTO) in the port is the largest container terminal in Ukraine.
title: Odesa

text: It has been operated by the Hamburg-based HHLA Group since 2001 and, in addition to containers, also handles bulk goods, general cargo and project cargo.
title: Odesa

text: This means that Odesa is networked with the ports of Hamburg, Muuga and Trieste via the logistics group HHLA.
title: Odesa

text: Industrial enterprises located in and around the city include those dedicated to fuel refinement, machine building, metallurgy, and other types of light industry such as food preparation, timber plants and chemical industry.
title: Odesa

text: Agriculture is a relatively important sector in the territories surrounding the city.
title: Odesa

text: The Seventh-Kilometer Market is a major commercial complex on the outskirts of the city where private traders now operate one of the largest market complexes in Eastern Europe.
title: Odesa

text: The market has roughly 6,000 traders and an estimated 150,000 customers per day.
title: Odesa

text: Daily sales, according to the Ukrainian periodical Dzerkalo Tyzhnia, were believed to be as high as US$20 million in 2004.
title: Odesa

text: With a staff of 1,200 (mostly guards and janitors), the market is also the region's largest employer.
title: Odesa

text: It is owned by local land and agriculture tycoon Viktor A.
title: Odesa

text: Tavria-V is the most popular retail chain in Odesa.
title: Odesa

text: Key areas of business include: retail, wholesale, catering, production, construction and development, private label.
title: Odesa

text: Consumer recognition is mainly attributed to the high level of service and the quality of services.
title: Odesa

text: Tavria-V is the biggest private company and the biggest tax payer.
title: Odesa

text: Deribasivska Street is one of the city's most important commercial streets, hosting many of the city's boutiques and higher-end shops.
title: Odesa

text: In addition to this there are a number of large commercial shopping centres in the city.
title: Odesa

text: The 19th-century shopping gallery Passage was, for a long time, the city's most upscale shopping district, and remains to this day an important landmark of Odesa.
title: Odesa

text: The tourism sector is of great importance to Odesa, which is currently the second most-visited Ukrainian city.
title: Odesa

text: In 2003 this sector recorded a total revenue of ₴189.2 million.
title: Odesa

text: Other sectors of the city's economy include the banking sector: the city hosts a branch of the National Bank of Ukraine.
title: Odesa

text: Imexbank, one of Ukraine's largest commercial banks, was based in the city, however on 27 May 2015, the Deposit Guarantee Fund of Ukraine made a decision to liquidate the bank.
title: Odesa

text: Odesa also has a well-developed IT industry with large number of IT outsourcing companies and IT product startups.
title: Odesa

text: The Port of Odesa became a provisional headquarters for the Ukrainian Navy, following the Russian occupation of Crimea in 2014.
title: Odesa

text: Before the fall of the Soviet Union, the Port of Odesa harbored the major Soviet cruise line Black Sea Shipping Company.
title: Odesa

text: Navrotsky, came to Odesa from France in 1891.
title: Odesa

text: He was a popular city publisher of the newspaper Odesskii listok ("Odesa leaf").
title: Odesa

text: Odesa is linked to the Ukrainian capital, Kyiv, by the M05 Highway, a high quality multi-lane road which is set to be re-designated, after further reconstructive works, as an 'Avtomagistral' (motorway) in the near future.
title: Odesa

text: Other routes of national significance, passing through Odesa, include the M16 Highway to Moldova, M15 to Izmail and Romania, and the M14 which runs from Odesa, through Mykolaiv and Kherson to Ukraine's eastern border with Russia.
title: Odesa

text: The M14 is of particular importance to Odesa's maritime and shipbuilding industries as it links the city with Ukraine's other large deep water port Mariupol which is located in the south east of the country.
title: Odesa

text: Odesa also has a well-developed system of inter-urban municipal roads and minor beltways.
title: Odesa

text: However, the city is still lacking an extra-urban bypass for transit traffic which does not wish to proceed through the city centre.
title: Odesa

text: From this station services connect Odesa with Warsaw, Prague, Bratislava, Vienna, Berlin, Moscow, St.
title: Odesa

text: Petersburg, the cities of Ukraine and many other cities of the former USSR.
title: Odesa

text: The city's first railway station was opened in the 1880s.
title: Odesa

text: During the Second World War, the iconic building of the main station, which had long been considered to be one of the Russian Empire's premier stations, was destroyed through enemy action.
title: Odesa

text: In 1952 the station was rebuilt to the designs of A Chuprina.
title: Odesa

text: The first metre-gauge steam tramway line ran from Railway Station to Great Fontaine and the second one to Hadzhi Bey Liman.
title: Odesa

text: These routes were both operated by the same Belgian company.
title: Odesa

text: Electric tramway started to operate on 22 August 1907.
title: Odesa

text: The city's public transit system is currently made up of trams, trolleybuses, buses and fixed-route taxis (marshrutkas).
title: Odesa

text: Odesa also has a cable car to Vidrada Beach, and recreational ferry service.
title: Odesa

text: There are two routes of public transport which connect Odesa Airport with the city center: trolley-bus No.14 and marshrutka No.117.
title: Odesa

text: One additional mode of transport in Odesa is the Potemkin Stairs funicular railway, which runs between the city's Primorsky Bulvar and the sea terminal, has been in service since 1902.
title: Odesa

text: In 1998, after many years of neglect, the city decided to raise funds for a replacement track and cars.
title: Odesa

text: This project was delayed on multiple occasions but was finally completed eight years later in 2005.
title: Odesa

text: The Black Sea is a marginal sea located between Europe and Asia, east of the Balkans, south of the East European Plain, west of the Caucasus, and north of Anatolia.
title: Black Sea

text: It is bordered by Bulgaria, Georgia, Romania, Russia, Turkey, and Ukraine.
title: Black Sea

text: The Black Sea is supplied by major rivers, principally the Danube, Dnieper and Dniester.
title: Black Sea

text: Consequently, while six countries have a coastline on the sea, its drainage basin includes parts of 24 countries in Europe.
title: Black Sea

text: The Black Sea, not including the Sea of Azov, covers 436,400 km2 (168,500 sq mi), has a maximum depth of 2,212 m (7,257 ft), and a volume of 547,000 km3 (131,000 cu mi).
title: Black Sea

text: These rises are the Pontic Mountains to the south, the southwest-facing peninsulas, the Caucasus Mountains to the east, and the Crimean Mountains to the mid-north.
title: Black Sea

text: In the west, the coast is generally small floodplains below foothills such as the Strandzha; Cape Emine, a dwindling of the east end of the Balkan Mountains; and the Dobruja Plateau considerably farther north.
title: Black Sea

text: The longest east–west extent is about 1,175 km (730 mi).
title: Black Sea

text: Important cities along the coast include (clockwise from the Bosporus) the northern suburbs of Istanbul, Burgas, Varna, Constanța, Odesa, Yevpatoria, Sevastopol, Yalta, Kerch, Novorossiysk, Sochi, Poti, Batumi, Rize, Trabzon, Ordu, Samsun and Zonguldak.
title: Black Sea

text: The Black Sea has a positive water balance, with an annual net outflow of 300 km3 (72 cu mi) per year through the Bosporus and the Dardanelles into the Aegean Sea.
title: Black Sea

text: This creates a significant and permanent layer of deep water that does not drain or mix and is therefore anoxic.
title: Black Sea

text: The Bosporus strait connects it to the small Sea of Marmara which in turn is connected to the Aegean Sea via the strait of the Dardanelles.
title: Black Sea

text: To the north, the Black Sea is connected to the Sea of Azov by the Kerch Strait.
title: Black Sea

text: The water level has varied significantly over geological time.
title: Black Sea

text: Due to these variations in the water level in the basin, the surrounding shelf and associated aprons have sometimes been dry land.
title: Black Sea

text: At certain critical water levels, connections with surrounding water bodies can become established.
title: Black Sea

text: It is through the most active of these connective routes, the Turkish Straits, that the Black Sea joins the World Ocean.
title: Black Sea

text: During geological periods when this hydrological link was not present, the Black Sea was an endorheic basin, operating independently of the global ocean system (similar to the Caspian Sea today).
title: Black Sea

text: Currently, the Black Sea water level is relatively high; thus, water is being exchanged with the Mediterranean.

text: Ancient Greek voyagers adopted the name as Á-xe(i)nos, identified with the Greek word áxeinos (inhospitable).
title: Black Sea

text: The name Πόντος Ἄξεινος Póntos Áxeinos (Inhospitable Sea), first attested in Pindar (c. 475 BC), was considered an ill omen and was euphemized to its opposite, Εὔξεινος Πόντος Eúxeinos Póntos (Hospitable Sea), also first attested in Pindar.
title: Black Sea

text: This became the commonly used designation in Greek, although in mythological contexts the "true" name Póntos Áxeinos remained favored.
title: Black Sea

text: Strabo's Geographica (1.2.10) reports that in antiquity, the Black Sea was often simply called "the Sea" (ὁ πόντος ho Pontos).
title: Black Sea

text: Popular supposition derives "Black Sea" from the dark color of the water or climatic conditions.
title: Black Sea

text: Some scholars understand the name to be derived from a system of Turkic color symbolism representing the cardinal directions, with black or dark for north, red for south, white for west, and green or light blue for east.
title: Black Sea

text: According to this scheme, the name could only have originated with a people living between the northern (black) and southern (red) seas: this points to the Achaemenids (550–330 BC).
title: Black Sea

text: In the Greater Bundahishn, a Middle Persian Zoroastrian scripture, the Black Sea is called Siyābun.
title: Black Sea

text: In the tenth-century Persian geography book Hudud al-'Alam, the Black Sea is called Georgian Sea (daryā-yi Gurz).
title: Black Sea

text: The Georgian Chronicles use the name zğua sperisa ზღუა სპერისა (Sea of Speri) after the Kartvelian tribe of Speris or Saspers.
title: Black Sea

text: Other modern names such as Chyornoye more and Karadeniz (both meaning Black Sea) originated during the 13th century.
title: Black Sea

text: The basin includes two distinct relict back-arc basins which were initiated by the splitting of an Albian volcanic arc and the subduction of both the Paleo- and Neo-Tethys oceans, but the timings of these events remain uncertain.
title: Black Sea

text: Arc volcanism and extension occurred as the Neo-Tethys Ocean subducted under the southern margin of Laurasia during the Mesozoic.
title: Black Sea

text: Uplift and compressional deformation took place as the Neotethys continued to close.
title: Black Sea

text: Seismic surveys indicate that rifting began in the Western Black Sea in the Barremian and Aptian followed by the formation of oceanic crust 20 million years later in the Santonian.
title: Black Sea

text: Since its initiation, compressional tectonic environments led to subsidence in the basin, interspersed with extensional phases resulting in large-scale volcanism and numerous orogenies, causing the uplift of the Greater Caucasus, Pontides, southern Crimean Peninsula and Balkanides mountain ranges.

text: During the Messinian salinity crisis in the neighboring Mediterranean Sea, water levels fell but without drying up the sea.

text: These geological mechanisms, in the long term, have caused the periodic isolations of the Black Sea from the rest of the global ocean system.
title: Black Sea

text: The large shelf to the north of the basin is up to 190 km (120 mi) wide and features a shallow apron with gradients between 1:40 and 1:1000.
title: Black Sea

text: The Karangat marine transgression occurred during the Eemian Interglacial (MIS 5e).

text: The Neoeuxinian transgression began with an inflow of waters from the Caspian Sea.
title: Black Sea

text: Neoeuxinian deposits are found in the Black Sea below −20 m (−66 ft) water depth in three layers.
title: Black Sea

text: The upper layers correspond with the peak of the Khvalinian transgression, on the shelf shallow-water sands and coquina mixed with silty sands and brackish-water fauna, and inside the Black Sea Depression hydrotroilite silts.
title: Black Sea

text: The middle layers on the shelf are sands with brackish-water mollusc shells.
title: Black Sea

text: Of continental origin, the lower level on the shelf is mostly alluvial sands with pebbles, mixed with less common lacustrine silts and freshwater mollusc shells.
title: Black Sea

text: The deep waters do not mix with the upper layers of water that receive oxygen from the atmosphere.
title: Black Sea

text: As a result, over 90% of the deeper Black Sea volume is anoxic water.
title: Black Sea

text: The Black Sea's circulation patterns are primarily controlled by basin topography and fluvial inputs, which result in a strongly stratified vertical structure.
title: Black Sea

text: Because of the extreme stratification, it is classified as a salt wedge estuary.
title: Black Sea

text: Inflow from the Mediterranean Sea through the Dardanelles and Bosporus has a higher salinity and density than the outflow, creating the classic estuarine circulation.
title: Black Sea

text: This means that the inflow of dense water from the Mediterranean occurs at the bottom of the basin while the outflow of fresher Black Sea surface-water into the Sea of Marmara occurs near the surface.
title: Black Sea

text: Inflow and outflow current speeds are averaged around 0.3 to 0.4 m/s (1.0 to 1.3 ft/s), but much higher speeds are found locally, inducing significant turbulence and vertical shear.
title: Black Sea

text: This allows for turbulent mixing of the two layers.
title: Black Sea

text: Surface water leaves the Black Sea with a salinity of 17 practical salinity units (PSU) and reaches the Mediterranean with a salinity of 34 PSU.
title: Black Sea

text: Likewise, an inflow of the Mediterranean with salinity 38.5 PSU experiences a decrease to about 34 PSU.
title: Black Sea

text: Mean surface circulation is cyclonic; waters around the perimeter of the Black Sea circulate in a basin-wide shelfbreak gyre known as the Rim Current.
title: Black Sea

text: The Rim Current has a maximum velocity of about 50–100 cm/s (20–39 in/s).
title: Black Sea

text: Within this feature, two smaller cyclonic gyres operate, occupying the eastern and western sectors of the basin.
title: Black Sea

text: The Eastern and Western Gyres are well-organized systems in the winter but dissipate into a series of interconnected eddies in the summer and autumn.
title: Black Sea

text: Mesoscale activity in the peripheral flow becomes more pronounced during these warmer seasons and is subject to interannual variability.
title: Black Sea

text: Outside of the Rim Current, numerous quasi-permanent coastal eddies are formed as a result of upwelling around the coastal apron and "wind curl" mechanisms.
title: Black Sea

text: The intra-annual strength of these features is controlled by seasonal atmospheric and fluvial variations.
title: Black Sea

text: During the spring, the Batumi eddy forms in the southeastern corner of the sea.
title: Black Sea

text: Beneath the surface waters—from about 50 to 100 metres (160 to 330 ft)—there exists a halocline that stops at the Cold Intermediate Layer (CIL).
title: Black Sea

text: This layer is composed of cool, salty surface waters, which are the result of localized atmospheric cooling and decreased fluvial input during the winter months.
title: Black Sea

text: It is the remnant of the winter surface mixed layer.
title: Black Sea

text: The base of the CIL is marked by a major pycnocline at about 100–200 metres (330–660 ft), and this density disparity is the major mechanism for isolation of the deep water.
title: Black Sea

text: Below the pycnocline is the Deep Water mass, where salinity increases to 22.3 PSU and temperatures rise to around 8.9 °C (48.0 °F).
title: Black Sea

text: The hydrochemical environment shifts from oxygenated to anoxic, as bacterial decomposition of sunken biomass utilizes all of the free oxygen.
title: Black Sea

text: Weak geothermal heating and long residence time create a very thick convective bottom layer.
title: Black Sea

text: The Black Sea undersea river is a current of particularly saline water flowing through the Bosporus Strait and along the seabed of the Black Sea.
title: Black Sea

text: The discovery of the river, announced on 1 August 2010, was made by scientists at the University of Leeds and is the first of its kind to be identified.
title: Black Sea

text: During periods of high surface productivity, short-lived algal blooms form organic rich layers known as sapropels.
title: Black Sea

text: Scientists have reported an annual phytoplankton bloom that can be seen in many NASA images of the region.

text: Modelling shows that, in the event of an asteroid impact on the Black Sea, the release of hydrogen sulfide clouds would pose a threat to health—and perhaps even life—for people living on the Black Sea coast.
title: Black Sea

text: As with all marine food webs, the Black Sea features a range of trophic groups, with autotrophic algae, including diatoms and dinoflagellates, acting as primary producers.
title: Black Sea

text: During winter, strong winds promote convective overturning and upwelling of nutrients, while high summer temperatures result in marked vertical stratification and a warm, shallow mixed layer.
title: Black Sea

text: Day length and insolation intensity also control the extent of the photic zone.
title: Black Sea

text: Subsurface productivity is limited by nutrient availability, as the anoxic bottom waters act as a sink for reduced nitrate, in the form of ammonia.
title: Black Sea

text: The benthic zone also plays an important role in Black Sea nutrient cycling, as chemosynthetic organisms and anoxic geochemical pathways recycle nutrients which can be upwelled to the photic zone, enhancing productivity.
title: Black Sea

text: Generally, the annual cycle of phytoplankton development comprises significant diatom and dinoflagellate-dominated spring production, followed by a weaker mixed assemblage of community development below the seasonal thermocline during summer months, and surface-intensified autumn production.
title: Black Sea

text: This pattern of productivity is augmented by an Emiliania huxleyi bloom during the late spring and summer months.
title: Black Sea

text: Dinoflagellates Annual dinoflagellate distribution is defined by an extended bloom period in subsurface waters during the late spring and summer.
title: Black Sea

text: In November, subsurface plankton production is combined with surface production, due to vertical mixing of water masses and nutrients such as nitrite.
title: Black Sea

text: The major bloom-forming dinoflagellate species in the Black Sea is Gymnodinium sp.
title: Black Sea

text: Estimates of dinoflagellate diversity in the Black Sea range from 193 to 267 species.
title: Black Sea

text: This level of species richness is relatively low in comparison to the Mediterranean Sea, which is attributable to the brackish conditions, low water transparency and presence of anoxic bottom waters.
title: Black Sea

text: It is also possible that the low winter temperatures below 4 °C (39 °F) of the Black Sea prevent thermophilous species from becoming established.
title: Black Sea

text: The relatively high organic matter content of Black Sea surface water favors the development of heterotrophic (an organism that uses organic carbon for growth) and mixotrophic dinoflagellates species (able to exploit different trophic pathways), relative to autotrophs.
title: Black Sea

text: Despite its unique hydrographic setting, there are no confirmed endemic dinoflagellate species in the Black Sea.
title: Black Sea

text: Diatoms The Black Sea is populated by many species of the marine diatom, which commonly exist as colonies of unicellular, non-motile auto- and heterotrophic algae.
title: Black Sea

text: The life-cycle of most diatoms can be described as 'boom and bust' and the Black Sea is no exception, with diatom blooms occurring in surface waters throughout the year, most reliably during March.
title: Black Sea

text: Additional factors such as predation by zooplankton and ammonium-based regenerated production also have a role to play in the annual diatom cycle.
title: Black Sea

text: Typically, Proboscia alata blooms during spring and Pseudosolenia calcar-avis blooms during the autumn.
title: Black Sea

text: Coccolithophores Coccolithophores are a type of motile, autotrophic phytoplankton that produce CaCO3 plates, known as coccoliths, as part of their life cycle.
title: Black Sea

text: In the Black Sea, the main period of coccolithophore growth occurs after the bulk of the dinoflagellate growth has taken place.
title: Black Sea

text: In May, the dinoflagellates move below the seasonal thermocline into deeper waters, where more nutrients are available.
title: Black Sea

text: This permits coccolithophores to utilize the nutrients in the upper waters, and by the end of May, with favorable light and temperature conditions, growth rates reach their highest.
title: Black Sea

text: The major bloom-forming species is Emiliania huxleyi, which is also responsible for the release of dimethyl sulfide into the atmosphere.
title: Black Sea

text: Overall, coccolithophore diversity is low in the Black Sea, and although recent sediments are dominated by E. huxleyi and Braarudosphaera bigelowii, Holocene sediments have been shown to also contain Helicopondosphaera and Discolithina species.
title: Black Sea

text: Cyanobacteria Cyanobacteria are a phylum of picoplanktonic (plankton ranging in size from 0.2 to 2.0 μm) bacteria that obtain their energy via photosynthesis, and are present throughout the world's oceans.

text: They exhibit a range of morphologies, including filamentous colonies and biofilms.

text: The Carpathian Mountains or Carpathians () are a range of mountains forming an arc across Central and Southeast Europe.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: Roughly 1,500 km (930 mi) long, it is the third-longest European mountain range after the Urals at 2,500 km (1,600 mi) and the Scandinavian Mountains at 1,700 km (1,100 mi).
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The highest peaks in the Carpathians are in the Tatra Mountains, exceeding 2,600 m (8,500 ft), closely followed by those in the Southern Carpathians in Romania, exceeding 2,550 m (8,370 ft).
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The range stretches from the Western Carpathians in Austria, the Czech Republic, Slovakia and Poland, clockwise through the Eastern Carpathians in Ukraine and Romania, to the Southern Carpathians in Romania and Serbia.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The term Outer Carpathians is frequently used to describe the northern rim of the Western and Eastern Carpathians.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The Carpathians provide habitat for the largest European populations of brown bears, wolves, chamois, and lynxes, with the highest concentration in Romania, as well as over one-third of all European plant species.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The mountains and their foothills also have many thermal and mineral waters, with Romania having one-third of the European total.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: Romania is likewise home to the second-largest area of virgin forests in Europe after Russia, totaling 250,000 hectares (65%), most of them in the Carpathians, with the Southern Carpathians constituting Europe's largest unfragmented forest area.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The Western Carpathians were called Carpates, a name that is first recorded in Ptolemy's Geographia (second century AD).
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: Thirteenth- to fifteenth-century Hungarian documents named the mountains Thorchal, Tarczal, or less frequently Montes Nivium ("Snowy Mountains").
title: Carpathian Mountains

title: Carpathian Mountains

title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The archaic Polish word karpa meant 'rugged irregularities, underwater obstacles/rocks, rugged roots, or trunks'.
title: Carpathian Mountains

title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The northwestern Carpathians begin in Slovakia and southern Poland.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: They surround Transcarpathia and Transylvania in a large semicircle, sweeping towards the southeast, and end on the Danube near Orșova in Romania.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The total length of the Carpathians is over 1,500 km (930 mi).
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The mountain chain's width varies between 12 and 500 km (7 and 311 mi).
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The highest altitudes of the Carpathians occur where they are widest, in the Transylvanian plateau and in the southern Tatra Mountains group.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The highest range, in which Gerlachovský štít in Slovakia is the highest peak, is 2,655 m (8,711 ft) above sea level.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The Carpathians cover an area of 190,000 km2 (73,000 sq mi).
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: After the Alps, they form the next-most extensive mountain system in Europe.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: Percentage of the range by country is: Czech Republic (3%) and Austria (1%) in the northwest through Slovakia (21%), Poland (10%), Ukraine (10%), Romania (50%) to Serbia (5%) in the south.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The Carpathians at their highest altitude are only as high as the middle region of the Alps, with which they share a common appearance, climate, and flora.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The Carpathians are separated from the Alps by the Danube, only meeting at the Leitha Mountains at Bratislava.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The river also separates the Carpathians from the Balkan Mountains at Orșova in Romania.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The valley of the March and Oder separates the Carpathians from the Silesian and Moravian chains, which belong to the middle wing of the great Central Mountain System of Europe.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: Unlike the other wings of the system, the Carpathians, which form the watershed between the northern seas and the Black Sea, are surrounded on all sides by plains.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The Carpathian mountains were formed during the Alpine orogeny in the Mesozoic and Cenozoic by moving the ALCAPA (Alpine-Carpathian-Pannonian), Tisza and Dacia plates over subducting oceanic crust.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The mountains take the form of a fold and thrust belt with generally north vergence in the western segment, northeast to east vergence in the eastern portion and southeast vergence in the southern portion.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: Currently, the area is the most seismically active in Central Europe.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The external, generally northern, portion of the orogenic belt is a Tertiary accretionary wedge of a so-called Flysch belt (the Carpathian Flysch Belt) created by rocks scraped off the sea bottom and thrust over the North-European plate.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The Carpathian accretionary wedge is made of several thin skinned nappes composed of Cretaceous to Paleogene turbidites.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: Thrusting of the Flysch nappes over the Carpathian foreland caused the formation of the Carpathian foreland basin.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: Internal zones in western and eastern segments contain older Variscan igneous massifs reworked in Mesozoic thick and thin-skinned nappes.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: During the Middle Miocene this zone was affected by intensive calc-alkaline arc volcanism that developed over the subduction zone of the flysch basins.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: At the same time, the internal zones of the orogenic belt were affected by large extensional structure of the back-arc Pannonian Basin.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The last volcanic activity occurred at Ciomadul about 30,000 years ago.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The mountains started to gain their current shape from the latest Miocene onward.
title: Carpathian Mountains

title: Carpathian Mountains

text: Iron, gold and silver were found in great quantities in the Western Carpathians.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: Foothill forests are primarily of broadleaf deciduous trees, including oak, hornbeam, and linden.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: European beech is characteristic of the montane forest zone.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: Higher-elevation subalpine forests are characterized by Norway spruce (Picea abies).
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: Krummholz and alpine meadows occur above the treeline.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: According to modern geopolitical division, Carpathians can be grouped as: Serbian, Romanian, Ukrainian, Polish, Slovakian, Czech and Austrian.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: Within each nation, specific classifications of the Carpathians have been developing, often reflecting local traditions, and thus creating terminological diversity, that produces various challenges in the fields of comparative classification and international systematization.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The geological border between the Western and Eastern Carpathians runs approximately along the line (south to north) between the towns of Michalovce, Bardejov, Nowy Sącz and Tarnów.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: In older systems the border runs more in the east, along the line (north to south) along the rivers San and Osława (Poland), the town of Snina (Slovakia) and river Tur'ia (Ukraine).
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: Biologists shift the border even further to the east.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: The section of the Carpathians within the borders of Romania is commonly known as the Romanian Carpathians.
title: Carpathian Mountains

text: Classification of eastern sections of the Carpathians is particularly complex, since it was influenced by several overlapping traditions.
