text: Ukrainian cuisine is the collection of the various cooking traditions of the people of Ukraine, one of the largest and most populous European countries.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: It is heavily influenced by the rich dark soil (chornozem) from which its ingredients come, and often involves many components.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Traditional Ukrainian dishes often experience a complex heating process – "at first they are fried or boiled, and then stewed or baked.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: This is the most distinctive feature of Ukrainian cuisine".
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The national dish of Ukraine is red borscht, a well-known beet soup, of which many varieties exist.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: However, varenyky (boiled dumplings similar to pierogi) and a type of cabbage roll known as holubtsi are also national favourites, and are a common meal in traditional Ukrainian restaurants.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: These dishes indicate the regional similarities within Eastern European cuisine.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The cuisine emphasizes the importance of wheat in particular, and grain in general, as the country is often referred to as the "breadbasket of Europe".
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The majority of Ukrainian dishes descend from ancient peasant dishes based on plentiful grain resources such as rye, as well as staple vegetables such as potato, cabbages, mushrooms and beetroots.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Ukrainian dishes incorporate both traditional Slavic techniques as well as other European techniques, a byproduct of years of foreign jurisdiction and influence.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: As there has been a significant Ukrainian diaspora over several centuries (for example, over a million Canadians have Ukrainian heritage), the cuisine is represented in European countries and those further afield, particularly Argentina, Brazil, and the United States.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: During the period of Soviet rule Ukrainian cuisine experienced significant standardization, and the lack of consumer products led to the loss of many authentic recipes or their replacement with simplified variants.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The Soviet period also saw the introduction of numerous dishes from other cuisines into the ration of Ukrainians.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Following Ukraine's independence, the development of market economy led to the emergence of new trends in the country's cooking practices, such as the spread of industrially-produced convenience foods and development of the restaurant scene.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The main food of the inhabitants of Kyivan Rus' was bread, most commonly made from rye.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The Ukrainian word for rye (жито) itself derives from the Slavic verb "to live", which demonstrates the importance of that culture for the historical population of Ukraine.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Wheat bread during that era was predominantly consumed by the upper classes.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Both leavened and unleavened bread was known in Rus', with the former produced with the addition of hops.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Cereal dishes such as kasha, usually made from millet, were common among all groups of the population, and also played a ritual role (koliva).
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Buckwheat, as well as flax, hemp, melons, watermelons, beets, poppies, oats and peas were also cultivated in Rus' territories.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Another important part of the popular diet during the Rus' period consisted of vegetables, especially cabbage and turnips.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: A significant portion of harvested vegetables would be salted or pickled to extend their storage period.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Other vegetables widespread in Rus' territories were carrots, dill, garlic and lentils.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In the 13th century onions were introduced in the territory of modern Ukraine.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Wild plants such as sorrel, goosefoot and berries such as raspberry, blackthorn, guelder-rose, brambles, and grapes, as well as mushrooms, were also widely consumed by the population.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Grapes were also cultivated for the production of raisins and as a condiment, but they were only available to the upper classes.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: To provide themselves with meat products, medieval Eastern Slavs engaged in animal husbandry and hunting.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The latter activity was popular among both the noble elite and common people.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Rus' people consumed the meat of various mammals and birds such as deer, elk, auroch, roe deer, bison, boar, hare, partridge, grouse, goose, pigeon, swan and crane.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Swan meat was considered a delicacy and is mentioned in bylinas stemming from that time.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Meat was usually boiled or roasted on an open fire, but with time, frying and braising in fat also became widespread.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Different varieties of fish, including pike, carp, sander and common bream constituted another crucial element of the diet in Rus' times.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: To prolong their shelf life, fish products would normally be salted, smoked or dried.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Milk products consumed in medieval Rus' included cheese and butter.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Milk was also used in some pagan rituals.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Colostrum was a popular treat among the population, despite a ban on its consumption introduced by the church.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: A popular speciality widespread in modern-day Ukraine during the Medieval era was kissel, which was first mentioned in the Laurentian codex under the year 997 as a drink consumed by inhabitants of Belgorod near Kyiv.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Among other drinks present in Rus' chronicles are kvass and honey.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In that period cereals and bread continued to form the base of the diet for most people in Ukraine, but legumes including peas and beans were also widely consumed, particularly in western regions such as Galicia.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Another early mention of borshch in Ukrainian lands comes from Orthodox polemicist Ivan Vyshenskyi from Galicia, who described the dish as a typical peasant food.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In the 18th century, after the incorporation of parts of Ukraine into the Russian Empire, borshch became popular at the imperial court in Saint Petersburg.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: It was also mentioned in Ivan Kotliarevsky's Eneida, the pioneering work of modern Ukrainian literature, on par with halushky, another popular traditional Ukrainian dish.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Ukrainian cuisine was also strongly influenced by Cossack traditions, especially after the establishment of the Cossack Hetmanate in 1648, when Cossack starshyna replaced the old nobility as the new elite in a significant part of Ukrainian lands.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Typical food consumed by Zaporozhian Cossacks consisted of milled grains and flour and included traditional Ukrainian dishes such as kasha, kulish, teteria and solomakha.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: During the Cossack era beef and game in Ukraine were consumed mostly by the upper classes; the most commonly eaten meat among the lower classes was mutton.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: According to a contemporary observation, due to the abundance of fasting days in the Orthodox Christian calendar, the consumption of meat in early 18th-century Ukraine was possible only during one-fourth of days per year.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: As a result, for most of the time meat products would be replaced with fish, which played an especially important role in the diet of Ukrainian Cossacks and other social groups.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In Ivan Kotliarevsky's Eneida, sturgeon, herring and roach are mentioned among the fish consumed by the poem's heroes, who were inspired by Zaporozhian Cossacks.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Ukrainian ethnographer Mykola Markevych also mentioned dishes like borshch with fish, loaches with horseradish, and cutlets made of pike or crucian carp, which were all popular among Ukrainian Cossacks.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The social elite of the Hetmanate also ate imported fish and seafood such as Dutch herring, eels, flounders, lampreys, salmon, and cuttlefish.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Other local fish species popular during that time included carp, catfish, common bream, and sander.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Much of the fish consumed by Cossacks in Ukrainian lands was salted or dried.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The fish trade between Ukraine and the Black Sea region during the Cossack era was controlled by chumaks, but much of the catch came from local rivers, such as the Dnieper and Desna, and ponds.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Dewberry, fried berries and honey, as well as drinks such as juice, tea, coffee, wine, horilka and prune brandy were mentioned by Zaporozhian Cossack colonel Yakiv Markovych in the early 18th century.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Consumption of coffee was a traditional attribute of Ukrainian Cossack starshina.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In the 17th century fruit confiture was a favourite treat of the monks of Kyiv Pechersk Lavra, accompanied with coffee.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Sweets were traditionally made from locally grown fruit such as quince, apples and apricots.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Common dishes included different types of gruel (solomakha, lemishka, kulish, zubtsi, putria, teteria), halushky, varenyky, flour porridge and noodles.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The most important vegetables in the diet of a commoner were beets and onions.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Beef and mutton were the most popular meats, followed by pork.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: A universal product valued for its long storage time was salo (salted lard).
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Hemp oil was also commonly used in preparation of food.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Potatoes first appeared in Dnieper Ukraine in the mid-18th century.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In Kyiv alone more than 600 tons of potatoes were harvested on suburban land plots in 1845, but this was still not enough, so the city had to import one cart of potatoes per one inhabitant every year on average.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Potato cultivation was most popular in less fertile regions of Northern Ukraine.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In the mid-19th century a rich peasant from Chyhyryn area would consume 150 kg of potatoes per year, which superseded the average annual per capita consumption of this product in modern Ukraine.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Initially potatoes would be cooked by boiling or baked; potato bread also became a popular product.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In his 1860 book ethnographer Mykola Markevych mentioned several traditional dishes including potatoes, which were popular in Left-bank Ukraine, such as fried potatoes with lard, boiled potatoes and mashed potatoes with poppy seeds.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In the first half of the 19th century Ukrainians started adding potatoes to soups and ukha.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In 1853 the addition of potatoes to borshch was first mentioned in the area of Khorol near Poltava.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: By the early 20th century varenyky filled with potatoes had become a usual dish in the region of Lubny.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Other parts of Ukrainian ethnic territory also introduced the new culture in their territories.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In the 1780s potatoes appeared in the region of Sumy, and by the early 1830s had become a staple food in Sloboda Ukraine, getting mentioned in a story by Ukrainian writer Hryhorii Kvitka-Osnovianenko.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Around the same time period potato cultivation became widespread in Transcarpathia.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In late-19th century Galicia potatoes were even more popular than in Dnieper Ukraine: in 1888 an average local would consume 310 kg of tubers.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Memoirs of Ukrainian publicist Mykhailo Drahomanov mention some common Galician dishes of that time, which included potato soup and kartoplianyky (potato cutlets); the latter could also be consumed with jam as a dessert.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In Southern Ukraine potatoes were less popular, as the region's natural environment allowed for more extensive grain cultivation.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Among the local population only urban inhabitants and German colonists were known for growing the culture.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Potatoes also became an important source for alcohol production in Ukraine.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Another new product introduced in Ukrainian lands during the 17-18th centuries was rice.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Initially imported from territories under Ottoman control, in Ukrainian lands that culture was known at that time as "Saracen millet" (Ukrainian: Сарацинське/сорочинське пшоно).
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Due to its high price, until the mid-19th century rice would be available only to richer strata of the Ukrainian society.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In 1768 Zaporozhian Cossack otaman Petro Kalnyshevsky mentioned rice in the list of products stolen from his residence.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Recipes with rice widespread during that era included other expensive foods and spices such as almond, saffron, cane sugar, raisins and prunes.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Rice served as an ingredient of soups and sweets, as well as a filling for poultry dishes.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: On Christmas richer families would also use rice for their kutia instead of the more traditional wheat grains.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In Ukraine rice remained a luxury product until the Soviet era, when mass cultivation of the cereal started in southern parts of the country (Kherson, Odesa and Crimea).
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Among other important cultures which became widespread in Ukraine during the 18th and 19th centuries, enriching its cuisine, were cucumbers and aubergines.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The tradition of pickling cucumbers is attributed to Greek merchants, who were provided freedom of taxation and self-government by Ukrainian hetman Bohdan Khmelnytsky.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The most notable centre of cucumber production in Ukraine has been Nizhyn.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: After 1787 Nizhyn cucumbers were supplied to the court of Empress Catherine II of Russia, and by 1897 they were exported to 56 countries around the world.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Unlike cucumbers, aubergines enjoyed only a limited degree of popularity, being consumed, among others, in the region of Kharkiv, but remaining practically unknown in Galicia.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In the 19th century Ukrainian lands saw the introduction of sunflowers and maize, which form an important part of the popular diet in the country nowadays.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Maize cultivation spread to Ukraine from modern-day Moldova and Romania and became most popular in the western region, especially in the Carpathians.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Maize porridges such as banosh, kulesha and mamaliga are still characteristic for the cuisine of southwestern Ukraine.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Other common cultures which appeared in Ukrainian lands in the 19th and early 20th centuries were tomatoes and bell peppers.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The recipe of borshch with tomato paste, which is nowadays standard for many Ukrainian households, became common only in the early 20th century: previously the dish had traditionally been made with fermented beets.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In the late 19th century Ukraine became a centre of industrial production of sunflower oil, which swiftly replaced traditional plant oils, including olive oil, which historically had been imported from Greece.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Due to the growth of sugar industry, connected with the names of such families as Tereshchenko, Symyrenko, Yakhnenko, Branicki, Brodsky and Bobrinsky, during the 19th century Ukraine became one of the major centres of sugar beet production.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Another historical Ukrainian speciality, which enjoyed popularity in the 18-19th centuries were plums, which would be dried or pickled in honey.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The most well-known centre of plum production in Ukraine was Opishnia, and local produce would be supplied to the tsar's court and exported abroad.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In parallel to the emergence of Ukrainian ethnic cuisine, the 19th century became a period of development for urban gastronomy.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Famine and lack of products in major cities of the Soviet Union led to the introduction of ration stamps, which were rated according to social class, with preference being given to workers.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: As a result of that system, taste lost its importance as a major criterion, and most people had to suffice themselves with their rations or with goods they cold find in stores.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: With the exception of NEP era, such attitude to food became standard for the Soviet society.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: During the Holodomor famine of 1932–1933, mass confiscation of food by Soviet authorities forced many Ukrainians to consume ersatz foods in order to survive.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Many starving peasants would be forced to eat snowdrop bulbs and acorns.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Even dead animals would be consumed during that time, and in many villages cats, dogs, hedgehogs and even storks and cranes would be hunted down by locals and consumed for their meat.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The break of taboos as a result of the famine eventually led to cases of cannibalism.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Deficit of goods led to the introduction of new products into the popular diet: for example, during the 1930s the government promoted the consumption of soy and rabbit meat, meanwhile during the 1960s citizens were encouraged to eat maize dishes.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: At the same time, starting from the early 1930s Soviet food industry was revolutionized by the introduction of Western technology, which allowed to start mass production of canned products, mayonnaise, sausages, juices, condensed milk, ice cream and other goods.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Bread, which until that time had been usually baked at home, was for the first time produced industrially.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: General standardization of norms led to the introduction of numerous local recipes into the canon of Soviet cuisine, among them Georgian kharcho, Caucasian shashlik and Central Asian plov.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Ukrainian cuisine also played a big role in this process.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: During the Soviet period some dishes, for example mlyntsi, were adopted as standard festive foods during holidays such as Masnytsia.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In order to introduce new culinary approaches, in 1939 Soviet authorities published The Book of Tasty and Healthy Food, which served as an advertisement of new Soviet food industry production and included standardized recipes.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The book's publication is considered to have contributed to the rise in popularity of salads, which hadn't been widespread during the Russian imperial era.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Salad recipes contained in the first edition included products such as lettuce, romaine and nuts; but as those were unavailable for an average citizen, the second edition from 1952 only included recipes using tomatoes, cucumbers, cabbage and radish.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: One of the causes for the popularity of salads was the deficit of meat, which became especially acute starting from the 1930s.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: A popular condiment for salads was mayonnaise, which could also be used to make deviled eggs.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Other ingredients for salads which were popular in the Soviet Union included green peas, canned crabs and vinegar.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: A popular recipe was "winter salad" made from potatoes and carrots, as those products were usually available in Soviet shops.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: An important part of daily food practices in the Soviet Union was soup.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Broth could be prepared from various products, including canned fish and bone, which made that dish available for all Soviet citizens.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Confectionery products in the Soviet Union were generally made of wheat flour.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Many festive foods were cooked according to own family recipes, but others could derive from standard cookbooks.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Popular dishes which spread during the Soviet era and became symbols of Ukrainian cuisine are Chicken Kiev and Kyiv cake.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: An important part of Soviet food culture was represented by catering.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Most Soviet restaurants cooked food according to the same recipes as those contained in The Book of Tasty and Healthy Food.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Local culinary traditions were almost totally ignored, and the same list of standard dishes, such as borshch, chicken Kiev and salad with mayonnaise, could be ordered at restaurants in Kyiv, Leningrad or Tallinn.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: This could be seen as a continuation of pre-revolutionary trends, in which restaurant cooks in the Russian Empire tended to simplify foreign recipes.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The new type of popular cuisine, which appeared during the Soviet era, emulated well-known European dishes, but used totally different ingredients.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: One example of this trend is the Olivier salad, whose recipe was made more available to the general population by replacing crayfish with carrots, and capers with pickled cucumbers.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: A peculiar practice in Soviet cuisine was the change of original names of many dishes, as a result of which consommé became known as broth, chicken Kiev - as "butter-filled chicken cutlet", meanwhile Soviet sparkling wines became known as "champagne".
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Starting with the introduction of New Economic Policy during the 1920s, Soviet authorities started organizing a network of canteens, which were seen as an instrument of social emancipation of women.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Residential buildings designed during that time had the size of their kitchens reduced, as it was believed that home cooking would soon become a thing of the past.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Canteens were the most economical places to have a meal, but the quality of food, service and general atmosphere in such establishments were in many cases substandard.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Following the Second World War, numerous restaurants, buffets and cafés started appearing in major Soviet cities, including Kyiv; meanwhile canteens were viewed by the broader public with skepticism, and frequently served as gathering places for alcoholics.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: By the 1960s the image of woman as hostess in popular Soviet culture had been rehabilitated, and eating at home was once again seen as a normal practice.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: During the 1970s, most Soviet restaurants were located in hotels.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Some establishments attempted to diversify their menus by including dishes like schnitzel, but in general the view of food as a source of pleasure was seen as a relic of bourgeois past.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Most people visited restaurants only in special cases, for example during birthdays or weddings.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: According to restaurateur Yevhen Klopotenko, the Soviet era played a decisive role in the history of Ukrainian cuisine, and as many as 90% of all dishes considered Ukrainian today in fact have Soviet origins.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The Soviet regime attempted to create one Soviet nation under its rule, and saw unification of cultural traditions, including cuisine, as one of the ways to achieve that goal.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Modern Ukrainian food culture has preserved numerous elements of its Soviet past.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Many canteens from that era still operate, offering a standard variety of typical Soviet foods, such as boiled potatoes, vinegret and "Stolichny" salad, and some even continue following the Soviet-introduced tradition of "fish days".
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Mayonnaise, olivier salad and deviled eggs remain popular festive foods in many Ukrainian families.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Other dishes of Soviet origin which are still widespread in modern Ukraine are shuba and mimosa salad.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Even some dishes which have their origins in traditional Ukrainian cuisine, for example kholodets, deruny and cutlets had their recipes changed under the Soviet rule.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: This process saw the fusion between traditional Ukrainian recipes and modern American cooking.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Many Ukrainian immigrant families adopted local practices of the new country, organizing picnics and baking turkey for Thanksgiving Day.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Nevertheless, typical Ukrainian dishes such as borshch, varenyky, holubtsi and pierogi retained their role as signature dishes among emgrants.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: A popular Ukrainian brand of instant noodles is Mivina, established by Vietnamese businessman Phạm Nhật Vượng in 1995 in Kharkiv.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The product became so popular, that eventually all instant noodles in Ukraine started to be known under the brand name.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In 2010 the company which had been producing the noodles was bought by Nestlé, which also acquired several other popular Ukrianian food producers such as Svitoch and Torchyn.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Due to the Russian invasion of Ukraine, the production facilities of Mivina had to be moved to Volyn Oblast.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The noodles also appeared in Germany, France and Great Britain under the brand Maggi.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Ukrainian cuisine of modern times has witnessed the decline of some traditions, but many authentic elements have been preserved.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The use of convenience and ready-made foods has led to a decrease in the number of people who cook at home, however some traditional foods such as korovai are still common during wedding celebrations and other festive occasions.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In contrast to Soviet times, kitchen has once again become an important part of private living space for Ukrainian families.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Emergence of new cooking technologies such as microwave oven, electric ovens and multicookers led to a rising popularity of simple recipes.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: At the same time, traditional ovens have disappeared in private houses, but reemerged in restaurants specializing in authentic Ukrainian cuisine.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The fall of the Soviet Union with its lack of competition and directive approach to cooking brought more freedom for cooks, who now have a chance to realize themselves in private business.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The development of Ukrainian gastronomy has led to the reemergence of seasonal products, and the taste and outward appearance of food have once again become an important factor.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Another trend in modern Ukrainian cuisine is the increase of consumption of meat products, which have become more available to the general population.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: At the same time, an opposite process can also be observed with an increase in the number of vegetarians.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In the modern world Ukrainian cuisine is associated with a limited number of recipes, such as chicken Kiev or borshch.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Numerous restaurants of Ukrainian cuisine are active among the diaspora in countries such as the United States and Canada.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Nevertheless, in many parts of the world Ukrainian cuisine is seen through a lens of Russian-imposed narratives, and is represented in a simplified manner with the inclusion of post-Soviet dishes from other nations formerly part of the USSR.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Chervonyi borshch (red borscht; usually simply called borshch) is a vegetable soup made out of beets, cabbage, potatoes, tomatoes, carrots, onions, garlic, and dill.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: There are about 30 varieties of Ukrainian borscht.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Although the modern variety is usually soured with tomatoes or tomato-derived products (such as tomato paste), traditionally beet kvas was used instead.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Zelenyi borshch (green borscht) or shchavlevyi borshch (sorrel soup): water- or broth-based soup with sorrel and various vegetables, served with chopped hard-boiled egg and sour cream.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Kholodnyi borshch (cold borscht) or kholodnyk: vegetable and beet soup blended with sour dairy (sour cream, soured milk, kefir, or yogurt), served cold with a hard-boiled egg.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Bilyi borshch (white borscht): refers to different soups depending on the region.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In southern Podolia, white borscht is cooked with fresh sugar beets, beans, and vegetables.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In the Hutsul region, it is cooked with fermented white beets and their liquid (kvas), onions, carrots, sour cream, and Carpathian oregano.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: In Polesia, it includes sugar beets, beet kvas, cabbage, mushrooms, potatoes and fresh herbs.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: White borscht may also refer to a żur-like soup from neighboring Poland.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Hrechanyi sup: soup made with buckwheat, vegetables, and sometimes meat.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Kapusniak: soup made with cabbage, pork, salo, beans, and served with smetana (sour cream).
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Solianka: thick, spicy and sour soup made with meat, fish or mushrooms and various vegetables and pickles.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Yushka: clear soup; the most common variety — rybna yushka (fish yushka) is made from various types of fish such as carp, bream, wels catfish, or even ruffe.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Another common variety is hrybna yushka (clear mushroom soup).
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Kovbasa: various kinds of smoked or boiled pork, beef or chicken sausage.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: One specific variety is krovianka, the blood sausage.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Usually served sliced, with pieces of bread, onion, and horseradish or hot mustard sauce.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: It may also be fried (shkvarky) or boiled.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Kaviar or ikra: caviar, served on top of buttered slices of bread.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Kholodets: aspic (studenets) made with meat or fish (zalyvna ryba).
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Olivier: salad made out of cooked and chopped potatoes, dill pickles, boiled chopped eggs, cooked and chopped chicken or ham, chopped onions, peas, mixed with mayonnaise.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The country has been considered one of the traditional "breadbaskets" of the world.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: French author Honoré de Balzac claimed to have counted 77 local varieties of bread during his visit to Ukraine in 1848.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Decorations on the top of Ukrainian bread loaves can be elaborate for celebrations.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Babka: Easter bread, usually a sweet dough with raisins and other dried fruit.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: It is usually baked in a tall, cylindrical form.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Bublyk: ring-shaped bread roll made from dough that has been boiled before baking.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: It is similar to bagel, but usually somewhat bigger and with a wider hole.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Kolach: ring-shaped bread typically served at Christmas and funerals.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The dough is braided, often with three strands representing the Holy Trinity.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The braid is then shaped into a circle (circle = kolo in Ukrainian) representing the circle of life and family.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Korovai: a round, braided bread, similar to the kolach.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: It is most often baked for weddings and its top decorated with birds and periwinkle.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Palianytsia: regular baked bread (famously difficult to pronounce for non-Ukrainian speakers).
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Savory pampushky: soft, fluffy bread portions, or deep-fried pieces of dough, topped with garlic butter.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Chicken Kyiv (kotleta po-kyivsky): Kyiv-style chicken cutlet filled with butter and fresh herbs.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Deruny: potato pancakes, usually served with sour cream.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Fish (ryba): fried in egg and flour; cooked in oven with mushrooms, cheese, and lemon; pudding "Baba-sharpanyna"; pickled, dried or smoked variety.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Huliash: refers to stew in general, or specifically Zakarpattian variety of Hungarian goulash.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Kasha: porridge, usually made out of buckwheat, wheat, barley, rye, millet, rice, oat, or corn.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: One specific variety is kasha hrechana zi shkvarkamy (buckwheat cereal with fried pork rinds and onion).
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Kartoplianyky: fried balls of potato mash with flour and eggs; may have a filling.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Kotlety or sichenyky (cutlets, meatballs): minced meat or fish mixed with onions, raw eggs, breadcrumbs or bread, and sometimes garlic and milk, fried in oil and sometimes rolled in breadcrumbs.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Kruchenyky or zavyvantsi: pork or beef rolls with various stuffing: mushrooms, onions, eggs, cheese, prunes, sauerkraut, carrots, etc.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Mlyntsi: thin pancakes, similar to French crêpes, Russian bliny, or Ashkenazi Jewish blintz.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Stuffed mlyntsi are called nalysnyky, and they are usually filled with quark, meat, cabbage, or fruits, and served with sour cream.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Potato (kartoplia, also dialectally barabolia, bulba, krumplia, mandeburka): young or peeled, served with butter, sour cream, dill; a more exclusive variety includes raw egg.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Pyrizhky: baked buns stuffed with different fillings, such as ground meat, liver, eggs, rice, onions, fried cabbage or sauerkraut, quark, cherries etc.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Roast meat (pechenia): pork, veal, beef or lamb roast.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Varenyky: dumplings made with fillings such as mashed potatoes and fried onions, boiled ground meat and fried onions, liver and fried onions, fried cabbage with fried onions, quark, cherries, and strawberries.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Served with sour cream and butter or sugar, when filled with fruits.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Kyiv cake: creamy dessert consisting of two layers of meringue with hazelnuts and a buttercream filling.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Molozyvo: dish made by baking a beestings and egg mixture.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Sweet pampushky: sweet dough similar to doughnut holes.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Traditionally filled with rose preserve, but can also be filled with poppy seed or other sweet fillings.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Ptashyne moloko (literally: bird's milk): milk soufflé with chocolate coating.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Syrnyky: fried quark fritters, sometimes with raisins, served with sour cream, jam (varennia), honey or apple sauce.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Tort: many varieties of layered cakes, from moist to puffy, most typical ones being Kyivskyi, Prazhskyi, and Trufelnyi.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: They are frequently made without flour, instead using ground walnuts or almonds.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Varennia: a whole fruit preserve made by cooking berries and other fruits in sugar syrup.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Verhuny: crispy deep-fried pastry, similar to angel wings.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Zhele (plural and singular): jellied fruits, like cherries, pears, etc.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Zefir: soft dessert made from fruit or berry puree, sugar and egg whites.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: One of the most exotic is flavoured with honey and red pepper.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Beer (pyvo): the largest producers of beer are Obolon, Lvivske, Chernihivske, Slavutych, Sarmat, and Rogan, which partly export their products.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Wine (vyno): from Europe and Ukraine (particularly from Crimea), mostly sweet.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Mead (med or medukha): a fermented alcoholic beverage made from honey, water, and yeast.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Its flavour depends on the plants frequented by the honeybees, the length of time and method of aging, and the specific strain of yeast used.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Its alcohol content will vary from maker to maker depending on the method of production.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Nalyvka: a homemade wine made from cherries, raspberries, gooseberries, bilberries, blackberries, plums, blackthorns or other berries or fruits.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Berries were put into a sulija (a big glass bottle), some sugar was added.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: After the berries fermented, the liquid was separated from the berries, and put into corked bottles.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The berries were used to make pyrizhky (baked or fried pastry).
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Kompot: a sweet beverage made of dried or fresh fruits or berries boiled in water.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Uzvar: a specific type of kompot made of dried fruit, usually apples, pears, and/or prunes.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Kysil: a kompot that is thickened with potato starch.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Kvas: a sweet-and-sour sparkling beverage brewed from yeast, sugar, and dried rye bread.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Kefir: milk fermented by both yeast and lactobacillus bacteria, that has a similar taste to yogurt.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Homemade kefir may contain a slight amount of alcohol.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Pryazhene moloko: baked milk, a milk product that has a creamy colour and a light caramel flavour.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: It is made by simmering milk on low heat for at least eight hours.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Galician cuisine formed on the base of numerous subethnic groups inhabiting the region, such as Hutsuls, Boykos and Lemkos.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Yuriy Vynnychuk considers rural and urban Galician cuisines to be separate phenomena.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Taras Voznyak classifies Galician cuisine as part of a broader Central European cuisine.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: A distinct feature of Galician culinary traditions is the presence of significant Austrian, Italian and Hungarian influences as a result of the territory spending part of its history under Austrian rule.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Galician cuisine also influenced the traditions of Polish cuisine.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: An important source on the history of Galician cuisine is Practical Kitchen by Olha Franko.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Among typical Galician dishes are zhur, knishes, machanka (a thick meat soup of Lemko origin), cabbage, white borshch, banosh, Yavoriv pie, holubtsi, spelt bread, knedle and maslianka.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: A popular dish of Ternopil Oblast is millet kulesha with cracklings.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Popular desserts of Galicia include strudel and cheesecake.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Among its signature dishes are pierogi with potatoes, cheese and onions; veal broth with beans and noodles; chicken broth with farina; kluski made of farina, eggs and greens; yabchanka with cloves; various fruit and berry soups; nut cake.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Typical breakfast foods consumed in Lviv are milk coffee, bread and butter, cottage cheese with sour cream.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: An important part in the diet of Leopolitans has traditionally been played by buckwheat kasha and maize porridge (mamaliga).
title: Ukrainian cuisine

title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: A traditional Lviv dessert is legumina, washed down with kompot.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: During the summer period, Leopolitans would also consume many vegetables.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: A traditional variety of Lviv kutia is prepared with boiled wheat grains, raisins, poppy seed, honey and lemon juice.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Historically, Lviv has also been an important centre of beer and wine production.

text: Lviv's culinary traditions were seriously damaged during the Soviet period, as the new regime declared the city's restaurant and coffeehouse culture to be "bourgeois".
title: Ukrainian cuisine

title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The pre-war tradition of wine consumption was replaced through the introduction of vodka, sweet fortified wines and brandy.

text: The most important ingredients of Hutsul cuisine are maize, potatoes, beans, mushrooms and bryndza.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Traditional Hutsul dishes include banosh (maize flour porridge), huslianka (sour milk), vurda (sheep milk cheese), budz, pancakes and shupenia (a dish made from beans).

text: A traditional Transcarpathian dish stemming from Hungarian cuisine is bogrács - gulyas meat cooked on an open fire with addition of paprika and various herbs.

title: Ukrainian cuisine

title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The region of Volhynia is known for dishes such as mazuryky - turkey sausages filled with butter and cheese.

text: An important part in Polesian cuisine is played by products of fishing and gathering, such as berries, mushrooms and grasses.
title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: The most widespread type of flour in the region is made of rye.

text: Typical Polesian pastries include pies, soft pancakes and various breads, some of which play a role in religious rituals.

text: A traditional local dish is buckwheat varenyky made from rye with addition of sour milk.

title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: Traditional Polesian festive specialities include borshch, kysil, pancakes with sour cream, cottage cheese with milk, as well as kvasovka - millet porridge with dried fruit.

text: Some other typical specialities of the region are kalatusha - mushroom soup served with flatbreads of pancakes; holubtsi with potatoes and mushrooms; potato babka (makar).

title: Ukrainian cuisine

text: A traditional dish of the territory of Kholmshchyna is volk or voylok, cooked from young leaves of beet, goosefoot or nettle.

text: A widespread product of Polesian cuisine are blueberries, traditionally consumed with milk, bread and sour cream.

text: In Chernihiv Polesia a popular traditional dish are fried or smothered cucumbers with carrots, onions and meat, known as hurky.

text: In the Old Russian period, the main food groups were bread, grains, and other foods that contained starch.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Women baked pies with many different fillings, such as mushrooms or berries.
