text: Kasha, such as buckwheat and oats, were represented as wellbeing to the household.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Many Russians used honey and berries and made them into gingerbread, which is still a popular Russian dessert.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Many current Russian dishes were inspired from Asian cultures, such as pelmeni.
title: Russian cuisine

text: In the 17th century, cuisine was separated based on economic class.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The rich had meat and delicacies, such as caviar, while the poor had the most simple dishes.
title: Russian cuisine

text: During this century, more food appeared, because new countries were annexed.
title: Russian cuisine

text: During the Peter and Catherine the Great era, minced meat was incorporated into dishes and other European countries' cuisine was also mixed into Russian foods.
title: Russian cuisine

text: In the last era (Petersburg cuisine), many French, German, Dutch, and Italian meals were incorporated into Russian foods, such as lamb and pork.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The French popularized potatoes and tomatoes in dishes.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Due to the long-lasting cold weather in Russia, many dishes were made to be preserved, so they would not have to take extra trips in the freezing snowy days.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Its foundations were laid by the peasant food of the rural population in an often harsh climate, with a combination of plentiful fish, pork, poultry, caviar, mushrooms, berries, and honey.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Crops of rye, wheat, barley and millet provided the ingredients for a plethora of breads, pancakes, pies, cereals, beer and vodka.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Soups and stews are centered on seasonal or storable produce, fish and meats.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Such food remained the staple for the vast majority of Russians well into the 20th century.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The 16th through 18th centuries brought more refined culinary techniques.
title: Russian cuisine

text: It was during this time period that smoked meats and fish, pastry cooking, salads, and green vegetables, chocolate, ice cream, wines, and juice were imported from abroad.
title: Russian cuisine

text: At least for the urban aristocracy and provincial gentry, this opened the doors for the creative integration of these new foodstuffs with traditional Russian dishes.
title: Russian cuisine

text: In the early 20th century, the Revolution saw a rapid decline of elite cuisine, driven both by the new egalitarian state ideology and by disappearance of the old Imperial elites who used to be its consumers.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The distinct Soviet cuisine was born, emphasizing fusion of the Union's national cuisines, scientific approach to a diet, and industrial approach to food preparation and serving.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The fall of the Soviet Union saw the end of state monopoly on food service, and a corresponding diversification of cuisine.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Despite such deep mutual influence, many national cuisines within the borders of the Russian Federation maintain their uniqueness, such as Bashkir cuisine, Tatar cuisine, Sakha cuisine, or Yamal cuisine.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The Russian cuisine itself is also geographically diverse, its variations dependent on raw materials and cooking methods available locally.
title: Russian cuisine

text: In the north of Russia, it incorporates local berries such as cloudberry or crowberry, fish such as cod, game meat such as elk, or even edible moss known as yagel.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Conversely, in Siberia it includes the local fish varieties, particularly those of the coregonus genus such as arctic cisco or muksun, and borrows the local cooking methods, to result in raw fish eaten frozen or combined with spices.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Russian soups can be divided into at least seven large groups: Chilled soups based on kvass, such as tyurya, okroshka, and botvinya.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Light soups and stews based on water and vegetables, such as svekolnik.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Thick soups based on meat broth, with a salty-sour base like rassolnik and solyanka.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The main ingredients are two types of vegetables that can be mixed with cold boiled meat or fish in a 1:1 proportion.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Thus vegetable, meat, poultry, and fish varieties of okroshka are made.
title: Russian cuisine

text: There are typically two types of vegetables in okroshka.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The first must have a neutral taste, such as boiled potatoes, turnips, rutabagas, carrots, or fresh cucumbers.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The second must be spicy and aromatic, like radishes or green onion as well as other herbs—greens of dill, parsley, chervil, celery, or tarragon.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Different meat and poultry can be used in the same soup.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The most common ingredient is beef alone or with poultry.
title: Russian cuisine

text: If it is made with fish, the best choice would be tench, European perch, pike-perch, cod, or other neutral-tasting fish.
title: Russian cuisine

text: In the coastal areas smoked and/or salted salmon is preferred instead, often in combination with other meats.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The kvass most commonly used in cooking is white okroshka kvass, which is much more sour than drinking kvass.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Spices used include mustard, black pepper and pickled cucumber (specifically, the liquid from the pickles), solely or in combination.
title: Russian cuisine

text: For the final touch, boiled eggs and smetana (similar to crème fraîche) are added.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Often, the mustard, chopped hard-boiled yolks, pepper and pickle brine are combined into a spicy sauce that is added to the soup to taste.
title: Russian cuisine

text: For sour milk-based okroshka, well-shaken natural sour milk (often with the addition of seed oil) is used with the addition of pure water and ground garlic.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Sometimes manufactured kefir is used instead of natural sour milk for time-saving reasons, though some say it detracts from the original taste of okroshka.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Tyurya is very similar to okroshka, the main difference being that instead of vegetables, bread, sometimes with addition of onion and vegetable oil, is soaked in kvass, similar to Silesian wodzionka or Portuguese açorda.
title: Russian cuisine

text: It was commonly consumed during rough times (such as the Russian Revolution, World War I, and World War II) and by poor peasants.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Also, due to its simplicity, it was very common as a meal during religious fasting.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Mustard, garlic, and horseradish are then added for flavor.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The vegetables are blanched, then rubbed through a sieve, and kvass is poured over them.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Svyokolnik (also known as kholodnik) is a cold borscht.
title: Russian cuisine

text: It consists of beet sour or beet juice blended with sour cream, buttermilk, soured milk, kefir or yogurt.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The mixture has a distinctive orange or pink color.
title: Russian cuisine

text: It is served chilled, typically over finely chopped beetroot, cucumbers, radishes and spring onion, together with halved hard-boiled eggs and sprinkled with fresh dill.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Shchi knew no social class boundaries, and even if the rich had richer ingredients and the poor made it solely of cabbage and onions, all these "poor" and "rich" variations were cooked in the same tradition.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The unique taste of this cabbage soup was from the fact that after cooking it was left to draw (stew) in a Russian stove.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The "spirit of shchi" was inseparable from a Russian izba (log hut).
title: Russian cuisine

text: Many Russian proverbs are connected to this soup, such as Shchi da kasha — pishcha nasha (Russian: Щи да каша — пища наша, "Shchi and porridge are our staples").
title: Russian cuisine

text: It can be eaten regularly, and at any time of the year.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The richer variant of shchi includes several ingredients, but the first and last components are necessary: Cabbage.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Spicy herbs (onions, celery, dill, garlic, pepper, bay leaf).
title: Russian cuisine

text: When this soup is served, smetana is added.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Borscht is made of broth, beets, and tomatoes with various vegetables, including onions, cabbage, tomato, carrots, and celery.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Borscht usually includes meat, particularly beef in Russia, and pork in Ukraine.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Borscht is generally served very hot, with sour cream, chopped chives or parsley, and crushed garlic.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Borscht is associated as national cuisine in various different Eastern European countries such as Ukraine, Poland, Belarus, and Lithuania.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Ukha is a warm watery fish dish; however, calling it a fish soup would not be absolutely correct. "Ukha" as a name for fish broth was established only in the late 17th to early 18th centuries.
title: Russian cuisine

text: In earlier times this name was first given to thick meat broths, and then later chicken.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Beginning from the 15th century, fish was more and more often used to prepare ukha, thus creating a dish that had a distinctive taste among soups.
title: Russian cuisine

text: A minimum of vegetables is added in preparation, and in classical cooking, ukha was simply a rich fish broth served to accompany fish pies (rasstegai, kuliebiaka, etc.).
title: Russian cuisine

text: These days it is more often a fish soup, cooked with potatoes, and other vegetables.
title: Russian cuisine

text: A wide variety of freshwater fish is traditionally used.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Rassolnik is a hot soup in a salty-sour cucumber base.
title: Russian cuisine

text: This dish formed in Russian cuisine quite late—only in the 19th century.
title: Russian cuisine

text: About this time the name rassolnik was attached to it, originating from the Russian word rassol which means "brine" (pickle water).
title: Russian cuisine

text: Pickle water was known to be used as a base for soups from the 15th century at the latest.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Its concentration and ratio with other liquids and soup components gave birth to different soups: solyanka, shchi, and of course rassolnik.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The latest is moderately sour-salty soups on pickled cucumber base.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Some are vegetarian, but more often with products like veal or beef kidneys or all poultry giblets (stomach, liver, heart, neck, feet).
title: Russian cuisine

text: For best taste, there has to be a balance between the sour part and neutral absorbers (cereals, potatoes, root vegetables).
title: Russian cuisine

text: Typical rassolnik is based on kidneys, brine (and pickles), vegetables and barley.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Kal'ya was a very common dish first served in the 16th–17th centuries.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Subsequently, it almost completely disappeared from Russian cuisine.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The cooking technique is mostly the same as of ukha, but to the broth were added pickled cucumbers, pickle water, lemons and lemon juice, either separately or all together.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The main characteristic of kal'ya is that only fat, rich fish was used; sometimes caviar was added along with the fish.
title: Russian cuisine

text: More spices are added, and the soup turns out more piquant and thicker than ukha.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Solyanka is much thicker than other soups, about 1/3 less liquid ratio.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Three types are distinguished: meat, fish, and simple solyanka.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The first two are cooked on strong meat or fish broths, and the last on mushroom or vegetable broth.
title: Russian cuisine

text: All the broths are mixed with cucumber pickle water.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Lapsha (noodle soup) was adopted by Russians from Tatars, and after some transformation became widespread in Russia.
title: Russian cuisine

text: It comes in three variations: chicken, mushroom, and milk.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Cooking all three is simple, including preparation of noodles, cooking of corresponding broth, and boiling of noodles in broth.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Noodles are based on the same wheat flour or buckwheat/wheat flour mix.
title: Russian cuisine

text: This dish is one of the main features of New Year buffets.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Sel'edka pod shuboy (or shuba, from Russian: шуба, lit. 'fur coat'), also known as "dressed herring", is chopped salted herring under a "coat" of shredded cooked beet, sometimes with a layer of egg or other vegetables.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Vinegret (from French vinaigrette) is a salad made of boiled beets, potatoes, carrots, pickles, onions, sauerkraut, and sometimes peas or white beans.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The variety of cereals is based on the local variety of crops.
title: Russian cuisine

text: In Russian, the word kasha refers to any kind of porridge.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The most popular cereals are buckwheat, millet, semolina, oats, barley, and rice.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Traditionally, such cereal porridge is cooked in milk, especially if it is to be served for breakfast.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Butter, salt, sugar, jams, fresh fruit and berries may be added.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Plain cooked porridges, especially buckwheat and rice, may be served as a side dish with other meals.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Canned meat, tushonka, is made with buckwheat/rice kasha traditions in mind, unlike the bully beef type of canned meat.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Pan juices, alone or mixed with sour cream or melted butter, were used as gravy to pour on garnishing vegetables and porridges.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Meat sauces, i.e. gravies based on flour, butter, eggs and milk, are not common for traditional Russian cuisine.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Pelmeni are a traditional Eastern European (mainly Russian) dish usually made with minced meat filling, wrapped in thin dough (made out of flour and eggs, sometimes with milk or water added).
title: Russian cuisine

text: For filling, pork, lamb, beef, or any other kind of meat can be used; mixing several kinds is popular.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The traditional Ural recipe requires the filling be made with 45% of beef, 35% of lamb, and 20% of pork.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Traditionally, various spices, such as pepper, onions, and garlic, are mixed into the filling.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Russians seem to have learned to make pelmeni from Finno-Ugric people.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The word means "ear-shaped bread" in Finno-Ugric languages such as Udmurt and Komi; pel' means 'ear' and n'an means 'bread'.
title: Russian cuisine

text: In Siberia they were made in large quantities and stored safely frozen outside for several winter months.
title: Russian cuisine

text: In mainland Russia, the term "Siberian pel'meni" refers to pel'meni made with a mix of meats (whether the 45/35/20 mix mentioned above or another ratio), rather than a single meat.
title: Russian cuisine

text: By the late 19th century, they became a staple throughout urban European Russia.
title: Russian cuisine

text: They are prepared immediately before eating by boiling in water until they float, and then 2–5 minutes more.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The resulting dish is served with butter or sour cream (mustard, horseradish, and vinegar are popular as well).
title: Russian cuisine

text: Some recipes suggest frying pelmeni after boiling until they turn golden brown.
title: Russian cuisine

text: They are akin to vareniki, a Ukrainian variety of dumplings with a filling made of, most commonly, mashed potatoes, farmer's cheese, or cherries.
title: Russian cuisine

text: They are not dissimilar to Chinese potstickers, Tibetan mo-mo and Italian ravioli, as well as the manti of the Kazakh and Kyrgyz cultures.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The process of making pelmeni is somewhat labor-intensive, but a pelmennitsa greatly speeds up the task.
title: Russian cuisine

text: This device typically consists of a round aluminum plate with a matrix of holes surrounded by ridges.
title: Russian cuisine

text: A sheet of dough is placed over the matrix, a filling is scooped into each "cell", and the dough sags under the weight of the filling, forming the body of the dumpling.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Another sheet of dough is placed on top, and a wooden roller is rolled over the top, pressing the dough layers together, cutting the dumplings apart by the ridges, and forcing the dumplings to fall through the holes.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Using a pelmennitsa, the chef can quickly manufacture batches of dumplings at a time.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Various minced meat dishes were adopted from other cuisines and became popular only in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries; for traditional Russian cuisine, they are not typical.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Kotlety (minced cutlets) are pan-fried cutlet-shaped patties, not dissimilar from Salisbury steak and other such dishes.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Kotlety are made from pork and beef, or from chicken, sometimes also from fish.
title: Russian cuisine

text: In common recipes, ground meat, pork, onions and bread are put in a bowl and mixed thoroughly until it becomes relatively consistent.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Once this effect is achieved, patties are formed and then put into a hot frying pan to cook.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Pozharsky cutlet is a more elaborated version which was adopted by French haute cuisine.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Beef Stroganoff: Sautéed pieces of beef served in a sauce with smetana (sour cream).
title: Russian cuisine

text: From its origins in mid-19th-century Russia, it has become popular around the world, with considerable variation from the original recipe.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Shashlyk is a form of shish kebab (marinated meat grilled on a skewer) popular in former Soviet Union countries, notably in Georgia, Russia, Armenia, Azerbaijan, and Uzbekistan.
title: Russian cuisine

text: It often features alternating slices of meat and onions.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Even though the word "shashlyk" was apparently borrowed from the Crimean Tatars by the Cossacks as early as the 18th century, kebabs did not reach Moscow until the late 19th century, according to Vladimir Gilyarovsky's "Moscow and Moscovites".
title: Russian cuisine

text: From then on, their popularity spread rapidly; by the 1910s they were a staple in St.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Petersburg restaurants and by the 1920s they were already a ubiquitous street food all over urban Russia.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Shashlik is also used in Russia as a food to be cooked in an outdoor environment, similarly to barbecue in English-speaking countries.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Kholodets (or studen'): Jellied chopped pieces of pork or veal meat with some spices added (pepper, parsley, garlic, bay leaf) and minor amounts of vegetables (carrots, onions).
title: Russian cuisine

text: It is served with horseradish, mustard, or ground garlic with smetana.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Gulyash is also a very popular dish in Russia.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Of course, this is the Hungarian dish Goulash originally, but it was still very popular in the USSR.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Strictly freshwater fish such as carp and sudak (Sander lucioperca, Zander) were commonly eaten in inland areas, as well as anadromous sturgeon and in northern areas salmon, pike and trout.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Pickling cabbage (sauerkraut), cucumbers, tomatoes and other vegetables in brine is used to preserve vegetables for winter use.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Pickled apples and some other fruit also used to be widely popular.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Later preparations dating to the 12th and 13th centuries included spices purchased in the markets of India and the Middle east.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Tula gingerbread is known as early as 1685, and was historically made by well-known confectioners with gingerbread molds hand carved into planks of wood taken from local birch and pear trees.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Pirozhki (singular: pirozhok; diminutive of pirog [pie]) are small stuffed buns (pies) made of either yeast dough or short pastry.
title: Russian cuisine

text: They are filled with one of many different fillings and are either baked (the ancient Slavic method) or shallow-fried (known as "priazhenie", this method was borrowed from the Tatars in the 13th century).
title: Russian cuisine

text: One feature of pirozhki that sets them apart from, for example, English pies is that the fillings used are almost invariably fully cooked.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The use of chopped hard-boiled eggs in fillings is another interesting feature.
title: Russian cuisine

text: They may be topped or filled with butter, smetana (sour cream), fruit preserves or caviar.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Blini are often served in connection with Maslenitsa (Масленица, Butter Week; also known as Pancake Week), a springtime religious festival celebrated before Lent, but it is also a common breakfast dish.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The word "blin" (singular of blini) comes from Old Slavic "mlin", which means "to mill".
title: Russian cuisine

text: Blin had a somewhat ritual significance for early Slavic peoples in pre-Christian times since they were a symbol of the sun, due to their round form.
title: Russian cuisine

text: They were traditionally prepared at the end of the winter to honor the rebirth of the new sun during Maslenitsa.
title: Russian cuisine

text: This tradition was adopted by the Orthodox Church and is carried on to the present day, as the last week of dairy and egg products before Lent.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Bliny are still often served at wakes, to commemorate the recently deceased.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Blini can be made from wheat, buckwheat, or other grains, although wheat blini are most popular in Russia.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The word "blin" is also often used as a soft curse word, expressing frustration.
title: Russian cuisine

text: This practice originates from the word's phonetic similarity to the much more vulgar word "Blyádt".
title: Russian cuisine

text: Syrniki are fried curd fritters, garnished with sour cream, jam, honey or applesauce.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Vatrushka is a kind of cake with a ring of dough and tvorog (cottage cheese) in the middle, often with raisins or bits of fruit, from about five inches to two and a half feet in diameter.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Kulich is a kind of Easter bread that is traditional in the Orthodox Christian faith and is eaten in countries like Russia, Belarus, Ukraine, Bulgaria, Romania, Georgia, Moldova, North Macedonia and Serbia.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Traditionally after the Easter service, the kulich, which has been put into a basket and decorated with colorful flowers, is blessed by the priest.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Blessed kulich is eaten before breakfast each day.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Any leftover kulich that is not blessed is eaten with Paskha for dessert.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Kulich is baked in tall, cylindrical tins (like coffee or fruit juice tins).
title: Russian cuisine

text: When cooled, kulich is decorated with white icing (which slightly drizzles down the sides) and colorful flowers.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Historically, it was often served with cheese paska bearing the symbol XB (from the traditional Easter greeting of Христос воскресе, "Christ is Risen").
title: Russian cuisine

text: Kulich is only eaten between Easter and Pentecost.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The recipe for kulich is similar to that of Italian panettone.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Paskha is a festive dish made in Eastern Orthodox countries which consists of food that is forbidden during the fast of Great Lent.
title: Russian cuisine

text: It is made during Holy Week and then brought to Church on Great Saturday to be blessed after the Paschal Vigil.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The name of the dish comes from Pascha, the Eastern Orthodox celebration of Easter.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Cheese paskha is a traditional Easter dish made from quark (curd cheese, Russian: творог, romanized: tvorog), which is white, symbolizing the purity of Christ, the Paschal Lamb, and the joy of the Resurrection.
title: Russian cuisine

text: It is formed in a mould, traditionally in the shape of a truncated pyramid (a symbol of the Church; this form is also said to represent the Tomb of Christ).
title: Russian cuisine

text: It is usually served as an accompaniment to a rich Easter bread called paska in Ukrainian and kulich in Russian.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The pascha is decorated with traditional religious symbols, such as the "Chi Ro" motif, a three-bar cross, and the letters X and B (Cyrillic letters which stands for Христосъ Воскресе.
title: Russian cuisine

text: This is the Slavonic form of the traditional Paschal greeting: "Christ is Risen!").
title: Russian cuisine

text: All of these religious decorations symbolize Christ's Passion and Resurrection.
title: Russian cuisine

text: It is made from cooking fruits or berries.
title: Russian cuisine

text: It is similar to jam except that fruits are not macerated and the consistency is more akin to fruit within syrup.
title: Russian cuisine

text: It is used as a topping for crepes and syrniki and as a sweetener for tea.
title: Russian cuisine

text: It is also eaten on its own as a sweet.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Pastila is a fruit confectionery (pâte de fruits).
title: Russian cuisine

text: It has been described as "small squares of pressed fruit paste" and "light, airy puffs with a delicate apple flavor".
title: Russian cuisine

text: In Imperial Russia, the "small jellied sweetmeats" were served for tea "with a white foamy top, a bit like marshmallow, but tasting of pure fruit".
title: Russian cuisine

text: In its modern form, it is essentially fruit meringue, but baked to a soft, not crisp consistency.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Unlike its derivative zefir, true pastila does not use gelling agents and has a much lower egg white content.
title: Russian cuisine

text: It is commonly produced and sold in the countries of the former Soviet Union.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The name given after the Greek god of the light west wind Zephyr symbolizes its delicate airy consistency.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Zefir is somewhat similar in its consistency to marshmallows, Schokokuss or krembo.
title: Russian cuisine

text: It is derived from the traditional Russian pastila but with added egg white foam and a gelling agent.
title: Russian cuisine

text: However, in contrast to commercial meringue, it is never crisp.
title: Russian cuisine

text: It is usually of white or rose color.
title: Russian cuisine

text: In contrast to the other chocolate-coated marshmallow-like confectioneries they normally do not include a biscuit layer.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Kissel or kisel is a viscous fruit dish, popular as a dessert and as a drink.
title: Russian cuisine

text: It consists of the sweetened juice of berries, like mors, but it is thickened with cornstarch, potato starch or arrowroot; sometimes red wine or fresh or dried fruits are added.
title: Russian cuisine

text: It is similar to the Danish rødgrød and German Rote Grütze.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Swedish blåbärssoppa is a similarly prepared bilberry dessert, although only fresh or frozen bilberries, not dried berries are used to prepare it.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Kissel can be served either hot or cold, also together with sweetened quark or semolina pudding.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Kissel can also be served on pancakes or with ice cream.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The most notable of these are vodka, sbiten', kvass, medovukha and mors.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Many of them are no longer common and have been replaced by drinks originating in Europe.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Nonetheless, these beverages were formerly drunk as a complement to meat and poultry dishes, sweet porridge, and dessert.
title: Russian cuisine

text: A stronger honey-based beverage, stavlenniy myod, also exists in Russia and is broadly equivalent to Scandinavian mead; it is typically made with the admixture of berry juices.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Vodka is the best known of Russia's alcoholic products and is produced, with some variation, throughout the country.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Vodka can be either grain or potato based and is frequently flavored with a great variety of ingredients ranging from hot-pepper and horseradish to fruits and berries.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Beer has been manufactured in Russia since at the very least the 9th century.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Its popularity was for many centuries concentrated in the Lands of Novgorod.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Beer continued to be made throughout Russian history, but real growth came in the 18th century when many breweries were founded in order to supply the newly modernized and expanded imperial army and fleet.
title: Russian cuisine

text: A real explosion in the popularity of beer came in the last decades of the Soviet Era and has continued into the present day, with Russia now ranking as the fourth largest producer in the world.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Wine is manufactured in the southern regions in the country but lags far behind other alcoholic beverages in popularity.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Kvass is an ancient and still widely popular bread-based drink.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The basic method of preparing kvass includes water, flour and liquid malt; these ingredients are used to make a dough that is subjected to fermentation.
title: Russian cuisine

text: This results in a beverage with very low alcohol content.
title: Russian cuisine

text: The fermented liquid, referred to as "zator", is diluted with water and mixed with yeast, sugar, and aromatic additives.
title: Russian cuisine

text: This final mixture is allowed to brew for several days.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Flavor additives may include fruit and berry juices (cherry, raspberry, lemon, etc.), as well as ginger and mint.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Sbiten, another non-alcoholic drink, is made of honey, water, fruit juices, and spices.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Sbiten was once the most popular non-alcoholic beverage in the country, but in the last few centuries, it has been superseded and largely replaced by tea and coffee.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Tea is by far the most common drink in almost all parts of Russia.
title: Russian cuisine

text: First introduced from China in the 17th century, its popularity has since spread throughout the country.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Black tea has always been the dominant variety, but after the Russian acquisition of Central Asia, awareness of and interest in green tea began to increase slowly.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Today Russia remains one of the largest tea consumers in the world.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Russian Caravan is perhaps the most well-known type of Russian tea around the world.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Until the Sino-Soviet split, tea was mostly brought in from China.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Now, Russia imports most of its tea from India and Sri Lanka, with Darjeeling being the most prized variety.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Domestic cultivation exists in the southern regions of the country (mostly in Krasnodar Krai), but local supply is very limited compared to national consumption.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Coffee is also popular but has never caught up to tea in popularity.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Peter the Great is credited with introducing coffee to Russia, with the drink becoming steadily more pervasive since that time.
title: Russian cuisine

text: Ukrainian (українська мова, ukrainska mova, IPA: [ʊkrɐˈjinʲsʲkɐ ˈmɔwɐ]) is an East Slavic language, spoken primarily in Ukraine.
title: Ukrainian language

text: It is the first (native) language of a large majority of Ukrainians.
title: Ukrainian language

text: Written Ukrainian uses the Ukrainian alphabet, a variant of the Cyrillic script.
title: Ukrainian language

text: The standard language is studied by the National Academy of Sciences of Ukraine and Potebnia Institute of Linguistics.
title: Ukrainian language

text: Comparisons are often made between Ukrainian and Russian, another East Slavic language, yet there is more mutual intelligibility with Belarusian, and a closer lexical distance to West Slavic Polish and South Slavic Bulgarian.
title: Ukrainian language

text: Ukrainian is a descendant of Old East Slavic, a language spoken in the medieval state of Kievan Rusʹ.
title: Ukrainian language

text: In the Grand Duchy of Lithuania, the language developed into Ruthenian, where it became an official language, before a process of Polonization began in the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth.
title: Ukrainian language

text: By the 18th century, Ruthenian diverged into regional variants, and the modern Ukrainian language developed in the territory of present-day Ukraine.
title: Ukrainian language

text: Russification saw the Ukrainian language banned as a subject from schools and as a language of instruction in the Russian Empire, and continued in various ways in the Soviet Union.
title: Ukrainian language

text: Only the fricativisation of Old East Slavic г /g/ occurred in Belarusian, where the present-day reflex is /ɣ/.
title: Ukrainian language

text: Ahatanhel Krymsky and Aleksey Shakhmatov assumed the existence of the common spoken language of Eastern Slavs only in prehistoric times.
title: Ukrainian language

text: According to them the diversification of the Old East Slavic language took place in the 8th or early 9th century.
title: Ukrainian language

text: Russian linguist Andrey Zaliznyak stated that the Old Novgorod dialect differed significantly from that of other dialects of Kievan Rusʹ during the 11th–12th century, but started becoming more similar to them around the 13th–15th centuries.
title: Ukrainian language

text: Ukrainian linguist Stepan Smal-Stotsky denies the existence of a common Old East Slavic language at any time in the past.
title: Ukrainian language

text: Similar points of view were shared by Yevhen Tymchenko, Vsevolod Hantsov, Olena Kurylo, Ivan Ohienko and others.
title: Ukrainian language

text: According to this theory, the dialects of East Slavic tribes evolved gradually from the common Proto-Slavic language without any intermediate stages during the 6th through 9th centuries.
title: Ukrainian language

text: The Ukrainian language was formed by convergence of tribal dialects, mostly due to an intensive migration of the population within the territory of today's Ukraine in later historical periods.
title: Ukrainian language

text: During the 13th century, when German settlers were invited to Ukraine by the princes of the Kingdom of Ruthenia, German words began to appear in the language spoken in Ukraine.
title: Ukrainian language

text: Their influence would continue under Poland not only through German colonists but also through the Yiddish-speaking Jews.
title: Ukrainian language

text: For the following four centuries, the languages of the two regions evolved in relative isolation from each other.
title: Ukrainian language

text: Direct written evidence of the existence of the Ukrainian language dates to the late 16th century.
title: Ukrainian language

text: By the 16th century, a peculiar official language formed: a mixture of the liturgical standardised language of Old Church Slavonic, Ruthenian and Polish.
title: Ukrainian language

text: The influence of the latter gradually increased relative to the former two, as the nobility and rural large-landowning class, known as the szlachta, was largely Polish-speaking.
title: Ukrainian language

text: Documents soon took on many Polish characteristics superimposed on Ruthenian phonetics.
title: Ukrainian language

text: Polish–Lithuanian rule and education also involved significant exposure to Latin.
title: Ukrainian language

text: Much of the influence of Poland on the development of the Ukrainian language has been attributed to this period, and is reflected in multiple words and constructions used in everyday Ukrainian speech that were taken from Polish or Latin.
title: Ukrainian language

text: Examples of Polish words adopted from this period include zavzhdy (always; taken from old Polish word zawżdy) and obitsiaty (to promise; taken from Polish obiecać) and from Latin (via Polish) raptom (suddenly) and meta (aim or goal).
title: Ukrainian language

text: Significant contact with Tatars and Turks resulted in many Turkic words, particularly those involving military matters and steppe industry, being adopted into the Ukrainian language.
title: Ukrainian language

text: Russian is an East Slavic language belonging to the Balto-Slavic branch of the Indo-European language family.

text: It is one of the four extant East Slavic languages, and is the native language of the Russian people.
title: Russian language

title: Russian language

text: It has remained an official language of Russia, Belarus, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, and Tajikistan, and is still commonly used as a lingua franca in Ukraine, Moldova, the Caucasus, Central Asia, and to a lesser extent in the Baltic states and Israel.

text: The Cathedral of Vasily the Blessed (Russian: Собор Василия Блаженного, romanized: Sobor Vasiliya Blazhennogo), commonly known as Saint Basil's Cathedral, is a Russian Orthodox church on Red Square in the historic centre of Moscow.
title: Saint Basil's Cathedral

text: It is one of the most popular cultural symbols of Russia.
title: Saint Basil's Cathedral

text: The building, now a museum, is officially known as the Cathedral of the Intercession of the Most Holy Theotokos on the Moat, or Pokrovsky Cathedral.
title: Saint Basil's Cathedral

text: It was built from 1555 to 1561 on orders from Ivan the Terrible and commemorates the capture of Kazan and Astrakhan.
title: Saint Basil's Cathedral

text: It was completed, with its colours, in 1683.
title: Saint Basil's Cathedral

text: In the 16th and 17th centuries, it was perceived as the earthly symbol of the Heavenly City.
title: Saint Basil's Cathedral

text: The cathedral has nine distinctive onion domes (each one corresponding to a different church) and is shaped like the flame of a bonfire rising into the sky.

title: Saint Basil's Cathedral

text: As part of the program of state atheism, the church was confiscated from the Russian Orthodox community as part of the Soviet Union's antireligious campaigns and has operated as a division of the State Historical Museum since 1928.
title: Saint Basil's Cathedral

text: It was completely secularized in 1929, and remains a federal property of the Russian Federation.

text: The State Hermitage Museum (Russian: Государственный Эрмитаж, romanized: Gosudarstvennyj Ermitaž, IPA: [ɡəsʊˈdarstvʲɪn(ː)ɨj ɪrmʲɪˈtaʂ]) is a museum of art and culture in Saint Petersburg, Russia, and holds the largest collection of paintings in the world.
title: Hermitage Museum

text: It was founded in 1764 when Empress Catherine the Great acquired a collection of paintings from the Berlin merchant Johann Ernst Gotzkowsky.
title: Hermitage Museum

text: The museum celebrates the anniversary of its founding each year on 7 December, Saint Catherine's Day.
title: Hermitage Museum

text: It has been open to the public since 1852.
title: Hermitage Museum

text: The Art Newspaper ranked the museum 10th in their list of the most visited art museums, with 2,812,913 visitors in 2022.
title: Hermitage Museum

text: Its collections, of which only a small part is on permanent display, comprises over three million items (the numismatic collection accounting for about one-third of them).

text: The term Ukrainian literature (Ukrainian: Українська література) is normally used to describe works of literature written in the Ukrainian language.
title: Ukrainian literature

text: In a broader sense it can also relate to all literary works created in the territory of Ukraine.
title: Ukrainian literature

text: Ukrainian literature mostly developed under foreign domination over Ukrainian territories, mainly by the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, Poland, the Russian Empire, the Kingdom of Romania, the Austria-Hungary Empire, and the Ottoman Empire.
title: Ukrainian literature

text: Ukrainian literature can be traced back to Kievan Rus' when Rus' chronicles and Epic poetry were written in Old Church Slavonic and Church Slavonic.
title: Ukrainian literature

text: Old Church Slavonic was used mostly in written documents, but the common spoken language in the territories which it dominated was Old East Slavic, which was eventually divided into Ukrainian, Russian, and Belarusian.
title: Ukrainian literature

text: Ukrainian culture was thriving under the rule of Ukrainian Cossacks, although their state was eventually divided into an eastern and western parts dominated by various neighbours.
title: Ukrainian literature

text: From the 19th century, Ukrainian literary works started to be written in a colloquial form.
title: Ukrainian literature

text: Poems, novels, plays, and other works were published.
title: Ukrainian literature

text: Following the trend, Ukrainian nationalist and independence movements started to grow.
title: Ukrainian literature

text: This point of view has been argued against by authors such as Dmytro Chyzhevsky and George Grabowicz, who state that a single literary tradition may use different languages at different stages of its development.
title: Ukrainian literature

text: In the language sense, modern Ukrainian literature can be broadly divided into Ukrainian-speaking writers and Russian-speaking writers who live in Ukraine and write on Ukrainian themes.
title: Ukrainian literature

text: However, works written in Surzhyk, or Ukrainian by foreign authors, can also be seen as Ukrainian literature.
title: Ukrainian literature

text: Works written in Crimean Tatar language or foreign languages by Ukrainian people also count.

text: Russian literature is the literature of Russia, its émigrés, and Russian-language literature.
title: Russian literature

text: Major contributors to Russian literature, as well as English for instance, are authors of different ethnic origins, including bilingual writers, such as Kyrgyz novelist Chinghiz Aitmatov and Vasil Bykaŭ, the latter wrote in Belarusian, but translated his works into Russian.
title: Russian literature

text: The roots of Russian written literature can be traced to the Early Middle Ages when Old Church Slavonic was introduced as a liturgical language and became used as a literary language.
title: Russian literature

text: The native Russian vernacular remained the use within oral literature as well as written for decrees, laws, messages, chronicles, military tales, and so on.
title: Russian literature

text: By the Age of Enlightenment, literature had grown in importance, and from the early 1830s, Russian literature underwent an astounding "Golden Age" in poetry, prose and drama.
title: Russian literature

text: The Romantic movement contributed to a flowering of literary talent: poet Vasily Zhukovsky and later his protégé Alexander Pushkin came to the fore.
title: Russian literature

text: Mikhail Lermontov was one of the most important poets and novelists.
title: Russian literature

text: Nikolai Gogol and Ivan Turgenev wrote masterful short stories and novels.
title: Russian literature

text: Fyodor Dostoevsky and Leo Tolstoy became internationally renowned.
title: Russian literature

text: Other important figures were Ivan Goncharov, Mikhail Saltykov-Shchedrin and Nikolai Leskov.
title: Russian literature

text: In the second half of the century Anton Chekhov excelled in short stories and became a leading dramatist.
title: Russian literature

text: The end of the 19th century and the beginning of the 20th century is sometimes called the Silver Age of Russian poetry.

text: Borscht (English: ) is a sour soup, made with meat stock, vegetables and seasonings, common in Eastern Europe, Central Europe and Northern Asia.
title: Borscht

text: In English, the word borscht, borrowed via Yiddish, is most often associated with the variant of the soup originating in Ukraine, made with red beetroots as one of the main ingredients, which give the dish its distinctive red color.
title: Borscht

text: The same name, however, is also used for a wide selection of sour-tasting soups without beetroots, such as sorrel-based green borscht, rye-based white borscht, and cabbage borscht.
title: Borscht

text: Borscht derives from an ancient soup originally cooked from pickled stems, leaves and umbels of common hogweed (Heracleum sphondylium), an herbaceous plant growing in damp meadows, which lent the dish its Slavic name.
title: Borscht

text: With time, it evolved into a diverse array of tart soups, among which the Ukrainian beet-based red borscht has become the most popular.
title: Borscht

text: It is typically made by combining meat or bone stock with sautéed vegetables, which—as well as beetroots—usually include cabbage, carrots, onions, potatoes, and tomatoes.
title: Borscht

text: Depending on the recipe, borscht may include meat or fish, or be purely vegetarian; it may be served either hot or cold, and it may range from a hearty one-pot meal to a clear broth or a smooth drink.
title: Borscht

text: It is often served with smetana or sour cream, hard-boiled eggs or potatoes, but there exists an ample choice of more involved garnishes and side dishes, such as uszka or pampushky, that can be served with the soup.
title: Borscht

text: Its popularity has spread throughout Eastern Europe and—by way of migration away from the Russian Empire—to other continents.
title: Borscht

text: In North America, borscht is often linked with either Jews or Mennonites, the groups who first brought it there from Europe.
title: Borscht

text: Several ethnic groups claim borscht, in its various local implementations, as their own national dish consumed as part of ritual meals within Orthodox, Greek Catholic, Roman Catholic, and Jewish religious traditions.
title: Borscht

text: In 2022, shortly after Russia's invasion of Ukraine, UNESCO recognized the "Culture of Ukrainian borscht cooking" on its List of Intangible Cultural Heritage in Need of Urgent Safeguarding.
title: Borscht

text: Together with cognates in other Slavic languages, such as Belarusian: бо́ршч (borshch), Polish: barszcz, and others, it comes from Proto-Slavic *bъ̃rščь, 'hogweed', and ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *bʰr̥stis, 'point', 'stubble'.
title: Borscht

text: Beef, pork or a combination of both are most commonly used, with brisket, ribs, shank and chuck considered to give the most flavorful results, especially if cooked on a high flame.
title: Borscht

text: Marrow bones are considered best for the bone stock.
title: Borscht

text: Meat stock is usually cooked for about two hours, whereas bone stock takes four to six hours to prepare.
title: Borscht

text: Meat and bones are usually removed afterwards and the meat is only added back into the soup about 10–15 minutes before the borscht is done.
title: Borscht

text: Some recipes call for smoked meats, resulting in a distinctively smoky borscht, while others use poultry or mutton stock.
title: Borscht

text: Fasting varieties are typically made with fish stock to avoid the use of meat, while purely vegetarian recipes often substitute forest mushroom broth for the stock.
title: Borscht

text: The vegetables most commonly added to borscht are beetroots, white cabbage, carrots, parsley root, potatoes, onions and tomatoes.
title: Borscht

text: Some recipes may also call for beans, tart apples, turnip, swede, celeriac, zucchini or bell peppers.
title: Borscht

text: Parsnip may be used as a substitute for parsley root, and tomato paste is often used as well as or instead of fresh tomatoes.
title: Borscht

text: Onions, carrots, parsley root, turnip and other root vegetables are sautéed (traditionally in animal fat, especially lard or butter) and then mixed with tomatoes or tomato paste.
title: Borscht

text: Potatoes and cabbage are boiled in the stock for about 15 minutes before the precooked vegetables are added.
title: Borscht

text: The traditional technique of preparing the soup is to precook the vegetables—by sautéing, braising, boiling or baking—separately from the meat and only then to combine them with the stock.
title: Borscht

text: The importance of this method is reflected in the Russian language, where a variant in which all vegetables are added raw directly into the stock is referred to by the diminutive form borshchok rather than borshch.
title: Borscht

text: The soup is typically flavored with a wide selection of herbs, spices and condiments.
title: Borscht

text: Salt, black pepper, garlic, bay leaves and dill are among the most commonly used.
title: Borscht

text: Other aromatics often added to borscht include allspice, celery stalks, parsley, marjoram, hot peppers, saffron, horseradish, ginger and prunes.
title: Borscht

text: Some recipes require flour or roux to further thicken the borscht.
title: Borscht

text: This combination is traditionally obtained by adding beet sour.
title: Borscht

text: The sour is made by covering sliced beetroots with lukewarm preboiled water and allowing bacteria to ferment some of the sugars present in beetroots into dextran (which gives the liquid a slightly viscous consistency), mannitol, acetic acid and lactic acid.
title: Borscht

text: Stale rye bread is often added to hasten the process, but usually omitted in Jewish recipes, as chametz (leavened bread) would make the sour unfit for Passover meals.
title: Borscht

text: Sugar, salt and lemon juice may also be added to balance the flavor.
title: Borscht

text: After about 2–5 days (or 2–3 weeks without the bread), the deep red, sweet and sour liquid may be strained and is ready to use.
title: Borscht

text: It is added to borscht shortly before the soup is done, as prolonged boiling would cause the tart flavor to dissipate.
title: Borscht

text: Apart from its employment in borscht, it may also be added to prepared horseradish or used as pot roast marinade.
title: Borscht

title: Borscht

text: The use of zucchini, beans and apples is characteristic of the Chernihiv borscht; in this variant, beetroots are sautéed in vegetable oil rather than lard, and the sour taste comes solely from tomatoes and tart apples.
title: Borscht

text: The 19th-century historian Cezary Biernacki wrote: Borscht was and is the most commonplace, and with the addition of spices, the most accurate, truly Polish soup, received with great taste, indeed, and respect.
title: Borscht

text: The 19th century was a turning point; it was then that red borscht with dumplings began to appear on Christmas Eve tables.
title: Borscht

text: Borscht also evolved into a variety of sour soups to the east of Poland.
title: Borscht

text: Examples include onion borscht, a recipe for which was included in a 1905 Russian cookbook, and sorrel-based green borscht, which is still a popular summer soup in Ukraine and Russia.
title: Borscht

text: Kvass-based variants were also known in Ukraine at that time; some of them were types of green borscht, while others were similar to the Russian okroshka.
title: Borscht

text: Before the advent of beet-based borscht, cabbage borscht was of particular importance.
title: Borscht

text: Made from either fresh cabbage or sauerkraut, it could be indistinguishable from the Russian shchi.
title: Borscht

text: Indeed, the mid-19th century Explanatory Dictionary of the Living Great Russian Language defines borshch as sour beet or "a kind of shchi" with sour beet base.
title: Borscht

text: Only the leaves were of culinary use, as the tapered, tough, whitish and bitter-tasting root was considered unfit for human consumption.
title: Borscht

text: It is probably that beet greens were used in variants of green borscht long before the invention of the beetroot-based red borscht.
title: Borscht

text: Beet varieties with round, red, sweet taproots, known as beetroots, were not reliably reported until the 12th century and did not spread to Eastern Europe before the 16th century.
title: Borscht

text: Mikołaj Rej, a Polish Renaissance poet and moralist, included the earliest known Polish recipe for pickled beetroots in his 1568 book, Life of an Honest Man.
title: Borscht

text: It would later evolve into ćwikła, or chrain mit burik, a beet-and-horseradish relish popular in Polish and Jewish cuisines.
title: Borscht

text: Rej also recommended the "very tasty brine" left over from beetroot pickling, which was an early version of beet sour.
title: Borscht

text: The sour found some applications in Polish folk medicine as a cure for hangover and—mixed with honey—as a sore throat remedy.
title: Borscht

text: It may never be known who first thought of using beet sour to flavor borscht, which also gave the soup its now-familiar red color.
title: Borscht

text: Jerzy Samuel Bandtkie's Polish-German dictionary published in 1806 was the first to define barszcz as a tart soup made from pickled beetroots.
title: Borscht

text: Spanish conquistadors brought potatoes and tomatoes from the Americas to Europe in the 16th century, but these vegetables only became commonly grown and consumed in Eastern Europe in the 19th century.
title: Borscht

text: Eventually, both became staples of peasant diet and essential ingredients of Ukrainian and Russian borscht.
title: Borscht

text: Potatoes replaced turnips in borscht recipes, and tomatoes—fresh, canned or paste—took over from beet sour as the source of tartness.
title: Borscht

text: The turnip is rarely found in modern recipes, and even then, together with potatoes.
title: Borscht

text: Among the rich it was not unusual to have several varieties of the dish during one meal.
title: Borscht

text: One recipe preserved from that time includes ingredients such as cardamom, eggs, mushrooms and cherry.
title: Borscht

text: Russian and Polish aristocrats used to employ celebrated French chefs, who later presented their dishes as foreign curiosity back in France.
title: Borscht

text: One of the first French chefs to do so was Marie-Antoine Carême, who worked briefly for Emperor Alexander I in 1819.
title: Borscht

text: In his take on borscht, the original Russian soup served only as inspiration for an extravagant haute cuisine dish with an air of eastern exoticism.
title: Borscht

text: Auguste Escoffier, Carême's apprentice, who was mostly fascinated by the soup's vivid ruby-red color, simplified his master's recipe, while also securing the place of potage bortsch (lit. 'borscht soup') in French cuisine.
title: Borscht

text: Borscht's westward expansion was less successful; Germans used to scoff at the soup along with other East European fare.
title: Borscht

text: What helped the spread of borscht, however, was the popularization by various haute cuisine chefs who had their own dishes to present to West Europe.
title: Borscht

text: Mass migration from the Russian Empire to North America—initially mostly by members of persecuted religious minorities—was instrumental in bringing borscht across the Atlantic.
title: Borscht

text: Jews from the Pale of Settlement, an area that stretched along the western edges of the Russian Empire and included much of present-day Ukraine, brought with them the Ukrainian variety of borscht with beetroot.
title: Borscht

text: The earliest waves of migration, however, occurred at a time when cabbage-based borscht was still the dominant variant of the soup in at least parts of Russia.
title: Borscht

text: The Mennonites, who began arriving in Canada and the United States from Russia's Volga region in the 1870s, still eschew beetroots in their borscht; instead, Mennonite varieties include Komst Borscht (with cabbage or sauerkraut) and Somma Borscht (sorrel-based "summer borscht").
title: Borscht

text: According to the Jewish Encyclopedia published in 1906, cabbage-based kraut borscht was also more popular than the beet-based variant in American Jewish cuisine at the time.
title: Borscht

text: Subsequent Jewish immigration helped popularize the red borscht in America.
title: Borscht

text: In the 1930s, when most American hotels refused to accept Jewish guests due to widespread anti-Semitism, New York Jews began flocking to Jewish-owned resorts in the Catskill Mountains for their summer vacations.
title: Borscht

text: The area grew into a major center of Jewish entertainment, with restaurants offering all-you-can-eat Ashkenazi Jewish fare, including copious amounts of borscht.
title: Borscht

text: Grossinger's, one of the largest resorts, served borscht throughout the day, every day of the year.
title: Borscht

text: The region became known, initially in derision, as the "Borscht Belt", reinforcing the popular association between borscht and American Jewish culture.
title: Borscht

text: As most visitors arrived in the summertime, the borscht was typically served cold.
title: Borscht

text: Marc Gold was one of its largest suppliers, producing 1,750 short tons (1,590 tonnes) a year in his business's heyday.
title: Borscht

text: Gold's borscht consists of puréed beetroots seasoned with sugar, salt and citric acid; it is usually blended with sour cream and served as a refreshing beverage, more aptly described as a "beet smoothie".
title: Borscht

text: The soup has even played a role in the Soviet space program.
title: Borscht

text: In March 1961, as part of a communications equipment test, a pre-recorded recipe for borscht was broadcast from the Korabl-Sputnik 4 spacecraft.
title: Borscht

text: The craft, carrying animals and a mannequin, had been launched into low Earth orbit in preparation for crewed space flights.
title: Borscht

text: All ingredients for the space borscht (which include beef, beetroots, cabbage, potatoes, carrots, onions, parsley root, and tomato paste) were cooked separately, then combined one by one in strictly controlled order, sterilized, packed into tubes, sealed airtight and autoclaved.
title: Borscht

text: In the 1970s, the tubes were replaced with packages of rehydratable freeze-dried borscht with regular-size bits of cooked vegetables.
title: Borscht

text: An article on borscht in the Soviet-era book Entsyclopedia Domashnego Hozyaystva (lit. 'Encyclopedia of Housekeeping') suggests to make a soup with beets, other vegetables, and tomato purée as a "borscht" in general.
title: Borscht

text: In East Slavic countries, "memorial borscht" is served as the first course at a post-funeral wake.
title: Borscht

text: In the region of Polesye, straddling the Belarusian-Ukrainian border, the same steaming-hot dishes, including borscht, are given as an offering to the souls of deceased ancestors during the annual semi-pagan remembrance ceremony known as Dzyady or Forefathers' Night.
title: Borscht

text: In Poland and Ukraine, borscht is usually one of the dishes served at a Christmas Eve dinner.
title: Borscht

text: Christmas Eve borscht is, therefore, either vegetarian or based on fish stock and is not typically mixed with sour cream.
title: Borscht

text: In Ukraine, the soup contains vegetables that are sautéed in vegetable oil rather than lard, as well as beans and mushrooms.
title: Borscht

text: It may also be thickened with wheat flour dry-roasted in a pan instead of the usual roux.
title: Borscht

text: The Polish version of Christmas Eve borscht is a clear ruby-red broth.
title: Borscht

text: Both Ukrainian and Polish variants are often served with uszka.
title: Borscht

text: While Christmas in Poland is traditionally linked to red borscht, Lent—the fasting period that leads up to Easter—is associated with a meatless version of white borscht, or żur.
title: Borscht

text: On the next day, the white borscht would reappear on the Easter table, but this time, in its more coveted, meat-based guise with sausage, bacon and eggs.
title: Borscht

text: In Eastern European Ashkenazi Jewish tradition, vegetarian borscht served with sour cream and boiled potatoes on the side, known as peysakhdiker borsht, is considered an essential dish during the Passover period.
title: Borscht

text: Cold borscht blended with sour cream is also popular on Shavuot (Feast of Weeks), a holiday customarily associated with dairy foods, observed in late May or early June.
title: Borscht

text: Seudah Shlishit, or the third meal of the Shabbat, often includes borscht as well.
title: Borscht

text: In 2022, the United Nations Educational, Scientific, and Cultural Organization (UNESCO) announced that it had placed "Culture of Ukrainian borscht cooking" on the List of Intangible Cultural Heritage in Need of Urgent Safeguarding.
title: Borscht

text: UNESCO noted that Borscht cooking was "also practised in communities in the broader region", and its designation "does not imply exclusivity, nor ownership, of the heritage concerned".
title: Borscht

text: Instead it recognized the significant cultural importance of Borscht to Ukrainians and the need to safeguard this culture, particularly in light of Russia's invasion of Ukraine.
title: Borscht

text: Borscht's role as a staple of everyday Ukrainian diet is reflected in the Ukrainian saying, "borscht and porridge are our food" (compare the equivalent Russian saying, where borscht is replaced with shchi).
title: Borscht

text: The hearty soup in which the beetroot is just one of sundry vegetables, as opposed to the typically Polish clear beet broth, is still known in Poland as "Ukrainian borscht".
title: Borscht

text: Borscht is associated with and claimed by several ethnic groups, especially Ukrainians, Russians, Poles, Lithuanians and Ashkenazi Jews, as their own national or ethnic dish and cultural icon.
title: Borscht

text: Such gastronationalistic claims are not necessarily mutually exclusive, as the soup's history predates the emergence in Eastern Europe of modern nation states with their ever-shifting borders.
title: Borscht

text: Borscht, in the words of Burlakoff, "is perfectly suited to a global culture".
title: Borscht

text: In 2020 Ukraine began the process to have borscht recognised as an element of the country's intangible cultural heritage, an initiative supported by chefs and food writers such as Marianna Dushar.
title: Borscht

text: The same cooking techniques and recipes were taught in culinary vocational schools throughout the country, establishing a common cooking style in Soviet cafés and restaurants.
title: Borscht

text: Though inspired by the cuisines of the country's various ethnic groups, many recipes were presented as part of an overall Soviet heritage, disassociated from their individual geographic origins.
title: Borscht

text: By many people both inside and outside the Soviet Union, borscht was increasingly seen not as an ethnic Ukrainian soup, but as a Soviet or—metonymically—Russian dish.
title: Borscht

text: Moscow is the capital and largest city of Russia, standing on the Moskva River in Central Russia.
title: Moscow

text: It has a population estimated at over 13 million residents within the city limits, over 19.1 million residents in the urban area, and over 21.5 million residents in its metropolitan area.
title: Moscow

text: The city covers an area of 2,511 square kilometers (970 mi2), while the urban area covers 5,891 square kilometers (2,275 mi2), and the metropolitan area covers over 26,000 square kilometers (10,000 mi2).
title: Moscow

text: Moscow is among the world's largest cities, being the most populous city entirely in Europe, the largest urban and metropolitan area in Europe, and the largest city by land area on the European continent.
title: Moscow

text: First documented in 1147, Moscow became the capital of the Grand Principality of Moscow, which led the unification of the Russian lands in the 15th century and became the center of a unified state.
title: Moscow

text: Following the proclamation of the Tsardom of Russia in 1547, Moscow remained the political and economic center for most of its history.
title: Moscow

text: During the reign of Peter the Great, the Russian capital was moved to the newly founded city of Saint Petersburg in 1712, leading to a decline in Moscow's importance throughout the imperial period.
title: Moscow

text: Following the Russian Revolution and the establishment of the Russian SFSR, the capital was moved back to Moscow in 1918.
title: Moscow

text: The city later became the political center of the Soviet Union and experienced significant population growth throughout the Soviet period.
title: Moscow

text: In the aftermath of the dissolution of the Soviet Union, Moscow remained the capital city of the newly reconstituted Russian Federation and has experienced continued growth.
title: Moscow

text: The northernmost and coldest megacity in the world, Moscow is governed as a federal city, where it serves as the political, economic, cultural, and scientific center of Russia and Eastern Europe.
title: Moscow

text: Moscow has one of the world's largest urban economies.
title: Moscow

text: Moscow has the second-highest number of billionaires of any city (tied with Hong Kong).
title: Moscow

text: The Moscow International Business Center is one of the largest financial centers in the world and features the majority of Europe's tallest skyscrapers.
title: Moscow

text: Moscow was the host city of the 1980 Summer Olympics and one of the host cities of the 2018 FIFA World Cup.
title: Moscow

text: For much of its architectural history, Moscow was dominated by Orthodox churches.

text: However, the overall appearance of the city changed drastically during Soviet times, especially as a result of Joseph Stalin's large-scale effort to "modernize" Moscow.

text: Among the many casualties of Stalin's demolitions was the Sukharev Tower, a longtime city landmark, as well as mansions and commercial buildings.
title: Moscow

text: The city's newfound status as the capital of a deeply secular nation, made religiously significant buildings especially vulnerable to demolition.
title: Moscow

text: Many of the city's churches, which in most cases were some of Moscow's oldest and most prominent buildings, were destroyed; some notable examples include the Kazan Cathedral and the Cathedral of Christ the Savior.
title: Moscow

text: While the later Stalinist period was characterized by the curtailing of creativity and architectural innovation, the earlier post-revolutionary years saw a plethora of radical new buildings created in the city.
title: Moscow

text: Especially notable were the constructivist architects associated with VKHUTEMAS, responsible for such landmarks as Lenin's Mausoleum.

text: Another prominent architect was Vladimir Shukhov, famous for Shukhov Tower, just one of many hyperboloid towers designed by Shukhov.

text: It was built between 1919 and 1922 as a transmission tower for a Russian broadcasting company.

text: Shukhov also left a lasting legacy to the Constructivist architecture of early Soviet Russia.

text: He designed spacious elongated shop galleries, most notably the GUM department store on Red Square, bridged with innovative metal-and-glass vaults.
title: Moscow

text: Perhaps the most recognizable contributions of the Stalinist period are the so-called Seven Sisters, seven massive skyscrapers scattered throughout the city at about an equal distance from the Kremlin.

text: A defining feature of Moscow's skyline, their imposing form was allegedly inspired by the Manhattan Municipal Building in New York City, and their style—with intricate exteriors and a large central spire—has been described as Stalinist Gothic architecture.
