text: Stalinist-era buildings, mostly found in the central part of the city, are massive and usually ornamented with Socialist realism motifs that imitate classical themes.
title: Moscow

text: However, small churches—almost always Eastern Orthodox–found across the city provide glimpses of its past.
title: Moscow

text: The Old Arbat Street, a tourist street that was once the heart of a bohemian area, preserves most of its buildings from prior to the 20th century.
title: Moscow

text: Many buildings found off the main streets of the inner city (behind the Stalinist façades of Tverskaya Street, for example) are also examples of bourgeois architecture typical of Tsarist times.
title: Moscow

text: Ostankino Palace, Kuskovo, Uzkoye and other large estates just outside Moscow originally belong to nobles from the Tsarist era, and some convents, and monasteries, both inside and outside the city, are open to Muscovites and tourists.
title: Moscow

text: Attempts are being made to restore many of the city's best-kept examples of pre-Soviet architecture.
title: Moscow

text: These restored structures are easily spotted by their bright new colors and spotless façades.
title: Moscow

text: There are a few examples of notable, early Soviet avant-garde work too, such as the house of the architect Konstantin Melnikov in the Arbat area.
title: Moscow

text: Many of these restorations were criticized for alleged disrespect of historical authenticity.
title: Moscow

text: Plaques on house exteriors will inform passers-by that a well-known personality once lived there.
title: Moscow

text: Frequently, the plaques are dedicated to Soviet celebrities not well known outside (or often, like with decorated generals and revolutionaries, now both inside) of Russia.
title: Moscow

text: There are also many "museum houses" of famous Russian writers, composers, and artists in the city.
title: Moscow

text: Moscow's skyline is quickly modernizing, with several new towers under construction.
title: Moscow

text: In recent years, the city administration has been widely criticized for heavy destruction that has affected many historical buildings.
title: Moscow

text: As much as a third of historic Moscow has been destroyed in the past few years to make space for luxury apartments and hotels.
title: Moscow

text: Other historical buildings, including such landmarks as the 1930 Moskva hotel and the 1913 department store Voyentorg, have been razed and reconstructed anew, with the inevitable loss of historical value.
title: Moscow

text: Critics blame the government for not enforcing conservation laws: in the last 12 years, more than 50 buildings with monument status were torn down, several of those dating back to the 17th century.
title: Moscow

text: Some critics also wonder if the money used for the reconstruction of razed buildings could not be used for the renovation of decaying structures, which include many works by architect Konstantin Melnikov and Mayakovskaya metro station.
title: Moscow

text: There are 450 square kilometres (170 sq mi) of green zones besides 100 square kilometres (39 sq mi) of forests.
title: Moscow

text: Moscow is a very green city, if compared to other cities of comparable size in Western Europe and North America; this is partly due to a history of having green "yards" with trees and grass, between residential buildings.
title: Moscow

text: There are on average 27 square meters (290 sq ft) of parks per person in Moscow compared with 6 for Paris, 7.5 in London and 8.6 in New York.
title: Moscow

text: Gorky Park (officially the Central Park of Culture and Rest named after Maxim Gorky), was founded in 1928.
title: Moscow

text: The main part (689,000 square metres or 170 acres) along the Moskva river contains estrades, children's attractions (including the Observation Wheel water ponds with boats and water bicycles), dancing, tennis courts and other sports facilities.
title: Moscow

text: It borders the Neskuchny Garden (408,000 square metres or 101 acres), the oldest park in Moscow and a former imperial residence, created as a result of the integration of three estates in the 18th century.
title: Moscow

text: The Garden features the Green Theater, one of the largest open amphitheaters in Europe, able to hold up to 15 thousand people.
title: Moscow

text: Several parks include a section known as a "Park of Culture and Rest", sometimes alongside a much wilder area (this includes parks such as Izmaylovsky, Fili and Sokolniki).
title: Moscow

text: Some parks are designated as Forest Parks (lesopark).
title: Moscow

text: Izmaylovsky Park, created in 1931, is one of the largest urban parks in the world along with Richmond Park in London.
title: Moscow

text: Its area of 15.34 square kilometres (5.92 sq mi) is six times greater than that of Central Park in New York.
title: Moscow

text: Bauman Garden, officially founded in 1920 and renamed in 1922 after the bolshevik Nikolay Bauman, is one of the oldest parks in Moscow.
title: Moscow

text: It is standing on the site of the former Golitsyn estate and eighteenth-century public garden.
title: Moscow

text: Sokolniki Park, named after the falcon hunting that occurred there in the past, is one of the oldest parks in Moscow and has an area of 6 square kilometres (2.3 sq mi).
title: Moscow

text: A central circle with a large fountain is surrounded by birch, maple, and elm tree alleys.
title: Moscow

text: A labyrinth composed of green paths lies beyond the park's ponds.
title: Moscow

text: Losiny Ostrov National Park ("Elk Island" National Park), with a total area of more than 116 square kilometres (45 sq mi), borders Sokolniki Park and was Russia's first national park.
title: Moscow

text: It is quite wild, and is also known as the "city taiga" – elk can be seen there.
title: Moscow

text: Tsytsin Main Botanical Garden of Academy of Sciences, founded in 1945 is the largest in Europe.
title: Moscow

text: It contains a rosarium with 20 thousand rose bushes, a dendrarium, and an oak forest, with the average age of trees exceeding 100 years.
title: Moscow

text: There is a greenhouse taking up more than 5,000 square metres (53,820 square feet) of land.
title: Moscow

text: Among the large spans of a recreational park, areas are scores of elaborate pavilions, each representing either a branch of Soviet industry and science or a USSR republic.
title: Moscow

text: In 2014 the park returned to the name Exhibition of Achievements of National Economy, and in the same year, huge renovation works had been started.
title: Moscow

text: Lilac Park, founded in 1958, has a permanent sculpture display and a large rosarium.
title: Moscow

text: Moscow has always been a popular destination for tourists.
title: Moscow

text: Some of the more famous attractions include the city's UNESCO World Heritage Site, Moscow Kremlin and Red Square, which was built between the 14th and 17th centuries.
title: Moscow

text: The Church of the Ascension at Kolomenskoye, which dates from 1532, is also a UNESCO World Heritage Site and another popular attraction.
title: Moscow

text: Near the new Tretyakov Gallery there is a sculpture garden, Museon, often called "the graveyard of fallen monuments" that displays statues of the former Soviet Union that were removed from their place after its dissolution.
title: Moscow

text: Other attractions include the Moscow Zoo, a zoological garden in two sections (the valleys of two streams) linked by a bridge, with nearly a thousand species and more than 6,500 specimens.
title: Moscow

text: Each year, the zoo attracts more than 1.2 million visitors.
title: Moscow

text: From there, roads generally span outwards to intersect with a sequence of circular roads ("rings").
title: Moscow

text: The first and innermost major ring, Bulvarnoye Koltso (Boulevard Ring), was built at the former location of the 16th-century city wall around what used to be called Bely Gorod (White Town).
title: Moscow

text: The Bulvarnoye Koltso is technically not a ring; it does not form a complete circle, but instead a horseshoe-shaped arc that begins at the Cathedral of Christ the Savior and ends at the Yauza River.
title: Moscow

text: The second primary ring, located outside the Boulevard Ring, is the Sadovoye Koltso (Garden Ring).
title: Moscow

text: Like the Boulevard Ring, the Garden Ring follows the path of a 16th-century wall that used to encompass part of Moscow.
title: Moscow

text: The Third Ring Road, was completed in 2003 as a high-speed freeway.
title: Moscow

text: The Fourth Transport Ring, another freeway, was planned, but cancelled in 2011.
title: Moscow

text: A system of chordal highways will replace it.
title: Moscow

text: Aside from the aforementioned hierarchy, line 5 of Moscow Metro is a circle-shaped looped subway line (hence the name Koltsevaya Liniya, literally "ring line"), which is located between the Sadovoye Koltso and Third Transport Ring.
title: Moscow

text: Two modern overlapping lines of Moscow Metro form "two hearts": Line 14.
title: Moscow

text: Since 10 September 2016, Moscow Central Circle renovated railroad (former Moskovskaya Okruzhnaya Zheleznaya Doroga) was introduced as Line 14 of Moscow Metro.
title: Moscow

text: The cone-shaped railroad initially opened in 1908 (freight-only railway from 1934 until the 2016 reopening).
title: Moscow

text: Another circle metro line - Big Circle Line (Bolshaya Koltsevaya Liniya) is under construction and will be finished in 2023.
title: Moscow

text: Kakhovskaya-Savyolovskaya western half of the line was launched in late 2021.
title: Moscow

text: The outermost ring within Moscow is the Moscow Ring Road (often called MKAD, acronym word for Russian Московская Кольцевая Автомобильная Дорога), which forms the cultural boundary of the city, and was established in the 1950s.
title: Moscow

text: It was built at ground level rather than elevated, so it forms a barrier to roads that would otherwise pass under it.
title: Moscow

text: Before 2012 expansion of Moscow, the MKAD was considered an approximate border for Moscow boundaries.
title: Moscow

text: Outside Moscow, some of the roads encompassing the city continue to follow this circular pattern seen inside city limits, with the notable examples of Betonka roads (highways A107 and A108), originally made of concrete pads.
title: Moscow

text: The Tretyakov Gallery is split into two buildings.
title: Moscow

text: The Old Tretyakov gallery, the original gallery in the Tretyakovskaya area on the south bank of the Moskva River, houses works in the classic Russian tradition.
title: Moscow

text: The works of famous pre-Revolutionary painters, such as Ilya Repin, as well as the works of early Russian icon painters can be found here.
title: Moscow

text: Visitors can even see rare originals by early 15th-century iconographer Andrei Rublev.
title: Moscow

text: The New Tretyakov gallery, created in Soviet times, mainly contains the works of Soviet artists, as well as of a few contemporary paintings, but there is some overlap with the Old Tretyakov Gallery for early 20th-century art.
title: Moscow

text: The new gallery includes a small reconstruction of Vladimir Tatlin's famous Monument to the Third International and a mixture of other avant-garde works by artists like Kazimir Malevich and Wassily Kandinsky.
title: Moscow

text: Socialist realism features can also be found within the halls of the New Tretyakov Gallery.
title: Moscow

text: Another art museum in the city of Moscow is the Pushkin Museum of Fine Arts, which was founded by, among others, the father of Marina Tsvetaeva.
title: Moscow

text: The Pushkin Museum is similar to the British Museum in London in that its halls are a cross-section of exhibits on world civilisations, with many copies of ancient sculptures.
title: Moscow

text: However, it also hosts paintings from every major Western era; works by Claude Monet, Paul Cézanne, and Pablo Picasso are present in the museum's collection.
title: Moscow

text: The State Historical Museum of Russia (Государственный Исторический музей) is a museum of Russian history located between Red Square and Manege Square in Moscow.
title: Moscow

text: Its exhibitions range from relics of the prehistoric tribes inhabiting present-day Russia, through priceless artworks acquired by members of the Romanov dynasty.
title: Moscow

text: The total number of objects in the museum's collection numbers is several million.
title: Moscow

text: The Polytechnical Museum, founded in 1872 is the largest technical museum in Russia, offering a wide array of historical inventions and technological achievements, including humanoid automata from the 18th century and the first Soviet computers.
title: Moscow

text: The Borodino Panorama museum located on Kutuzov Avenue provides an opportunity for visitors to experience being on a battlefield with a 360° diorama.
title: Moscow

text: It is a part of the large historical memorial commemorating the victory in the Patriotic War of 1812 over Napoleon's army, that includes also the triumphal arch, erected in 1827.
title: Moscow

text: There is also a military history museum that includes statues, and military hardware.
title: Moscow

text: Memorial Museum of Cosmonautics under the Monument to the Conquerors of Space at the end of Cosmonauts Alley is the central memorial place for the Russian space officials.
title: Moscow

text: The Shchusev State Museum of Architecture is the national museum of Russian architecture by the name of the architect Alexey Shchusev near the Kremlin area.
title: Moscow

text: Among Moscow's theaters and ballet studios is the Bolshoi Theatre and the Malyi Theatre as well as Vakhtangov Theatre and Moscow Art Theatre.
title: Moscow

text: The Moscow International Performance Arts Center, opened in 2003, also known as Moscow International House of Music, is known for its performances in classical music.
title: Moscow

text: It has the largest organ in Russia installed in Svetlanov Hall.
title: Moscow

text: There are also two large circuses in Moscow: Moscow State Circus and Moscow Circus on Tsvetnoy Boulevard named after Yuri Nikulin.
title: Moscow

text: The Mosfilm studio was at the heart of many classic films, as it is responsible for both artistic and mainstream productions.
title: Moscow

text: However, despite the continued presence and reputation of internationally renowned Russian filmmakers, the once prolific native studios are much quieter.
title: Moscow

text: Rare and historical films may be seen in the Salut cinema, where films from the Museum of Cinema collection are shown regularly.
title: Moscow

text: Forty other sports complexes are located within the city, including 24 with artificial ice.
title: Moscow

text: The Olympic Stadium was the world's first indoor arena for bandy and hosted the Bandy World Championship twice.
title: Moscow

text: Moscow was again the host of the competition in 2010, this time in Krylatskoye.
title: Moscow

text: That arena has also hosted the World Speed Skating Championships.
title: Moscow

text: There are also seven horse racing tracks in Moscow, of which Central Moscow Hippodrome, founded in 1834, is the largest.
title: Moscow

text: Moscow was the host city of the 1980 Summer Olympics, with the yachting events being held at Tallinn, in present-day Estonia.
title: Moscow

text: Large sports facilities and the main international airport, Sheremetyevo Terminal 2, were built in preparation for the 1980 Summer Olympics.
title: Moscow

text: Moscow had made a bid for the 2012 Summer Olympics.
title: Moscow

text: However, when final voting commenced on 6 July 2005, Moscow was the first city to be eliminated from further rounds.
title: Moscow

text: The most titled ice hockey team in the Soviet Union and in the world, HC CSKA Moscow comes from Moscow.
title: Moscow

text: Other big ice hockey clubs from Moscow are HC Dynamo Moscow, which was the second most titled team in the Soviet Union, and HC Spartak Moscow.
title: Moscow

text: The most titled Soviet, Russian, and one of the most titled Euroleague clubs, is the basketball club from Moscow PBC CSKA Moscow.
title: Moscow

text: Moscow hosted the EuroBasket in 1953 and 1965.
title: Moscow

text: Moscow had more winners at the USSR and Russian Chess Championship than any other city.
title: Moscow

text: The most titled volleyball team in the Soviet Union and in Europe (CEV Champions League) is VC CSKA Moscow.
title: Moscow

text: In football, FC Spartak Moscow has won more championship titles in the Russian Premier League than any other team.
title: Moscow

text: They were second only to FC Dynamo Kyiv in Soviet times.
title: Moscow

text: PFC CSKA Moscow became the first Russian football team to win a UEFA title, the UEFA Cup (present-day UEFA Europa League).
title: Moscow

text: FC Lokomotiv Moscow, FC Dynamo Moscow and FC Torpedo Moscow are other professional football teams also based in Moscow.
title: Moscow

text: Moscow houses other prominent football, ice hockey, and basketball teams.
title: Moscow

text: Because sports organisations in the Soviet Union were once highly centralized, two of the best Union-level teams represented defence and law-enforcing agencies: the Armed Forces (CSKA) and the Ministry of Internal Affairs (Dinamo).
title: Moscow

text: There were army and police teams in most major cities.
title: Moscow

text: As a result, Spartak, CSKA, and Dinamo were among the best-funded teams in the USSR.
title: Moscow

text: The Irina Viner-Usmanova Gymnastics Palace is located in the Luzniki Olympic Complex.
title: Moscow

text: The building works started in 2017 and the opening ceremony took place on 18 June 2019.
title: Moscow

text: The investor of the Palace is the billionaire Alisher Usmanov, husband of the former gymnast and gymnastics coach Irina Viner-Usmanova.
title: Moscow

text: Because of Moscow's cold local climate, winter sports have a following.
title: Moscow

text: Many of Moscow's large parks offer marked trails for skiing and frozen ponds for skating.
title: Moscow

text: Moscow hosts the annual Kremlin Cup, a popular tennis tournament on both the WTA and ATP tours.
title: Moscow

text: It is one of the ten Tier-I events on the women's tour and a host of Russian players feature every year.
title: Moscow

text: SC Olimpiyskiy hosted the Eurovision Song Contest 2009, the first and so far the only Eurovision Song Contest arranged in Russia.
title: Moscow

text: Slava Moscow is a professional rugby club, competing in the national Professional Rugby League.
title: Moscow

text: Former rugby league heavyweights RC Lokomotiv have entered the same league as of 2011.
title: Moscow

text: The Luzhniki Stadium also hosted the 2013 Rugby World Cup Sevens.
title: Moscow

text: In bandy, one of the most successful clubs in the world is 20 times Russian League champions Dynamo Moscow.
title: Moscow

text: They have also won the World Cup thrice and European Cup six times.
title: Moscow

text: MFK Dinamo Moskva is one of the major futsal clubs in Europe, having won the Futsal Champions League title once.
title: Moscow

text: Tverskaya Street is also one of the busiest shopping streets in Moscow.
title: Moscow

text: The adjoining Tretyakovsky Proyezd, also south of Tverskaya Street, in Kitai-gorod, is host to upmarket boutique stores such as Bulgari, Tiffany & Co., Armani, Prada and Bentley.
title: Moscow

text: Nightlife in Moscow has moved on since Soviet times and today the city has many of the world's largest nightclubs.
title: Moscow

text: The hottest area is located around the old chocolate factory, where bars, nightclubs, galleries, cafés and restaurants are placed.
title: Moscow

text: Dream Island is an amusement park in Moscow that opened on 29 February 2020.
title: Moscow

text: It is the largest indoor theme park in Europe.
title: Moscow

text: The Mayor of Moscow is the leading official in the executive, leading the Government of Moscow, which is the highest organ of executive power.
title: Moscow

text: The Moscow City Duma is the city duma (city council or local parliament) and local laws must be approved by it.
title: Moscow

text: It includes 45 members who are elected for a five-year term on single-mandate constituency basis.
title: Moscow

text: From 2006 to 2012, direct elections of the mayor were not held due to changes in the Charter of the city of Moscow, with the mayor appointed by presidential decree.
title: Moscow

text: Local administration is carried out through eleven prefectures, uniting the districts of Moscow into administrative districts on a territorial basis, and 125 regional administrations.
title: Moscow

text: The supreme executive authority – the Government of the Russian Federation – is located in the House of the Government of the Russian Federation (the White House) on Krasnopresnenskaya Embankment in the center of Moscow.
title: Moscow

text: The Federation Council is located in a building on Bolshaya Dmitrovka.
title: Moscow

text: The Supreme Court of the Russian Federation is also located in Moscow.
title: Moscow

text: The Moscow Kremlin is the official residence of the President of the Russian Federation.
title: Moscow

text: The general level of crime is quite low.
title: Moscow

text: More than 170,000 surveillance cameras in Moscow are connected to the facial recognition system.
title: Moscow

text: The authorities recognized the successful two-month experiment with automatic recognition of faces, gender, and age of people in real-time – and deployed the system to the whole city.
title: Moscow

text: The city of Moscow is divided into twelve administrative okrugs and 125 districts.
title: Moscow

text: The Russian capital's town-planning development began to show as early as the 12th century when the city was founded.
title: Moscow

text: The central part of Moscow grew by consolidating with suburbs in line with medieval principles of urban development when strong fortress walls would gradually spread along the circle streets of adjacent new settlements.
title: Moscow

text: The first circular defence walls set the trajectory of Moscow's rings, laying the groundwork for the future planning of the Russian capital.
title: Moscow

text: The following fortifications served as the city's circular defense boundaries at some point in history: the Kremlin walls, Zemlyanoy Gorod (Earthwork Town), the Kamer-Kollezhsky Rampart, the Garden Ring, and the small railway ring.
title: Moscow

text: The Moscow Ring Road (MKAD) has been Moscow's boundary since 1960.
title: Moscow

text: Also in the form of a circle are the main Moscow subway line, the Ring Line, and the so-called Third Automobile Ring, which was completed in 2005.
title: Moscow

text: Hence, the characteristic radial-circle planning continues to define Moscow's further development.
title: Moscow

text: However, contemporary Moscow has also engulfed a number of territories outside the MKAD, such as Solntsevo, Butovo, and the town of Zelenograd.
title: Moscow

text: A part of Moscow Oblast's territory was merged into Moscow on 1 July 2012; as a result, Moscow is no longer fully surrounded by Moscow Oblast and now also has a border with Kaluga Oblast.
title: Moscow

text: In all, Moscow gained about 1,500 square kilometers (580 sq mi) and 230,000 inhabitants.
title: Moscow

text: Moscow's Mayor Sergey Sobyanin lauded the expansion that will help Moscow and the neighboring region, a "mega-city" of twenty million people, to develop "harmonically".
title: Moscow

text: All administrative okrugs and districts have their own coats of arms and flags as well as individual heads of the area.
title: Moscow

text: In addition to the districts, there are Territorial Units with Special Status.
title: Moscow

text: These usually include areas with small or no permanent populations.
title: Moscow

text: Such is the case with the All-Russia Exhibition Centre, the Botanical Garden, large parks, and industrial zones.
title: Moscow

text: In recent years, some territories have been merged with different districts.
title: Moscow

text: There are no ethnic-specific regions in Moscow, as in the Chinatowns that exist in some North American and East Asian cities.
title: Moscow

text: And although districts are not designated by income, as with most cities, those areas that are closer to the city center, metro stations or green zones are considered more prestigious.
title: Moscow

text: As of 2021, the GRP of Moscow reached almost ₽24.5 trillion(US$332 billion).
title: Moscow

text: GMP of Moscow Region was ₽31.3 trillion or around US$425 billion.
title: Moscow

text: The average gross monthly wage in the city is ₽123,688 (US$2,000), which is around twice the national average of ₽66,572 (US$1,000), and one of the highest among the federal subjects of Russia.
title: Moscow

text: Moscow is home to the third-highest number of billionaires of any city in the world, and has the highest number of billionaires of any city in Europe.
title: Moscow

text: It is the financial center of Russia and home to the country's largest banks and many of its largest companies, such as oil giant Rosneft.
title: Moscow

text: Moscow accounts for 17% of retail sales in Russia and for 13% of all construction activity in the country.
title: Moscow

text: Since the 1998 Russian financial crisis, business sectors in Moscow have shown exponential rates of growth.
title: Moscow

text: Many new business centers and office buildings have been built in recent years, but Moscow still experiences shortages in office space.
title: Moscow

text: As a result, many former industrial and research facilities are being reconstructed to become suitable for office use.
title: Moscow

text: The Mil Moscow Helicopter Plant is a manufacturer of military and civil helicopters.
title: Moscow

text: Khrunichev State Research and Production Space Center produces various space equipment, including modules for space stations Mir, Salyut and the ISS as well as Proton launch vehicles and military ICBMs.
title: Moscow

text: Sukhoi, Ilyushin, Mikoyan, Tupolev and Yakovlev aircraft design bureaus also situated in Moscow.
title: Moscow

text: Automobile plants ZiL and AZLK, as well as the Voitovich Rail Vehicle plant, are situated in Moscow and Metrovagonmash metro wagon plant is located just outside the city limits.
title: Moscow

text: The Poljot Moscow watch factory produces military, professional and sport watches well known in Russia and abroad.
title: Moscow

text: The Electrozavod factory was the first transformer factory in Russia.
title: Moscow

text: The Kristall distillery is the oldest distillery in Russia producing vodka types, including "Stolichnaya" while wines are produced at Moscow wine plants, including the Moscow Interrepublican Vinery.
title: Moscow

text: The Moscow Jewelry Factory and the Jewellerprom are producers of jewelry in Russia.
title: Moscow

text: There are other industries located just outside the city of Moscow, as well as microelectronic industries in Zelenograd, including Ruselectronics companies.
title: Moscow

text: Gazprom, the largest extractor of natural gas in the world and the largest Russian company, has head offices also in Moscow, as well as other oil, gas, and electricity companies.
title: Moscow

text: Moscow hosts headquarters of the many of telecommunications and technology companies, including 1C, ABBYY, Beeline, Kaspersky Lab, Mail.Ru Group, MegaFon, MTS, Rambler&Co, Rostelecom, Yandex, and Yota.
title: Moscow

text: Private ownership of apartments was limited until the 1990s when people were permitted to secure property rights to their inhabited places.
title: Moscow

text: Since the Soviet era, estate owners have had to pay the service charge for their residences, a fixed amount based on persons per living area.
title: Moscow

text: The price of real estate in Moscow continues to rise.
title: Moscow

text: Today, one could expect to pay $4,000 on average per square meter (11 sq ft) on the outskirts of the city or US$6,500–$8,000 per square meter in a prestigious district.
title: Moscow

text: The price sometimes may exceed US$40,000 per square meter in a flat.
title: Moscow

text: It costs about US$1,200 per month to rent a one-bedroom apartment and about US$1,000 per month for a studio in the center of Moscow.
title: Moscow

text: A typical one-bedroom apartment is about thirty square metres (320 square feet), a typical two-bedroom apartment is forty-five square metres (480 square feet), and a typical three-bedroom apartment is seventy square metres (750 square feet).
title: Moscow

text: Many cannot move out of their apartments, especially if a family lives in a two-room apartment originally granted by the state during the Soviet era.
title: Moscow

text: Some city residents have attempted to cope with the cost of living by renting their apartments while staying in dachas (country houses) outside the city.
title: Moscow

text: In 2006, Mercer Human Resources Consulting named Moscow the world's most expensive city for expatriate employees, ahead of perennial winner Tokyo, due to the stable Russian ruble as well as increasing housing prices within the city.
title: Moscow

text: Moscow also ranked first in the 2007 edition and 2008 edition of the survey.
title: Moscow

text: However, Tokyo has overtaken Moscow as the most expensive city in the world, placing Moscow at third behind Osaka in second place.
title: Moscow

text: In 2008, Moscow ranked top on the list of most expensive cities for the third year in a row.
title: Moscow

text: In 2014, according to Forbes, Moscow was ranked the 9th most expensive city in the world.
title: Moscow

text: Forbes ranked Moscow the 2nd most expensive city the year prior.
title: Moscow

text: In 2019 the Economist Intelligence Unit's Worldwide Cost of Living survey put Moscow to 102nd place in the biannual ranking of 133 most expensive cities.
title: Moscow

text: Clients were divided between the various enterprises based on their geographical location.
title: Moscow

text: A major reform launched in 2004 consolidated the various companies under the umbrella of MIPC which became the municipal heat supplier.
title: Moscow

text: Its subsidiaries were the newly transformed Joint-stock companies.
title: Moscow

text: The city's main source of heating is the power station of Mosenergo which was reformed in 2005, when around ten subsidiaries were separated from it.
title: Moscow

text: One of the newly independent companies was the District Heating Network Company (MTK) (Russian: Московская теплосетевая компания).
title: Moscow

text: The portal is being developed by the State Public Institution "New Management Technologies" together with the Moscow Department of Information Technologies.
title: Moscow

text: Besides these, there are 222 institutions of higher education, including 60 state universities and the Lomonosov Moscow State University, which was founded in 1755.
title: Moscow

text: The main university building located in Vorobyovy Gory (Sparrow Hills) is 240 metres (790 ft) tall and when completed, was the tallest building on the continent.
title: Moscow

text: The university has over 30,000 undergraduate and 7,000 postgraduate students, who have a choice of twenty-nine faculties and 450 departments for study.
title: Moscow

text: The Moscow State University library contains over nine million books, making it one of the largest libraries in all of Russia.
title: Moscow

text: Sechenov First Moscow State Medical University named after Ivan Sechenov or formerly known as Moscow Medical Academy (1stMSMU) is a medical university situated in Moscow, Russia.
title: Moscow

text: It was founded in 1785 as the faculty of the Moscow State University.
title: Moscow

text: It is a Russian Federal Agency for Health and Social Development.
title: Moscow

text: It is one of the largest medical universities in Russia and Europe.
title: Moscow

text: More than 9200 students are enrolled in 115 academic departments.
title: Moscow

text: The Pirogov Russian National Research Medical University (formerly known as Russian State Medical University) is a medical higher education institution in Moscow, Russia founded in 1906.
title: Moscow

text: It is fully accredited and recognized by Russia's Ministry of Education and Science and is currently under the authority of the Ministry of Health and Social Development.
title: Moscow

text: Pirogov (1810–1888), it is one of the largest medical institutions and the first university in Russia to allow women to acquire degrees.
title: Moscow

text: Moscow is one of the financial centers of the Russian Federation and CIS countries and is known for its business schools.
title: Moscow

text: Among them are the Financial University under the Government of the Russian Federation; Plekhanov Russian University of Economics; The State University of Management, and the National Research University - Higher School of Economics.
title: Moscow

text: They offer undergraduate degrees in management, finance, accounting, marketing, real estate, and economic theory, as well as Masters programs and MBAs.
title: Moscow

text: Most of them have branches in other regions of Russia and countries around the world.

text: Saint Petersburg, formerly known as Petrograd (Петроград) and later Leningrad (Ленинград), is the second-largest city in Russia, after Moscow, the nation's capital.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Situated on the Neva River at the head of the Gulf of Finland on the Baltic Sea, its area of 1,439 square kilometers (556 sq mi) renders it the smallest administrative division of Russia by area.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The city had a population of 5,601,911 residents as of 2021, with more than 6.4 million people living in the metropolitan area.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Saint Petersburg is the fourth-most populous city in Europe, the most populous city on the Baltic Sea, and the world's northernmost city of more than 1 million residents.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: As the former capital of the Russian Empire, and a historically strategic Baltic port, it is governed as a federal city.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The city was founded by Tsar Peter the Great on 27 May 1703 on the site of a captured Swedish fortress, and was named after the apostle Saint Peter.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: In Russia, Saint Petersburg is historically and culturally associated with the birth of the Russian Empire and Russia's entry into modern history as a European great power.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: It served as a capital of the Tsardom of Russia, and the subsequent Russian Empire, from 1712 to 1918 (being replaced by Moscow for a short period between 1728 and 1730).
title: Saint Petersburg

text: After the October Revolution in 1917, the Bolsheviks moved their government to Moscow.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The city was renamed Leningrad after Lenin's death in 1924.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: It was the site of the siege of Leningrad during World War II, the most lethal siege in history.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: In June 1991, only a few months before the Belovezha Accords and the dissolution of the Soviet Union, voters in a city-wide referendum supported restoring the city's original name.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: As Russia's cultural centre, Saint Petersburg received over 15 million tourists in 2018.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: It is considered an important economic, scientific, and tourism centre of Russia and Europe.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: In modern times, the city has been nicknamed "the Northern Capital of Russia" and is home to notable federal government bodies such as the Constitutional Court of Russia and the Heraldic Council of the President of the Russian Federation.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The Historic Centre of Saint Petersburg and Related Groups of Monuments constitute a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The name was later normalized and russified to Санкт-Петербург (Sankt-Peterburg).
title: Saint Petersburg

text: A former spelling of the city's name in English was Saint Petersburgh.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: This spelling survives in the name of a street in the Bayswater district of London, near St Sophia's Cathedral, named after a visit by the Tsar to London in 1814.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: A 14-to-15-letter-long name, composed of the three roots, proved too cumbersome, and many shortened versions were used.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The first General Governor of the city Menshikov is maybe also the author of the first nickname of Petersburg, which he called Петри (Petri).
title: Saint Petersburg

text: It took some years until the known Russian spelling of this name finally settled.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: In 1740s Mikhail Lomonosov uses a derivative of Greek: Πετρόπολις (Петрополис, Petropolis) in a Russified form Petropol' (Петрополь).
title: Saint Petersburg

text: A combo Piterpol (Питерпол) also appears at this time.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: In any case, eventually the usage of the prefix "Sankt-" ceased except for formal official documents, where a three-letter abbreviation "СПб" (SPb) was very widely used as well.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: In the early 19th century a slavicised variant appeared: Petrograd (Russian: Петроград, IPA: [pʲɪtrɐˈgrat]), used also by Alexander Pushkin.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: However, it was only on 31 August [O.S. 18 August] 1914, after the war with Germany had begun, that Tsar Nicholas II renamed the city Petrograd to expunge the German words Sankt and Burg.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Since the prefix "Saint" was omitted, this act also changed the eponym and the "patron" of the city from Saint Peter to Peter the Great, its founder.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: On 26 January 1924, shortly after the death of Vladimir Lenin, it was renamed to Leningrad (Russian: Ленинград, IPA: [lʲɪnʲɪnˈgrat]), meaning 'Lenin City'.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: On 6 September 1991, the original name, Sankt-Peterburg, was returned by a citywide referendum.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Today, in English, the city is known as Saint Petersburg.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Residents often refer to the city by its shortened nickname, Piter (Russian: Питер, IPA: [ˈpʲitʲɪr]).
title: Saint Petersburg

text: After the October Revolution, the name Red Petrograd (Красный Петроград, Krasny Petrograd) was often used in newspapers and other prints until the city was renamed Leningrad in January 1924.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The referendum on restoring the historic name was held on 12 June 1991, with 55% of voters supporting "Saint Petersburg" and 43% supporting "Leningrad".
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Renaming the city Petrograd was not an option.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: This change officially took effect on 6 September 1991.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Meanwhile, the oblast which surrounds Saint Petersburg is still named Leningrad.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Having passed the role of capital to Petersburg, Moscow never relinquished the title of "capital", being called pervoprestolnaya ('first throned') for 200 years.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: An equivalent name for Petersburg, the "Northern Capital", has re-entered usage today since several federal institutions were recently moved from Moscow to Saint Petersburg.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Solemn descriptive names like "the city of three revolutions" and "the cradle of the October Revolution" used in the Soviet era are reminders of the pivotal events in national history that occurred here.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Saint Petersburg has been traditionally called the "Window to Europe" and the "Window to the West" by the Russians.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Furthermore, it has strongly Western European-inspired architecture and culture, which is combined with the city's Russian heritage.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The small town of Nyen grew up around the fort.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Before the 17th century, this area was inhabited by Finnic Izhorians and Votians.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The Ingrian Finns moved to the region from the provinces of Karelia and Savonia during the Swedish rule.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: There was also some Estonian, Karelian, Russian and German population in the area.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: At the end of the 17th century, Peter the Great, who was interested in seafaring and maritime affairs, wanted Russia to gain a seaport to trade with the rest of Europe.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: He needed a better seaport than the country's main one at the time, Arkhangelsk, which was on the White Sea in the far north and closed to shipping during the winter.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: On 12 May [O.S. 1 May] 1703, during the Great Northern War, Peter captured Nyenskans and soon replaced the fortress.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The city was built by conscripted peasants (serfs) from all over Russia; in some years several Swedish prisoners of war were also involved under the supervision of Alexander Menshikov.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Tens of thousands of serfs died while building the city.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Later, the city became the centre of the Saint Petersburg Governorate.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Peter moved the capital from Moscow to Saint Petersburg in 1712, nine years before the Treaty of Nystad of 1721 ended the war.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: He referred to Saint Petersburg as the capital (or seat of government) as early as 1704.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: During its first few years, the city developed around Trinity Square on the right bank of the Neva, near the Peter and Paul Fortress.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: However, Saint Petersburg soon started to be built out according to a plan.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: By 1716, the Swiss–Italian Domenico Trezzini had elaborated a project whereby the city centre would be on Vasilyevsky Island and shaped by a rectangular grid of canals.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The project was not completed but is evident in the layout of the streets.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: In 1716, Peter the Great appointed Frenchman Jean-Baptiste Alexandre Le Blond as the chief architect of Saint Petersburg.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The style of Petrine Baroque, developed by Trezzini and other architects and exemplified by such buildings as the Menshikov Palace, Kunstkamera, Peter and Paul Cathedral, and Twelve Collegia, became prominent in the city architecture of the early 18th century.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: In 1724, the Academy of Sciences, the University, and the Academic Gymnasium were established in Saint Petersburg by Peter the Great.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: His endeavors to modernise Russia had been opposed by the Russian nobility.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: There were several attempts on his life and a treason case involving his son.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: In 1728, Peter II of Russia moved his seat back to Moscow.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: But four years later, in 1732, under Empress Anna of Russia, Saint Petersburg was again designated as the capital of the Russian Empire.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: It remained the seat of the Romanov dynasty and the Imperial Court of the Russian tsars, as well as the seat of the Russian government, for another 186 years until the communist revolution of 1917.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: In 1736–1737, the city suffered from catastrophic fires.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: To rebuild the damaged boroughs, a committee under Burkhard Christoph von Münnich commissioned a new plan in 1737.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The city was divided into five boroughs, and the city centre was moved to the Admiralty borough, on the east bank between the Neva and Fontanka.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: It developed along three radial streets, which meet at the Admiralty building and are now known as Nevsky Prospect (which is considered the main street of the city), Gorokhovaya Street, and Voznesensky Prospekt.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Baroque architecture became dominant in the city during the first sixty years, culminating in the Elizabethan Baroque, represented most notably by Italian Bartolomeo Rastrelli with such buildings as the Winter Palace.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: In the 1760s, Baroque architecture was succeeded by neoclassical architecture.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Established in 1762, the Commission of Stone Buildings of Moscow and Saint Petersburg ruled that no structure in the city could be higher than the Winter Palace and prohibited spacing between buildings.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: During the reign of Catherine the Great in the 1760s–1780s, the banks of the Neva were lined with granite embankments.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: However, it was not until 1850 that the first permanent bridge across the Neva, Annunciation Bridge, was allowed to open.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Obvodny Canal (dug in 1769–1833) became the southern limit of the city.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The most prominent neoclassical and Empire-style architects in Saint Petersburg included: Jean-Baptiste Vallin de la Mothe (Imperial Academy of Arts, Small Hermitage, Gostiny Dvor, New Holland Arch, Catholic Church of St.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Many monuments commemorate the Russian victory over Napoleonic France in the Patriotic War of 1812, including the Alexander Column by Montferrand, erected in 1834, and the Narva Triumphal Arch.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: In 1825, the suppressed Decembrist revolt against Nicholas I took place on the Senate Square in the city, a day after Nicholas assumed the throne.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: By the 1840s, neoclassical architecture had given way to various romanticist styles, which dominated until the 1890s, represented by such architects as Andrei Stackenschneider (Mariinsky Palace, Beloselsky-Belozersky Palace, Nicholas Palace, New Michael Palace) and Konstantin Thon (Moskovsky railway station).
title: Saint Petersburg

text: With the emancipation of the serfs undertaken by Alexander II in 1861 and an Industrial Revolution, the influx of former peasants into the capital increased greatly.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Poor boroughs spontaneously developed on the outskirts of the city.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Saint Petersburg surpassed Moscow in population and industrial growth; it became one of the largest industrial cities in Europe, with a major naval base (in Kronstadt), the Neva River, and a seaport on the Baltic.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The third emperor's assassination took place in Saint Petersburg in 1881 when Alexander II was murdered by terrorists (see the Church of the Savior on Spilled Blood).
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The Revolution of 1905 began in Saint Petersburg and spread rapidly into the provinces.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: After that the city acquired a new descriptive name, "the city of three revolutions", referring to the three major developments in the political history of Russia of the early 20th century.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: In September and October 1917, in Operation Albion, German troops invaded the West Estonian archipelago and threatened Petrograd with bombardment and invasion.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: On 12 March 1918, Lenin transferred the government of Soviet Russia to Moscow, to keep it away from the state border.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: During the Russian Civil War, in mid-1919, Russian anti-communist forces with the help of Estonians attempted to capture the city, but Leon Trotsky mobilized the army and forced them to retreat to Estonia.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: On 26 January 1924, five days after Lenin's death, Petrograd was renamed Leningrad.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Later many streets and other toponyms were renamed accordingly, with names in honour of communist figures replacing historic names given centuries before.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The city has over 230 places associated with the life and activities of Lenin.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Some of them were turned into museums, including the cruiser Aurora– a symbol of the October Revolution and the oldest ship in the Russian Navy.In the 1920s and 1930s, the poor outskirts were reconstructed into regularly planned boroughs.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Housing became a government-provided amenity; many "bourgeois" apartments were so large that numerous families were assigned to what were called "communal" apartments (kommunalkas).
title: Saint Petersburg

text: By the 1930s, 68% of the population lived in such housing under very poor conditions.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: In 1935, a new general plan was outlined, whereby the city should expand to the south.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Constructivism was rejected in favour of a more pompous Stalinist architecture.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Moving the city centre further from the border with Finland, Stalin adopted a plan to build a new city hall with a huge adjacent square at the southern end of Moskovsky Prospekt, designated as the new main street of Leningrad.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: After the Winter (Soviet-Finnish) war in 1939–1940, the Soviet–Finnish border moved northwards.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Nevsky Prospekt with Palace Square maintained the functions and the role of a city centre.

text: In December 1931, Leningrad was administratively separated from Leningrad Oblast.

title: Saint Petersburg

text: During the Soviet era, many historic architectural monuments of the previous centuries were destroyed by the new regime for ideological reasons.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: While that mainly concerned churches and cathedrals, some other buildings were also demolished.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: On 1 December 1934, Sergei Kirov, the Bolshevik leader of Leningrad, was assassinated under suspicious circumstances, which became the pretext for the Great Purge.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The siege lasted 872 days, or almost two and a half years, from 8 September 1941 to 27 January 1944.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The Siege of Leningrad proved one of the longest, most destructive, and most lethal sieges of a major city in modern history.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: It isolated the city from food supplies except those provided through the Road of Life across Lake Ladoga, which could not make it through until the lake froze.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: More than one million civilians were killed, mainly from starvation.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: There were incidents of cannibalism, with around 2,000 residents arrested for eating other people.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Many others escaped or were evacuated, so the city became largely depopulated.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: On 1 May 1945, Joseph Stalin, in his Supreme Commander Order No. 20, named Leningrad, alongside Stalingrad, Sevastopol, and Odesa, hero cities of the war.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: A law acknowledging the honorary title of "Hero City" passed on 8 May 1965 (the 20th anniversary of the victory in the Great Patriotic War), during the Brezhnev era.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The Presidium of the Supreme Soviet of the Soviet Union awarded Leningrad as a Hero City the Order of Lenin and the Gold Star medal "for the heroic resistance of the city and tenacity of the survivors of the Siege".

text: These included the town of Terijoki (renamed Zelenogorsk in 1948).
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Leningrad and many of its suburbs were rebuilt over the post-war decades, partially according to pre-war plans.

text: The 1948 general plan for Leningrad featured radial urban development in the north as well as in the south.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: In 1953, Pavlovsky District in Leningrad Oblast was abolished, and parts of its territory, including Pavlovsk, merged with Leningrad.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: In 1954, the settlements Levashovo, Pargolovo and Pesochny merged with Leningrad.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Leningrad gave its name to the Leningrad affair (1949–1952), a notable event in the postwar political struggle in the USSR.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: It was a product of rivalry between Stalin's potential successors where one side was represented by the leaders of the city Communist Party organization – the second most significant one in the country after Moscow.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The entire elite leadership of Leningrad was destroyed, including the former mayor Kuznetsov, the acting mayor Pyotr Sergeevich Popkov, and all their deputies; overall, 23 leaders were sentenced to the death penalty, 181 to prison or exile (rehabilitated in 1954).
title: Saint Petersburg

text: About 2,000 ranking officials across the USSR were expelled from the party and the Komsomol and removed from leadership positions.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The Leningrad Metro underground rapid transit system, designed before the war, opened in 1955 with its first eight stations decorated with marble and bronze.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: However, after Stalin died in 1953, the perceived ornamental excesses of Stalinist architecture were abandoned.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Meanwhile, economic conditions started to deteriorate as the country's people tried to adapt to major changes.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: For the first time since the 1940s, food rationing was introduced, and the city received humanitarian food aid from abroad.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: This dramatic time was depicted in photographic series of Russian photographer Alexey Titarenko.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Economic conditions began to improve only at the beginning of the 21st century.

text: In 1995, a northern section of the Kirovsko-Vyborgskaya Line of the Saint Petersburg Metro was cut off by underground flooding, creating a major obstacle to the city development for almost ten years.

text: On 13 June 1996, Saint Petersburg, alongside Leningrad Oblast and Tver Oblast, signed a power-sharing agreement with the federal government, granting it autonomy.

text: The distinct moderating influence of Baltic Sea cyclones results in mild to hot, humid, short summers and long, moderately cold, wet winters.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The climate of Saint Petersburg is close to that of Helsinki, although slightly more continental (i.e., colder in winter and warmer in summer) because of its more eastern location, while slightly less continental than that of Moscow.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The average high temperature in July is 23 °C (73 °F), and the average low temperature in February is −8.5 °C (16.7 °F); an extreme temperature of 37.1 °C (98.8 °F) occurred during the 2010 Northern Hemisphere summer heat wave.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: A winter low of −35.9 °C (−32.6 °F) was recorded in 1883.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The average annual temperature is 5.8 °C (42.4 °F).

title: Saint Petersburg

text: The frost-free period in the city lasts on average for about 135 days.

text: Petersburg's northern location, its winters are warmer than Moscow's due to the Gulf of Finland and some Gulf Stream influence from Scandinavian winds that can bring temperatures slightly above freezing.

text: The city also has a slightly warmer climate than its suburbs due to the urban heat island effect.

text: Weather conditions are quite variable all year round.

text: SKIF focuses on experimental pop and avant-garde music, Electro-Mechanica on electronic music, and Ethnomechanica on world music.

title: Saint Petersburg

text: The rave band Little Big also hails from Saint Petersburg.

text: Dostoyevsky called it "The most abstract and intentional city in the world", emphasizing its artificiality, but it was also a symbol of modern disorder in a changing Russia.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: It often appeared to Russian writers as a menacing and inhuman mechanism.

text: According to Lotman in his chapter, 'The Symbolism of Saint Petersburg' in Universe and the Mind, these writers were inspired by symbolism from within the city itself.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: The effect of life in Saint Petersburg on the plight of the poor clerk in a society obsessed with hierarchy and status also became an important theme for authors such as Pushkin, Gogol, and Dostoyevsky.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Anna Akhmatova became an important leader for Russian poetry.

text: Her poem Requiem adumbrates the perils encountered during the Stalinist era.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Another notable 20th-century writer from Saint Petersburg is Joseph Brodsky, recipient of the 1987 Nobel Prize in Literature.
title: Saint Petersburg

title: Saint Petersburg

title: Saint Petersburg

title: Saint Petersburg

title: Saint Petersburg

text: Other movies include GoldenEye (1995), Midnight in Saint Petersburg (1996), Brother (1997), and Dhaam Dhoom (2008).
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Onegin (1999) is based on the Pushkin poem and showcases many tourist attractions.

text: In addition, the Russian romantic comedy, Piter FM, intricately showcases the cityscape, almost as if it were a main character in the film.

text: The largest of the public higher education institutions is Saint Petersburg State University, enrolling approximately 32,000 undergraduate students; and the largest non-governmental higher education institutions is the Institute of International Economic Relations, Economics, and Law.

title: Saint Petersburg

text: Petersburg River Yacht Club and the Neva Yacht Club; the latter is the oldest yacht club in the world.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Several historic arenas were built for equestrianism since the 18th century to maintain training all year round, such as the Zimny Stadion and Konnogvardeisky Manege.

text: Legends of the club include Alexander Belov and Vladimir Kondrashin.

text: The first Russian railway was built here in 1837, and since then, the city's transport infrastructure has kept pace with the city's growth.

text: The city is connected to the rest of Russia and the wider world by several federal highways and national and international rail routes.

text: In the 1980s, the city had the largest tram network globally.

title: Saint Petersburg

text: Buses carry up to 3 million passengers daily, with over 250 urban and suburban bus routes.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Saint Petersburg Metro was opened in 1955; it now has 6 lines with 75 stations, connecting all five railway terminals, and carrying 2.3 million passengers daily.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Many stations are elaborately decorated with materials such as marble and bronze.

text: There are plans for extending several lines and building one new depot.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: Saint Petersburg is an important transport corridor linking Scandinavia to Russia and Eastern Europe.

text: It is a terminus of both the Volga–Baltic and White Sea–Baltic waterways.
title: Saint Petersburg

text: In the warmer months, many smaller boats and water taxis navigate the city's canals.

text: Saint Petersburg has international railway connections to Helsinki, Finland; Berlin, Germany; and many former republics of the USSR.

text: Sevastopol ( SEV-ə-STOH-pəl, sə-VAS-tə-pohl), sometimes written Sebastopol, is the largest city in Crimea and a major port on the Black Sea.
title: Sevastopol

text: Due to its strategic location and the navigability of the city's harbours, Sevastopol has been an important port and naval base throughout its history.
title: Sevastopol

text: Since the city's founding in 1783, it has been a major base for Russia's Black Sea Fleet.
title: Sevastopol

text: During the Cold War of the 20th century, it was a closed city.
title: Sevastopol

text: The total administrative area is 864 square kilometres (334 sq mi) and includes a significant amount of rural land.
title: Sevastopol

text: The urban population, largely concentrated around Sevastopol Bay, is 479,394, and the total population is 547,820.
title: Sevastopol

text: Sevastopol, along with the rest of Crimea, is internationally recognised as part of Ukraine, and under the Ukrainian legal framework, it is administratively one of two cities with special status (the other being Kyiv).
title: Sevastopol

text: However, it has been occupied by Russia since 27 February 2014, before Russia annexed Crimea on 18 March 2014 and gave it the status of a federal city of Russia.
title: Sevastopol

text: Both Ukraine and Russia consider the city administratively separate from the Autonomous Republic of Crimea and the Republic of Crimea, respectively.
title: Sevastopol

text: The city's population has an ethnic Russian majority and a substantial minority of Ukrainians and Crimean Tatars.
title: Sevastopol

text: Sevastopol's unique naval and maritime features have been the basis for a robust economy.
title: Sevastopol

text: The city enjoys mild winters and moderately warm summers, characteristics that help make it a popular seaside resort and tourist destination, mainly for visitors from the former Soviet republics.
title: Sevastopol

text: The city is also an important centre for marine biology research.
title: Sevastopol

text: The city was probably named after Empress ("Augusta") Catherine II of the Russian Empire who founded Sevastopol in 1783.
title: Sevastopol

text: She visited the city in 1787, accompanied by Joseph II, the Emperor of Austria, and other foreign dignitaries.
title: Sevastopol

text: In the west of the city, there are well-preserved ruins of the ancient Greek port city of Chersonesos, founded in the 5th century BC by settlers from Heraclea Pontica.
title: Sevastopol

text: This name means "peninsula", reflecting its immediate location.
title: Sevastopol

text: It is not related to the ancient Greek name for the Crimean Peninsula as a whole: Chersonēsos Taurikē ("the Taurian Peninsula").
title: Sevastopol

text: The name of the city is spelled as: English: Sevastopol, the current prevalent spelling; the previously common spelling Sebastopol was used because the letter 'V' in the Cyrillic spelling, "Севасто́поль", looks like a Western letter 'B'.
title: Sevastopol

text: This form is still used by some publications, and formerly by The Economist.
title: Sevastopol

text: The current spelling has the pronunciation , while the former spelling has the pronunciation .
title: Sevastopol

text: Ukrainian: Севасто́поль, pronounced [sewɐˈstɔpolʲ], or Sivastopol; Russian: Севасто́поль, pronounced [sʲɪvɐˈstopəlʲ].
title: Sevastopol

text: The Greek city of Chersonesus existed for almost two thousand years, first as an independent democracy and later as part of the Bosporan Kingdom.
title: Sevastopol

text: In the 13th and 14th centuries, it was sacked by the Golden Horde several times and was finally totally abandoned.
title: Sevastopol

text: Five years earlier, Alexander Suvorov had ordered that earthworks be erected along the harbour and Russian troops be placed there.
title: Sevastopol

text: The Crimean Tatar version of this name is now written Aqyar.
title: Sevastopol

text: In February 1784, Catherine the Great ordered Grigory Potyomkin to build a fortress there and call it Sevastopol.
title: Sevastopol

text: The realisation of the initial building plans fell to Captain Fyodor Ushakov who in 1788 was named commander of the port and of the Black Sea squadron.
title: Sevastopol

text: The city was established on the western shore of Southern Bay which branches away from the bigger Sevastopol Bay.
title: Sevastopol

text: The ruins of ancient Chersonesus were situated to the west.
title: Sevastopol

text: The newly built settlement became an important naval base and later a commercial seaport.
title: Sevastopol

text: In 1797, under an edict issued by Emperor Paul I, the military stronghold was again renamed Akhtiar.
title: Sevastopol

text: Finally, on 29 April (10 May), 1826, the Senate returned the city's name to Sevastopol.
title: Sevastopol

text: In 1803 to 1864 along with Mykolaiv the city was part of Nikolayev–Sevastopol Military Governorate.
title: Sevastopol

text: After a minor skirmish at Köstence (now Constanța), the allied commanders decided to attack Sevastopol as Russia's main naval base in the Black Sea.
title: Sevastopol

text: After extended preparations, allied forces landed on the peninsula in September 1854 and marched to a point south of Sevastopol after winning the Battle of the Alma on 20 September.
title: Sevastopol

text: The Russians counterattacked on 25 October in what became the Battle of Balaclava and were repulsed, but the British Army's forces were seriously depleted as a result.
title: Sevastopol

text: A second Russian counterattack, at Inkerman in November, ended in a stalemate as well.
title: Sevastopol

text: The front settled into the siege of Sevastopol, involving brutal conditions for troops on both sides.
title: Sevastopol

text: Sevastopol finally fell after eleven months, after the French had assaulted Fort Malakoff.
title: Sevastopol

text: Isolated and facing a bleak prospect of invasion by the West if the war continued, Russia sued for peace in March 1856.
title: Sevastopol

text: France and Britain welcomed the development, owing to the conflict's domestic unpopularity.
title: Sevastopol

text: The Treaty of Paris, signed on 30 March 1856, ended the war and forbade Russia from basing warships in the Black Sea.

title: Sevastopol

text: Soviet academic publications since 1954, including the Great Soviet Encyclopedia, indicated that Sevastopol, Crimean Oblast was part of the Ukrainian SSR.
title: Sevastopol

text: In 1954, under Nikita Khrushchev, both Sevastopol and the remainder of the Crimean peninsula were administratively transferred from being territories within the Russian SFSR to being territories administered by the Ukrainian SSR.
title: Sevastopol

text: Administratively, Sevastopol was a municipality excluded from the adjacent Crimean Oblast.
title: Sevastopol

text: The territory of the municipality was 863.5 km2 and it was further subdivided into four raions (districts).
title: Sevastopol

text: Besides the City of Sevastopol proper, it also included two towns—Balaklava (having had no status until 1957), Inkerman, urban-type settlement Kacha, and 29 villages.
title: Sevastopol

text: For the 1955 Ukrainian parliamentary elections on 27 February, Sevastopol was split into two electoral districts, Stalinsky and Korabelny (initially requested three Stalinsky, Korabelny, and Nakhimovsky).

title: Sevastopol

text: As the key naval base of the former Soviet Black Sea Fleet, it was a source of tensions for Russia–Ukraine relations until a set-term lease agreement was signed in 1997.
title: Sevastopol

text: On 10 July 1993, the Russian parliament passed a resolution declaring Sevastopol to be "a federal Russian city".
title: Sevastopol

text: At the time, many supporters of President Boris Yeltsin had ceased taking part in the parliament's work.
title: Sevastopol

text: On 20 July 1993, the United Nations Security Council denounced the decision of the Russian parliament.
title: Sevastopol

text: According to Anatoliy Zlenko, it was the first time that the council had to review and qualify actions of a legislative body.
title: Sevastopol

text: On 14 April 1993, the Presidium of the Crimean Parliament called for the creation of the presidential post of the Crimean Republic.
title: Sevastopol

text: A week later, the Russian deputy, Valentin Agafonov, said that Russia was ready to supervise a referendum on Crimean independence and include the republic as a separate entity in the CIS.
title: Sevastopol

text: On 28 July 1993, one of the leaders of the Russian Society of Crimea, Viktor Prusakov, said that his organisation was ready for an armed mutiny and the establishment of Russian administration of Sevastopol.
title: Sevastopol

text: In response, Ukraine proposed a "special partnership" with NATO in January 1997.
title: Sevastopol

text: In May 1997, Russia and Ukraine signed the Russian–Ukrainian Friendship Treaty, ruling out Moscow's territorial claims to Ukraine.
title: Sevastopol

text: This was followed by the Partition Treaty on the Status and Conditions of the Black Sea Fleet on 28 May 1997.
title: Sevastopol

text: A separate agreement established the terms of a long-term lease of land, facilities, and resources in Sevastopol and the Crimea by Russia.
title: Sevastopol

text: Russia kept its naval base, with around 15,000 troops stationed in Sevastopol.
title: Sevastopol

text: The ex-Soviet Black Sea Fleet and its facilities were divided between Russia's Black Sea Fleet and the Ukrainian Naval Forces.
title: Sevastopol

text: The two navies co-used some of the city's harbours and piers, while others were demilitarised or used by either country.
title: Sevastopol

text: Sevastopol remained the location of the Russian Black Sea Fleet headquarters, and the Ukrainian Naval Forces Headquarters were also located in the city.
title: Sevastopol

text: A judicial row periodically continued over the naval hydrographic infrastructure both in Sevastopol and on the Crimean coast (especially lighthouses historically maintained by the Soviet and Russian Navy and also used for civil navigation support).
title: Sevastopol

text: As in the rest of Crimea, Russian remained the predominant language of the city, although following the independence of Ukraine there were some attempts at Ukrainisation, with very little success.
title: Sevastopol

text: Russian society in general and even some outspoken government representatives never accepted the loss of Sevastopol and tended to regard it as temporarily separated from Russia.
title: Sevastopol

text: The ratification process in the Ukrainian parliament encountered stiff opposition and even resulted in a brawl in the parliament chamber.
title: Sevastopol

text: Eventually, the treaty was ratified by a 52% majority vote—236 of 450.
title: Sevastopol

text: On 27 February, pro-Russian militia, including Russian troops, seized control of government buildings in Crimea, and by 28 February, controlled other strategic locations such as the military airport in Sevastopol.
title: Sevastopol

text: On 16 March 2014, an internationally unrecognised referendum was held in Sevastopol with official results claiming an 89.51% turnout and 95.6% of voters choosing to join Russia.
title: Sevastopol

text: Ukraine and almost all other countries of the United Nations General Assembly consider the referendum illegal and illegitimate.
title: Sevastopol

text: On 18 March, Russia annexed Crimea, incorporating the Republic of Crimea and federal city of Sevastopol as federal subjects of Russia.
title: Sevastopol

text: However, the annexation remains internationally unrecognised, with most countries recognising Sevastopol as a city with special status within Ukraine.
title: Sevastopol

text: The city is designated a special city-region of Ukraine which besides the city itself includes several of its outlying settlements.
title: Sevastopol

text: The city itself is concentrated mostly in the western portion of the region and around the long Bay of Sevastopol.
title: Sevastopol

text: This bay is a ria, a river canyon drowned by Holocene sea-level rise, and the outlet of Chorna River.
title: Sevastopol

text: Away in a remote location southeast of Sevastopol is located the former city of Balaklava (since 1957 incorporated within Sevastopol), the bay of which in the Soviet era served as a main port for the Soviet diesel-powered submarines.
title: Sevastopol

text: The coastline of the region is mostly rocky, in a series of smaller bays, a great number of which are located within the Bay of Sevastopol.
title: Sevastopol

text: The biggest of them are Southern Bay (within the Bay of Sevastopol), Archer Bay, a gulf complex that consists of Deergrass Bay, the Bay of Cossack, Salty Bay, and many others.
title: Sevastopol

text: There are over thirty bays in the immediate region.
title: Sevastopol

text: Three rivers flow through the region: the Belbek, Chorna, and Kacha.
title: Sevastopol

text: Due to the summer mean straddling 22 °C (72 °F) it borders on a four-season oceanic climate, with cold winters and warm summers.
title: Sevastopol

text: The average yearly temperature is 15–16 °C (59–61 °F) during the day and around 9 °C (48 °F) at night.
title: Sevastopol

text: In the coldest months, January and February, the average temperature is 5–6 °C (41–43 °F) during the day and around 1 °C (34 °F) at night.
title: Sevastopol

text: In the warmest months, July and August, the average temperature is around 26 °C (79 °F) during the day and around 19 °C (66 °F) at night.
title: Sevastopol

text: Generally, the summer/holiday season lasts 5 months, from around mid-May and into September, with the temperature often reaching 20 °C (68 °F) or more in the first half of October.
title: Sevastopol

text: The average annual temperature of the sea is 14.2 °C (58 °F), ranging from 7 °C (45 °F) in February to 24 °C (75 °F) in August.
title: Sevastopol

text: From June to September, the average sea temperature is greater than 20 °C (68 °F).
title: Sevastopol

text: In the second half of May and the first half of October; the average sea temperature is about 17 °C (63 °F).
title: Sevastopol

text: The average rainfall is about 400 millimetres (16 in) per year.

text: Crimean Tatars (Crimean Tatar: qırımtatarlar, къырымтатарлар), or simply Crimeans (qırımlılar, къырымлылар), are an Eastern European Turkic ethnic group and nation native to Crimea.

text: Their ethnogenesis lasted thousands of years in Crimea and the northern regions along the coast of the Black Sea, uniting Mediterranean populations with those of the Eurasian Steppe.

text: Decisions as to whether the Tatars will accept Russian passports or whether the autonomy sought would be within the Russian or Ukrainian state have been deferred pending further discussion.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: As of 2016, the Mejlis worked in emergency mode in Kyiv.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: After the annexation of Crimea by the Russian Federation, Crimean Tatars were persecuted and discriminated by Russian authorities, including cases of torture, arbitrary detentions, forced disappearances by Russian security forces and courts.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: An additional 1,800 (or about 0.7%) lived in the city of Sevastopol, also on the Crimean peninsula, but outside the border of the autonomous republic.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: This territory was annexed by Russia in 2014.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: About 150,000 remain in exile in Central Asia, mainly in Uzbekistan.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: Crimean Tatars in Turkey mostly live in Eskişehir Province, descendants of those who emigrated in the late 18th, 19th and early 20th centuries.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: According to Tatar activists, Eskişehir Province houses about 150,000 Crimean Tatars.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: Some activists set the national level figure as high as 6 million, which is considered an overestimation.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: There are different types of yurts; some are large and collapsible, called "terme", while others are small and non-collapsible (otav).
title: Crimean Tatars

text: Two types of alphabet are in use: Cyrillic and Latin.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: In 1928, it was replaced with the Latin alphabet.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: Cyrillic was introduced in 1938 based on the Russian alphabet.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: The Cyrillic alphabet was the only official one between 1938 and 1997.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: All its letters coincide with those of the Russian alphabet.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: The 1990s saw the start of the gradual transition of the language to the new Latin alphabet based on the Turkish one.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: The songs (makam) of the nomadic steppe Crimean Tatars are characterized by diatonic, melodic simplicity and brevity.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: The songs of mountainous and southern coastal Crimean Tatars, called Crimean Tatar: Türkü, are sung with richly ornamented melodies.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: Occasionally, song competitions take place between young men and women during Crimean holidays and weddings.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: Ritual folklore includes winter greetings, wedding songs, lamentations and circular dance songs (khoran).
title: Crimean Tatars

text: Epic stories or destans are very popular among the Crimean Tatars, particularly the destans of "Chora batyr", "Edige", "Koroglu", and others.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: On the Nowruz holiday, Crimean Tatars usually cook eggs, chicken soup, puff meat pie (kobete), halva, and sweet biscuits.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: Children put on masks and sing special songs under the windows of their neighbours, receiving sweets in return.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: Many Uzbek dishes were incorporated into Crimean Tatar national cuisine during exile in Central Asia since 1944, and these dishes have become prevalent in Crimea since the return.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: Uzbek samsa, laghman, and Crimean Tatar: plov (pilaf) are sold in most Tatar roadside cafes in Crimea as national dishes.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: In turn, some Crimean Tatar dishes, including chibureki, have been adopted by peoples outside Crimea, such as in Turkey and the North Caucasus.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: Sunnet toy in Crimean Tatar culture is a celebration (toy) for the religious rite of circumcision (sunnet) for boys.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: Yuri Osmanov, founder of the organization, was highly critical of Dzhemilev, saying that the OKND, the predecessor of the Mejilis, did not sufficiently try to mend ethnic tensions in Crimea.
title: Crimean Tatars

text: From the 1990s until October 2013, the political leader of the Crimean Tatars and the chairman of the Mejlis of the Crimean Tatar People was former Soviet dissident Mustafa Dzhemilev.
