text: Taras Hryhorovych Shevchenko (Ukrainian: Тарас Григорович Шевченко, Ukrainian pronunciation: [tɐˈraz‿ɦrɪˈɦɔrowɪt͡ʃ ʃeu̯t͡ʃɛnko]; Russian: Тарас Григорьевич Шевченко, romanized: Taras Grigoryevich Shevchenko; 9 March 1814 – 10 March 1861) was a Ukrainian poet, writer, artist, public and political figure, folklorist and ethnographer.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: His literary heritage, in particular the poetry collection Kobzar, is regarded to be the foundation of modern Ukrainian literature and to some degree also of the modern Ukrainian language.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: The significance of Shevchenko's creative genius for the Ukrainian and wider Slavic culture has led some to compare his figure to that of Robert Burns.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Shevchenko was born into a poor family of serfs during the period of Russian rule over Ukraine.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In his youth, he demonstrated a talent for art and became a fellow of the Imperial Academy of Arts in Saint Petersburg.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: After his return to Ukraine, he joined the emerging national movement.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Exiled to Central Asia due to his association with the Brotherhood of Saints Cyril and Methodius, Shevchenko continued to create art and poetry despite prohibitions, and his figure attained fame among the liberal-minded circles of the Russian Empire.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: He wrote poetry in Ukrainian and prose (nine novellas, a diary and his autobiography) in Russian.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: He was the third child after his sister Kateryna and brother Mykyta; his younger siblings were a brother, Yosyp, and a sister, Maria, who was born blind.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Their parents were Kateryna Shevchenko (née Boiko) and Hryhoriy Ivanovych Shevchenko, former subjects of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth who became serf peasants, working the land owned by Vasily Engelhardt, a nephew of Russian statesman Grigory Potemkin.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Hryhoriy and his father themselves worked as wheelwrights.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In 1816, the family moved to Kyrylivka (modern Shevchenkove), another village owned by Engelhardt, where Taras's father and grandfather had been born.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Once, he went looking for "the pillars that prop up the sky" and got lost.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Chumaks (travelling merchants) who met the boy took him back to the village.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: From 1822, Shevchenko was sent to a school, where he was taught to read and write.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: His teacher was the precentor of the village church, whose nickname was "Sovhyr".
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: He was a harsh disciplinarian, who had a tradition of birching the children in his class every Saturday.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: On 1 September [O.S. 20 August] 1823 Kateryna Shevchenko died.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: The widowed Hryhoriy, left to look after six children aged from thirteen to four, had little choice but to remarry.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: He was married to Oksana Tereshchenko, a widow from Morintsy, who had three children of her own.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: As a child, Shevchenko was cared for by his elder sister Kateryna, who had a great influence on him.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: When Hryhoriy Shevchenko became a chumak, Taras travelled twice with his father and his older brother away from his neighbourhood and, for the first time in his life, on to the open steppe.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: The only family member to stand for him during that time was his sister Iryna, who was two years younger.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: For a period Taras lived with his grandfather and his father's brother Pavlo, and was made to work as a swineherd and a groom's assistant.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: At the age of 12, Taras left home to work as a student assistant and a servant for a drunkard named Bohorsky, who had replaced Sovhyr as the village precentor and teacher and was even more violent than his predecessor.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: One of Shevchenko's duties was to read psalms over the dead.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: He was treated still more violently by Bohorsky once the boy's stepmother became his mistress.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In February 1827, the 13-year-old Shevchenko escaped from the village and worked for a few days for a deacon in Lysianka, before moving on to Tarasivka.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Frustrated in his attempts to become an artist, he returned to his home village.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: At around this time, Shevchenko met Oksana Kovalenko, a "curly" girl, who became his first love.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Their acquaintance didn't result in any serious relationship, but bore great significance for Taras, and Oksana would later be mentioned in a number of his works, including a dedication he later wrote in the poem Mariana, the nun.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Taras Shevchenko, then aged 14, was trained to become a kitchen servant and the kozachok (court servant) of his new master at the Vilshana estates.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: There he saw for the first time the luxuries of the Russian nobility.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In 1829, Shevchenko was part of Engelhardt's retinue that travelled to Warsaw, where his regiment was based.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: By the end of 1829 they had reached Vilno (modern Vilnius).
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: On 18 December [O.S. 6 December] 1829, Engelhardt caught Shevchenko at night painting a portrait of the Cossack general Matvei Platov.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: He boxed the boy's ears and ordered him to be whipped.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: When the party reached Warsaw, Engelhardt arranged for his servant to be apprenticed to a painter-decorator, who, recognising the boy's artistic talents, recommended he receive lessons from the Polish painter and professional artist, Franciszek Ksawery Lampi.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: While studying art in Vilno and Warsaw, the 16-year old Taras, still a serf, fell in love with Dunia Gusikowska, a Polish noble girl.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: According to his later confession, that love affair for the first time awoke in him the idea of equality between people from different social classes.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: When the November Uprising broke out in 1830, Engelhardt and his regiment were forced to leave Warsaw.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: His servants, including Shevchenko, were later expelled from the city, forced to leave Polish territory under armed guard, and then made their way to St.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Upon arriving there, Shevchenko returned to the life of being a page-boy.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: His artistic training was delayed for a year, after which he was permitted to study for four years with the painter Vasiliy Shiriayev, a man who proved to be much more cruel and controlling than his master in Warsaw.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: The summer nights were light enough for Shevchenko to visit the city's Summer Garden, where he drew the statues.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In his novel Artist, Shevchenko described that during the pre-academical period he painted such works as Apollo Belvedere, Fraklete, Heraclitus, Architectural barelief, and Mask of Fortune.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: He participated in the painting of the Bolshoi Theatre as an apprentice.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Soshenko showed an interest in Shevchenko's drawings, and recognised the young man's talent.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: He was allowed to receive drawing and watercolour painting lessons from Soshenko on weekends, and when he had spare time during the week.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Shevchenko made such progress as a portraitist that Engelhardt asked him to portray several of his mistresses.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Soshenko took Shevchenko to Saint Petersburg's art galleries, including the Hermitage.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: He introduced him to other compatriots, such as the writer and poet Yevhen Hrebinka, the art historian Vasyl Hryhorovych, and the Russian painter Alexey Venetsianov.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Through these men, around June 1832, Shevchenko was introduced to the most fashionable painter of the day, the artist Karl Briullov.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Briullov took an interest in Shevchenko, praising his work and indicating a willingness to take him on as a student.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: However, as a serf, Shevchenko was ineligible to study under Briullov at the Academy, who requested his freedom from Engelhardt.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: The request was met with a refusal, which enraged Briullov.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Engelhardt was persuaded to release his servant on condition that a fee of 2500 rubles was paid.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: To raise this sum, Briullov painted a portrait of the Russian poet Vasily Zhukovsky as a lottery prize for the imperial family; the winning lottery ticket was drawn by the tsarina.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Together they attended literary and musical evenings, and visited writers and artists.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Shevchenko's social life enriched and expanded his horizons and stimulated his creativity.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: An early drawing from this class, Cossack Banquet, was completed in December that year.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: The following month his work was recognised by the Imperial Society for the Encouragement of the Arts, who agreed to pay him a monthly maintenance fee of 30 rubles.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: After his liberation from serfdom in 1838, Shevchenko shared a flat in St.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Petersburg with Soshenko, and both fell in love with Mania, a niece of their landlord.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: This caused a conflict between the two friends, as a result of which Shevchenko had to move.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Mania continued to date Taras at the new location, but the relationship didn't end happily, and he and Soshenko soon reconciled.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In April 1839, Shevchenko was awarded a silver medal by the Council of the Academy.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: He began to master the technique of oil painting, with The Model in the Pose of St.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: From November, he became seriously ill with typhus.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: That year, he received another silver medal, this time for his oil painting The Beggar Boy Giving Bread to a Dog.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In September 1841, the Academy of Arts awarded Shevchenko his third silver medal, for the painting The Gypsy Fortune Teller.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: The following May, continual absenteeism from classes forced the Society for the Encouragement of Artists to exclude him from among its free boarders.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: He offered to publish them, but Shevchenko did not immediately agree.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Hrebinka took an active and direct part in the publication of Kobzar (1840); it was he who submitted the manuscript to the St.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: It did not openly call for revolutionary actions, but it expressed a protest against social injustice and a desire for a free life.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In March 1840, Hrebinka submitted the manuscript of the almanac Lastivka to the censors, which also included Shevchenko's Prychynna and the poems The wind is raging, the wind is raging! and Water flows into the blue sea.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In 1841, Shevchenko paid for his epic poem Haidamaky.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: The poem was met with sharp criticism by the literary critic Vissarion Belinsky; in the magazine Otechestvennye Zapiski he criticized Shevchenko's "inclination to romantic pompous ingenuity".
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: The difficult conditions Ukrainians endured had a profound impact on the poet-painter.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Shevchenko visited his siblings, still enserfed, and other relatives.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: He met with prominent Ukrainian writers and intellectuals Yevhen Hrebinka, Panteleimon Kulish, and Mykhaylo Maksymovych, and was befriended by the princely Repnin family, especially Varvara, the daughter of Little Russian governor-general Nikolai Repnin.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: The princess especially adored Shevchenko's singing, and the two developed an intimate friendship.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In a dedication to one of his poems Shevchenko compared Varvara to an angel, and there were rumours that some of his Russian-language works were composed specifically for her.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In May 1843, Shevchenko travelled to Ukraine, where he met as many intellectuals, poets, and artists as possible, including the future Brotherhood of Saints Cyril and Methodius member Vasyl Bilozersky.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: During his stay in Kyiv, Shevchenko sketched the city's historical sights and landscapes.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: After a month he went to Yahotyn, where he befriended the Repnin family.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In October 1843, he wrote his poem "The Dug Grave", after visiting recent excavations of burial mounds that many Ukrainians considered to be symbolic of the heroic past of the Cossacks.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: During the same year Shevchenko was introduced to members of the informal "Mug-Watering Society" (Russian: Общество мочемордия) organized by Pyriatyn landowner Viktor Zakrevsky, a former hussar.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: The society's participants competed in their drinking abilities and developed an entire system of titles and awards provided to its members depending on the amount of alcohol they were able to consume.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Varvara Repnina strongly warned Shevchenko against associating with Zakrevsky and his circle.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In autumn of 1843 Taras visited Kyrylivka, seeing his brothers and sisters for the first time in 14 years.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: During his stay in the village he met Fedosia, a 16-year old daughter of the local priest Hryhoriy Koshytsia.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Next year he returned to Kyrylivka, planning to get engaged with her.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: However, Fedosia's parents opposed the marriage due to Shevchenko being their former servant.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Fedosia later suffered from mental illness and died unmarried.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In 1844, distressed by the condition of Ukrainian regions in the Russian Empire, Shevchenko decided to capture some of his homeland's historical ruins and cultural monuments in an album of etchings, which he called Picturesque Ukraine.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Shevchenko planned the album to consist of his annotated etchings of places and events connected with Ukraine and its past, and use the proceeds to buy his family their freedom.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Only the first six etchings were printed because of the lack of means to continue.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In January 1847 Shevchenko was an usher at the wedding of Panteleimon Kulish and Olena Bilozerska.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Shevchenko's 1844 poem Dream, which described the social and national oppression of Ukrainians by the Russian upper classes, became subject of a scandal after its copies had been confiscated from the Brotherhood's members.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Tsar Nicholas I personally confirmed his sentence, and added to it, "Under the strictest surveillance, without the right to write or paint." He was subsequently sent on a forced march from Saint Petersburg to Orenburg and Orsk.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Having previously survived typhus, in exile Shevchenko started suffering from rheumatism and scurvy.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: The illness, exacerbated by poor living conditions and harsh military service, contributed to the decline of his psychological condition.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: During that period Shevchenko found his joy in reading letters sent to him by his old friends and acquaintances.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Although officially a common private, he was effectively treated as an equal by the other members of the expedition.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: He was tasked to sketch various landscapes around the coast of the Aral Sea.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: After an 18-month voyage (1848–49), Shevchenko returned with his album of drawings and paintings to Orenburg.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Most of those drawings were created for a detailed account of the expedition.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Nevertheless, he created many unique works of art about the Aral Sea nature and Kazakh people at a time when Russian conquest of Central Asia had begun in the middle of the nineteenth century.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: During the Aral expedition, Shevchenko, like other of its participants, suffered from heat, thirst and lack of food.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: After crossing the Karakum desert on foot, his party arrived to Kosaral fortress.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Shevchenko was met by Ural Cossacks, who served in the local garrison, with great reverence, as his thick beard grown during the campaign caused him to be mistaken for an Old Believer priest.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: The lack of post deliveries to the fortress caused him great discomfort, which he overcame by writing new poetry.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: By the end of 1849 Shevchenko was allowed to visit Orenburg, where he met a number of friends, getting inspiration for new poetic works.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: However, news about Shevchenko's planned involvement into a new expedition to the Aral Sea, as well as a new ban on drawing, once again led him to fall into despair.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: The new arrest resulted in his eventual transportation to one of the worst penal settlements, the remote fortress of Novopetrovsk at Mangyshlak Peninsula on the Caspian Sea, where he arrived in October 1850.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Qualified as a political prisoner, Shevchenko was subjected to strict drills and abuse by officers, which caused him great moral pain.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: To make matters worse, strict censorship prevented him from exchanging letters with his friends.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In 1851, at the suggestion of fellow serviceman Bronisław Zaleski, lieutenant colonel Mayevsky assigned Shevchenko to the Mangyshlak (Karatau) geological expedition.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In order to overcome the bans on artistic creativity, in 1853 Shevchenko worked on clay sculptures, creating a low relief and many statuettes, which didn't survive during transportation.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In 1854 he volunteered to paint an icon for the local church, but was denied permission by military authorities.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Petersburg and was forced to stay in Nizhniy Novgorod.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: There he started courting young actress Ekaterina Piunova, getting her acquainted with his friends and giving her lessons of Ukrainian language for her roles.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: However, Piunova's parents opposed the relationship due to the big age difference between the two, and she herself rejected the marriage proposal.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: On his birthday, 25 February 1858, Shevchenko received the news, that he had been permitted by authorities to return to St.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: After arriving to Moscow in March, he met his old friend Varvara Repnina, who would in her memoirs note Shevchenko's "apathetic" look and described him as "ruined" both physically and morally.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Petersburg after 11 years of exile, Shevchenko was received by the educated classes as a celebrity.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: At a dinner in the house of Counts Tolstoy, he was praised by the hosts and guests for his great love to his native Ukraine and for not breaking in face of difficult life circumstances.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Such praise from members of the high society surprised Shevchenko.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Following his arrival to the capital, he would be regularly invited as a guest to homes of influential people, and got acquainted with a number of prominent artistic figures, among others Mikhail Mikeshin and Ivan Turgenev.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Planning to continue his career in art, soon after returning to St.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Petersburg Shevchenko visited the Hermitage in order to choose a painting for his first aquatint reproduction.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In June 1858 he was provided two small rooms at the Arts Academy, which he used as a residence and a workshop.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In order to finance his daily expenses, Shevchenko gave art lessons to children of his friends.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: He also reestablished contacts with Kostomarov, who visited St.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In the winter of 1858, Shevchenko saw African-American Shakespearean actor Ira Aldridge perform with his troupe.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Using translators, the two became good friends over discussions of art and music and their shared experiences of oppression.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Aldridge was later gifted a portrait of Shevchenko by Mikhail Mikeshin.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In late 1858 or early 1859 Shevchenko met Marko Vovchok, the wife of Opanas Markovych, a former member of the Society of Sts.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Impressed by Vovchok's works, the poet soon developed warm feelings for her, and dedicated one of his poems to the author.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Petersburg, Shevchenko lobbied the liberation of his remaining family in Ukraine from serfdom, but Florowski, the landlord of Kyrylivka, issued a condition, according to which they had to leave the village in exchange for their freedom.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: After spending some time at the manor of Khrushchov family near Lebedyn, Shevchenko paid a visit to his old friend Mykhailo Maksymovych, who lived in the vicinity of Kaniv.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: From there he arrived to Horodyshche, visiting the sugar factory operated by industrialist Platon Semerenko, and then moved on to Kyrylivka, where he met his elder brother Mykyta and sister Iryna.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Shevchenko then moved to Korsun, staying with Varfolomey, the brother of his sister-in-law.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: The two men surveyed land along the Dnieper, looking for a possible location for a house, where Taras planned to reside in the future.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: However, in July Shevchenko was arrested following a report to the local police, which accused him of blasphemy, and transported to Kyiv via Cherkasy.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: After being released, Shevchenko stayed in Kyiv, renting a house at Priorka and visiting Soshenko, his old friend, before departing for St.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In the old age he dreamt of marrying and settling on land.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Petersburg, in November 1859 Shevchenko expressed his wish for marriage, asking his brother-in-law in Ukraine to help with his engagement to Kharytyna Dovhopolenko, a simple girl from a nearby village.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: However, Varfolomey discouraged Shevchenko from that idea, advising him to find a more culturally refined party.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In 1859 Shevchenko got acquainted with Lykera Polusmakova, who worked as a maidservant in the family of one of his Ukrainian friends from St.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Petersburg, and soon fell in love with her.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: After recuperating from tuberculosis, in 1860 Lykera moved to the house of Hanna Barvinok, the wife of Panteleimon Kulish, but was eventually accused of "laziness" and expelled.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: Shevchenko arranged her accommodation at the house of his friend, a sister of Countess Tolstoy.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: There Lykera was taught by a teacher, who reportedly started courting her.
title: Taras Shevchenko

text: This made Shevchenko so furious, that he was said to have cursed the girl and told her to go away, breaking the relationship.

title: Taras Shevchenko

title: Taras Shevchenko

title: Taras Shevchenko

text: In contrast to the idealized version of the past, in many of his early works Shevchenko expressed his profound sympathy for the sad conditions of serfdom experienced by his contemporaries in Ukraine.

text: Alexander Sergeyevich Pushkin (6 June [O.S. 26 May] 1799 – 10 February [O.S. 29 January] 1837) was a Russian poet, playwright, and novelist of the Romantic era.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: He is considered by many to be the greatest Russian poet, as well as the founder of modern Russian literature.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Pushkin was born into the Russian nobility in Moscow.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: His father, Sergey Lvovich Pushkin, belonged to an old noble family.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Pushkin published his first poem at the age of 15, and was widely recognized by the literary establishment by the time of his graduation from the Tsarskoye Selo Lyceum.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Upon graduation from the Lyceum, Pushkin recited his controversial poem "Ode to Liberty", one of several that led to his exile by Emperor Alexander I.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: While under strict surveillance by the Emperor's political police and unable to publish, Pushkin wrote his most famous play, Boris Godunov.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: His grand opus novel in verse Eugene Onegin was serialized between 1825 and 1832.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Pushkin's mother, Nadezhda (Nadya) Ossipovna Gannibal (1775–1836), was descended through her paternal grandmother from German and Scandinavian nobility.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: She was the daughter of Ossip Abramovich Gannibal (1744–1807) and his wife, Maria Alekseyevna Pushkina (1745–1818).
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Ossip Abramovich Gannibal's father, Pushkin's great-grandfather, was Abram Petrovich Gannibal (1696–1781), an African page kidnapped and taken to Constantinople as a gift for the Ottoman Sultan and later transferred to Russia as a gift for Peter the Great.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Abram wrote in a letter to Empress Elizabeth, Peter the Great's daughter, that Gannibal was from the town of "Lagon", largely on the basis of a mythical biography by Gannibal's son-in-law Rotkirch.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Vladimir Nabokov, when researching Eugene Onegin, cast serious doubt on this origin theory.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Later research by the scholars Dieudonné Gnammankou and Hugh Barnes eventually conclusively established that Gannibal was instead born in Central Africa, in an area bordering Lake Chad in modern-day Cameroon.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: He became acquainted with the Russian language through communication with household serfs and his nanny, Arina Rodionovna, whom he loved dearly and to whom he was more attached than to his own mother.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: He published his first poem at the age of 15.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: When he finished school, as part of the first graduating class of the prestigious Imperial Lyceum in Tsarskoye Selo, near Saint Petersburg, his talent was already widely recognized on the Russian literary scene.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: At the Lyceum, he was a student of David Mara, known in Russia as David de Boudry, a younger brother of French revolutionary Jean-Paul Marat.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: After school, Pushkin plunged into the vibrant and raucous intellectual youth culture of St.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Petersburg, which was then the capital of the Russian Empire.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Pushkin gradually became committed to social reform, and emerged as a spokesman for literary radicals.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: That angered the government and led to his transfer from the capital in May 1820.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: He went to the Caucasus and to Crimea and then to Kamianka and Chișinău in Bessarabia.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: He joined the Filiki Eteria, a secret organization whose purpose was to overthrow Ottoman rule in Greece and establish an independent Greek state.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: In 1823, Pushkin moved to Odessa, where he again clashed with the government, which sent him into exile on his mother's rural estate of Mikhailovskoye, near Pskov, from 1824 to 1826.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: In Mikhailovskoye, Pushkin wrote nostalgic love poems which he dedicated to Elizaveta Vorontsova, wife of Novorossiya's General-Governor.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Then Pushkin worked on his verse-novel Eugene Onegin.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: In Mikhailovskoye, in 1825, Pushkin wrote the poem To***.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: It is generally believed that he dedicated this poem to Anna Kern, but there are other opinions.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Poet Mikhail Dudin believed that the poem was dedicated to the serf Olga Kalashnikova.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Pushkinist Kira Victorova believed that the poem was dedicated to the Empress Elizaveta Alekseyevna.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Vadim Nikolayev argued that the idea about the Empress was marginal and refused to discuss it, while trying to prove that poem had been dedicated to Tatyana Larina, the heroine of Eugene Onegin.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: During that same year (1825) Pushkin also wrote what would become his most famous play, the drama Boris Godunov, while at his mother's estate.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: He could not, however, gain permission to publish it until five years later.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: The original and uncensored version of the drama was not staged until 2007.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Authorities summoned Pushkin to Moscow after his poem Ode to Liberty was found among the belongings of the rebels from the Decembrist Uprising (1825).
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: After his exile in 1820 Pushkin's friends and family continually petitioned for his release, sending letters and meeting Emperor Alexander I and then Emperor Nicholas I on the heels of the Decembrist Uprising.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Upon meeting Emperor Nicholas I Pushkin obtained his release from exile and began to work as the emperor's Titular Counsel of the National Archives.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: However, because insurgents in the Decembrist Uprising (1825) in Saint Petersburg had kept some of Pushkin's earlier political poems, the emperor retained strict control of everything Pushkin published and he was banned from travelling at will.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Pushkin’s conversation with Nicholas I is not known to us, and it can only be restored from Pushkin’s later statements.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: After this conversation Pushkin became a supporter of Nicholas I.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: This attitude was vividly expressed in the stanzas: “No, I am not a flatterer when I compose free praise to the Tsar”.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Pushkin's patriotic poem To the Slanderers of Russia written during the 1830–1831 Polish uprising aroused hostility among some of the Russian liberals.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Around 1825–1829 he met and befriended the Polish poet Adam Mickiewicz, during exile in central Russia.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: In 1829 he travelled through the Caucasus to Erzurum to visit friends fighting in the Russian army during the Russo-Turkish War.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: At the end of 1829 Pushkin wanted to set off on a journey abroad, the desire reflected in his poem Let's go, I'm ready.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: He applied for permission for the journey but received negative response from Nicholas I on 17 January 1830.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Around 1828 Pushkin met Natalia Goncharova, then 16 years old and one of the most talked-about beauties of Moscow.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: After much hesitation Natalia accepted a proposal of marriage from Pushkin in April 1830, but not before she received assurances that the Tsarist government had no intention of persecuting the libertarian poet.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Later Pushkin and his wife became regulars of court society.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: They officially became engaged on 6 May 1830 and sent out wedding invitations.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Owing to an outbreak of cholera and other circumstances, the wedding was delayed for a year.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: The ceremony took place on 18 February 1831 (Old Style) in the Great Ascension Church on Bolshaya Nikitskaya Street in Moscow.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Pushkin's marriage to Goncharova was largely a happy one, but his wife’s characteristic flirtatiousness and frivolity would lead to his fatal duel seven years later, for Pushkin had a highly jealous temperament.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: In 1831, during the period of Pushkin's growing literary influence, he met other influential early writers, such as Russo-Ukrainian Nikolai Gogol.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: On 4 November, he sent a challenge to a duel to Georges d'Anthès, also known as Dantes-Gekkern.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Jacob van Heeckeren, d'Anthès' adoptive father, asked that the duel be delayed by two weeks.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: With efforts by the poet's friends, the duel was cancelled.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: On 17 November, d'Anthès proposed to Natalia Goncharova's sister, Ekaterina.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: D'Anthès continued to pursue Natalia Goncharova in public and rumours circulated that d'Anthès had married Natalia's sister just to save her reputation.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: On 26 January (7 February in the Gregorian calendar) 1837 Pushkin sent a "highly insulting letter" to Gekkern.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: The only answer to that letter could be a challenge to a duel, as Pushkin knew.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Pushkin received the formal challenge to a duel through his sister-in-law, Ekaterina Gekkerna, approved by d'Anthès, on the same day through the attaché of the French Embassy, Viscount d'Archiac.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Pushkin asked Arthur Magenis, then attaché to the British Consulate-General in Saint Petersburg, to be his second.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Magenis did not formally accept but on 26 January (7 February) approached Viscount d'Archiac to attempt a reconciliation; however d'Archiac refused to speak with him as he was not yet officially Pushkin's second.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: The pistol duel with d'Anthès took place on 27 January (8 February) at the Black River, without the presence of a second for Pushkin.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: The duel they fought was of a kind known as a "barrier duel".
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: The rules of this type dictated that the duellists began at an agreed distance.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: After the signal to begin, they walked towards each other, closing the distance.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: They could fire at any time they wished, but the duellist that shot first was required to stand still and wait for the other to shoot back at his leisure.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: D'Anthès fired first, critically wounding Pushkin; the bullet entered at his hip and penetrated his abdomen.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: D'Anthès was only lightly wounded in the right arm by Pushkin's shot.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Two days later, at 2.45 pm on 29 January (10 February), Pushkin died of peritonitis.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: I’ve been told that by people who know.’ At Pushkin's wife's request he was put in the coffin in evening dress, not in chamber-cadet uniform, the uniform provided by the emperor.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: The funeral service was initially assigned to St Isaac's Cathedral but was moved to Konyushennaya church.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: After the funeral the coffin was lowered into the basement, where it stayed until 3 February, when it was removed to Pskov province.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Pushkin was buried in the grounds of Svyatogorsky monastery in present-day Pushkinskiye Gory, near Pskov, beside his mother.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Her daughter Sophie married Grand Duke Michael Mikhailovich of Russia, a grandson of Emperor Nicholas I.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Only the lines of Alexander and Natalia remain.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Natalia's granddaughter, Nadejda, married into the extended British royal family, her husband being the uncle of Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh, and is the grandmother of the present Marquess of Milford Haven.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Pushkin is also known for his short stories.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: In particular his cycle The Tales of the Late Ivan Petrovich Belkin, including "The Shot", were well received.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Pushkin is the progenitor of the long and fruitful development of Russian realist literature, for he manages to attain the realist ideal of a concise presentation of reality".
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Pushkin himself preferred his verse novel Eugene Onegin, which he wrote over the course of his life and which, starting a tradition of great Russian novels, follows a few central characters but varies widely in tone and focus.
title: Alexander Pushkin

text: Onegin is a work of such complexity that, though it is only about a hundred pages long, translator Vladimir Nabokov needed two full volumes of material to fully render its meaning into English.

text: Composed of 12 "books", the novel tells the story of three brothers: the novice monk Alyosha, the non-believer Ivan, and the soldier Dmitri.
title: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: The main plot is the death of their father, Fyodor Karamazov, while other parts are philosophical and religious arguments by Father Zosima to Alyosha.
title: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: The most famous chapter is "The Grand Inquisitor", a parable told by Ivan to Alyosha about Christ's Second Coming in Seville, Spain, in which Christ is imprisoned by a ninety-year-old Catholic Grand Inquisitor.
title: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: Instead of answering him, Christ gives him a kiss, and the Inquisitor subsequently releases him, telling him not to return.
title: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: The tale has been misunderstood as a defence of the Inquisitor, but some, such as Romano Guardini, argue that the Christ of the parable was Ivan's own interpretation of Christ, "the idealistic product of the unbelief".

text: Ivan, however, has stated that he is against Christ.

text: Most contemporary critics and scholars agree that Dostoevsky is attacking Roman Catholicism and socialist atheism, both represented by the Inquisitor.

text: In May 2020, Shakhtar started to play home matches at NSC Olimpiyskyi in Kyiv.

text: Shakhtar has appeared in several European competitions and is often a participant in the UEFA Champions League.

text: The club became the first club in independent Ukraine to win the UEFA Cup in 2009, the last year before the competition was revamped as the Europa League.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: FC Shakhtar Donetsk is one of two Ukrainian clubs, the other being Dynamo Kyiv, who have won a major UEFA competition.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Following the winter break of the 2016–17 season the club then moved again to the Metalist Stadium in Kharkiv (250 kilometres (160 mi) to the northwest of Donetsk) early in 2017.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Shakhtar Donetsk is one of Ukraine's most popular football clubs, and is particularly favoured in the eastern Donbas region.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: On 28 October 2021, Shakhtar had 1.4 million followers on Facebook, the highest in Ukraine.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: The club draws its history from the very start of the Soviet football league competitions and is one of the oldest clubs in Ukraine.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: The club was a member of the Soviet Voluntary Sports Society of Shakhtyor, having connections with other Soviet teams from Karaganda (Kazakhstan), Soligorsk (Belarus), among others.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: The club has a meaningful association with the Donets Basin underground coal-mining using vertical mining shafts, called Schacht in German.

title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: As part of the so-called Stalin industrialisation and Stakhanovite movement, in 1936 the local football teams of Dynamo sports societies of Horlivka and Stalino (today Donetsk) established a joint team that represented the mining volunteer sports society Stakhanovets (later Shakhter).
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: The team was transferred from the sports society for the NKVD to a trade union "volunteered sports society" (DSO).
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Following the World War II, the DSO Stakhanovets changed its name to DSO Shakhter which in the Ukrainian SSR had its local corresponding nomenclature as DSS Shakhtar.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: The word "Shakhter" or "Shakhtar" means a coal miner working at a sub-surface mine, shafted mine, shakhta is a derivative of shaft.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: During the dissolution of the Soviet Union (1989–1992), the Donetsk club was reorganized and commercialized as a professional football team.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: The term Shakhtar or Shakhter (Russian variation) is occupational referring to a miner working in a subterranean mining shaft (see shaft mining), the word "shakhtar" is a local adaptation and derivative of shaft.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: In the Soviet Union, due to an elevated risk working underground, a subterranean mining job was more compensated in relation to other unskilled work and always in demand.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: In 1950, Viktor Fomin was named Ukrainian Footballer of the Year, despite the club finishing only 11th in the league.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: The first success for the team was in 1951, when it achieved third place in the USSR Championship.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: The most notable player of that achievement was the striker Aleksandr Ponomarev, who came to finish his football career in Donbas, the region he was born in, and was named Ukrainian Footballer of the Year for 1951.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Despite the latest achievement, Shakhtar was relegated at the end of the 1952 season and as part of the re-organization of the team, former player Aleksandr Ponomarev became the head coach of the club.

title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Despite the departure of the team's leader midfielder Anatoliy Konkov, in 1975, Shakhtar under management of former player Vladimir Salkov, earned second place in the USSR Championship and received the right to represent the Soviet Union in European competition.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: At the end of the season, Shakhtar received the Progress Cup for making the biggest progress from previous season in the league.

text: In 1978, Shakhtar finished third in the USSR Championship.

title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: The club was only two points away from the first place, despite having important players leaving the club before the season, and other important players receiving injuries.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Shakhtar twice, in 1980 and 1983, brought home the crystal USSR Cup to Donetsk and in 1983, it won the USSR Super Cup over then-domestic league champions Dnipro Dnipropetrovsk.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: In 1987, Shakhtar received the fewest yellow and red cards in the championship, for which the club was awarded the "Soviet Top League Fair Play Award" by Man and Law magazine.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Between 1982 and 1988, Shakhtar received the "Together With The Club" award five times, an award given for good organization of home games and behaviour of the home fans.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: During the Perestroika period, the Shakhtar team of masters separated from the Shakhtar Donetsk sports society into a professional club.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: It was part of the so-called khozraschet period or a "self-management", which was part of many Soviet organizations at that time.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: A bombing-assassination took place at the team's stadium, killing club president Akhat Bragin in October 1995.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: In 1996, Rinat Akhmetov took over as president and invested heavily in the club.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: In the 1997–98 UEFA Cup Winners' Cup, Shakhtar were eliminated after a 5–2 aggregate loss to Vicenza, losing the first and second legs.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Important players at the time were defenders Serhiy Popov and Mykhaylo Starostyak, goalkeeper Dmytro Shutkov, striker Oleh Matvyeyev (who was top scorer of the Premier League in the 1996–97 season), and midfielders Hennadiy Orbu, Valeriy Kryventsov and Ihor Petrov.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Most of the players playing for the team of the time came through the team's youth ranks.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Towards the end of the decade, the team finally started to look like a team able to become champion.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: In 1999, a Shakhtar football academy was opened and now hosts football training for roughly 3,000 youth.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: In 2000, Andriy Vorobey was named Ukrainian Footballer of the Year by Komanda, the first Shakhtar player in independent Ukraine to do so, and became the top scorer in the 2000–01 Vyshcha Liha.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: That year, Shakhtar competed in the UEFA Champions League for the first time, drawn in a group with Lazio, Arsenal and Sparta Prague.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: They were also victorious in the 2001–02 Ukrainian Cup, defeating Dynamo 3–2 after extra time in the final.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Among the key players at the club at the time were captain defensive midfielder Anatoliy Tymoshchuk, striker Andriy Vorobey, midfielder Hennadiy Zubov and defender Mykhaylo Starostyak.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: At the end of the season, Tymoshchuk, who emerged as the club's leader on the field, was named Ukrainian Footballer of the Year by Komanda and Ukrainskiy Football.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: After multiple managerial changes, in 2004 the Romanian Mircea Lucescu was invited to build a team in Shakhtar.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: The large amount of Brazilians arriving at the club earned Shakhtar the nickname "the most Brazilian club in Europe".
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: They won their second Premier League title in the 2004–05 season, but lost to Dynamo Kyiv in the inaugural Ukrainian Super Cup tournament in 2004.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: They finished as runners up in the 2004–05 Ukrainian Cup, losing to Dynamo in a penalty shoot-out the final.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: They retained the Premier League crown in the 2005–06 season and managed to avenge the defeat to Dynamo in the previous Super Cup by defeating them on penalties to win their first-ever Super Cup title.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: At the end of the season, Anatoliy Tymoshchuk was named Ukrainian Footballer of the Year for by Ukrainian Football for the second time, becoming the first Shakhtar player to be named so more than once.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Brazilian striker Brandão became the league's joint top scorer.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Shakhtar appeared in all three editions of the Channel One Cup, winning the 2007 edition and finishing runners-up in 2008.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Having missed out on the league title in 2006–07, Shakhtar regained the title in 2007–08, also being victorious in the Ukrainian Cup after defeating Dynamo Kyiv 2–0 in the final.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: The victory earned the player Mariusz Lewandowski the 2009 Polish Footballer of the Year award.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: This also made them the last UEFA Cup winners before the tournament was rebranded as the UEFA Europa League.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Before the start of the 2009–10 season, Shakhtar won the friendly Uhrencup tournament.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Shakhtar won the Premier League title in the 2009–10 season, goalkeeper Andriy Pyatov was named Ukraine Premier League MVP by Komanda, and Manager Mircea Lucescu was named Romania Coach of the Year for the second time.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: The 2010–11 season was a very successful one for Shakhtar.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: They reached the quarter-finals of the Champions League, their then-best-ever performance in the competition.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Captain Darijo Srna was chosen to be part of the Champions League Team of the Season as voted by fans.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: They also won a domestic treble with victory in the Premier League, Ukrainian Cup and the Super Cup.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: The successful season did not go unnoticed by the experts, and in 2011, the IFFHS gave Shakhtar a special award for making the biggest progress of the decade among football clubs.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: They went on to win the Premier League and Ukrainian Cup in the 2011–12 season.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: The main players at that time were captain Darijo Srna, defender Yaroslav Rakitskyi, Armenian midfielder Henrikh Mkhitaryan (who was named Armenian Footballer of the Year twice while playing for Shakhtar) and Brazilian midfielders Fernandinho and Willian.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: In the 2012–13 season Shakhtar won the Premier League, Cup and Super Cup.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Henrikh Mkhitaryan became the top scorer of the league, setting a Ukrainian championship record of 25 goals.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Shakhtar spent the following summer trying to integrate new young players into the team, who along with the remaining players were to form the backbone of the renewed Shakhtar.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Despite selling its leaders, before the 2013–14 season, Shakhtar set a new record for East Europe for number of season tickets sold.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Before the beginning of the 2013–14 season, Shakhtar won two friendly tournaments in Abu Dhabi, the Match World Cup, and the Super Cup of Champions played against Russian champions Zenit Saint Petersburg.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: In the mid-season break, Shakhtar won the 2014 United Supercup (the second edition of the United Tournament), a tournament between the top-two placed clubs of Ukraine and of Russia, which strengthened Shakhtar's status as the strongest club in Eastern Europe.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: At the end of the 2013–14 season, Shakhtar won the Ukraine Premier League, while Luiz Adriano was the league top scorer.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Shakhtar also won the 2014 Ukrainian Super Cup, holding the trophy for the sixth time.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Manager Mircea Lucescu was named the 2014 Romania Coach of the Year, receiving the award for the fourth time.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Due to the war in Donbas, Shakhtar had to temporarily move and play its games in Arena Lviv, resulting in very low attendance.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: As an anti-war protest, the players of Shakhtar refused the initiative to wear the "Glory to the Ukrainian Army" shirts.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: In the 2014–15 UEFA Champions League, Shakhtar finished second in the group stage, therefore qualifying to the next stage.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Striker Luiz Adriano equaled both Lionel Messi's record of five goals in a Champions League match and Cristiano Ronaldo's record of scoring nine goals in the group stage; as a result, UEFA named him MVP of the competition's group stage.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Shakhtar finished the season second in the 2014–15 Ukrainian Premier League after playing the whole season away from Donbas, with Alex Teixeira finishing as a joint top scorer in the league.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: At the end of the season, Douglas Costa was sold to Bayern Munich, while Luiz Adriano moved to Milan.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: While the club itself moved to a Ukraine-controlled zone, a few prominent Shakhtar players remained in the Donetsk People's Republic and supported the unrecognised state.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Among them were former defender Viktor Zvyahintsev, former goalkeeper Yuriy Dehteryov, former Shakhtar and Ukraine national football team captain Ihor Petrov, and the club's first press officer Vyacheslav Sharafutdinov.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: During the 2015–16 Ukrainian Premier League, on 16 October, Shakhtar beat Dynamo Kyiv 3–0 in Kyiv and set two new records.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: One record was that for the first time during a Klasychne derby game in Kyiv a team scored three goals.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: The other record was that for the first time Shakhtar had more Klasychne derby victories, 26, than Dynamo.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: In the middle of the season, Alex Teixeira moved to Chinese club Jiangsu Suning for a fee of €50 million, breaking both the Asian and Ukrainian transfer record.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: The club finished the 2015/16 season as runner up and Marlos was recognised best league player by Komanda.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: After the 2015–16 season, long-time manager Mircea Lucescu moved on to Zenit Saint Petersburg; he was replaced by the Portuguese Paulo Fonseca, previously of Braga.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Following the winter break of the 2016–17 season, a season when the club won the league, the cup, and the supercup, the club moved to the Metalist Stadium in Kharkiv (241 km to the north of Donetsk).
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: In 2017–18, the club won the league and the cup again, with Facundo Ferreyra becoming the league top scorer and Marlos the league assist leader.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: In the 2018–19 season, the club won Ukrainian Premier League for the third time in a row.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: In 2019–20, Shakhtar retained their league title for the fourth time in a row.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: The stadium was built during the Soviet period for another Donetsk club, FC Lokomotyv Donetsk, and carried its name.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: On 29 August 2009, the construction of its new stadium, Donbas Arena, was accomplished, and the stadium was opened.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Located in the City Park of Culture and Recreation, it has a capacity of 50,149 and has been honored with a UEFA five-star rating, the highest rating achievable.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Shakhtar's old home, the Central Stadium "Shakhtar", which was built in 1936 and reconstructed four times, had been used by Shakhtar Donetsk Reserves.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: The stadium received some major renovations, including the installation of bench seats in 2000 when Shakhtar made it to the Champions League Group Stage.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: A mascot mole (moles is a nickname for the club) will entertain spectators during the home matches.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Shakhtar are rated 40th by the average game attendance, being the top Eastern European club on the rating charts.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Before the 2013–14 season, Shakhtar set a new record for Eastern Europe for number of season tickets sold, selling 27,000 season tickets, which means 52% of the seats in Donbas Arena belong to season ticket holders.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: From 2014 until the end of 2016, due to the war in Donbas, Shakhtar played its home matches at the Arena Lviv.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Following the winter break of the 2016–17 season the club moved to the Metalist Stadium in Kharkiv (250 kilometers to the northwest of Donetsk).
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Shakhtar played their 2022–23 matches in the Champions League and Europa League at Stadion Wojska Polskiego in Warsaw due to the 2022 invasion.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: They played their 2023–24 UEFA Champions League and 2023–24 UEFA Europa League matches at Volksparkstadion in Hamburg, Germany.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Next season of their 2024–25 UEFA Champions League Shakhtar played at Arena AufSchalke in Gelsenkirchen, Germany.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Due to the war conditions in the eastern Ukraine, Shakhtar temporary venue for its home matches has changed several times, while it was announced that the team will use training facilities in Kyiv.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: By 2015, all reserve teams, such as FC Shakhtar-2 Donetsk and FC Shakhtar-3 Donetsk were withdrawn from professional competitions.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: The club, however, fields its youth Shakhtar U-21 team in the youth championship of Ukrainian Premier League.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Shakhtar also has its football academy that fields four teams in a special youth league designated for teenagers.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Since 2012, the club has also had a team for the U-19 championship of the Ukrainian Premier League.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: During the Soviet times, the club used to have one youth team named Shakhter-D Donetsk that participated in a separate Soviet championship for doubles.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Shakhter-D later was reorganized into FC Shakhtar-2 Donetsk and admitted to the Ukrainian First League.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: In 1946, when the club was renamed, the logo was changed to black and white, with the addition of the club's name.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Later, in the middle of the 1960s, their logo depicted two crossed hammers, with "Shakhtar Donetsk" written in the circle.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: The crest was added to the kit and remained there, except for several seasons at the beginning of the 1990s.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: The club's name was depicted in the Russian language until the latest logo was chosen.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Sometime later, the logo was remodelled into the present one.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: The emblem was added to the kit in 1997.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: In 2007, during the presentation of the club's new stadium, Shakhtar's new logo was unveiled.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: For the first time in over 30 years, the crossed hammers, the traditional symbols of the club, were present on the crest.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Also, for the first time, the name was written in the Ukrainian language and not Russian.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: In all matches involving Donetsk, many people came that promoted the development of fan movement in Donetsk.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Active development of football movement began in the early 1980s.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: In the early 2000s to the Ukrainian stadiums came English style – hooliganism.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: In 2003, during the final of the Ukrainian Cup there was a fierce fight between Dynamo and Shakhtar fans.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: This was another local club and, although not as significant as games against the rivals from the capital, the games between the two Donetsk teams were proclaimed the Donetsk derby.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Among the extinguished rivalries are the games against Spartak Moscow and, particularly, the third place champions Dinamo Tbilisi of Georgia that took place at times during the Soviet Top League.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Another interesting rivalry, the Donbas Derby, is with Zorya Luhansk, which gathered a significant crowd in Luhansk.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: During the early Ukrainian championships, another interesting rivalry developed with Chornomorets Odesa labelled the "Miners vs.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Since 1997, the club has participated in UEFA competition annually with variable amounts of success, and first took part in the UEFA Champions League competition in 2000.
title: FC Shakhtar Donetsk

text: Football Club 'Dynamo Kyiv', also known as Dynamo Kyiv, or simply Dynamo, (Ukrainian: Футбольний клуб «Динамо» Київ, romanized: Futbolnyy klub «Dynamo» Kyiv, [dɪˈnɑmo ˈkɪjiu̯]) is a Ukrainian professional football club based in Kyiv.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Founded in 1927 as a branch of the bigger Soviet Dynamo Sports Society, the club as a separate business entity was officially formed only in 1989 and currently plays in the Ukrainian Premier League.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The club has secured brand rights from the Ukrainian Dynamo society and has no direct relations to the sports society since 1989.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Their home is the 70,050 capacity Olimpiyskiy National Sports Complex.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Since 1936, Dynamo Kyiv has spent its entire history in the top league of Soviet and later Ukrainian football, having never been relegated to a lower division.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: During the Soviet era, the club was one of the main rivals, and often the only rival, to football clubs from Moscow.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Its ability to challenge the dominance of the Moscow clubs in Soviet football, and frequently defeat them to win the Soviet championship, was a matter of national pride for Ukraine.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Leaders of the Ukrainian SSR unofficially regarded the club as their national team and provided it with generous support, making Dynamo a professional team of international importance.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The club's most successful periods are associated with Valeriy Lobanovskyi, who coached the team during three stints, leading them to numerous domestic and European titles.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: In 1961, the club became first-ever in the history of Soviet football that managed to overcome the total hegemony of Moscow-based clubs in the Soviet Top League.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The Spartak Moscow–Dynamo Kyiv rivalry that began in the mid-1970s, is widely considered to have been one of the most exciting football rivalries in the Soviet Union.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Since becoming the first Soviet football club to participate in UEFA competition in 1965, Dynamo Kyiv has played in European competitions almost every season.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Over its history, Dynamo Kyiv have won 17 Ukrainian top-flight league titles, 13 Soviet top-flight league titles, 14 Ukrainian national cup competitions, 9 Soviet national cup competitions, and three continental titles (including two UEFA Cup Winners' Cups).
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Its two European Cup Winners' Cups make it one of the only two Soviet clubs to have won a UEFA trophy, the other being Dinamo Tbilisi.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The Dynamo Kyiv first team became a base team for the Soviet Union national football team in the 1970–1980s and the Ukraine national football team in the 1990–2000s.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The three stars on the club's crest each signify 10 top-flight seasons Dynamo Kyiv won.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The all-Union Dynamo sports society was a sports department of the Soviet state security KGB, originally Cheka-OGPU.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: During the Soviet period Dynamo's players same as players of all Dynamos in the Soviet Union were officially Soviet uniform servicemembers earning rank, salary, and pension when playing on the team of masters.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: On 13 May 1927, the statute of the Kyivan Proletarian Sport Society (PST) Dynamo was officially registered by the special commission in affairs of public organizations and unions of the Kyiv district.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The All-Union sport society of Dynamo in Moscow was formed earlier in 1923 on the initiative of the Felix Dzerzhinsky.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: A year later the first Ukrainian branch cell of the Dynamo sports society was formed in Kharkiv.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Under the banner of Kyivan Dynamo gathered the representatives of the local GPU (State Political Directorate), the Soviet secret police, the best footballers of which defended the honors of the Trade Union club "Sovtorgsluzhashchie", a portmanteau for Soviet retail servicemen.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: It was a common practice of the early Soviet sports societies that were formed based on already existing "pre-revolutionary" (1917 Bolshevik Revolution) sports societies in 1920s.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The leadership of Dynamo did not dare to reorganize the well-established club and the main title contender in the middle of a playing season.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Therefore, the first mention of the football club Dynamo could only be found on 5 April 1928 in the Russian-language newspaper (at that time) Vecherniy Kiev ("Evening Kyiv").
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The Kyivan Sport Society Dynamo currently is organizing its own football team. "Dynamo" petitioned to Okrsofik for inclusion of its team in the playing season.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: It was then when by the initiative of Semyon Zapadny, chief of the Kyiv GPU, the football team was created.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: His deputy, Sergei Barminsky, started to form the team not only out of regular chekists (members of the Soviet secret police), but also footballers of other clubs in the city among which is mentioned a team "Sovtorgsluzhaschie".
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: All the footballers were either part of the consolidated city team or the city champions.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The newly created team played its first official match on 1 July 1928 against a local consolidated city team while visiting Bila Tserkva.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Already on the fifth minute the Dynamo-men opened the score in the game, however, at the end the club lost it 1–2.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: On 15 July, the Bila Tserkva newspaper Radyanska Nyva ("Soviet Fields") put it in such words: In the second halftime Bila Tserkva easily strikes the ball in the net, thus, equalizing the score.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Kyiv tried several counter attacks and even earned a free kick which was not able to convert.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Near the end Bila Tserkva under the applause of thousands of spectators strikes in the second ball.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The final whistle of the referee has fixated the victory of Bila Tserkva with the score 2:1.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The next match played by Dynamo was on 17 July 1928 hosting another Dynamo from the port city of Odesa.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: At the end of July Dynamo toured Belarus playing against the republican team of Belarus (1:5) and the districtal team of Gomel District (3:2).
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: On 1 September 1928 Dynamo Kyiv was hosting the Dynamo's primary team from Moscow and were thrashed 2:6.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: It was then Dynamo Kyiv was led by a playing coach Vasyl Boiko whose role is indicated as an instructor-organizer.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Later in October 1928 Dynamo Kyiv took part in its first official tournament the 1928 Kyiv city championship and won it.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: On 18 November 1928 Dynamo Kyiv overpowered the Kyiv's main football team of that period, Zheldor, 1:0.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: On 14 September 1929 Dynamo Kyiv played its first international match against visiting workers' team from Deutsch-Wagram, Lower Austria and lost it 3:4.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Its club stadium Dynamo opened on 12 June 1933, a year before the Soviet government turned the city into capital of the Soviet Ukraine.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: As the club gained more experience and played on a regular basis, it started to fill the stadium with spectators with both the club and football in general gaining popularity in Soviet Ukraine.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: During the first years of its existence, Dynamo predominantly took part in friendly games and until 1935 participated in the annual Ukrainian championship contested by member teams of Dynamo Society.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: After the Soviet government relocated Ukraine's capital to Kyiv in 1934, Dynamo received patronage from the republican leadership.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: In 1936, the first Soviet Championship was played, and Dynamo Kyiv was one of the pioneers of the newly formed league.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Under the leadership of head coach Mikhail Tovarovsky, the club finished second in the 1936 Soviet Top League, losing in the final to Spartak Moscow.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: In 1937 Dynamo took the 3rd place in the championship.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Some sports organizations and individual athletes were evacuated to Soviet Central Asia or east of the Volga River.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Many footballers joined the ranks of the Soviet Red Army either voluntarily or through mobilization.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Kyiv ended up under German occupation within a few months of Operation Barbarossa due to the successful encirclement of Soviet troops by German forces.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: After the Nazi occupation of Ukraine began, former professional football players (Dynamo and Lokomotyv) found employment in the city's Bakery No. 3, and continued to play amateur football.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The team participated in exhibition games that took place in the city among various other teams, including teams composed of the Wehrmacht soldiers.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: In July and August 1942, "Start" played a series of matches against the Germans and their allies.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: On 12 July, a German army team was defeated.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: A stronger army team was selected for the next match on 17 July, which "Start" defeated 6–0.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: On 19 July, "Start" defeated the Hungarian team MSG Wal 5–1.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: On 9 August, "Start" played a "friendly" against Flakelf and again defeated them.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The team defeated Rukh 8:0 on 16 August, and afterwards, some of "Start"'s players were arrested by the Gestapo, tortured – Mykola Korotkykh died during the torture – and sent to the nearby labour camp at Syrets.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: There is speculation that the players were arrested due to the intrigues of Georgy Shvetsov, founder and trainer of the "Rukh" team, as the arrests were made a couple of days after "Start" defeated "Rukh".
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: In February 1943, following an attack by partisans or a conflict between the prisoners and administration, one-third of the prisoners at Syrets were killed in reprisal, including Ivan Kuzmenko, Oleksiy Klymenko, and goalkeeper Mykola Trusevych.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Soviet war propaganda used the history of "Start" for mobilization purposes, claiming that the whole team had been executed by a firing squad in the summer of 1942 for defeating an All-Star team from the German armed forces by 5–1.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The match has subsequently came to be known in the Soviet media as "The Death Match", becoming part of the post-Soviet myth of the Great Patriotic War.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: From the pre-war (World War II) team there remained Anton Idzkovsky, Mykola Makhynia, Petro Laiko, Pavlo Vinkovatov, Mykola Balakin, Kostyantyn Kalach, including those who participated in the 1942 matches Makar Honcharenko and former Lokomotyv Kyiv players Volodymyr Balakin, Vasyl Sukharev.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: In the first post-war years, all those who remained in the team were already quite old to play on the first team.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: In 1945, Dynamo took the penultimate place in the championship, and in 1946, the very last, and, according to the regulations, it was supposed to be relegated, but an exception was made for the team, remembering the wartime losses.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: In addition, these events were accompanied by coaching fever: from 1946 to 1951, the club changed ten coaches.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The 1948 season was the last that Dynamo took part in republican competitions, particularly the 1948 Football Cup of the Ukrainian SSR.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: To the Ukrainian football competitions, Dynamo returned only after dissolution of the Soviet Union in 1992.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The first post-war success was the victory in the doubles (reserves) tournament in the 1949 season.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Since 1946, the Soviet first-tier league has been conducting a championship among younger players, which ran parallel to the championship among the first squads.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The turning point came during the 1951 season, before which Oleg Oshenkov took charge of the club.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The new coach introduced to the main team younger players who had proven themselves well in doubles (reserves) competitions.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: He also drastically shortened the winter vacation of his players, offering them a serious physical training program that included sports games, various exercises, and even boxing.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Already in the next championship, which took place in a round robin in Moscow, it brought the first results.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Dynamo Kyiv turned from a mid-table team into one of the favorites, winning the silver medals, just behind Spartak Moscow.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The club's success was contributed to by the support of Tymofiy Strokach, who served as the club's patron between the late 1930s and 1956.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Oshenkov's players achieved their first big victory during the 1954 Soviet Cup.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: On the way to the finals, the Kyiv team defeated Spartak Vilnius (4:2), Spartak Moscow (3:1), CDKA (3:1, in extra time), Zenit Leningrad (1:0, in extra time).
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: In the cup's final at Moscow's "Dynamo Stadium", Kyiv's team faced off with a poorly known (at that time) Spartak Yerevan.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The match took place in heavy rain and fog, but all the same, the Kyivans were able to defeat their opponents and win the Soviet Cup for the first time in their history.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Goals in the final were scored by Terentiev and Koman.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: On 29 July 1959, an international friendly match between the football teams "Dynamo" (Kyiv, Ukraine) and "Dynamo" (Bacău, Romania) took place in Kyiv, which ended with a score of 3:0.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: At the end of the 1950s, the Dynamo revamped its squad.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The club left Yevhen Lemeshko, Leonid Ostroushko, Ernest Yust, Mykola Romanov, Yuriy Shevchenko, Vitaliy Sobolev.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The club's ranks were refilled with Serhiy Bohachyk, Ishtvan Sekech, Valeriy Lobanovskyi, Yevhen Snitko, Andriy Havashi, Vasyl Turyanchyk, Yozhef Sabo, while a well-known former CDKA player (the "Team of Lieutenants"), Vyacheslav Solovyov became the head coach.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The 1960 season brought the Kyivans the "silver".
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: In the 1961 season, Dynamo won the Soviet Union championship for the first time.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The team from the capital of the Ukrainian SSR finished ahead of Torpedo Moscow (title holders) by 4 points.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Dynamo Kyiv played 30 matches in the national championship (16 participants).
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Only three of those matches Dynamo lost, and nine ended in a draw.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The fact that they scored as many as 54 goals in 30 games testifies to the strength of the Dynamo's offensive line, where played such players like Oleh Bazylevych, Viktor Kanevskyi, Valeriy Lobanovskyi, Viktor Serebryanikov.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: And about the strength of the defensive line - the fact that the experienced goalkeeper Oleg Makarov never had to take the ball out of the net in 12 matches.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: It was the first time in the history of the Soviet Union championships, when the national title of the country's champion was gained by a non-Moscow club.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

title: FC Dynamo Kyiv
