title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Midfielders: Yozhef Sabo (27, 3), Yuriy Voynov (21, 3), Volodymyr Anufriyenko (15), Vladimir Sorokin (3), Valeriy Verigin (3), Viktor Pestrykov (1).
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Forwards: Viktor Serebryanikov (29, 4), Valeriy Lobanovskyi (28, 10), Viktor Kanevskyi (26, 18), Oleh Bazylevych (26, 10), Andriy Biba (17, 5), Valentyn Troyanovskyi (15, 2), Mykola Kashtanov (11, 1), Igor Zaytsev (8, 1).
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Thanks to the support of Volodymyr Shcherbytsky, the players were provided favourable conditions to achieve highest results.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: After the triumphant season of 1961, in the following two seasons, Dynamo's position significantly worsened.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: In 1962, the team took 5th place, and the following year, 7th.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: In January 1964, Viktor Maslov took over as the club's head coach.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: On September 27, 1964, Dynamo won the 1964 Soviet Cup by defeating Krylia Sovetov Kuibyshev (Samara) in the final with a score of 1:0.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Maslov and his subordinates were entrusted with becoming the first Soviet club to participate in a European club tournament.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: This was the 1965–66 UEFA Cup Winners' Cup.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The political motives of the USSR leadership explain everything.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The communist ideology did not accept the possibility of Soviet athletes losing to capitalist rivals and played it safe for a long time.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: For example, the 1964 Soviet champion, Dinamo Tbilisi, was not trusted to participate in the 1965–66 European Cup.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: We had to play "blindly": none of the opponents were known to us.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: There were no tapes of matches of future opponents anywhere near us.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: And for a representative of the coaching staff to go and watch the opponent's game live was something out of the realm of fantasy (see Iron Curtain).
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: However, the start of the tournament was successful as Dynamo defeated Coleraine from Northern Ireland with a score of 6:1 and 4:0.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: After that, Dynamo beat the Norwegian Rosenborg twice more - 4:1 and 2:0.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: But in the quarterfinals they were eliminated by Celtic (0:3 and 1:1), with whom they had to play in mid-January.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Therefore, the playing form of the Kyiv team was far from optimal, and in addition, they played their home game not in Kyiv but in Tbilisi.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The following year, 1966, was one of the best in the history of Dynamo Kyiv.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: In 1967 and 1968, Dynamo again won the Soviet Class A Group 1, thereby repeating the record of Moscow's CDKA, three championship titles in a row.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: In the same 1967, the Kyiv team made its debut in the 1967–68 European Cup.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Maslov's team sensationally eliminated the current holder of the trophy, Scottish Celtic F.C., in the first stage, but lost to Polish champion Górnik Zabrze in the round of 16.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: In 1969, Dynamo Kyiv finished second in the league, behind Spartak Moscow.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: In the 1969–70 European Cup, the Kyiv team defeated FK Austria Wien 2-1 and 3–1, but lost to Italian AC Fiorentina 1-2 and 0-0.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The following season, Dynamo finished only 7th in the championship.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Turyanchyk, Sabo, Bannikov, and Porkujan left the team, and after the season ended, coach Viktor Maslov also left Dynamo.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: In 1971, the team was coached by Honored coach of the USSR Aleksandr Sevidov, and 22-year-old Viktor Kolotov joined the club from Rubin.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: He later became one of the best midfielders in the history of Soviet football, being the captain of Dynamo for seven years, and in 1975–1976, the Soviet Union national team.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Dynamo immediately won the Soviet championship, and the team's goalkeeper, Yevhen Rudakov, was recognized as the best goalkeeper and football player of the Soviet Union.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: In January 1974, Lobanovskyi was joined by his former Dynamo partner Oleh Bazylevych, who had coached Shakhtar Donetsk after his retirement.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: This tandem worked until the end of 1976.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Both coaches had equal rights, although Bazylevych was primarily a prominent theorist, while Lobanovskyi organized the training process.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: In 1974, Anatoliy Puzach also joined the staff.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Oleh Bazylevych initiated an invitation to join the club of physical training scientists.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: But good results were achieved in 1974, the Kyiv team won the 1974 Soviet Top League and the 1974 Soviet Cup.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: A team was formed that could compete with the strongest teams in Europe.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Yevhen Rudakov was in goal, Viktor Matvienko, Stefan Reshko, Mykhailo Fomenko, and Volodymyr Troshkin played in defense.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Together with Oleh Blokhin, at the point of attack played Volodymyr Onyshchenko.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: The traditional Soviet "list of the 33 best football players" in 1974 included 8 Dynamo players, 7 of them at the number 1 position.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: During the 1970s and 1980s Dynamo's players constituted the base of the Soviet Union national team.
title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

title: FC Dynamo Kyiv

text: Vyshyvanka (Ukrainian: вишиванка [wɪʃɪˈwɑnkɐ]; Belarusian: вышыванка, romanized: vyšyvanka [vɨʂɨˈvanka]) is a casual name for the embroidered shirt in Ukrainian and Belarusian national costumes.

text: Varenyky (Ukrainian: вареники, sing.: varenyk) are traditional Ukrainian dumplings made from unleavened dough and filled with sweet or savoury ingredients.
title: Varenyky

text: They are regarded as one of the most emblematic dishes of Ukrainian cuisine and are closely related to other East European dumplings such as Polish pierogi.
title: Varenyky

text: They were commonly prepared for festive occasions, harvest celebrations, and religious holidays.
title: Varenyky

text: In western Ukrainian regions, including Hutsul areas, dumplings are prepared by placing them on a cloth stretched over a pot of boiling water and steaming them until soft.
title: Varenyky

text: This traditional method produces a delicate texture and is often associated with potato and cheese fillings.
title: Varenyky

text: They are often associated with prosperity and family traditions.
title: Varenyky

text: A monument dedicated to varenyky was erected in Cherkasy Oblast, highlighting the dish's cultural popularity.
title: Varenyky

text: A yearly festival commemorating varenyky is held at the Ukrainian ski resort town of Bukovel in the Carpathian Mountains.
title: Varenyky

text: In 2013, a snow monument to varenyky was made in Bukovel, and was submitted to the Guinness Book of Records as the biggest snow varenyk in the world.
title: Varenyky

text: In Ukrainian tradition, varenyky were equated with a young moon due to the similar shape, and were used as part of pagan and sacrificial rituals.
title: Varenyky

text: The most striking general characteristic of authentic Ukrainian folk music is the common use of minor keys containing augmented seconds, an indication that the tonality system developed in Western European music did not become as entrenched in Ukraine.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Ukrainian folk music rarely uses unusual time signatures, but compound metres are encountered.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: The music can be harmonically complex.Three- and four-part harmony spread from the central steppe regions of Ukraine, but had become less popular in the mountain regions by the late 19th century.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Ritual songs show the greatest tendency to preservation.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: They are frequently in recitative style, essentially monodic, based on notes in the range of a third or a fourth.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: An example of this style is the theme for the Shchedrivka "Shchedryk" known in the West as "Carol of the Bells".
title: Music of Ukraine

text: A large group of Ukrainian ritual melodies fall within a perfect fourth with the main central tone as the lowest note.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Many of the ritual Easter melodies known as Haivky fall into this category.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: The tetrachordal system is also found in wedding and harvest songs.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Folk dances often have melodies based on two tetrachords fused together.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: The pentatonic scale in anhemitonic form is common in Vesnianka songs.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: The bulk of Ukrainian folk songs melodies are based on scales identical to medieval modes, but differ in melodic structure.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: The Mixolydian and Dorian modes are used more often than Ionian and Aeolian modes.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: This is a feature of traditional paraliturgical koliadky.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: The augmented 2nd interval is found, as well as the raising of the fourth and seventh degree of the scale.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: This melodic manner gives an effect that is described as adding severe tension or sadness in some Ukrainian songs.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: They included holosinnya that would be sung at a Ukrainian wake.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Solo singing with an instrumental accompaniment was performed by a kobzar or a lirnyky, who were professional itinerant musicians.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: They organized themselves into regional professional guild-like structures, known as a Kobzars'kyj Tsekh.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: The Soviets killed almost all of the traditional kobzari during the purges of the 1930s, and their instruments were prohibited.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Under the inspiration of noted traditional bandurist Heorhiy Tkachenko a Kobzar Guild was re-established in 1991 in Kyiv by Mykola Budnyk in order to revive and foster the ancient kobzar traditions.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: The Guild unites many singer-musicians in Ukraine and the Ukrainian diaspora.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Many of its members are not formally Conservatory trained.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Another style of singing involved a type of modal a cappella, in which a musical phrase is sung by a soloist before being answered by the chorus in a harmonic phrase.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: It is medieval in character, with a distinctly Ukrainian characteristics such as parallel fifths, octaves, and types of plagal cadences.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: This type of music lost its dominance after 1650 to newer tonal types, but can still be heard in isolated villages.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Traditional instrumental ensembles are often known as troïstï muzyki (literally ‘three musicians’ that typically make up the ensemble, e.g. violin, sopilka and buben).
title: Music of Ukraine

text: When performing dance melodies instrumental performance usually includes improvisation.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: The traditional dances of Ukraine include: the kozachok, tropak, hopak, hrechanyky, kolomyjka, hutsulka, metelytsia, shumka, arkan, kateryna, and the chabarashka.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Dances originating outside the Ukrainian ethnic region but which are also popular include: the polka, mazurka, krakowiak, csárdás, waltz, kamarinskaya, and the barynya.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: This gradually developed into an intolerance towards organized religion, and religious music was purged by the regime.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Much classical music was also denounced as being bourgeois and decadent.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: During the 1920s, "proletarian songs"—music of the working people—emerged.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: When it was realised that this music did not cater for the rural population, their songs were included as being of working class origin, and "fakeloric" ensembles were created.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: After World War II, resources continued to be given to support this style of music, in order to counter the arrival of mass culture from the West.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Numerous folk choirs were established such as the Veriovka folk choir (directed by Hryhoriy Veryovka).
title: Music of Ukraine

text: A stylized dance troupe was established by Pavlo Virsky based on a synthesis of ethnographic dance and ballet.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: It is estimated that at the beginning of the 19th century, a third of the total Jewish population of Europe lived in Ukrainian lands.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Russian Empire and Soviet folklorists who collected and documented Jewish music—primarily in Ukraine—included Shloyme Ansky, Susman Kiselgof, Moisei Beregovsky, Joel Engel, and Sofia Magid.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Many of their collections are kept in the National Library of Ukraine.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Many of the early performers of Russian folk instruments came from Ukraine, and they sometimes included Ukrainian melodies in their repertoire.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Other composers who lived in territories now in modern Ukraine—such as Franz Xavier Mozart, Isaak Dunayevsky, Rheinhold Gliere, and Sergei Prokofiev—were rarely influenced in this way.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: It had an important place in the curriculum of the Kyiv-Mohyla Academy.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Many of the nobility were skillful musicians, with Cossack leaders—including Ivan Mazepa, Semyon Paliy, and Ivan Sirko—being accomplished players of the kobza, bandura, or torban.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: During the 18th century, imperial court musicians were typically trained at the music academy in Hlukhiv, and largely originated from Ukraine.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Notable performers included Timofiy Bilohradsky, who studied the lute under Sylvius Leopold Weiss in Dresden, his niece the soprano Yelizaveta Belogradskaya, and the court bandurist Alexey Razumovsky.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: The first professional music academy in the Russian Empire was established in Ukraine in Hlukhiv in 1738, where students were taught to sing, and to play violin and the bandura.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: A Ukrainian nationalist movement in music appeared during the late 19th century, in which Ukrainian writers, poets, and professional musicians used folklore to arrange traditional folk songs.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: A key figure in the development of Ukrainian nationalist music was the composer, conductor and pianist Mykola Lysenko.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: His compositions include nine operas, and music for piano.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: He used Ukrainian poetry, including that of the poet Taras Shevchenko.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: In 1904, Lysenko opened the Russian Empire's first Ukrainian music school in Kyiv (now the Lysenko State Music and Drama Institute).
title: Music of Ukraine

text: He is credited with establishing a classical music tradition that was a blend of folk music and classical music forms.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: The short-lived Ukrainian People's Republic took a consistent position in the field of cultural development, including music.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: In particular, by a decree of Pavlo Skoropadsky in 1918, the State Symphony Orchestra of Ukraine, the Ukrainian State Chapel, and the First and Second National Choirs were established.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: The Kyiv opera was named the Ukrainian Drama and Opera Theatre, and a number of world-famous operas were translated into Ukrainian.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: In 1918, the Kobzar Choir was founded, which would later be revived as the State Bandura Chapel.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: The arrival of Soviet authorities in Ukraine was marked by a number of tragic events.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: In 1921, Mykola Leontovych was murdered by a Cheka agent; the society established in his memory soon afterwards was banned in 1928.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: The Soviet authorities established music institutions across Ukraine.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Choral and symphonic groups, and opera and ballet theatres were formed in Kharkiv (1925), Poltava (1928), Vinnytsia (1929), Dnipropetrovsk (1931), and Donetsk (1941).
title: Music of Ukraine

text: According to the 1932 Resolution of the Central Committee of the Communist Party "On the Restructuring of Literary and Artistic Organizations", The Union of Soviet Musicians of Ukraine was formed.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: It was entrusted with ideological control over Ukraine's composers.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Among Ukrainian composers of the Soviet period were Borys Lyatoshynsky, Lev Revutsky, Mykola Vilinsky, Anatoliy Kos-Anatolsky, Andriy Shtoharenko, Mykola Dremlyuga, and Vitaliy Kyreiko.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: The 1960s and beyond have been marked by the breakthrough of the Ukrainian school of composition on the world stage, and the mastery by Ukrainian composers of the latest trends in European musical culture.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: An informal group of composers formed the Kyiv avant-garde.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Festivals of avant-garde music such as Kyiv Music Fest, Kontrasty, and Two Days and Two Nights of New Music were established.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: In the 2000s, experimental electronic music in Ukraine began to develop.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Ukraine's new generation composers include Oleksandr Kozarenko, Volodymyr Runchak, Hanna Havrylets', Alla Zahaikevych, and Ivan Nebesnyy.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Musicians and ensembles that are difficult to categorize include the avant-garde and folk singer and composer Mariana Sadovska, the historicist lutenist-composer Roman Turovsky, Dakha Brakha, Dakh Daughters, and the New Era Orchestra.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: In 2026, the Verkhovna Rada established Ukrainian Music Day, a national commemorative day observed annually on the third Saturday of September.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: The initiative aims to promote Ukrainian musical heritage, performers, composers, and the contemporary music industry.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: It has grown in popularity since the 1960s; over the next decade, a number of folk rock groups emerged.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Major contributions were made by the songwriters Volodymyr Ivasyuk and Oleksandr Bilash.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: After Ivasyuk's death in 1979, developments in Ukrainian pop music largely stopped.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Even established folk-rock groups such as Kobza began to sing in Russian instead of Ukrainian.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Ukrainian pop music revived in 1990s after Ukraine became independent.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: The Chervona Ruta Festival played an important role in popularisation and evolution of the Ukrainian pop music.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Modest Mussorgsky – unfinished opera The Fair at Sorochyntsi, based on Nikolai Gogol's story.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Act 1 has a song based on a Ukrainian tune.

title: Music of Ukraine

text: Rachmaninoff – the opening of the Piano Concerto No 3 (1909) is based on a Ukrainian Orthodox church chant.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Tchaikovsky – Symphony No. 2 ('Little Russian') (1872); opera Mazepa is set in Ukraine.
title: Music of Ukraine

text: Russian-language songs gradually lost popularity in Ukraine after 2014, while Ukrainian-language music experienced a surge; this trend accelerated when the 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine began in February 2022.

text: The Battle of Bakhmut was a major battle between the Russian Armed Forces and the Ukrainian Armed Forces for control of the city of Bakhmut, during the eastern front, a theatre of the Russo-Ukrainian war.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: It is regarded by some military analysts to be the bloodiest battle since World War II.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The shelling of Bakhmut began in May 2022, and Russian offensives on the distant approaches to the city began in early July.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The main assault towards the city itself started after Russian forces advanced from the direction of Popasna following a Ukrainian withdrawal from that front.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The main assault force consisted primarily of mercenaries from the Russian paramilitary organization Wagner Group, supported by regular Russian troops and reportedly Donetsk People's Republic militia elements.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: In late 2022, following Ukraine's Kharkiv and Kherson counteroffensives, the Bakhmut–Soledar front became an important focus of the war, being one of the few front lines where Russia remained on the offensive.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Attacks on the city intensified in November 2022, as assaulting Russian forces were reinforced by units redeployed from the Kherson front, together with newly mobilized recruits.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: By this time, much of the front line had descended into positional trench warfare, with both sides suffering high casualties without any significant advances.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: By using repeated assaults composed of former convicts, Wagner troops were able to gradually gain ground and by February 2023, they captured territory in the north and south of Bakhmut and threatened encirclement.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Ukrainian forces began slowly withdrawing deeper into the city and the battle turned into fierce urban warfare.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: By March 2023, Russian forces captured the eastern half of the city, up to the Bakhmutka river.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: By 20 May 2023, Bakhmut had been mostly captured by Russian forces, with the Ukrainian military claiming control of a small strip of the city proper along the T0504 highway.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Nonetheless, Ukraine started counterattacks on Russia's flanks, seeking to encircle the city.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Around the same time on 25 May, Wagner began withdrawing from the city to be replaced by regular Russian troops, amidst heavy internal squabbles between Wagner leadership and Russian high command.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: In September 2023, President Zelensky said Ukraine would continue to fight to retake Bakhmut.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The battle of Bakhmut has been described as a "meat grinder" and a "vortex" for both the Ukrainian and Russian militaries.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Pro-Russian separatists had captured parts of the city during the 2014 pro-Russian unrest in Ukraine in April, and a Ukrainian special forces unit together with the National Guard were dispatched to expel the separatists from the city.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The separatists were expelled to the city's outskirts where clashes continued until July 2014, when they finally retreated from the area.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: During the Russian invasion of Ukraine, a key Russian goal was to capture the Donbas region, consisting of Donetsk and Luhansk oblasts.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The initial push for Bakhmut was part of an attempt to encircle the Ukrainian forces at the Sievierodonetsk-Lysychansk salient; together with another push from the Lyman direction, it would create a pocket and trap Ukrainian forces there.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Starting on 17 May 2022, Russian forces began shelling Bakhmut, killing five people including a two-year-old child.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: After the fall of Popasna, Ukrainian forces retreated from the city to reinforce positions at Bakhmut by 22 May.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Meanwhile, Russian forces managed to advance on the Bakhmut-Lysychansk highway, endangering the remaining Ukrainian troops in the Lysychansk-Sievierodonetsk area.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The Russian checkpoint along the highway was later demolished, although fighting resumed on 30 May along the Kostiantynivka-Bakhmut highway, where Ukrainian forces successfully defended the highway.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Shelling of Bakhmut continued throughout the rest of June and July.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Following the battles of Sievierodonetsk and Lysychansk in early July, Russian and separatist forces captured all of Luhansk oblast, and the battlefield shifted towards the cities of Bakhmut and Soledar.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 25 July, Ukrainian forces withdrew from the Vuhlehirska Power Station, along with the nearby town of Novoluhanske, giving Russian and separatist forces a "small tactical advantage" towards Bakhmut.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Two days later on 27 July, Russian shelling of Bakhmut killed three civilians and wounded three more.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Five days later, it was reported that Russian troops had taken full control of the village, as part of setting conditions to advance west on Bakhmut.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 1 August 2022, Russian forces launched assaults on settlements southeast and northeast of Bakhmut, including Yakovlivka, Soledar, and Vershyna, which the Ukrainian General Staff said it had repelled.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Pro-Russian Telegram channels claimed that Russian forces were within two kilometers of the city.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The following day, Ukraine reported that Russian forces had increased airstrikes and shelling of Bakhmut, beginning a ground attack on the southeastern part of the city.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 4 August, Wagner Group mercenaries managed to break through Ukrainian defenses and reach Patrice Lumumba street on the eastern outskirts of Bakhmut.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: In the following days, Russian forces continued to push towards Bakhmut from the south, with the Ukrainian general staff stating on 14 August that Russian forces had achieved "partial success" near Bakhmut, but offering no specifics.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Night shelling in the city center on 21 September burned the Martynov Palace of Culture, where the humanitarian headquarters worked.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: During the extinguishing of the fire, the local fire department was shelled, which reported that two SES staff were injured and equipment damaged.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: At night, a five-story building was also partially destroyed by Russian shelling.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: A Russian missile strike on 22 September destroyed the main bridge across the Bakhmutka river that bisects the city, disrupting both civilian travel and Ukrainian military logistics.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 7 October, Russian forces advanced into the villages of Zaitseve and Opytne on the southern and southeastern outskirts of Bakhmut, while on 10 October, the UK Defence Ministry claimed that Russian troops advanced closer to Bakhmut.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 12 October, Russian forces claimed to have captured Opytne, located 3 km south of Bakhmut, and Ivanhrad, although these towns were still contested.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 1 November, Ukrainian journalist Yurii Butusov described the evolving nature of the battle in an interview.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Butusov noted that Russian forces had suffered "huge losses every day" assaulting Bakhmut and its outskirts since early May, but insisted that they were adapting their tactics against increasingly exhausted Ukrainian defenders.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: He noted that the Russians were concentrating multiple small groups of infantry to break defense lines on "narrow" sections of the front.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Russian milbloggers claimed that Russian forces breached defense lines on Bakhmut's southern approach, allegedly capturing the villages of Andriivka, Ozarianivka, and Zelenopillia and making minor advances in Opytne through 28–29 November.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Wagner fighters attacked Kurdyumivka, adjacent to Ozarianivka, with some Russian milbloggers claiming the settlement was captured.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Russian forces also attacked Ukrainian positions southeast of Bakhmut.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The same day, a Georgia military volunteer told the media that a group of Georgian volunteers had been surrounded during clashes near Bakhmut.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The commander was wounded and five or six volunteers, serving in Ukraine's 57th Brigade, had been killed, prompting Georgian president Salome Zourabichvili to express condolences.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 6–7 December, the Russian defense ministry claimed that their forces, including Wagner fighters, had successfully repelled Ukrainian counterattacks south of Bakhmut.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The commander of the Ukraine National Guard's Svoboda Battalion, defending Bakhmut's southern flank, said they were "fighting for every bush" and predicted Russia would struggle to overcome a canal above and behind Kurdiumivka.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 9 December, President Volodymyr Zelenskyy accused Russia of "destroying" Bakhmut, calling it "another Donbas city that the Russian army turned into burnt ruins".
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Former soldier and eyewitness to the battle Petro Stone called the Bakhmut front a "meat grinder", saying the Russians were "covering Bakhmut with fire 24/7".
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Soldiers of Ukraine's 24th Mechanized Brigade recounted recent battlefield engagements to media, such as one multi-day firefight with 50 Russian troops dug into a treeline where in some places "we were only 100 metres apart".
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Ukrainian soldiers claimed that front line Russian troops often attacked with little tank support, with Wagner PMC fighters serving as the main assault troops and under-equipped mobiks (recently mobilized Russian recruits) holding defensive positions.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: One Ukrainian artillerymen alleged that "80 percent" of the remaining civilian population, surviving in basements and supplied by mobile grocery trucks that periodically enter the city, was pro-Russian.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 11 December a railway bridge over the E40 (M-03) highway north of Bakhmut was destroyed; the Russians accused the Ukrainians of demolishing it to hamper future Russian advances towards Sloviansk.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The Ukrainian General Staff said they successfully repelled assaults northeast and south of Bakhmut from the Soledar and Kurdiumivka directions, respectively.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 17 December, footage emerged online of trenches in Bakhmut's city center, indicating Ukrainian defenders were preparing for urban combat.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 18–19 December, Ukrainian forces, purportedly including dismounted infantry supported by British-donated Wolfhound Tactical Supply Vehicles, counterattacked along Fyodor Maksimenko Street and pushed Wagner forces back to the eastern outskirts of the suburban area amid "grinding" street clashes.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Meanwhile, Ukraine's Joint Forces Task Force reported repelling "five to seven" Russian infiltration groups near Bakhmut daily.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: A Ukrainian commander reported that an abundance of drone surveillance allowed for quick responses to small Russian assaults on the outskirts, while also alleging that Russia did not control Bakhmut's eastern industrial zone.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The Institute for the Study of War (ISW), a Western think tank and war observer, could not independently verify the claim of Ukraine entirely controlling the outskirts at the time.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 20–21 December, President Zelenskyy made an unannounced visit to the Bakhmut front, where he met with soldiers, awarded medals and delivered speeches.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Meanwhile, heavy shelling and fighting on Bakhmut's outskirts continued as Russian forces continuously attempted to break entrenched Ukrainian positions on the city's flanks.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Reportedly, Wagner fighters were assaulting strongholds in Bakhmutske, Pidhorodne, and Klishchiivka, located along Bakhmut's northeastern and southwestern flanks respectively, while the Ukrainians continued to hold northern Opytne, blunting Russia's advance from the south.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 26 December, Ukraine's governor of Donetsk, Pavlo Kyrylenko, said over 60 percent of Bakhmut's infrastructure was damaged or destroyed.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The ISW judged that Russia's advance on Bakhmut had "culminated" by 28 December, assessing that Russian and Wagner forces had grown increasingly unable to sustain the previous scale of infantry assaults and artillery barrages.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: In February, Russian forces solidified gains north of Bakhmut and began pressuring Ukrainian troops on the northern axis, making incremental gains in the towns north of the city.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 1 February, The New York Times reported that the Russians had increased the intensity of attacks on Bakhmut and its surrounding areas.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Days later on 5 February, the British Ministry of Defence stated that Russian troops were able to fire upon the M03 and H32 roads north of the city, the main Ukrainian supply route for northern Bakhmut.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 11 February, Russian forces captured the village of Krasna Hora northeast of Bakhmut.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Around this time, analysts suggested Russian losses had increased to 820 casualties a day between Bakhmut and the battle of Vuhledar.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: By 13 February, the Ukrainian government claimed their defenses in the village of Paraskoviivka were waning, with fierce battles around the clock.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: By 22 February, Russian forces partially encircled Bakhmut from the east, south, and north.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: By 3 March, Ukrainian soldiers had destroyed two key bridges, creating the possibility for a controlled fighting withdrawal.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 4 March, Bakhmut's deputy mayor told news services that there was street fighting but that Russian forces had not taken control of the city.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The same day, the chief of the Wagner Group, Yevgeny Prigozhin, said that the city was encircled except for one road still controlled by the Ukrainian military, as had been the case since 22 February.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 5 March Ukrainian commander Oleksandr Syrskyi said the fighting had reached the "highest level of tension".
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 7 March, Ukraine withdrew from areas of Bakhmut east of the Bakhmutka river, making the river the front line between the opposing forces.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 9 March, the MiG-17 monument, which had become a symbol of Ukrainian resistance in Bakhmut during the battle, was destroyed by Russian forces.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 6 April, Russian forces took control of the city center.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 14 April, the British defence ministry observed that Russia had "re-energised its assault on the Donetsk Oblast town of Bakhmut as forces of the Russian MoD and Wagner Group have improved co-operation ...
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 18 April, Ukrainian General Oleksandr Syrskyi reported that Russia was "increasing the activity of heavy artillery and the number of air strikes, turning the city into ruins".
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 26 April, the Ukrainian Air Force dropped four 500-pound GBU-62/B JDAM glide bombs on a high-rise building in the Russian-controlled part of Bakhmut, possibly from two MiG-29s.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Meanwhile, as Russian attacks culminated, Wagner Group leader Yevgeny Prigozhin sharply increased his public criticism of the Russian Ministry of Defence's management of the battle.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 1 May, Ukrainian spokesman Serhiy Cherevaty gave an estimate of the current strength of Russian forces attacking the city.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: He stated that "on the Bakhmut direction, 25,600 personnel, 65 tanks, 450 armored fighting vehicles, 154 guns, 56 rocket salvo systems are fighting against us".
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 2 May, Yevgeny Prigozhin complained that there was a shortage of ammunition, with his forces needing 300 tonnes a day.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Within a few days, Prigozhin threatened that he would withdraw all Wagner personnel from the city by 10 May, the day after Victory Day.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: He further denounced Sergei Shoigu and Valery Gerasimov, saying: "in the absence of ammunition [Wagner personnel are] doomed to perish senselessly." Chechen leader Ramzan Kadyrov agreed to replace Wagner forces with Chechen units in the city, according to Prigozhin.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 6 May, Ukrainian military sources reported that the Russians were shelling the city with phosphorus munitions, with several videos and photos being released that showed the effects of the bombardment.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The BBC, analyzing the footage, said that while the footage definitely showed Russian forces using some sort of incendiary munitions, it was unable to verify that it was phosphorus specifically.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 7 May, Prigozhin announced that Gerasimov would resume artillery ammunition distribution to Wagner forces, that Wagner forces would remain in Bakhmut, and that Sergey Surovikin would be acting as an intermediary between Wagner and the Russian Ministry of Defence.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The ISW assessed that Gerasimov likely agreed to keep Kadyrov out of the Russian High Command and that the situation showcased that the Russian Ministry of Defense was having difficulty commanding Wagner forces.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Colonel General Oleksandr Syrskyi, Ukrainian Commander of Ground Forces, said that Prigozhin's claims of ammunition shortages were false, saying that the Russians were "pummeling" their positions.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 9–10 May, Ukrainian forces launched counterattacks on Bakhmut's outskirts, expelling Russian troops from the southern bank of the Berkhivka Reservoir, located about 4 kilometers northwest of Bakhmut.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The Ukrainians said their 3rd Separate Assault Brigade had cleared an area 1,730 metres wide and 700 metres deep, routing units of Russia's 72nd Separate Motorized Rifle Brigade and killing 23 soldiers and wounding over 40.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: One tank and four AFVs were also destroyed, with Ukrainian Border Guards also repelling Russian advances from within the city itself, destroying an 11-man assault group.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The brigade's 3rd Separate Assault Battalion alone was reportedly responsible for 64 Russians killed, while information on another 87 "eliminated" was being "clarified" at the time.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: They also claimed to have taken five prisoners.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Prigozhin accused the regular Russian forces of "[running] away" from their positions in response to the counterattacks, while Wagner forces were allegedly forbidden from retreating under threat of "high treason" charges.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: He claimed Russia's defence ministry was more focused on internal power struggles and "intrigues" than actually fighting, and also accused the ministry of only providing his troops 10% of what was promised, resulting in high Wagner casualties.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: He again threatened that if he did not receive ammunition, Wagner would withdraw from their positions.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 18 May, Prigozhin said that regular Russian forces had withdrawn up to 570 meters (1,880 feet) to the north of the city, exposing Wagner's flanks, while his soldiers had advanced up to 400 meters inside the city.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 19 May, Ukrainian sources claimed Ukrainian troops had advanced a mile in some areas, or "150 to 1,700 metres".
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: A Ukrainian commander, Petro Podaru, said that Russian artillery fire had transitioned towards preventing Ukrainian forces from "deliver(ing) more infantry, ammunition and other things" via access roads west of Bakhmut.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The Russian defence ministry also subsequently stated that the city had been captured.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The ISW was unable to immediately confirm Prigozhin's statement and reiterated Ukrainian reports that fighting continued, particularly citing that the 3rd Separate Assault Brigade had recaptured a region 1,750 meters wide and 700 meters deep in the suburbs.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 21 May, the Ukrainian defence ministry claimed that its forces had "taken the city in a semi-encirclement".
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On the other hand, officials described their holdout around the T0504 highway as "insignificant".
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Based on this, the ISW assessed that Ukrainian forces had indeed withdrawn from the city, except for some fortified areas adjacent to the two highways on the city's western approach.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Prigozhin again insisted the entirety of Bakhmut had been captured "right up to the last centimetre" and added that Wagner made no advances on 21 May as they were preparing to withdraw later in the week.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Geolocated footage published on 21 May showed that Wagner forces had advanced towards the T0504 entrance to southwestern Bakhmut.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Clashes were reportedly taking place as of 22 May in localities neighboring Bakhmut.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 23 May, the Ukrainian General Staff did not declare fighting in Bakhmut for the first time since December 2022.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Ukrainian officials insisted that Ukraine held positions near the former MiG-17 monument in southwestern Bakhmut in spite of footage purportedly showing Wagner forces near the monument.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Fighting in the localities outside of Bakhmut's city limits continued.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 25 May, Prigozhin promised to withdraw all Wagner forces from Bakhmut to transfer full control to Russian units by 1 June.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The ISW assessed that the units supplanting Wagner forces within the city were from the Donetsk People's Republic militia.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: In their 26 May report, the ISW cited Russian milbloggers as reporting on successful Ukrainian counterattacks near Orikhovo-Vasylivka and Klischiivka, northwest and southwest of Bakhmut respectively.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: In their 27 May report, the ISW noted a substantial decrease in Russian offensive activities in and around Bakhmut.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Hanna Maliar attributed this to Russian forces performing relief in place operations to cover Wagner withdrawals.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: They also noted failed Russian attacks against the suburbs of Khromove and Predtechyne and that there was no change to the status of the reported Ukrainian-controlled strip in the city's southwestern outskirts.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The ISW further assessed that the 31st Guards Air Assault Brigade had recently been sent to Bakhmut to cover Russian flanks.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The ISW also speculated that Russia would redirect their forces from Bakhmut to the Avdiivka-Donetsk front.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Prigozhin said on 28 May that regular Russian forces were performing relief operations, and that the full withdrawal of Wagner forces would not take place until 5 June.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The rate of Russian assaults remained low, with only two reported attacks against the suburbs of Orikhovo-Vasylivka and Ivanivske, both of which were unsuccessful.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Ukrainian Colonel Serhiy Cherevaty reported one instance of fighting near Bakhmut and published geolocated footage that indicated that Russian forces had made marginal gains west of Klishchiivka.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 29 May, the ISW reported only one unsuccessful attack against Orikhovo-Vasylivka and stated that Russia's 106th Guards Airborne Division had been sent to reinforce the northern flank.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Both armies were less active in offensive operations in the Bakhmut direction on 30 May.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The ISW reported more unsuccessful Russian attacks against Orikhovo-Vasylivka, Bila Hora, Khromove, Ivanivske and Klishchiivka on 31 May.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: It also noted that the DPR's 1st "Wolves" Sabotage and Reconnaissance Brigade were active in Zaliznianske.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: They further assessed that Russia was cycling the DPR irregulars out of the Avdiivka-Donetsk front to be replaced by regular Russian forces in Bakhmut which, in turn, were to be sent to Avdiivka.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Maliar claimed that Ukrainian forces maintained control over the southwestern outskirts and entrance to Bakhmut city.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: By 1 June, only 90 Wagner personnel remained within the city.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Meanwhile, Russian attacks against Orikhovo-Vasylivka and Bila Hora remained unsuccessful.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Ukrainian Colonel Serhiy Cherevaty claimed that Russian units did not participate in Wagner's attrition style of warfare, and instead were using defensive tactics.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 2 June, Prigozhin accused Russian forces of attempting to attack Wagner forces using anti-tank mines, adding that there were no injuries.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 3 June, the ISW and the British defence ministry stated that under-strength Russian Airborne Forces, including those of the 76th Guards Air Assault Division, 106th Guards Airborne Division, and two other unspecified brigades had deployed to the Bakhmut area.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: However, Russian assault forces were primarily spearheaded by Wagner Group private military contractors, ex-convicts, reinforcements from other front lines in Ukraine, and recently mobilized recruits.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Then-Wagner Group leader Yevgeny Prigozhin reported that Wagner had employed 35,000 regular mercenary fighters.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: This had been supplemented by 50,000 mercenaries recruited from Russian prisons.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Wagner Group forces enjoyed advantages such as having its flanks covered by regular Russian airborne units, Wagner's own alleged use of Russian convicts as expendable infantry for attacks, and a 5:1 advantage in artillery firepower.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Wagner's forces reportedly consisted of a majority of recruited, under-trained ex-convicts and a minority of well-trained contractors serving as group commanders that operated efficiently and encrypted radio communications.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Some observers likened Russian tactics to Soviet-style human wave attacks, with Russian troops repeatedly assaulting Ukrainian positions with waves of infantry.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Some Ukrainian soldiers alleged that Wagner used its recruited ex-convicts as first wave "human bait" to reveal Ukrainian positions, with those refusing to advance being threatened with execution by firing squads or barrier troops.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: In late January 2023, Russia began supplanting some Wagner units with better-trained National Guard of Russia (Rosgvardia) and paratroopers, enabling them to make further advances on the front line.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: During the battle, Russian forces also targeted Bakhmut with Iranian-made drones after 450 of them were reportedly delivered to Russia in mid-October 2022.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Ukrainian forces reportedly consisted of a "hodgepodge of units", consisting initially of the 93rd Mechanized Brigade and the 58th Motorized Brigade, who were later reinforced by many other units, including special forces and territorial defense units.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Military analyst Michael Kofman characterized the Ukrainian strategy as "not one inch back", choosing to hold onto Bakhmut instead of falling back to the more defensible terrain west of the city.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Ukrainian commanders expended significant resources in Bakhmut, with their strategy being to keep Russia preoccupied with Bakhmut in order to prevent further offensives elsewhere on the front lines.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Conversely, Kofman argued that Ukraine's usage of well-trained National Guard and infantry units against poorly trained Wagner forces was tying down Ukraine's well-trained units and preventing them from conducting offensives of their own.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 10 January 2023, Polish think tank Rochan Consulting estimated Ukraine may have had ten brigades fighting in Bakhmut at the time, or around 30,000 personnel.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Prigozhin claimed on 17 April that 80,000 Ukrainian troops were defending Bakhmut.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 2 June 2023, Prigozhin reported that Wagner forces had been almost totally withdrawn from the battle.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: However, only small amounts of territory were recaptured, most notably Andriivka on 15 September and Klishchiivka around 17 September.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: By 29 November 2023, after the counteroffensive had stalled, Russian forces had regained the initiative and captured Khromove on the western outskirts of Bakhmut.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: They had also recaptured considerable portions of territory south of the Berkhivka Reservoir that they previously lost in May.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The British defence ministry and US National Security Council both insisted capturing Bakhmut would only be a "symbolic" victory for Russia rather than a strategic one.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: George Barros from the Institute for the Study of War characterized Russia's costly capture of Bakhmut as "a tactical victory" but "operational failure—contributing to the continued Russian strategic failure".
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: However, other observers and sources noted that Bakhmut is a key regional logistics and transport hub where two roads, the T0504 to Kostiantynivka and T0513 to Siversk, pass through.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Jon Roozenbeek, British Academy postdoctoral fellow at the University of Cambridge, likewise observed that securing Bakhmut would put the larger Donbas cities of Kramatorsk and Sloviansk within sufficient Russian artillery range.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Other sources concurred with this analysis, detailing how the city's significance "cannot be overstressed" as it lay at a fork pointing toward Kostiantynivka, Kramatorsk and Sloviansk.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Retired Ukrainian colonel Serhiy Hrabskyi suggested the Wagner Group was seeking glory in capturing Bakhmut, as leader Yevgeny Prigozhin was poised to reap significant monetary and political rewards if Wagner captured the city on behalf of the Russian government.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Researchers also pointed out that Bakhmut lies on the southeast of the Yuzivska gas field, and "control over the Yuzivska field would considerably strengthen Putin's strategy of weaponizing Russia's gas exports.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Even rendering Yuzivska a militarized no-man's-land or ruined waste could set Ukraine's movement toward energy sovereignty back for decades".
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Meanwhile, other Russian ultranationalist milbloggers celebrated the capture of Bakhmut but emphasized that "Bakhmut is not Berlin" and that the capture of the city would be simply another step in ongoing difficult operations to achieve Russian objectives in Ukraine.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: A Channel One broadcast on 1 July 2023 compared the siege of Mariupol to the battle for Bakhmut.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: It claimed that the latter had not been "the most important city from the point of view of the front", and that the Wagner Group fighters destroyed and captured the city for over 7.5 months.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The broadcast contrasted this to Mariupol (portrayed as "one of the most important centres of Ukrainian metallurgy"), which had been captured by the Russian Army and the National Guard of Russia after 71 days.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Furthermore, the battle was marked by some of the fiercest urban combat not seen in Europe since World War II, along with brutal hand-to-hand combat, drawing a number of comparisons to the Battle of Stalingrad.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: A Ukrainian soldier fighting in Bakhmut stated that the battle was "worse than Stalingrad", while another soldier described it as "real hell".
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: It was noted that "bloody battles unprecedented in recent decades" were occurring during the urban combat phase of the battle, as reported by Serhiy Cherevatyi, the spokesperson for Ukraine's eastern military command.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: In December 2022, an article reported that the battle was only matched by the "siege of Mariupol in its sheer brutality and casualty count".
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Intense shelling and frontal attacks by the Russians with minimal gains have been compared to the conditions of the First World War.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Russia and Ukraine have been engaged in a battle of attrition.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: However, according to NATO and Ukrainian officials Russian losses have been disproportionate by comparison.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 6 March, an assessment by NATO was that five times as many Russians were being killed, while the Ukrainian government claimed the Russians had "potentially" lost seven times as many soldiers as Ukraine.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: By early June, the Ukrainian military increased this claim to 7.5 times.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Between 6 and 31 January 2023, the Ukrainians claimed that 17,000 Russian soldiers lost their lives in the battle, which is nearly double the monthly average they had reported the previous year.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: If accurate, this would make it one of the deadliest days of the war by that point.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Meanwhile, two days later on 26 February, former Ukrainian Defence Minister Oleksii Reznikov stated that the Russians were losing 500 men killed and another 900 wounded in action every day fighting for Bakhmut.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: General Milley, appearing before the US House Committee on Armed Services on 29 March, described the reportedly high Russian casualties as a "slaughter-fest".
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 13 March, President Volodymyr Zelenskyy claimed that another 1,100 Russians had been killed and 1,500 wounded over the past week.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Meanwhile, the Russians claimed that 220 Ukrainian soldiers were killed in the city on 13 March alone.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Meanwhile, on 17 March, NATO estimated that the Russians were suffering 1,500 casualties per day, mainly from the fighting around Bakhmut.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: They also stated that Ukraine's losses were "an order of magnitude less" in fighting where "several thousand" shells a day had been fired by both sides.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 13 April, the Ukrainians claimed to have inflicted more than 4,500 casualties on the Russians in the previous two weeks alone.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 1 May, US National Security Council spokesman John Kirby said Russia had suffered more than 20,000 soldiers killed in combat and 80,000 wounded on the Bakhmut front since December 2022.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: He also said that half of these losses were from the Wagner Group.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: According to Western analysts, the spike in Russian casualties around this time had led to fears that the upcoming Ukrainian counteroffensive, planned for later in the spring, may become "carnage".
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The US said that such fierce fighting shows that the bloody carnage may become even worse after the start of Kyiv's counteroffensive to retake the occupied territories.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: At the same time, the UK Ministry of Defense reported 60,000 Russian personnel killed or wounded near Bakhmut since May 2022, while the Ukrainian military would claim 100,000 personnel killed or wounded in the ten months since August 2022.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Meanwhile, Ukraine claimed that Wagner had suffered over 81,000 casualties during the battle with 21,000 killed.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 2 July, the Ukrainians, while maintaining the figure of 21,000 "Wagnerites" killed, increased the number of wounded to 80,000 in "the east".
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: High losses were highlighted by Yevgeny Prigozhin as one of the key points in his criticism of Russian Ministry of Defense which eventually culminated in him launching an armed rebellion in June 2023.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 2 June, the ISW reiterated reports by the British defence ministry that Russia gained "48 centimetres for each of the 60,000 personnel killed or wounded near Bakhmut since May 2022".
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: In February 2024, Meduza and Mediazona assessed a total of 21,000 dead among all Russian forces attacking Soledar and Bakhmut.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: In June 2024 an investigation by the BBC and Mediazona revealed that 19,547 Wagnerites were killed during the Bakhmut offensive, of which 17,175 were convicts.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The number is based on documented family members' compensation payments and does not include at least several dozens of killed Wagner members who have not indicated any relatives.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Historian Nikolay Mitrokhin assessed the number of losses by the Russian side as 25,000 killed, with the same number being killed in Soledar.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 24 May 2023, Yevgeny Prigozhin stated that over 20,000 Wagner fighters had been killed in the Bakhmut battle, with half that number being former prisoners.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: He also reported that they had as many wounded as killed.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: However, former Russian officer and milblogger Igor Girkin opined that Wagner has likely suffered more than 40,000 killed in action (including 15,000 mercenaries and 25,000 convicts), with again the same number wounded.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: In part, this is based on a claimed discrepancy between the number of prisoners recruited and, the number either reported killed or repatriated at the end of their service.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Major General John Harrel, in his analysis of reported figures, highlights that out of the 50,000 convicts recruited by the Wagner Group, only 25,000 received pardons, with the remaining 25,000 killed.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: According to one report, the number of professional mercenaries killed may have reached 15,000, bringing the total number of Wagner personnel killed to approximately 40,000, with overall casualties – both killed and wounded – amounting to 80,000.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Harrel estimates Wagner's personnel strength at 85,000 at the beginning of 2023, declining to 5,000 following the Battle of Bakhmut.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: During the battle, Ukrainian soldiers gave Oleksandr Syrskyi, the officer commanding Ukrainian forces in Bakhmut, the nicknames "Butcher" and "General 200" due to Syrskyi's use of high casualty tactics during the battle.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The nickname General 200 is a reference to Cargo 200, the Soviet military code for military fatalities.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Military observers estimated that the average Ukrainian soldier's survival time in Bakhmut was 3 days.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 31 May 2023, the city's mayor, Oleksii Reva, reported 204 residents had been killed and 505 injured since the invasion began, with 500 residents remaining in the city.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 17 May, the Ukrainian military published an interview with a captured Wagner mercenary, who was also a former Russian convict.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: In the video, the man claimed that he had been wounded and then abandoned to die by his comrades in a trench for four days before he was taken prisoner, indicating that Russian forces were leaving behind their wounded soldiers.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: He also said that Russian forces were suffering great losses, saying there is a "trench where a corpse is lying on a corpse".
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Wagner leader Yevgeny Prigozhin had previously suggested Wagner was deliberately turning Bakhmut into a "meat grinder" to inflict heavy attritional casualties on Ukrainian forces.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: One Western official gave an inverse view, saying the battle was "giving Ukraine a unique opportunity to kill a lot of Russians", due to purportedly poor Russian tactics.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Military analysts characterize the battle as attritional for both sides, and "both sides thought that attrition favoured them".
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: During the battle, regular Russian forces managed to fortify the southern and northern fronts and train the 300,000 soldiers conscripted in the fall of 2022.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: After extensive attrition at Bakhmut, Russia did not conduct further advances west towards Kramatorsk and Sloviansk in the months after the city's capture.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: British military analyst Ben Barry cautioned that Russia may have reserved its more elite, well-prepared units for future operations, but nevertheless considered the victory to be "pyrrhic".
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Other independent analysts and news organizations, nonetheless, considered the attritional battle an overall Russian victory.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: American officials repeatedly voiced concern over Ukraine's decision to keep a large presence in the Bakhmut area despite unfavorable conditions.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Some Ukrainian media sources and bloggers suggested that the POW was Oleksandr Igorevich Matsievskyi, a soldier from Nizhyn who had been deployed to Bakhmut and gone missing in January.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 8 April 2023, a video was posted on a pro-Russian social media channel, showing a pair of decapitated corpses on the ground beside a destroyed armoured vehicle.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The video's (Russian-speaking) recorder, stating how the vehicle had been destroyed by a mine, laughed that "They killed them.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: They came up to them and cut their heads off." According to the channel the incident occurred in Bakhmut and was done by mercenaries from the Wagner Group.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Another video posted on Twitter shows a soldier with a yellow armband (a symbol of the Ukrainian military) screaming before he is beheaded by a Russian-speaking soldier with a knife.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: Foliage in the background suggested the video was taken during the summer.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The Institute for the Study of War also reported that an image of the head of a Ukrainian soldier mounted on a spike was circulating around Russian social media.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: On 5 July 2023, the Ukrainians claimed that the Russians had attacked them with an arsenic based chemical weapon called lewisite in an artillery bombardment, which had previously been used during World War I.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The strike reportedly left some Ukrainian soldiers with symptoms of chemical weapons exposure.
title: Battle of Bakhmut

text: The siege of Mariupol began on 24 February 2022, as part of the Russian invasion of Ukraine, and lasted until 20 May.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: It saw fighting between the Russian Armed Forces (alongside the Donetsk People's Republic People's Militia) and the Ukrainian Armed Forces for control over the city of Mariupol in southeastern Ukraine.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Mariupol is located in Ukraine's Donetsk Oblast, and following the siege, it was initially controlled by the DPR, supported by occupying Russian troops.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: However, it was later subjected to Russia's annexation of southeastern Ukraine, and remains under direct Russian control as of 2026.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: During the Russian siege, the Red Cross described the situation in Mariupol as "apocalyptic" while Ukrainian authorities accused Russia of engineering a major humanitarian crisis in the city.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Ukrainian officials reported that approximately 25,000 civilians had been killed and that at least 95% of the city had been destroyed during the fighting, primarily by large-scale Russian bombardments.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Major combat operations in the city effectively ended on 16 May 2022, after Ukraine's Azov Regiment surrendered at the Azovstal Iron and Steel Works.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Some Western reports described the siege as a pyrrhic or symbolic Russian victory, with others noting that the humanitarian impact of the takeover was a "reputational disaster" for Russia.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Nonetheless, the loss of the city was a significant defeat for Ukraine.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Numerous Russian war crimes were alleged, including unlawful attacks on civilians, killings, blocking of humanitarian aid, forced displacement or deportation, looting and rape.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: It was the largest city in the Ukrainian-controlled portion of Donetsk Oblast, and was also one of the largest Russian-speaking cities in Ukraine.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Mariupol was a major industrial hub, home of the Illich and Azovstal Iron and Steel Works, and the largest city on the Sea of Azov.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Control of its port on the western shore of the Sea of Azov is vital to the economy of Ukraine.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: For Russia, it would allow a land route to Crimea and full control over the sea, thereby facilitating passage of Russian marine traffic.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: In 2014 after the Revolution of Dignity, Mariupol was swept by pro-Russian protests against the new government.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Tensions erupted into the war in Donbas in early May, and during the unrest, militiamen of the separatist and Russian-backed DPR took control of the city and forced Ukrainian troops to abandon it during the first battle for Mariupol.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: However, the following month, Ukrainian forces recaptured the city in an offensive.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: In August, the DPR and Russian troops captured the village of Novoazovsk, 45 km east of Mariupol near the Russo-Ukrainian border.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: With the town captured and forces renewed, in September the DPR attempted to capture the city again in the second battle for Mariupol.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Fighting reached the eastern outskirts, but the separatists were repelled.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: In October, then-DPR Prime Minister Alexander Zakharchenko vowed to retake the city.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Mariupol was then indiscriminately bombed by rockets in January 2015.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Fearing a future third offensive into Mariupol, in February Ukrainian forces launched a surprise attack into Shyrokyne, a village located 11 km east of Mariupol.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Their objective was the expulsion of separatist forces from the city limits and the creation of a buffer zone.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: The separatists withdrew from Shyrokyne four months later.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: The Kerch Strait incident raised tensions; fearing war, Ukraine briefly declared martial law.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: One of the most instrumental groups for the recapture and subsequent defense of Mariupol was Azov Battalion, a Ukrainian volunteer militia, controversial for its openly neo-Nazi and ultranationalist members.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: By November 2014 Azov was integrated into the National Guard of Ukraine, with Mariupol as its headquarters.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: As one of Vladimir Putin's stated goals for the invasion was the "denazification" of Ukraine, Mariupol represented an important ideological and symbolical target for the Russian forces.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Prior to the siege, around 100,000 residents left Mariupol, according to the city's deputy mayor.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: On the morning of 25 February, Russian forces advanced from DPR territory in the east towards Mariupol.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: They encountered Ukrainian forces near the village of Pavlopil, who repelled the Russian advance.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: The mayor of Mariupol, Vadym Boychenko, said 22 Russian tanks had been destroyed in the skirmish.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: That evening, the Russian Navy, drawing on the capabilities provided by the Black Sea Fleet, reportedly began an amphibious assault on the Sea of Azov coastline 70 kilometres (43 mi) west of Mariupol.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: A US defense official stated that the Russians may have deployed thousands of marines from this beachhead.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: On 26 February, Russian forces continued to bombard Mariupol with artillery.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Later, the government of Greece announced that 10 ethnic Greek civilians had been killed by Russian strikes at Mariupol — six in the village of Sartana and four in the village of Buhas.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: On the morning of 27 February, mayor Boychenko said that a Russian tank column had advanced on Mariupol from the DPR but were repulsed by Ukrainian forces, with six Russian soldiers captured.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Later that day, a six-year-old girl in Mariupol was killed by Russian shelling.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Pavlo Kyrylenko, governor of Donetsk Oblast, stated that fighting in Mariupol had continued throughout the night of 27 February.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Throughout 28 February, the city remained under Ukrainian control despite being surrounded by Russian troops and constantly shelled.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Electricity, gas, and internet connection to most of the city was cut during the evening.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Russian artillery later bombarded Mariupol, causing over 21 injuries.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: The city was fully surrounded on 2 March, after which the siege intensified.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Russian shelling killed a teenager and wounded two other teenagers who were playing soccer outside.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Boychenko announced the city was suffering from a water outage and had experienced massive casualties.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: He also said Russian forces were preventing civilians from exiting.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Later on 2 March, Russian artillery targeted a densely populated neighborhood of Mariupol, shelling it for nearly 15 hours.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: The neighborhood was massively damaged as a result, with deputy mayor Sergiy Orlov reporting that "at least hundreds of people are dead".
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: On the morning of 3 March, the city was shelled again by Russian troops.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Eduard Basurin, the spokesman for the DPR militia, formally called on the besieged Ukrainian forces in Mariupol to surrender or face "targeted strikes".
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Russian Ministry of Defense spokesman Igor Konashenkov reported that DPR forces had tightened the siege, and that three nearby settlements had been captured: Sartana, Vynohradne, and Vodiane.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: On 4 March, Boychenko stated that the city's supplies were running out, and called for a humanitarian evacuation corridor and Ukrainian military reinforcements.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: He also stated that Russian BM-21 Grads were shelling the city's hospitals and that Mariupol residents no longer had heat, running water, or electricity.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Later that day, a temporary ceasefire was proposed for the Mariupol region in order to allow citizens to evacuate.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: On 5 March, the Ukrainian government announced its desire to evacuate 200,000 civilians from Mariupol.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: The International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) announced that it would act as a guarantor for a new ceasefire to allow for this evacuation.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: The Red Cross described the situation as "extremely dire".
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: After three days of shelling, a ceasefire was announced to be in effect from 11:00 to 16:00.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Civilians began to evacuate from Mariupol along a humanitarian corridor to the city of Zaporizhzhia.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: As civilians entered the evacuation corridor, Russian forces continued shelling the city, forcing evacuees to turn back.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Ukrainian authorities later reported that Russian forces had failed to observe the ceasefire and continued to shell the city.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Russian officials accused Ukrainian forces of not allowing civilians to evacuate towards Russia.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: The DPR reported that only 17 civilians had been evacuated from Mariupol.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: On 6 March, the Red Cross announced that a second attempt to evacuate civilians from Mariupol had again failed.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Anton Herashchenko, a Ukrainian official, said the second attempt at a humanitarian corridor for civilians in Mariupol ended with a Russian bombardment.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: The Red Cross reported "devastating scenes of human suffering" in Mariupol.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: The bombardment also hit the city's last functioning cellular tower.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: On 7 March, the ICRC Director of Operations stated that humanitarian corridor agreements had only been made in principle, without the precision required for implementation, needing routes, times and whether goods could be brought in to be agreed.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: The ICRC team found that one of the proposed corridor roads was mined.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: The ICRC was facilitating talks between Russian and Ukrainian forces.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: On 8 March, another attempt to evacuate civilians was made, but the Ukrainian government accused Russia of violating the ceasefire again by bombing the evacuation corridor.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: On 9 March, the Associated Press reported that scores of Ukrainian civilians and soldiers were being buried by city workers in a mass grave at one of the city's cemeteries.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Russian shelling had hit the cemetery the previous day, interrupting the burials and damaging a wall.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Later, another attempted ceasefire failed after Orlov reported that Russian soldiers had opened fire on construction workers and evacuation points.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Orlov described the city's supply shortage as so severe that residents were melting snow to get water.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Later that day, the Mariupol City Council issued a statement that a Russian airstrike had struck and destroyed a maternity ward and children's hospital.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Later, a vehicle convoy of 82 ethnic Greeks was able to leave the city via a humanitarian corridor.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: On 13 March, Boychenko stated that Russian forces had bombed the city at least 22 times in the previous 24 hours, with a hundred bombs, and added that the last food and water reserves in the city were being depleted.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: More than 160 cars were able to leave the city on 14 March at 13:00 local time, the first evacuation allowed during the siege.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: The Russian Ministry of Defense stated that 450 tonnes of humanitarian aid had been brought to the city after Russian forces captured the outskirts.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Ukrainian military officials were later said to have killed 150 Russian soldiers and destroyed 10 Russian vehicles.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: On the same day, Ramzan Kadyrov, the head of Chechnya, stated that Chechen soldiers were participating in the siege and had briefly entered Mariupol before retreating.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Kadyrov also stated that Adam Delimkhanov, a close ally and member of the State Duma, was the commander of Chechen forces in Mariupol.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: The funeral for Captain Alexey Glushchak of the GRU was held in Tyumen, and it was revealed he died near Mariupol, likely in the early stages of the siege.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: On 15 March, around 4,000 vehicles with about 20,000 civilians were able to leave the city.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Ukrainian government official Anton Herashchenko said that Russian Major General Oleg Mityaev, commander of the 150th Motorized Rifle Division, was killed when Russian forces tried to storm the city.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: The Donetsk Regional Drama Theatre, sheltering hundreds of civilians, was hit by a Russian airstrike on 16 March and destroyed.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Pavlo Kyrylenko, the governor of Donetsk Oblast, later stated that Russian forces had also targeted the Neptune swimming pool.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: On 18 March, DPR forces said they had captured the Mariupol airport from Ukrainian forces.
title: Siege of Mariupol

text: Clashes later reached the city center, according to the mayor and on 19 March, Russian and Ukrainian forces began fighting at the Azovstal steel plant.
title: Siege of Mariupol
